File.Name: f129b_f129-010.jpg
Notes:
16213583217 This looks like a garden of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora, Orobanchaceae) plants, but it's all natural. They are growing under Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fabaceae) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2015)

These handsome flowering plants are root parasites that grow from the roots of another shrub. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to a host species. See this photo and read the interesting comments.
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Bigberry Manzanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fabaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fabaceae; haustoria

File.Name: f129c_f129-005.jpg
Notes: 16398483802 This is looking past the lower part of Fremont Ridge towards Little Pine Mountain in the background. I got this photo from a place along Stagecoach Road. The lower hills are covered with white-flowered shrubs of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The shrubs look a lot like Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus), but they have opposite leaves and favor the interior side of our mountains. I haven't found the shrubs flowering yet in an accessible place, but they're out there. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2015)

File.Name: f129d_f129-022.jpg
Notes: 16397558291 Here are two shots of (I guess) the Tiger Tricholma (Tricholoma pardinum) mushroom in the woods today. It is growing in deep oak mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The almost monochrome cap is striking. It has notched gills and a mealy "farinaceous" smell. It's a large, attractive mushroom that is said to be poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2015)

File.Name: f129e_f129-013.jpg
Notes:
15779162253 This is a sun-checked single Western Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushroom growing at the base of a dead shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods. This is only the second one I've found this year, see this photo. I've been checking all the usual trees, but they haven't been there. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2015)

Usually these mushrooms grow in clusters, but not the two I've found this year. They are said to glow in the dark, but our west coast mushrooms don't seem to do that. They are quite poisonous - don't eat them!

File.Name: f129f_f129-011.jpg
Notes: 16397390741 I believe these are two more Wine-Colored Agaricus (Agaricus subrutilescens) mushrooms in the woods today, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They are large beautiful mushrooms, probably edible though I haven't tried them yet. Our woodland agaricus species are a risky bunch, but I'm ready to try this one. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2015)

File.Name: f130a_f130-005.jpg
Notes:
16406185992 This is looking closer at the remarkable tiny micro-moth (Lepidoptera) in my [Previous] photo. It's perched in the throat of one of the first new flowers of California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family that I've seen this year. These moths have metallic silver on their backs, though this one is picking up some color from the flower. I guess it is family Heliodonidae, maybe genus Neoheliodines. These moths are said to have an affinity with this plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 January 2015)

It was both gloomy and breezy today, a tough combination for photos. I did the best I could. It's colder tonight, and there is fog coming in from the valley, which is not the usual direction. A change in the weather?
CommonNames: micro-moth; California Four O'Clock; Wishbone Bush
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Heliodonidae; Neoheliodines
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Heliodonidae; Neoheliodines

File.Name: f130b_f130-003.jpg
Notes:
16220684169 This is the first beautiful new flower of California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. If you look close, you can spot a couple of tiny micro-moths (Lepidoptera) on the flower - one in the throat and another on the upper petal. I found several more of these tiny moths on the same plant, so I guess there is a connection. I guess they are family Heliodonidae, maybe genus Neoheliodines. These moths are said to have an affinity with this plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 January 2015)

The flowers normally close early in the morning and open in the afternoon - around 4:00, really! I wonder if the moths hide in the flowers during the day? The flowers stay open later on gloomy days like this was, which suits my schedule. It was both gloomy and breezy today, a tough combination for photos.
CommonNames: California Four O'Clock; Wishbone Bush; micro-moths
ScientificName: Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Lepidoptera; Heliodonidae; Neoheliodines
associatedTaxa: Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Lepidoptera; Heliodonidae; Neoheliodines

File.Name: f130c_f130-035.jpg
Notes: 16380783796 I'm not proud of this photo, but it does show a Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly, the first I've managed to photograph this year. I've been seeing them flying through the woods for the past week. I stopped by Paradise Store on my way home today, and there it was, working the flowers of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) on the edge of the parking lot! I got this one photo from a distance and tried to follow it around, but it didn't like my attention and took off. This is a deep crop. I hope I'll get better photos of this butterfly on native plants, but this is my first. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 January 2015)

File.Name: f130d_f130-018.jpg
Notes: 16220758907 These are the first open flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I've gotten close to this year. They've been going for a while, see this photo from yesterday. The shrubs look a lot like Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) on the coastal side of the mountains, but they have opposite rather than alternate leaves and favor the interior side of our mountains. They also seem to have yellowish nectar disks at the base of the flowers, as opposed to the deep blue nectar disks of C. megacarpus - compare this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 January 2015)
ScientificName: Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus megacarpus; C. megacarpus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus megacarpus; C. megacarpus

File.Name: f130e_f130-026.jpg
Notes: 15786575063 I'm not sure what kind of mushrooms these are, but they are rather pretty. They are growing on an old pile of wood chips, covered with oak leaves. They appear to have brownish spores. The genera Stropharia and Bolbitius come to mind. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 January 2015)

File.Name: f130f_f130-027.jpg
Notes: 15784054074 These are some kind of odd Cup Fungus (Pezizaceae or kin) growing from the oak leaves. There might well be shredded oak mulch under the leaves. I found these not too far from the mushrooms in my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 January 2015)

File.Name: f131a_f131-027.jpg
Notes:
16414752425 These are the first beautiful new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. All parts of the plant except the actual flowers are very glandular. Last year I found the first flowers on January 18 in our garden, but the garden flowers aren't ready yet. These natives are looking better than they did last year. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

It was another beautiful day, without a drop of rain in the forecast. I am still finding new mushrooms, but I don't know how much longer it will last. I'll get them while I can!

File.Name: f131b_f131-025.jpg
Notes:
16414489765 I believe this is a Snail Eater beetle in the genus Scaphinotus, family Carabidae, order Coleoptera posed on an oak leaf. They have a wide abdomen, a narrow thorax, and long jaws to penetrate snail shells. They are not common. Unfortunately, this one is dead - at least it didn't move while I got the photo or for many minutes afterwards. It must be recently deceased, because its legs are still flexible and it still has mites. I wouldn't be surprised if it walked away after I left! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

At first glance, it looks like a Darkling Beetle (family Tenebrionidae), but all the details are wrong.

File.Name: f131c_f131-005.jpg
Notes: 15794268973 I'm sure this is the caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). It's quite small, maybe 1/2 inch long or less, perched twig-like of a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It looks a lot like a miniature of the large caterpillar in this photo. It's head end is on the right, with two pair of prolegs supporting it on the left. It seems to have a strand of webbing going from its head back to the leaf. Sure, the white balance is a little funky, above the retaining wall where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

File.Name: f131d_f131-017.jpg
Notes:
15794232313 This is a beautiful pair of "The Blusher" mushrooms - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens (see below) under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. They are large and stately. The pinkish "blushing" of the cap and stem is the hallmark of this beautiful species. I didn't pick these, but see this photo and the one [Previous] to that. They are probably not edible, but they sure are good for the eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

Apparently this west coast form has been renamed Amanita novinupta to distinguish it from the east coast A. rubescens. Those darned mycologists!
ScientificName: Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita novinupta; A. rubescens
associatedTaxa: Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita novinupta; A. rubescens

File.Name: f131e_f131-016.jpg
Notes:
16388258856 Pizza anyone?

This is looking down at the cap of "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens (see below) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. The pinkish "blushing" of the cap and stem is the hallmark of this beautiful species. See this photo and the one [Previous] to that. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

Apparently this west coast form has been renamed Amanita novinupta to distinguish it from the east coast A. rubescens. Those darned mycologists!
ScientificName: Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita novinupta; A. rubescens
associatedTaxa: Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita novinupta; A. rubescens

File.Name: f131f_f131-030.jpg
Notes: 16414058635 I found these cute Elfin Saddle mushrooms in the woods today, with non-fluted stalks and the undersides of the caps not obviously hairy. Maybe they are Helvella compressa or H. albella? They seem very dark, almost black - probably too dark for H. compressa. They are growing under oak and bay trees. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

File.Name: f201a_f201-030.jpg
Notes:
16423109012 This is looking close at the surprisingly intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are related to avocado and sassafras. There's a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with four anthers each, with two orange glands at the base of each, finally surrounded by an outer circle of six more fat stamens with four anthers each. The whole thing is contained within a circle of (usually) 6 sepals. They are strange and wonderful flowers for such a common tree in our woods. If you haven't looked close at the flowers of this tree, I recommend you do so! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2015)

It was another lovely day, in the 70s on our porch and no doubt warmer in the sun. No wonder our native plants are confused!

File.Name: f201b_f201-009.jpg
Notes:
16236456590 These are the first new flowers of native Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I've found this year. Last year I found the first flowers on March 11, so this is another shrub that is flowering early this year. Everything is coming up early in this unusually warm winter. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2015)

We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that cover the mountains, at least in wetter years - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes, and this Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits. All the flowers look about the same. This one has opposite toothed leaves and "corky stipules" - those dark cones at the base of the leaves, not very visible in this photo. The leaves are whitish-tomentose underneath.
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius

File.Name: f201c_f201-005.jpg
Notes: 16422775012 These are the first new flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I've found this year. Last year I also found the first flowers on the same date, so I guess they are right on schedule. These flowers were high in the air, so you'll have to wait for a closer shot of the pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2015)

File.Name: f201d_f201-012.jpg
Notes:
16235995618 These are the first new flowers of Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family that I've found this year. The feathery seed plumes that follow the flowers later in summer are probably more impressive, but without the flowers there will be no seeds! See this photo. I didn't find the first flowers until April 6 last year, though I found them on February 23 in 2013. Everything is coming up early in this unusually warm winter. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2015)

I didn't notice at the time, but that sure looks like a gall on the second leaf from the left in the upper half of this photo. That's a new one for me. I'll have to get back to this plant.

File.Name: f201e_f201-002.jpg
Notes: 16236094680 These are pretty little yellow Bolbitius titubans aka Bolbitius vitellinus mushrooms coming up in the shade along our driveway. Despite appearances, they leave a rusty-brown spore print. I got these photos of the same mushroom a day apart. They are not quite to scale, but compare the oak leaves to the right of the stem to get a sense of it. They are quite ephemeral - not quite "here today and gone tomorrow", but close to it. The buttons are quite beautiful, but they lose some of their beauty as they expand. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2015)

File.Name: f201f_f131-009.jpg
Notes: 16423395115 This is a cluster of very dark mushrooms coming up in our garden. You can see a blue-black spore print on a couple of leaves, e.g. under the front mushroom on the left. The spore print looks like an oil slick on the leaves! (That blue color may be my funky white balance in this place where the sun never shines at this time of year.) I guess they are some kind of Psathyrella mushrooms, though I can't recall seeing such dark ones. The caps are "hygrophanous" - lightening in color as they dry out. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

File.Name: f202a_f202-028.jpg
Notes:
16244598518 Here are new flower buds on an uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family, growing along East Camino Cielo road. The buds look about ready to open so I'll go ahead and record this as a "first flower", but it may take them a while to fully open. I only find this plant in a few shady places where the sun never shines, and I've never seen a berry. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on against climate change. The foliage is holly-like and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2015)

The last time I saw one of these in flower was on April 24, 2012 - see this photo. That year I found buds a full month before on March 24, which is why I say it might take a while for the buds to open. The rain year of 2011-12 was a lot like this year - good early rains, but then the pipe went dry after the new year. These plants have got through the last two drought years without flowering. Everything is coming out early in this unusually warm winter.

It was another lovely day, in the 70s on our porch and no doubt warmer in the sun. No wonder our native plants are confused! There is a slight chance of rain next Friday and Saturday. If there's nothing else, you grasp at straws!

File.Name: f202b_f202-011.jpg
Notes:
16246198127 This is the first of our native Sanicles in the genusSanicula of the Apiaceae plant family that I've seen in flower this year. I believe this one is the Coast Sanicle (Sanicula laciniata). The species name "laciniata" sound like "lacerate" - and the leaves look like someone went at them with scissors. Last year I didn't find the first one until late March, but everything is coming out early in this unusually warm winter. That's cool, but I wonder what it means for later in the year? (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2015)

The Sanicles are kind of a "collector's genus". We have a number of species in our mountains. The flowers all look the same - little yellow balls - but the foliage is different. This one has sharp points on its palmate leaves, which distinguishes it from the similar Pacific Sanicle (S. crassicaulis). The leaves are also much thinner.
CommonNames: Sanicles; Coast Sanicle; Sanicles; Pacific Sanicle
ScientificName: Sanicula; Apiaceae; Sanicula laciniata; S. crassicaulis
associatedTaxa: Sanicula; Apiaceae; Sanicula laciniata; S. crassicaulis

File.Name: f202c_f202-006.jpg
Notes: 16244637810 These are the first new flowers of native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family that have been low enough to photograph. It's my favorite native tree for sure, though this flowering stem looks a little anemic. The urn-like flowers resemble manzanita, but the wheel-like base of the flowers is different - look above the first flower left of center. Last year, I didn't find the first flowers until the end of March, see this photo. Everything is coming out early in this unusually warm winter. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2015)

File.Name: f202d_f202-018.jpg
Notes: 15809327394 I found this lovely Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in the woods today, with a pup at the base. They are edible and tasty, though I didn't collect this one for the table. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start violet-blue, but they soon fade to tan. This one is starting to turn. We also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar. I've confused them before. See this photo and this one. One useful field trait is that real Blewitts usually bring up a large ball of mycelium-connected material at the base. I trimmed this one a bit for the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2015)

File.Name: f202e_f202-012.jpg
Notes: 16430757912 I believe this is the Yellow-Staining Milk Cap (Lactarius xanthogalactus) mushroom, formerly known as Lactarius vinaceorufescens or Lactarius chrysorheus. (Those darn mycologists!) This was the only one I found today, so here are two shots of it. I tried slicing the gills with my pocket knife, but I only got a drop or two of the white latex that quickly turns yellow. When I tried digging it out, the maggot-eaten stem broke off. I stuck it back in place for the photo. This one is growing under both Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fabaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2015)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Milk Cap; Bigberry Manzanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Lactarius xanthogalactus; Lactarius vinaceorufescens; Lactarius chrysorheus; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lactarius xanthogalactus; Lactarius vinaceorufescens; Lactarius chrysorheus; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fabaceae

File.Name: f202f_f130-007.jpg
Notes: 16405638916 This is another photo of the remarkable micro-moth (Lepidoptera) perched on a dead stem of California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. These moths have metallic silver on their backs, and they seem to have an affinity with these plants. I guess it is family Heliodonidae, maybe genus Neoheliodines. See this photo and the one [Previous] to that. I got this photo a few days ago and saved it for the Moth Monday group. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 January 2015)
CommonNames: micro-moth; California Four O'Clock; Wishbone Bush
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Heliodonidae; Neoheliodines
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Mirabilis californica; Nyctaginaceae; Heliodonidae; Neoheliodines

File.Name: f203a_f203-001.jpg
Notes:
15819486283 These are the first new catkin-flowers of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Last year I didn't find real flowers until March 18, though I did find one oddball tree flowering on January 12, and the year before I found them on February 9. I looked close, and I didn't find any catkin galls yet. The new leaves and catkins are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2015)

I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, and while driving home I saw Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) starting to leaf out and flower. After I got home, I drove up into the higher mountains and saw Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum, Aceraceae) starting to flower. I didn't get photos of either, but it's happening. "Curiouser and curiouser!" as Alice said. Everything is coming out early in this unusually warm winter.

It was another lovely day, up to 75 on our upstairs porch and no doubt warmer in the sun. No wonder our native plants are confused! We do have an increasing chance of rain next Friday and Saturday. Bring it on!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Valley Oak; Bigleaf Maple
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Quercus lobata; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Quercus lobata; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae

File.Name: f203b_f203-022.jpg
Notes:
16439501355 These are the first new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family in the mountains, though I've seeing some flowering shrubs lower in the mountains as I drive to town. Last year I found them on January 21, so I reckon they are right on schedule. This shrub was growing down an embankment, so I couldn't get closer. Give me time! (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 February 2015)

The old Spanish name for this plant is Islay. The ripe fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is very thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching them. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry!

File.Name: f203c_f203-031.jpg
Notes: 16253482277 These are the first new flowers of the White-flowering Current (Ribes indecorum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family that I've found this year, growing near the summit of La Cumbre Peak. This shrub looks similar to the more common Chaparral Currant (R. malvaceum) - but it seems more compact, it grows higher in the mountains on mostly north-facing slopes, and it has almost white flowers which open later in the season. If you look close, you can see a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) working the top flower on the right. My previous "first flower" sighting for this plant was March 12 last year, though I don't get up into these higher mountains every week. Everything is coming out early in this unusually warm winter. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 February 2015)
CommonNames: White-flowering Current; Chaparral Currant; Bee Fly
ScientificName: Ribes indecorum; Grossulariaceae; R. malvaceum; Bombyliidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Ribes indecorum; Grossulariaceae; R. malvaceum; Bombyliidae; Diptera

File.Name: f203d_f203-012.jpg
Notes: 16251943700 Here are beautiful new flowers and foliage of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Nothing makes a horizontal spray of flowers like these guys! They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects, and there were hummers working the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2015)

File.Name: f203e_f203-025.jpg
Notes: 16252989679 This is a pink-spored Volvariella speciosa mushroom in the Pluteacea family growing in the weedy grass near La Cumbre Peak today. They look like Amanita mushrooms with a "death cup" on the stem - but they don't have a ring, they have pink spores, and they don't grow under oaks. They really don't have "amanita vibes". They are said to be edible but mediocre. I'm not tempted to try them. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 February 2015)

File.Name: f203f_f203-016.jpg
Notes:
15819008323 These are the tiny galls of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). They are about the size of a BB or a millet grain. Note that the lower one already has an exit hole. I guess they are the galls of the spring bisexual generation of wasps, producing both male and female wasps. They will mate and produce the larger and more interesting galls of their summer unisexual generation, which contain only females that reproduce parthenogenetically. These will make next years crop of spring bisexual galls. Galls are complicated like that. I have no idea what kind of summer galls these wasps will produce. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2015)

I wonder if this complicated life cycle has something to do with the biology of the Scrub Oaks. These oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring. So maybe the wasps that form their galls on leaves need something different to bridge the gap?
CommonNames: Cynipid Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak; Scrub Oaks

File.Name: f204a_f204-029.jpg
Notes:
15824336454 This is a mating pair of "true bugs" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, maybe Western Boxelder Bugs (Boisea rubrolineata) in the family Rhopalidae, though the closest Boxelder tree is about 20 miles away down the river past Buellton. They are having their nuptials on new catkins of native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)

It was another lovely day, up to 75 on our upstairs porch where we are getting a bit more sun as the season progresses. We do have an increasing chance of rain next Friday and Saturday, now it's up to 80%. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Boisea rubrolineata; Rhopalidae; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Boisea rubrolineata; Rhopalidae; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae

File.Name: f204b_f204-026.jpg
Notes: 15826625513 This is a small succulent insect gall growing on a new catkin of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know whose gall it is. It's interesting that the stamens of the catkins emerge through the gall tissue. Joyce Gross shows a similar gall on her Undescribed Galls webpage. There is a Succulent Gall Wasp (Neuroterus fragilis, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but its said make its galls on petioles and leaves rather than catkins - see this photo. This is something else. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)
ScientificName: Quercus lobata; Fagaceae; Neuroterus fragilis; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus lobata; Fagaceae; Neuroterus fragilis; Hymenoptera

File.Name: f204c_f204-020.jpg
Notes: 16260661527 Here are a bunch of "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Two things are odd about this photo. First, the tree still has lots of old leaves at a time when the same tree is starting to flower on different branches. Second, there are already new galls forming on the stem in the middle, above the second large gall on the bottom. The new gall looks like a lime! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae

File.Name: f204d_f204-009.jpg
Notes: 16445571862 Here are the first new flowers of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Actually these are not-quite-open buds, but they are so close that I'll record this a "first flower". Last year I found the first flower buds in March. Everything is coming out early in this unusually warm winter. They are pretty to look at, but don't touch if you are sensitive! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)

File.Name: f204e_f204-007.jpg
Notes: 16444656651 These are stunning new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. I found the first flowers in the mountains a month ago in shady mountain places, but these are the first flowers I've captured in the sunlight. The sun really is climbing a bit higher every day and the days are a bit longer! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)

File.Name: f204f_f204-014.jpg
Notes: 16420332656 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Soap Plant (Chenopodium californicum) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. These flowers mostly have open pistils, which I guess come before the stamens. This is the other soap plant, very different from the monocot Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum, Agavaceae) that was also used for soap. The Chumash dug and scrapped the roots of this one for use in washing hair, skin, and clothing. I've never dug up a root to see if it works - I'd rather see the whole plant. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)
ScientificName: Chenopodium californicum; Chenopodiaceae; the other; Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Chenopodium californicum; Chenopodiaceae; the other; Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Agavaceae

File.Name: f205a_f205-011.jpg
Notes:
16266347518 This is a new Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working a flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I've been waiting to get a photo of these fine butterflies on a native flower, but this gooseberry was a surprise. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2015)

Our chance of rain looks better and better for Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. We're on the southern end of this storm, but it still sounds like we'll get an inch or more of rainfall. This storm is coming just when we need it. It's also good timing for a rainy weekend. I have the new newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to build, and I need an excuse to start.

File.Name: f205b_f205-019.jpg
Notes: 16266285548 Here is a caterpillar of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). That's its head on the right munching a leaf and its rear end on the left, leaving a package of frass. Our caterpillars are growing up, an inch long now. Darn it's hard to get enough DoF to photograph these caterpillars with those tall spines on their back! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2015)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f205c_f205-027.jpg
Notes: 16427878356 This is a troop of "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) growing from a dead stump in the woods today. They are brown mushrooms with reddish-brown gills. I don't know know anything about them except here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2015)

File.Name: f205d_f204-018.jpg
Notes: 16267561329 This is the first new flower of native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family, with a little Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the upper-right petal. I think of these as the "real California Poppy" in our area because so many seeds of Eschscholzia californica are scattered along the roads. The difference is that these poppies lack the collar or disk below the flower that California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) has. I guess you'll have to take my word for that, though I did check! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)
CommonNames: Tufted Poppy; Assassin Bug; California Poppy
ScientificName: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia californica
associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia californica

File.Name: f205e_f204-024.jpg
Notes: 16453731595 Here is another photo of new staminate catkins of a native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's so odd to see these new catkins with old leaves on this deciduous tree, but that's how it is this year. It's been so warm this winter that the trees never did shed all of last year's old leaves. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 February 2015)

File.Name: f205f_f131-023.jpg
Notes: 15831108554 This is a "Beautiful Bug Butt" shot of an uncommon Snail Eater beetle in the genus Scaphinotus, family Carabidae, order Coleoptera burrowed in the oak leaf. They have a wide abdomen, a narrow thorax, and long jaws to penetrate snail shells. See this photo for the whole insect. This is just how I found it. I didn't realize when I got this photo that the beetle was deceased, but I should have - it's missing the tarsi on its left leg. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2015)

File.Name: f206a_f206-017.jpg
Notes:
16273621750 This is looking into a new flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, with a troop of stamens and three green pistils looking out of the large carpels. Some flowers only have two carpels, see my [Previous] photo. Usually the flowers point down towards the earth, but I propped this one up with a stick. They are one of my favorite flowers, probably fire-followers in this place recovering from the White Fire not quite two years ago. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 February 2015)

We had my two dependable rain signs today - winds from the south and east, and a cloud cap on the mountains. We have a 100% chance of rain later tonight, a 90% chance on Saturday, and a 60% chance on Sunday night! The night is clear as I write this, and I hope it's all not a dream.

File.Name: f206b_f206-018.jpg
Notes: 16275089357 The flowers of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family fall apart as they get older. (Hah - don't we all!) This photo shows a flower with spent stamens and two large carpels, but compare my [Next] photo with three carpels. Those strange yellow "candy corn" things at the base of the flower are part of the nectary disk that these flowers have. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 February 2015)

File.Name: f206c_f206-022.jpg
Notes: 16273200758 This is another lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working a new flower of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family today. Their host plants are native mustards, but these flowers are where I expect to find them. It was breezy as our new storm approached today, so this is not a perfect photo. But isn't it beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 February 2015)
CommonNames: Sara Orangetip; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea
ScientificName: Anthocharis sara; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthocharis sara; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae

File.Name: f206d_f206-036a.jpg
Notes:
16273392460 Here are the first new lovely flowers of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family that I've seen this year. The flowers have pretty orange stamens. They are not common in our mountains. I found these on a shady bank where the sun never shines. The white-balance isn't right, but I didn't like my attempts to change it, and this is pretty much how they looked. I don't expect to find these flowers until March or April, so this is another early one - though I did find them on February 14 in 2011. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2015)

It's interesting that I usually find them with their cousins Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria). I almost always find them together - but not this year, at least not yet.
Locality: Lithophragma cymbalaria). I almost always find them together - but not this year

File.Name: f206e_f206-028.jpg
Notes:
16434691306 I believe these are the first new flowers of the San Diego Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family - the first flower of any galium that I found this year. I hesitate because this flower looks perfect, and this species is said to be dioecious with separate male and female plants. I don't expect to find these flowers until April, so this is another early one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 February 2015)

Plants in the genus Galium are a kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds. They are one of my favorites - even if I get confused by them!

File.Name: f206f_f206-007.jpg
Notes: 16460551635 This is a lovely new flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) in the Malvaceae plant family, showing the "goodies" inside the flower. This one is growing on a bluff above the river. You can see a couple of insects in this photo - one at the base of the goodies and another in silhouette behind the petals. I'm not sure if these shrubs ever stopped flowering this winter! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 February 2015)

File.Name: f207a_f128-020.jpg
Notes:
16281780430 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on top of a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. It's perfectly placed to grab any insect that ventures into the throat of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2015)

Today was a rain day! No new photos, but a chance to upload a few from the past week that I never got to. It rained or drizzled almost all day - not a lot of rain, but we've had a bit over an inch so far and we might get more tomorrow. This was less than I hoped for, but more than I expected! We've had a string of well-timed "little rains" this year just when we needed them to keep the seedlings and mushrooms going.

File.Name: f207b_f206-026.jpg
Notes: 16281238438 These are the first new flowers of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family that I've seen this year. The grown-together "connate leaves" that surround the stems make a delicious salad. The plant is kind of a weed in our garden - and around the world! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 February 2015)

File.Name: f207c_f205-028.jpg
Notes: 16468787635 These are the first new flowers of native Sweet Cicely (Osmorhiza brachypoda) in the Apiaceae plant family. They are growing in deep shade under the oaks in Kinevan Canyon. The roots of these plants have a wonderful licorice taste, but you have to dig up a plant to find them. The small stems taste the sweetest, and be sure to re-plant the rest. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2015)

File.Name: f207d_f204-002.jpg
Notes: 16468691965 This is a small dark-capped mushroom growing above above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. It's one-half inch across or less. When I first found it I assumed it was white-spored, but in time it has developed the pink colors that place it in the family Entolomataceae. If you look close, you can see some deposits of pink spores on the gills. It might be genus Nolanea because the dark cap is not at all nippled or "umbilicate". There's just this one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2015)

File.Name: f207e_f206-023.jpg
Notes: 16282685027 This is a small blister-like gall on the lower edge of a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). It's a quarter inch long or less. Look at that interesting opening on the edge of the leaf - like teeth! I don't know whose gall it is, but at might be a spring generation gall of a Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). See this photo for more info. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 February 2015)

File.Name: f207f_f130-021.jpg
Notes: 16280815438 I believe this is a gall of the Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) flies in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera growing on a leaf of native Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla, Labiatae). The gall grows right through the leaf and has an opening like one of those candy easter eggs that you can look inside of. The focus is a bit off in this photo, but good enough. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 January 2015)
CommonNames: Sage Leaf Gall Midge; Purple Sage
ScientificName: Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Salvia leucophylla; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Salvia leucophylla; Labiatae

File.Name: f208a_f208-023.jpg
Notes:
16292334329 This is one the first new male staminate flower catkins of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek in the canyon at home - with raindrops! See this photo for the first pistillate flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2015)

Today was a mix of sun and clouds coming from the south, with a bit of the slightest drizzle you can imagine. We have a 50% chance of more rain tonight, and we've already had a few drops, so I guess the forecast is gold. I don't expect much.

File.Name: f208b_f208-011.jpg
Notes: 16292578567 These are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon - probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). They are producing sporangia, like little surfing palm trees. Most of our liverworts hide in the dark places, but these are out in the open which probably explains why they are fruiting first. Compare this photo from January 23. The plants in the shade are just staring to produce sporangia, but I have to catch them at just the right time for some sunlight. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2015)

File.Name: f208c_f208-003.jpg
Notes: 15855871004 This is a Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) munching the new leaves of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae). I got this shot from our upstairs deck, looking down on the edge of our garden. I believe this is last year's fawn, a yearling now. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2015)
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: f208d_f208-022.jpg
Notes:
16452293096 This is a pretty red Russula mushroom in the leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's red enough to be in the "Russula emetica" group - probably Russula mairei, our west coast version. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2015)

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful mushrooms!
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula mairei
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula emetica; Russula mairei

File.Name: f208e_f208-016.jpg
Notes: 16452208896 This is "The Blusher" mushroom - the well-named blushing Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. The green grass-like stems are our native Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae). They are large and stately mushrooms. The pinkish "blushing" of the cap and stem are the hallmark of this beautiful species. David Arora says its indigestible, but Mycoweb says it's edible and good. I'm curious, but not curious enough to try it! (Any report Jacob?) (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2015)
CommonNames: The Blusher; Coast Live Oak; Roundfruit Sedge
ScientificName: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: f208f_f208-010.jpg
Notes:
16290668960 These are Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushrooms in the woods today, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I forgot my knife, but I scratched the gills with a stick to show the white latex "milk" which doesn't change to yellow. The caps are viscid and slightly zoned, and the stems are "scrobiculate" with darker and lighter spots, though that's hard to see in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2015)

I don't believe these mushrooms came up from our rain yesterday, but I didn't see them before. They must have been hiding under the oak leaves. Mushrooms are fast - but not that fast!
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: f209a_f209-006.jpg
Notes:
16485901551 This is a small Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) on a dead grass stem above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden this morning. It has swollen pedipalps, which I think means it's a male, possibly thinking of mating. It doesn't have a web, but you can see a few "safety lines" in place. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2015)

This was my only critter shot today, but I did find lots of mushrooms. I don't believe these mushrooms came up from our weekend rain, but I didn't see them before. They must have been hiding under the oak leaves. Mushrooms are fast - but not that fast!

We got another 2/10 inch of rain last night, bringing our weekend total to 1-1/4 inches. That brings us up to 15.4 inches for the year, about 3/4 of normal. That's better than last year, and we have some time left in this rain year! The wind did pick up today, but it was almost reassuring. I expect wind after a storm, and it's been disturbingly (but pleasantly!) calm this winter.

File.Name: f209b_f209-016.jpg
Notes: 16299782708 Maybe these odd mushrooms are the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin), but I really don't know. I found a number of these under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods, growing from old wood chips. I picked the cups on the right, and you can see there is no stalk to speak of and no ribs at the base. I put my 3-1/2 inch long pocket knife in the photo for scale. These cups are not tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2015)
CommonNames: Spreading Brown Cup Fungus; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: f209c_f209-020.jpg
Notes: 16461311436 These are lovely Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods today, growing in oak leaves along the road in our canyon. They are edible and tasty, though I didn't collect these for the table. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start violet-blue, but they soon fade to tan. These are starting to turn. We also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar. I've confused them before. See this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2015)

File.Name: f209d_f209-011.jpg
Notes:
16299496368 When a good mushroom goes bad...

This is all that remains of a Bolete mushroom after the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus has done its work. I found this one growing in the woods under oak and bay trees, about a foot away from my [Previous] photo. This mushroom was probably Boletus dryophilus, but who knows at this point. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2015)

The first stage of of an infection is white mold, see this photo. The second stage is a yellow crust. Then there is a reddish-brown final stage which is visible in this photo, but also see this photo. A different Hypomyces fungus is responsible for the edible "Lobster Mushrooms" that we find in Oregon, see this photo.

File.Name: f209e_f209-008.jpg
Notes: 16486978145 I believe this is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom on the right because of that thick flaring ring on the stem. It's overlooking the remains of a Bolete mushroom which has been engulfed by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus. See this photo of what I believe is the same mushroom in an earlier stage of infection, and see my [Next] photo for a closer look. I found these in the woods today under (mostly) a Bay tree, but there were oaks nearby. The agaricus looks edible, but it has a horrible phenol taste. It also develops reddish stains on the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2015)

File.Name: f209f_f209-018.jpg
Notes: 16485158151 I found these Agaricus mushrooms in the woods today, growing in a thick mulch of oak leaves. I don't know what kind they are. I remain confused by our woodland agaricus species, so I wont try to guess which one this is. They develop reddish stains on the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2015)

File.Name: f210a_f210-013.jpg
Notes:
16311418639 This is a perfect Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of a native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. The butterfly is so perfect that it must be newly emerged. I also saw Pale Swallowtail (P. eurymedon) butterflies working the same flowers, but I couldn't get a photo. The butterflies were skittish, and they quickly disappeared when I tried to get close. Maybe tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2015)

It was a good day for new butterflies - I also saw a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) and the first California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) of the year. I couldn't get photos of either.

It was also a good day to be alive. There's nothing like a bit of rain to bring the woods to life!
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Prickly-Phlox; Pale Swallowtail; California Tortoiseshell; California Sister
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; P. eurymedon; Nymphalis californica; Adelpha bredowii
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; P. eurymedon; Nymphalis californica; Adelpha bredowii

File.Name: f210b_f210-005.jpg
Notes: 15877427393 This is the first new flower of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) that I've found in the mountains this year. (They've been flowering in the foothills for some time.) Look close at the upper-left petal under the stamens - do you see the well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae)? Nah, I didn't either until I got the photo on my computer screen. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2015)

File.Name: f210c_f210-003.jpg
Notes:
16495708121 These are the first new flowers and seed pods of Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The flowers are quite tiny, only a couple of mm across. The seed pods become long cylinders. I believe this tiny mustard is native, but it grows like a weed in our mountains. It's easy to walk right past it without noticing. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2015)

"Native weed" is not an oxymoron! Also in this group would be Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) and Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia, Boraginaceae) and many more.
CommonNames: Bitter Cress; Miner's Lettuce; Common Fiddleneck
ScientificName: Cardamine oligosperma; Brassicaceae; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Amsinkia menziesii; intermedia; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Cardamine oligosperma; Brassicaceae; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Amsinkia menziesii; intermedia; Boraginaceae

File.Name: f210d_f210-010.jpg
Notes:
16309831250 This is a wonderful flower head of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. The real flowers are those small greenish tubes with goodies sticking out, the rest of it is red bracts. I got this photo in the same place as this photo that was weed-whacked by an SBC fire crew on the same day. Happily they missed a few! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2015)

The fire crew was just trying to remove the weedy Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in this place. Of course they only got the old stems and the plants are still alive and well. I'll say it again, it would be nice if the fire crews had someone who knew the natives and how to deal with the weeds!
ScientificName: Castilleja affinis; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja affinis; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: f210e_f210-025.jpg
Notes:
16310873369 This is another new red-capped Russula mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" in the leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm not sure if it's red enough to be Russula mairei in the "Russula emetica" group, but it may be. On the other hand, I got this photo in deep shade so the colors are subdued. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2015)

I was looking for new mushrooms in a likely place today, but I didn't find much. It takes them a few days to appear after a rain. That makes sense, because they need moisture to grow but dry weather to disperse their spores.
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula mairei; Russula emetica
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula mairei; Russula emetica

File.Name: f210f_f210-001.jpg
Notes:
16311087417 This is a tiny mushroom growing in sandy soil above our retaining wall - at eye level! - with my pen for scale. That brownish thing on the left is an old acorn which seem to have fungi of its own. I put my pen with a 0.7 mm ball in the photo for scale. I reckon the mushroom is about three pen-balls across, about 2 mm. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2015)

This mushroom has "Clitocyboid" decurrent gills that run down the stalk, and it sure seems to have pinkish spores. Maybe it is genus Clitopilus in the Entolomataceae? Sure, that's just a guess.

File.Name: f211a_f211-043.jpg
Notes:
16319424180 This is a closer photo of what I believe is the uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. I have no idea what the common name means. This one is growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire two years ago come May. Delphinium is a difficult group, so I might be wrong about the ID, but if I'm right, it's an endemic between Monterey and Ventura Counties. This plant is rated 1B.3 by the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants which I think means: uncommon, endemic to California, and not very endangered. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

I found this one along the road in Upper Oso canyon today. I found the same delphiniums last year in Aliso Canyon, on canyon to the west. See this photo and this one. Last year I found them at the end of April, and here they are in the middle of February. *Sigh* Everything is early this year.

It was another beautiful day today - up to 82∞ by the river where I was. No wonder the plants are confused! But there's a cloud on the horizon... I have to put together the new newsletter for the the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, which will keep me tied to the computer for a couple of days. (I'm not complaining ;=) I'll try to get outside when I can.

File.Name: f211b_f211-036.jpg
Notes:
15886486453 Here are two photos of what I believe is the uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. I have no idea what the common name means. This one is growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire two years ago come May. Delphinium is a difficult group, so I might be wrong about the ID, but if I'm right, it's an endemic between Monterey and Ventura Counties. This plant is rated 1B.3 by the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants which I think means: uncommon, endemic to California, and not very endangered. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

I found this one along the road in Upper Oso canyon today. I found the same delphiniums last year in Aliso Canyon, on canyon to the west. See this photo and this one. Last year I found them at the end of April, and here they are in the middle of February. *Sigh* Everything is early this year.

File.Name: f211c_f211-007.jpg
Notes: 16318969790 This is the first flower I've seen of Gambel's Phacelia (Phacelia viscida var. albiflora) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. This white-flowered form seems quite distinct from the beautiful blue-flowered species, but the botanists are in charge. Marlin Harms posted this recent photo of the species plants. They have a beautiful deep blue color, but I've only found them in our mountains a few times. The white form takes over here. Judging from the distribution map at the Jepson eFlora, it's not very widespread - but they are quite common here. This one is growing as a fire-follower after the White Fire two years ago come May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

File.Name: f211d_f211-032.jpg
Notes: 16480368546 These are the first new flowers of native Common Eucrypta (Eucrypta chrysanthemifolia) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's growing as a fire-follower on ground that was burned by the White Fire two years ago come May. This native plant has a distinctive strong smell - it stinks! This is another one that is flowering early. I usually find the first flowers a month from now, in March. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

File.Name: f211e_f211-014.jpg
Notes: 16318773820 This is the whole plant of a small native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing on bare soil in a place that was burned by the White Fire two years ago come May. The foliage is especially beautiful and and distinctive. I dig the shadows! It's not flowering yet, but other larger plants already have flower buds and it won't be long. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

File.Name: f211f_f211-003.jpg
Notes:
16318404848 I'm pretty sure these "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) are the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox). They have a smooth brown cap that cracks, adnate gills, and a faint ring on the stem. They are said to be edible, but not recommended because there are too darn many LBMs! These are growing with the weeds in a well-traveled place. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

I usually find these mushrooms in April or May, and once in March. I guess the mushrooms are as confused by this warm winter as the wildflowers.

File.Name: f212a_f211-034.jpg
Notes:
16489268756 This is Dodder - probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae plant family - reaching out to ensnare a plant of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. It looks like a wire insectoid! I've noticed a few plants in the foothills, but this is the first one I've seen in the mountains (including the river). Dodder is a parasitic plant without chlorophyll that gets its nutrients from another green plant like this. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

Just two photos today. I'm feeling fried and computer-weary after spending the day tied to my computer working on the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. The good news is that the hard part is done! I plan to get out with my camera for a while tomorrow morning.
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: f212b_f211-023.jpg
Notes:
16327584220 These are the first new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I've seen this year. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the 4-merous flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. The flowers remind me of genus Galium - see this photo. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland. It's one of the shrubs that keeps the woods interesting year after year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 February 2015)

Last year I found the first flowers on April 13. *Sigh* Everything is early this year.

File.Name: f213a_f213-003.jpg
Notes:
16522292312 This is a tiny pollen-dusted Fly (order Diptera) working a new staminate flower catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek in the canyon at home. I guess from its round head and flattened proboscis that it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae - or are they all predators? Willow flowers look wind-pollinated, but they attract many insects like these. Look at the fly's long proboscis and how it's exploring between the scales on top of the catkin, not on the bottom where the pollen is. I'm pretty sure there's nectar in there. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2015)

It was crazy warm today, up to 88∞ in Santa Barbara! It's not right, this should be our rainiest month.

Just four photos today, and that's pushing it. My newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society is just about done. I'll print a copy tomorrow for proofing, then email it to the the SYVNHS board for review, then off to the printers on Monday. I bought myself some time to get out today by sitting at my computer all day yesterday. Soon it will be done and I can get back to taking photos like I should be doing!

File.Name: f213b_f213-012.jpg
Notes: 16337292307 This is a tiny Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera). It's body without the antennae is less than 1/4 inch long. I guess it's a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) as the Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymphs are more ornate. This one is hanging out on a new leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). Those antennae look so lobster-like! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2015)
CommonNames: Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid; Bush Katydid; Mugwort
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: f213c_f213-004.jpg
Notes: 15902956063 Here are the first new grass-like flowers of (I think) native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family in our canyon. You can see the yellow male staminate flowers on top, and if you look close you can see the white female pistillate flowers lower on the stem. I have lots of photos of these wonderful plants if you search my photos. It's a remarkably tough plant! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2015)

File.Name: f213d_f209-002.jpg
Notes: 16522007272 There's no telling what kind of Bolete this old mushroom is. It's sun-cracked and wormy, but it has no sign of the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus that usually devours Boletus dryophilus, so maybe it's Boletus subtomentosus aka Xerocomus subtomentosus? This one is growing under oak and blackberry. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2015)
ScientificName: Hypomyces chrysospermus; Boletus dryophilus; Boletus subtomentosus; Xerocomus subtomentosus
associatedTaxa: Hypomyces chrysospermus; Boletus dryophilus; Boletus subtomentosus; Xerocomus subtomentosus

File.Name: f214a_f214-012.jpg
Notes:
16345164238 This is an interesting Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) growing in our woods. Mostly it's interesting just because it's here in our oak woods! I found this one growing under a shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), about 15 feet from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I'm not sure what kind of coral mushroom it is. In the past, I've guessed the Pink-tipped Coral Mushroom (Ramaria botrytis), but that doesn't seem right. It looks kind like a cauliflower coming from the earth. It's a few inches across. I've been finding them here every year, but I've never found them anywhere else in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2015)

I did the proofing of my newsletter today, but I still have to make some changes. I'll do that in the morning and get it off to the group for comments, and then to the printers on Tuesday. "Good enough!" It was too nice a day to spend at my computer! It was a degree or two cooler than yesterday, but not like you'd notice.
CommonNames: Coral Mushroom; California Coffeeberry; Coast Live Oak; Pink-tipped Coral Mushroom
ScientificName: Ramaria; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramaria botrytis
associatedTaxa: Ramaria; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramaria botrytis

File.Name: f214b_f214-016.jpg
Notes: 16346403609 I found these tiny sporangia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I guess they are genus Didymium, maybe Didymium iridis? At first I thought the brownish caps in the lower-right were a different species, but they both seem to be growing from similar strands of plasmodia. Maybe they are just different stages of development? (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2015)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Didymium; Didymium iridis
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Didymium; Didymium iridis

File.Name: f214c_f214-007.jpg
Notes: 16530818161 Here are new fully-open flowers of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family, like a fireworks starburst. I'd like this photo better if it also showed the beautiful foliage, but it's hard to capture both in one photo. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2015)

File.Name: f214d_f214-014.jpg
Notes: 15909890524 This is a larger "yearling" plant of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family, with the distinctive whorls of leaves. It probably won't flower this year, but its bulb will grow larger and maybe next year... But it's a long way from here to there! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2015)

File.Name: f214e_f214-015.jpg
Notes: 16346425337 This is the single leaf of a seedling of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family. (Is that really a "seed leaf"?) We have many seedlings in the canyon right now, a number of larger "yearling" plants that won't flower this year, and just a few large vigorous plants that might actually flower if they don't suffer parasite damage to their flower buds as so many do. It's a long way from here to there! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2015)

File.Name: f214f_f214-018.jpg
Notes: 16506211146 When I first found this in the shade I thought it was a bear turd! Nope, it's a remarkable cluster of old galls of the Gouty Stem Gall Wasp (Callirhtis quercussuttoni) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, riddled with the exit holes of the tiny gall wasps. You see a bit of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) bark at the bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2015)
CommonNames: Gouty Stem Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhtis quercussuttoni; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhtis quercussuttoni; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f215a_f215-003.jpg
Notes:
15921743884 Here are the first new flowers and leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae plant family along San Jose Creek a mile from home. Last year I found the first flowers on March 9, so it's a bit early - but not crazy early. This is our only native maple, though we also have the related native Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) growing along the river downstream from Buellton. This tree looks untidy, but beautiful. You can even see an old seedpod that never flew away, hanging below and to the right of photo-center. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2015)

I'm done with the newsletter! I was really hoping for butterflies on this summer-like day, but they would oblige. Maybe tomorrow.
ScientificName: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Acer negundo; californicum
associatedTaxa: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Acer negundo; californicum

File.Name: f215b_f215-007.jpg
Notes:
16544094675 Here are lots of new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's one of my favorite native shrubs. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the 4-merous flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. It's curious - the little 4-merous flowers are said to be unisexual, and I can't see any pistils in there. I assume later flowers will be pistillate as those red berries come from somewhere! I've never noticed this before. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2015)

This particular shrub has unusually small leaves, approaching Rhamnus crocea with small flat leaves, but that one usually grows lower in the valley. Other plants in the area look more typical.

File.Name: f215c_f215-016.jpg
Notes:
15921421064 This is looking closer at new flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers are beautifully intricate, like little origami boxes. See my [Previous] photo. When you think how many flowers there are in a cluster, and how many clusters there are on a shrub, and how many shrubs there are in the mountains... the numbers are beyond counting. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2015)

This individual shrub has deeper blue flowers than most. It's the stuff of cultivars.

File.Name: f215d_f215-013.jpg
Notes: 16356214898 These are new flowers clusters of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. If you look close at the leaves, you can see that they have a single vein from the base which distinguishes them from the Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in my [Previous] photo. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot of the pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2015)

File.Name: f215e_f215-019.jpg
Notes: 16542037651 These are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in our mountains. Compare my [Next] photo of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). The flowers of this one are deeper blue and in tighter clusters, and the leaves are three-veined from the base and gland-tipped on the margins. This is my favorite of our local Ceanothus species. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2015)

File.Name: f215f_f215-010.jpg
Notes: 16517666046 Here are new flower buds on Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. Last year I found the first open flowers a month from now, on March 15. I think we'll beat that record this year! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2015)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: f216a_f216-002.jpg
Notes:
16556121825 This little Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) has a problem with that large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera caught it its web. B. Kliban has a old book of cartoons called "Never Eat Anything Bigger Than Your Head" and I think this qualifies! Not to worry, I'm sure it will handle this situation just fine. The spider might be a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) - without a garbage line yet. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)

It was another beautiful day today - we've had no end of them. I have to deliver my newsletter to the printers in Solvang tomorrow morning, but it's also a chance to do one of my favorite walks by Lake Cachuma to see what's happening in those woods.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Crane Fly; Garbage Line Spider

File.Name: f216b_f216-005.jpg
Notes:
16369668389 This is another perfect husk of a spider above our retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the floor of the woods. See this photo. With that color and those legs, I guess this is the shed skin of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), probably the same Diaea livens spider that I show in this photo. I've never found this spider at home, but I guess they're here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)

If you look close, you can see that it has its old head (with eyes) on its butt! That must have something to do with the way it sheds its old skin. I can't explain those chambers within its cephalothorax, but I reckon it has something to do with spider internal anatomy. HBBBT!
Locality: with eyes) on its butt!  That must have something to do with the way it sheds its old skin.  I can't explain those chambers within its cephalothorax

File.Name: f216c_f216-026.jpg
Notes: 16369865797 These are the first new flowers of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I've been waiting for these, since new leaves mean new interesting galls! Last year, I found them on March 18, but everything seems to be a month or more early this year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)

File.Name: f216d_f216-042.jpg
Notes: 16553983851 These are the first new flowers of native Woolly-fruited Lomatium (Lomatium dasycarpum) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a distinctive native growing on the red soil of the "Sespe Formation". It has tightly clustered leaves and octopus-like flower heads. The plants are sometimes known as "Biscuit Root" because of their edible roots. I like to nibble on the seeds - they have a spicy taste that stays with you. Last year I first found these in April, but everything is early this year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)

File.Name: f216e_f216-036.jpg
Notes:
16529452036 This is the first new flower of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family that I've found in our mountains. It's WAY too early. Last year I found the first flowers in mid-April, and I think of them as harbingers of summer. These early native flowers are getting weird and disturbing! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)

Well, I have been looking closer than usual for "first flowers" this year. Maybe I've just missed the earliest ones before?
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f216f_f216-028.jpg
Notes: 16369076189 These are the first new "oak apple galls" I've seen this year of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). You can just tell that they are new, so clean and shiny. I've never been clear about the timing of these galls, and finding these today just increases my confusion! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f217a_f217-002.jpg
Notes:
16378700150 This is a large (not giant) Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera hanging by it's front legs from a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). The front legs go on a long ways from the top edge of my photo. What amazing faces these flies have! They always remind me of wise old philosophers contemplating the infinite. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 February 2015)

It's not only the wildflowers that are early this year, we also had some real "June gloom" weather last night and this morning as marine layer fog spilled over the pass. I had to deliver my newsletter to the printers in Solvang, but I stopped by Lake Cachuma to see what's happening. It was a foggy morning, but the gloom sometimes helps bring out the colors in photos. It did finally clear out in the afternoon.

File.Name: f217b_f217-009.jpg
Notes: 16564895442 I believe this is Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. This was the only plant with flowers that I found today, and it was leaning over sideways, hiding in the mostly non-native grasses. At home, we have the very similar Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum which isn't ready to flower yet, but see this photo from last year. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 February 2015)
ScientificName: Delphinium parryi; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum
associatedTaxa: Delphinium parryi; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum

File.Name: f217c_f217-007.jpg
Notes: 16565947845 This is a blue (oh-so-blue) flower of native Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. This is the first flower I've seen this year. Even on a gloomy morning, this flower stands out. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 February 2015)

File.Name: f217d_f217-001.jpg
Notes:
16379532809 This are new flowers of what I believe is native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae plant family. The stems and leaves are almost glabrous, with just a bit of "peach fuzz" on them. See this photo for a comparison with the species. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 February 2015)

This variety is not recognized in the new Jepson manual, but it seems distinct and localized.

File.Name: f217e_f216-015.jpg
Notes: 16379421129 This is most of one plant of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family, covered with flowers. I love the soft, woolly foliage. I found the first flowers way back in November, see this photo, but they are really just coming into their own. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)

File.Name: f217f_f216-025.jpg
Notes: 16379375139 Here are many flowers on a shrub of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Nothing makes a horizontal spray of flowers like these guys! They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects, and there were hummers guarding the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2015)

File.Name: f218a_f218-050.jpg
Notes:
15953683224 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly ovipositing on a new leaf of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This was one of those "shoot fast, think later" shots - not quite in focus and over-exposed, but it captures the moment. I can't recall seeing this before. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)
