It was foggy again this morning, but it cleared out by the time I got up. It was breezy at home, so I went down to the river, where it was still breezy. The morning fog does bring "fog winds".

File.Name: f218b_f218-026.jpg
Notes: 16575005132 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. The butterfly seem too big for the flowers, but here it is. This is the first one I've managed to capture a photo of this year, though I've been seeing them for a while. It looks pretty fresh, but it's already tattered. Look at that bit of pollen stuck to its proboscis. Thus pollination happens. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae

File.Name: f218c_f218-049.jpg
Notes:
16574892602 This Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly has fallen prey to a much smaller Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a new flower head of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. The flower has also fallen prey to a vine of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata, Convulvulaceae). The vine isn't a parasite, but it's overpowering the flower stem. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)

Heh, I like these photos that call for a huge list of tags!
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Crab Spider; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Thomisidae; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convulvulaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Thomisidae; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convulvulaceae

File.Name: f218d_f218-031.jpg
Notes: 16388448510 I believe this is an Orange Sulphur (Colias eurytheme) butterfly doing something odd with its proboscis while perched on a leaf blade. A moment before, I saw its upper wings while it was working a flower of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. It's not quite a dog-face, but there is a resemblance. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)
CommonNames: Orange Sulphur; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea
ScientificName: Colias eurytheme; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
associatedTaxa: Colias eurytheme; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae

File.Name: f218e_f218-044.jpg
Notes:
15953188284 Here are many native Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus, Fabaceae) and a few poppies surrounding a burned-out stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree, with more burned trees in the background. The White Fire two years ago burned hotter on this hillside than in my [Previous] photo, though I can see some new growth on the tree on the right. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)

As Robert Frost said, "In three words I can sum up everything I've learned about life: It goes on."
ScientificName: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; "In three words I can sum up everything I've learned about life: It goes on.
associatedTaxa: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; "In three words I can sum up everything I've learned about life: It goes on.

File.Name: f218f_f218-014.jpg
Notes: 16389337189 Here are lupines and poppies on a bare hillside near the Aliso Canyon trailhead, what a perfect combination. I wasn't sure I'd see fields of flowers this year, but here they are - just starting. The poppies are all Tufted Poppies (Eschscholzia caespitosa, Papaveraceae) and the lupines are all Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus, Fabaceae). This hillside is near the start of the White Fire that burned through here two years ago. The fire got hotter as it moved east, but these trees burned first. They look like they've recovered pretty well. Compare my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)
ScientificName: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f219a_f219-028.jpg
Notes:
15964096224 This is a beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched (for just a moment) on a dead stem in the woods. This was another "shoot fast, think later" shots - over-exposed by a full stop, darn it. But you can see see its beautiful blue wings with bright orange patches. Its host plant is growing nearby, native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. This the first one I've seen this year, in fact the first Blue of any kind I've managed to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2015)

It wasn't foggy this morning, but it was breezy - probably the "fog winds" from marine-layer fog that quite get over the mountains. I stayed in the canyon, and got nice photos of some interesting insects and mushrooms. There's no place like home!
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever

File.Name: f219b_f219-027.jpg
Notes: 16398792288 This is not a wild bee, though it looks vaguely bee-like. It's a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably in the genus Abia. One indication is that it has no hairs on it's legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. I haven't seen one of these for a few years. I found this one on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2015)
ScientificName: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: f219c_f219-006.jpg
Notes: 16398920490 This is a tiny Lace Bug in the family Tingidae (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this year. These adult bugs have remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but it also looks like bubble-wrap! They are hard to photograph because they have such a varied body-topography. There's a skinny bug inside the armor. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2015)
ScientificName: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f219d_f219-017.jpg
Notes:
16398515368 Beautiful, but deadly...

I believe this is the Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom that I found in the woods this morning. These poisonous mushrooms are easy to recognize. Pure white cap and gills - like a styrofoam cup in the woods; free gills; membranous ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. The saclike volva of this one is collapsed and a bit and hard to see, but it's there. These mushrooms are beautiful, but deadly - don't eat them! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2015)

People get into fatal trouble by pulling this mushroom from the ground - and leaving the volva behind - rather than digging it up. Always dig your mushrooms out!

I was surprised to find any mushrooms in the woods today, but we did have rain the weekend before last, and it takes the mushrooms a while to appear. The ground is still damp under the oak leaves, but the air feels very dry.

File.Name: f219e_f219-016.jpg
Notes: 16398365648 These are more confusing Amanita mushrooms in the woods today, see my [Previous] photo. They look almost pure white like the Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata), but I believe they are "The Blusher" mushroom, Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens. It does have some reddish stains, and the tapered volva - or lack of one - definitely looks like the blusher. Compare this photo. I don't think I would consider eating this one! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2015)
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita ocreata; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita ocreata; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens

File.Name: f219e_f219-022.jpg
Notes: 16399599829 This is a small Amanita that I found under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today - but what kind? My first thought was the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) with those warts on the cap, but it also looks like "The Blusher" mushroom, Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens. I tried to dig it up to check the volva, but it fell apart from insect activity. What I saw of the volva looked more like the blusher, tapered and not abrupt. I'm not sure what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2015)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Panther Amanita; The Blusher
ScientificName: Amanita; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita pantherina; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita pantherina; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens

File.Name: f220a_f220-001.jpg
Notes:
16596995825 I found this ornate Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae) this morning. It's rather small, less than 1/2 inch long. Looking at BugGuide, I think it might be species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The speckles on its legs and the hyaline wings are remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2015)

I didn't have much hope for new photos this morning. The marine layer fog spilled over the mountains, the fog winds kicked up, and I agreed to help Julie do an errand in Santa Barbara. Still, I did pretty good with this insect, a wildflower, a slime mold, and a bunch of mushrooms, all close to home. Sometimes it helps when you have to stay in one place and look close!
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis; Harpactorinae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis; Harpactorinae

File.Name: f220b_f220-021.jpg
Notes:
16596185702 These are pretty little flowers of native Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. It's not the first flower I've found this year, but these are the first flowers with a bit of sunlight on them! (I'll call it a "first flower" anyway.) This plant has little opposite leaves on its long flower stalks, but we also have the very similar Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) with little alternate leaves on the stem. I can never remember which is which. I have to relearn these two flowers every year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2015)

Last year I found the first flowers on March 29. One problem with the early flowers this year - a month or more early! - is that they appear before the sun gets high enough in the sky to favor them. I was lucky to find this one in a beam of sunlight.

File.Name: f220c_f220-012.jpg
Notes:
15976926763 Here are two colonies of tiny Stemonitis slime molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods. They look like a tangle of rasta dreadlocks, or little hot dogs on a stick, or the inside of your vacuum cleaner bag - your choice! Here they are shedding their spores. These are the first ones of this genus that I've noticed this year, though I'm sure I've overlooked many. They are tiny! See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2015)

That dead wood has an interesting texture, almost like granite rock.

File.Name: f220d_f220-011.jpg
Notes: 16409361638 I believe these are part of the Psathyrella longipes group of mushrooms in the dark-spored Coprinaceae family. You can see a few white veil remnants on the margin of the caps. They grow on debris in the woods, under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The dark spore-covered gills don't turn to ink like the genus Coprinus, and they don't grow on dung like the genus Panaeolus. The difference in color might be a matter of white balance - or lack there-of. On the other hand, the oak leaves look about the same. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2015)
ScientificName: Psathyrella longipes; Coprinaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Coprinus
associatedTaxa: Psathyrella longipes; Coprinaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Coprinus

File.Name: f220e_f220-004.jpg
Notes: 16409330110 I believe these are some kind of brown-spored Inocybe mushrooms in the woods today, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The two mushrooms look different, but I'm sure they are the same, in different stages of being stained by the falling spores. These mushrooms have a faint fruity smell that is quite pleasant. They push up remarkably large mushrumps in the oak leaves for their size. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2015)

File.Name: f220f_f220-008.jpg
Notes: 16409004028 I found another mushroom of "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) in the woods near home this morning. They sure are variable! Compare this photo and this photo. The one thing that they all have in common is the "blushing" of the cap and stem which develop reddish colors in age or with handling. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2015)

File.Name: f221f_f221-001.jpg
Notes:
16421150659 These are new "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, growing on a twig of a flowering Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). This branch in actually gall city! In addition to the big red galls, there's a gall of an Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) and at the very tip of the branch is an old gall of the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis). (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2015)

Our internet connection went dead last night just as I was entering my comment . It's frustrating when that happens because I'm not sure if the problem is my computer, the router, the DSL service, or our ISP. In this case, I think it was the carrier (Verizon) because the DSL light was flashing on our router suggesting there was no signal. It came back around 9 this morning.

Oh well, today will probably be a crummy day for new photos anyway - we're getting a bit of much-needed rain! We've only had a few 100th of an inch so far, but it's something. I'll catch up on yesterday's photos later today.
Locality:
Verizon) because the DSL light was flashing on our router suggesting there was no signal. It came back around 9 this morning.

Oh well
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Disholcaspis washingtonensis
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Disholcaspis washingtonensis

File.Name: f222a_f222-017.jpg
Notes:
16431524188 This is looking down on a remarkable furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) in an odd pose in the drizzle today. It's on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). The fly is actually vertical, and it's hanging off almost horizontally, without moving. Maybe it's been had by a crab spider or a fungus? Or maybe it's just doing fly yoga to get through a chilly day! I'll check tomorrow to see if it's still there, and also post another photo that shows it's position better, but I dig this view of it's proboscis. I have no doubt that this is one of the bee flies I've been photographing lately, see this photo, maybe genus Bombylius. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2015)

Interesting - our router died today, no lights, no activity. I tried replacing the "wall wart" power pack with an old one from our junk drawer, and it came back to life! Our internet connection now seems faster than ever, and the wifi goes farther. Maybe the fault, dear Brutus, is not in our service, but in our router!

We've had light soaking rain and drizzle all day today. We're approaching 1/4 inch and it's still coming down - not much, but more than I expected. This will keep the mushrooms and seedlings going for another week.
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Bombylius

File.Name: f222b_f222-012.jpg
Notes: 16592582406 This is a small Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly on a twig in the woods today. It's actually leaning sideways trying to to catch some rays like a little solar collector, but I got this photo looking down, so it looks almost normal. It was chilly and drizzly today. I've been seeing these butterflies on the wing, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2015)

File.Name: f222c_f222-003.jpg
Notes: 16617261221 This is a small Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden, with a few raindrops. It was chilly today, in the 40s - too chilly for the fly to take off, though it did move around a bit. It was drizzling a bit when I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2015)

File.Name: f222d_f221-030.jpg
Notes:
16410906657 I believe this is a pretty little Sawfly in the "Symphyta" group of the order Hymenoptera, possibly an Argid Sawfly in the family Argidae. (It looks right, but the antennae seem wrong, not 3-segmented?) It doesn't have a "wasp waist". I found it nibbling a leaf bud of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2015)

If you look close, you can see many tiny insect eggs scattered on the leaves!
ScientificName: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Argidae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Argidae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: f222e_f221-016.jpg
Notes:
15998267423 These are the first fantastic flowers I've found this year of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I used to wonder what insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for, but maybe I discovered it in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2015)

*Sigh* They are flowering way too early. Last year I didn't find the first flowers until April 17 - two months from now! And they aren't freaks - most plants in this place had buds. I guess our warm winter weather is the reason, with most storms coming from the south. It's cool to find the flowers now, but I wonder what will still be flowering this summer.

File.Name: f222f__f221-002.jpg
Notes: 15995732334 Here are more new "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - at least that one on the left is new. They are growing on twigs of a flowering Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae), the same shrub as my [Previous] photo. They look like embryos! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2015)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f223a_f223-003.jpg
Notes:
16442726758 As Galileo is alleged to have said after his inquisition, "Eppur si muove" - still it moves!

This is the same remarkable furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) as my photo yesterday, with raindrops. It's moved from one side of the leaf to the other! So it's alive, just in a strange "fly yoga" pose. Maybe it's trying to catch some rays like the butterfly in my [previous] photo? It might have worked. I came back to this spot an hour later, and the fly was gone. I have no doubt that this is one of the bee flies I've been photographing lately, see this photo, maybe genus Bombylius. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2015)

We ended up with just over 1/4 inch of rain yesterday. That's not much, but more than I expected. This will keep the mushrooms and seedlings going for another week. The clouds were collecting over our mountains today, and we did have a few brief showers. We stand at 15.65 inches of rain for the year, about 66% of normal. That's better than last year.

File.Name: f223b_f223-014.jpg
Notes: 16422538697 This is another small Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today. It's leaning sideways trying to to catch some warming rays from the sun like a little solar collector. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2015)

File.Name: f223c_f223-027.jpg
Notes: 16443965129 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family - with raindrops acting like a magnifying glass. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2015)

File.Name: f223d_f223-016.jpg
Notes:
16443759709 Here are two photos of the same Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom in the woods this morning. These poisonous mushrooms are easy to recognize. Pure white cap and gills - like a styrofoam cup in the woods; free gills; membranous ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. These mushrooms are beautiful, but deadly - don't eat them! That goes for dogs too - a neighbor lost a beloved dog to these mushrooms. It's not dangerous to touch or admire them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2015)

This one was rather small, not much more than an inch across, though it went deep in the ground. People get into fatal trouble by pulling this mushroom up - and leaving the volva behind - rather than digging it out. If you plan on collecting any wild mushrooms for the table, learn this one first!

File.Name: f223e_f223-011.jpg
Notes: 16442385660 I found this odd "elfin cup" fungus in the woods today, which I guess is the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum). You can see the ribs on the outside of the cap, which extend almost to the cap margin. At first I suspected genus Peziza (see this photo), but the stem and ribs are distinctive. These are growing in a thick mulch of oak leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2015)

File.Name: f223f_f223-023.jpg
Notes: 16441975768 These mushrooms look at first like genus Clitocybe with those decurrent gills, but I think they might be some form of Leucopaxillus albissimus. The caps are dry and unpolished, and they seem long-lasting, but the stems are much thinner than the typical species. These are growing under (I think) native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) as well as Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I've been finding quite a few of these in the woods lately. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2015)
ScientificName: Clitocybe; Leucopaxillus albissimus; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe; Leucopaxillus albissimus; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f224a_f224-004.jpg
Notes:
16017761714 This small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is about to make a move on this little wasp, no doubt a Sawfly in the "Symphyta" group of the order Hymenoptera. I found this drama on an old leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. Like a Disney true adventure, the wasp got away unharmed. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2015)

Today was a good day for spider pics! I was grounded today, because my truck has a coolant leak. I'll carefully drive it to the repair shop tomorrow, maybe stopping on the way to add water. Damn cars. I also have to drive south on Thursday to pick up our van which died a week ago when Julie tried to drive it to the desert. Damn cars! At least the photo archives are in good shape.

File.Name: f224b_f224-023.jpg
Notes: 16017554874 This is an odd little spider resting on the leaf of a succulent near our front porch. It's less than 1/4 inch long. I guess it's a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) with that bottle gourd-shaped abdomen, though it doesn't have any web to speak of. Maybe it's a male? I got a photo in the morning, then came back a few hours later and found it in the same place, and got this photo. I like the way it holds its legs up like tent poles.
Locality: genus Cyclosa) with that bottle gourd-shaped abdomen

File.Name: f224c_f224-027.jpg
Notes:
16452142188 This is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our roof, with her egg case. Sure, the white balance is a little funky in this place where the sun never shines. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but I've seen them before, e.g. here. They seem to hold their web strands to the roof with a glob of goo. I can still make out old nests from years ago by the globs they left behind. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2015)

I'm puzzled by the timing of these large spiders. That previous photo was from last December, and here it is in February. I guess it takes a while to hatch a brood of spiderlings, and maybe I just noticed this spider today who's been here all along.

File.Name: f224d_f224-001.jpg
Notes: 16452171070 This is a small insect larva munching the tiny aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a gladiolus leaf in the garden. It looks like the larva has cleared a patch of aphids! I'm not sure whose larva it is. It looks superficially like a caterpillar, but a beetle is more likely. I don't think it's a Syrphid Fly, though I often find those munching aphids. Of course, I might be wrong- always! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2015)

File.Name: f224e_f224-021.jpg
Notes: 16451954248 This is a beautiful coiled "fiddlehead" of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta, Polypodiaceae) in the woods near home. It looks like it's still being unwrapped. New as it is, you can make out small sporangia on the surface of the new fronds. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2015)

File.Name: f224f_f224-016.jpg
Notes: 16451924890 This is a neat little cluster of dark-spored mushrooms in the Coprinaceae family coming up with weedy grasses by our mailbox. If you look close, you can see that the top mushroom has left a natural black spore print on the mushrooms under it, especially the one to the right. I don't recognize these mushrooms, and I don't want to guess what they are. The yellowish stems and fissured caps seem distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2015)

File.Name: f225a_f225-010.jpg
Notes:
16623735756 This is a tiny caterpillar on new buds of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) in front of our house today. I have no doubt that it's the caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera), though I've ever noticed such a small one - just a bit over 1/8 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2015)

It was a busy day today - we nursed my truck down to the repair shop, and then had a meeting with "Important People" that I couldn't get out of. I did manage to get a few nice photos close to home.

Tomorrow will be busier. We have to pick up my truck and then drive down to Santa Clarita to retrieve our van from the repair shop there. *Sigh* It's been a bad week for cars. But there are lots of photos in the archives that I've been waiting to post.
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: f225b_f225-002.jpg
Notes: 16442300507 This is a large (not giant) Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera stretched out between two stems of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. Its thin legs go on for a long ways in both directions. What amazing faces these flies have! They always remind me of wise old philosophers contemplating the infinite. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2015)
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f225c_f225-014.jpg
Notes:
16027171714 Here are two photos of what I'm pretty sure is the same kind of odd little spider I showed yesterday in this photo. I believe it's a male "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) with that bottle gourd-shaped abdomen. The photo on the left shows its enlarged pedipalps in front, and the photo on the right shows its oddly shaped abdomen in back. This male doesn't have the typical orb web that the females make. It's small, less than 1/4 inch long. This one is perched on a Lavendar shrub in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2015)

The photo on the right is a pretty good "butt shot" too. HBBBT!

File.Name: f225d_f225-006.jpg
Notes: 16027089724 I found these interesting bolete mushrooms in the woods today. They have very yellow pores which suggests Boletus flaviporus aka Aureoboletus flaviporus - except that the cap doesn't look like it was ever viscid. The pores don't stain blue, and the stem is mostly yellow with just a bit of red. See this photo for something similar. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2015)

File.Name: f225e_f224-012.jpg
Notes: 16461898780 I found this family of Agaricus mushrooms in the woods yesterday, growing along our road in the canyon. I don't know what kind they are. I remain confused by our woodland agaricus species, so I won't try to guess which one this is. They appear to develop yellowish stains on the cap and have a ring very low on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2015)

File.Name: f225f_f224-008.jpg
Notes: 16029204833 I call these "Wood Chipper Mushrooms" because they grow on wood chips, from tree work we had done last spring. I suspect they come with the wood chipper. I guess they are some kind of Agrocybe other than A. praecox. These mushrooms have brown spores and a partial veil that leaves an evanescent ring, look at the lower-left mushroom in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2015)

File.Name: f226a_f221-015.jpg
Notes:
16471367180 These are the first new flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family in our mountains. The yellow flowers develop reddish colors as they age, but these new flowers haven't had time yet. Last year I found the first flowers on March 16, so these are early, but not too early. They are important butterfly plants in our mountains, especially Blues and Hairstreaks. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2015)

Today's photos are all "first flowers" that I've found in the last week or so. I had no time for new photos today as I spent the day shuttling cars from there to here, not my favorite pastime. Oh well, there's always tomorrow, and it's nice to be able to post these photos that didn't make the "first cut" because of butterflies and other distractions.

We pretty much have an even chance of rain every day from Saturday to Tuesday. I hope that's like flipping a coin and betting on heads - probably a head will turn up on one of those flips! Apart from any rain, it should be much cooler than it's been.
ScientificName: Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; probably
associatedTaxa: Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; probably

File.Name: f226b_f221-013.jpg
Notes: 16471093208 Here are the first new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is another native that is surprising early this year, as I didn't find the first flowers last year until April 17. I really think of them as early summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2015)

File.Name: f226c_f220-016.jpg
Notes: 16657560612 This is (almost) the first flower I've seen this year of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Our plants are not looking so good in this fourth drought year, and some extensive patches of them have died out. This is one wildflower that is late this year. Last year I found the first sad specimen on January 28, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2015)

File.Name: f226d_f218-020.jpg
Notes: 16657105991 Here are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the Fabaceae plant family. The small plants are kind of weedy, but they are natives and quite pretty if you get close enough to really see them. They have the same-shaped palmate leaves as all other lupines. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)

File.Name: f226e_f218-021.jpg
Notes:
16658377635 These are odd "flowers" of native California Plantain (Plantago erecta) in the Plantaginaceae) plant family growing as a fire-follower on bare soil burned by the White Fire almost two years ago. They are related to common weeds, but these are native. They are kind of weedy themselves, mostly growing in poor soil. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)

Hah, a real weed made it's way into my photo. In the lower-right you can see the different flower of non-native Filaree (Erodium, Geraniaceae) - sneaky little devils!

File.Name: f226f_f218-011.jpg
Notes:
16658232955 These are the first new catkins of native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, at least the first I've been able to get close too. It's interesting that this tree is getting new leaves and flowers, while a tree right next to it has never lost its old leaves. It's been a surprisingly warm winter, and the plants are confused. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 February 2015)

I'm still waiting for Quercus chrysolepis and the oddball Quercus pacifica/Q. shrevei.
ScientificName: Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae; Quercus chrysolepis; Quercus pacifica; Q. shrevei
associatedTaxa: Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae; Quercus chrysolepis; Quercus pacifica; Q. shrevei

File.Name: f227a_f227-057.jpg
Notes:
16047501543 This is a stunning Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly on non-native weedy grasses by the river today. It must be newly-emerged to have those brilliant colors, especially the blues. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)

Today was a crummy day for new photos, but it turned out to be a good day for butterflies! It was gloomy and windy at home this morning, so I went down to the river hoping to find some sun and get away from the fog winds. There was sun, but the real winds soon kicked in and drove me away. Really, when it's this windy, you may as well put your camera away, at least for my kind of photos.

The winds are coming from the North-West, which is not a good sign for our promised storm. South winds are better. But the NWS forecasts still stand, so hopefully we'll get some rain.

File.Name: f227b_f227-029.jpg
Notes: 16047395693 This is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working a new weedy flower of non-native Redstem Filaree (Erodium cicutarium, Geraniaceae) by the river today. You can see the odd plants of California Plantain (Plantago erecta, Plantaginaceae) on the right, see this photo. This is the first Buckeye I've managed to photograph this year, though I've been seeing them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)
CommonNames: Common Buckeye; Redstem Filaree; California Plantain
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Erodium cicutarium; Geraniaceae; Plantago erecta; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Erodium cicutarium; Geraniaceae; Plantago erecta; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: f227c_f227-032.jpg
Notes: 16666216062 This is a little Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis or P. albescens) butterfly working a weedy flower of non-native Filaree (Erodium, Geraniaceae), probably the Redstem Filaree (Erodium cicutarium). You can see a forest of seedpods in the background. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)
ScientificName: Pyrgus communis; P. albescens; Erodium; Geraniaceae; Erodium cicutarium
associatedTaxa: Pyrgus communis; P. albescens; Erodium; Geraniaceae; Erodium cicutarium

File.Name: f227d_f227-034.jpg
Notes: 16665773731 This is a lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly perched for just a moment on a grass blade. It seems to be tasting the leaf, probably looking for a drop of dew. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)

File.Name: f227e_f227-041.jpg
Notes: 16044769074 This is the first Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly that I've managed to photograph this year, perched on (I think) a plant of native Bishop's Lotus (Acmispon strigosus aka Lotus strigosus) in the Fabaceae plant family. "Strigose" is one of those botanical words that means "with stiff, straight, sharp, appressed hairs" - which you can see. The butterfly is unmistakable with those speckles on the eyespots of its hind wings. It's not the best photo, but this is a record shot. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Acmispon strigosus; Lotus strigosus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Acmispon strigosus; Lotus strigosus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f227f_f222-016.jpg
Notes:
16665924032 Here is one more photo of a remarkable furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) in an odd pose in the drizzle last weekend. It's on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). The leaf is vertical, and the fly is hanging off almost horizontally, without moving. At first, I thought it been had by a crab spider, but it did move overnight and then was gone. I guess it's just exposing its furry belly to what there is of the sun! I have no doubt that this is one of the bee flies I've been photographing lately, see this photo, maybe genus Bombylius. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2015)

I have more good photos of this critter here and here (with raindrops!) I've been saving this one for Fly Day Friday. HFDF!
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Bombylius

File.Name: f228a_f228-025.jpg
Notes:
16055172334 Here are the first new flowers I've found this year of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. Plants in the var. inezianus are more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. It's a fire-follower plant, but this one is growing in a place that was cleared and not burned. Sometimes that's enough. This is one of my favorite wildflowers. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2015)

We had interesting weather today! It started gloomy, then cleared up - then clouds moved in and brought 1/2 inch (so far) of showery rain and hail! It was an odd sort of rain. It would rain like hell for 10 minutes then stop, then rain again. I watched our rain gauge rise 1/10 inch in about 10 minutes on the porch during one downpour. All the time I could see bits of blue sky off in the distance. The cities of Santa Barbara and Solvang on both sides of us have had nothing. It was only a 40% chance of rain today, but there's a 60% chance tonight and a 50/40% chance on Sunday through Tuesday. This rain will do nothing but good for the life in our mountains. Bring it on!
Locality: so far) of showery rain and hail!  It was an odd sort of rain.  It would rain like hell for 10 minutes then stop
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: f228b_f228-036.jpg
Notes: 16489943800 This is looking up at the first open flower I've found this year of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They are pretty common on San Marcos pass - both in woodland and chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2015)

File.Name: f228c_f228-045.jpg
Notes: 16057217923 These are new flowers of the Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. Flowers and bracts are quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. Note that the flower tubes are quite red on top, whereas the flowers of Castilleja affinis are greenish, compare this photo. These are the first flowers I've noticed this year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2015)
ScientificName: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Castilleja affinis
associatedTaxa: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Castilleja affinis

File.Name: f228d_f227-038.jpg
Notes:
16490929899 This is a pretty new spiny flower head of native Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire almost two years ago. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)

The seeds of this plant are nutritious and have a reputation for being an energy source. "Chia Seeds" are available commercially, but I'm sure they are not from this plant. See this page at Wikipedia.

File.Name: f228e_f227-053.jpg
Notes: 16489424158 Here are new flowers of native Stinging Lupine aka Nettle Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing as a fire-follower on burned soil from the White Fire a couple of years ago. The photo on the left shows the fat-fingered leaves, and the one on the right show the bristly flower spike. I don't think they actually sting, but they look like they could. They are not common in our area - except after wildfires. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)

File.Name: f228f_f227-050.jpg
Notes: 16489366688 This is a new flower stalk on native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing as a fire-follower in a place that was burned by the White Fire two years ago come May. The foliage is especially beautiful and distinctive with those skinny truncate leaflets, and the flowers are not at all whorled. Quite a few of them had flowers, the first I've seen this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 February 2015)

File.Name: f301a_f301-053.jpg
Notes:
16688412901 This California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus, or is it A. columbianus?) is doing "coprophagia" - look it up if you don't know the word. It's also The Slug Ouroboros! Well, the ancient myth has the "Worm Ouroboros" biting its own tail and enclosing the world. It's a yin-yang thing. This slug is almost biting its tail with those slug-like pseudo-teeth. It's interesting that this is the first banana slug I've seen since our big December rains, see this photo. I was surprised to find this one so late in the season, but here it is - doing the unmentionable. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)

It was a dynamic weather day - sun, clouds, a bit of rain, and a bit of wind. I also got ticked today, twice. We've ended up with just over 7/10 inch of rain so far, though there's still a 50% chance of more rain tonight, and a 30% chance tomorrow. We did get 1/4 inch of rain last night, and a bit more today, so the NWS forecasts have been right on the money. The stormy weather looks over, but I don't want to rule out a bit more.

Today's photos are mostly mushrooms, yesterday's were all wildflowers, and the day before's were mostly butterflies. I wonder what tomorrow will bring?
CommonNames: California Banana Slug; The Slug Ouroboros

File.Name: f301b_f301-041.jpg
Notes: 16689642945 This is rather large Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom in the woods today, with my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife for scale. I didn't dig it out because I've already done that - see this photo. These mushrooms are beautiful, but deadly poison. Don't eat them! That goes for dogs too - a neighbor lost a beloved dog to one of these mushrooms. However, it's not dangerous to touch or admire them. They are probably the most elegant mushroom in our woods, very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)

File.Name: f301c_f301-030.jpg
Notes: 16069446673 I found this puzzling Amanita mushroom in the woods today. Here are two photos. The photo on the left shows it in the ground, with a patch of volval tissue on the slightly viscid cap. The photo on the right shows the whole mushroom out of the ground. Note that it has no sign of a ring, and the volva has a constricted flaring volva. It appears to be orange-staining from my handling. (The fragile mushroom fell apart when I dug it out. I put the pieces back together for this photo.) This mushroom resembles a Constricted Grisette (Amanita constricta), but the colors seem all wrong. If you have any ideas about this mushroom, please let me know. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)

File.Name: f301d_f301-048.jpg
Notes: 16503030829 I believe these are Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens) mushrooms that I found in the woods today. Thanks for the ID, Matt - looks right. (See Matt's comment on this photo.) You can see the amber-colored stains on the cap. They have a sweet almond-like smell, and David Arora descibes them as "delectably delicious" - that is, if they are really that species! I'm wary of all our woodland agarics, but I'm ready to try this one. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)

File.Name: f301e_f301-024.jpg
Notes: 16689070145 I guess these are the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushrooms growing in deep oak mulch in the woods today. They have a thick flaring ring on the stem, though it's slightly collapsed in this specimen. It looks edible, but it has an unpleasant phenol-like smell from the base of the stem. It's definitely not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)

File.Name: f301f_f301-029.jpg
Notes: 16687969332 Here is another photo of an interesting Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) growing in our woods. See this photo from two weeks ago. Mostly it's interesting just because it's here in our oak woods! I found this one growing under a shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), about 15 feet from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I'm not sure what kind of coral mushroom it is. In the past, I've guessed the Pink-tipped Coral Mushroom (Ramaria botrytis), but that doesn't seem right. It looks kind like a cauliflower coming from the earth. It's a few inches across. I've been finding them here every year, but I've never found them anywhere else in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)
CommonNames: Coral Mushroom; California Coffeeberry; Coast Live Oak; Pink-tipped Coral Mushroom
ScientificName: Ramaria; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramaria botrytis
associatedTaxa: Ramaria; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramaria botrytis

File.Name: f302a_f302-015.jpg
Notes:
16674031156 I believe this is the Golden Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe flavescens aka Hygrophorus flavescens) mushroom growing under a mix of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Quercus pacifica/Q. shrevei, and Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia). It was starting to rain today as I got this photo, but what a striking mushroom! The last one of these I found was in the Oregon woods, under conifers. I don't think I've ever seen them before under oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2015)

I was caught by rain today at the wrong end of my trail, but I had to get this photo before I hurried back to my truck. We've had something over 8/10 inch so far from our storms, bring our total for the year up to 16.5 inches, about 65% of normal. It's not great, but this is still the best year I've seen in a few years for mushrooms and wildflowers.
CommonNames: Golden Waxy Cap; Coast Live Oak; Toyon
ScientificName: Hygrocybe flavescens; Hygrophorus flavescens; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Q. shrevei; Heteromeles arbutifolia
associatedTaxa: Hygrocybe flavescens; Hygrophorus flavescens; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Q. shrevei; Heteromeles arbutifolia

File.Name: f302b_f302-004.jpg
Notes: 16512264318 These beautiful mushrooms in the woods today must be Zeller's Bolete (Boletus zelleri). They have a brilliant color combo - dark "fuscous" cap with a frosted look, yellow pores (no gills), and a red stem. I found them growing under a mix of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Quercus pacifica/Q. shrevei, and Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). They seem unusually small, only about an inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2015)
CommonNames: Zeller's Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Boletus zelleri; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Q. shrevei; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Boletus zelleri; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus pacifica; Q. shrevei; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: f302c_f302-002.jpg
Notes: 16698554062 I believe these odd mushrooms are the Thimble Morel (Verpa conica). I tried to pick one to include in the photo, but they are brittle and it fell apart. I put the cap in the photo between the two stems, and you can see that the sides are free from the stalk. They are growing in oak mulch with weedy plants of non-native Chickweed (Stellaria media, Caryophyllaceae) along the base of our retaining wall at home. I haven't seen these for quite a few years. They are said to be edible, but fragile and hard to collect. I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2015)

File.Name: f302d_f301-022.jpg
Notes: 16492251207 This is a Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. I call these "brain mushrooms" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. I've only found a few of these this year, but this is their time, and I expect to find more. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)
CommonNames: Fluted Black Elfin Saddle; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: f302e_f301-023.jpg
Notes: 16512074490 Maybe these odd mushrooms are the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin), but I really don't know. I found a number of these under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods, growing from old wood chips. I've found them here before, see this photo. There is no stalk to speak of and no ribs on the outside, unlike the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum) in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)
CommonNames: Spreading Brown Cup Fungus; Coast Live Oak; Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup
ScientificName: Peziza repanda; Quercus agrifolia; Helvella acetabulum
associatedTaxa: Peziza repanda; Quercus agrifolia; Helvella acetabulum

File.Name: f302f_f301-052.jpg
Notes:
16511759608 Here is another photo of the large California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus, or is it A. columbianus or A. dolichophallus?) eyeing its next meal. See my [Previous] photo! It's interesting that this is the first banana slug I've seen since our big December rains, see this photo. I was surprised to find this one so late in the season, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2015)

I wonder if this odd "coprophagaceous" behavior is an adaptation? I believe domestic rabbits reingest certain of their own droppings to digest their food further and extract nutrients. Maybe the slugs are doing something similar?
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus

File.Name: f303a_f303-008.jpg
Notes:
16709195822 This is a perfect little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2015)

I'm grounded again. My little truck has sprung another coolant leak, and I don't want to drive it until we nurse it down to the repair shop tomorrow. Darn, they just replaced all the radiator hoses last week, so maybe they forgot to tighten something.

I'm not complaining, it was a beautiful day to spend in the canyon. The clouds were moving in and out of the sun all day, and the temperature fell a good 10 degrees every time it went away. There's nothing in my photos today that I haven't shown before, but these are all new takes. There's a spider, an insect, a mushroom, some galls, a wildflower, and a liverwort!

File.Name: f303b_f303-011.jpg
Notes:
16522655890 The Caterpillar Ouroboros!

Well, the ancient myth has the "Worm Ouroboros" biting its own tail and enclosing the world. It's a yin-yang thing. This caterpillar is doing the same thing, except for the "enclosing the world" part. It's the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a dead stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in a shrub of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2015)
CommonNames: The Caterpillar Ouroboros; Variable Checkerspot; Wild Sweetpea; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f303c_f303-005.jpg
Notes:
16090031703 Sometimes you just have to chuckle!

I believe this suggestive mushroom is the same Thimble Morel (Verpa conica) that I showed yesterday, though the stem looks quite different. The sides of the cap are free from the stalk. This one is growing in oak mulch along the base of our retaining wall at home. I haven't seen these for quite a few years. Yes, it's about 6 inches long. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2015)

File.Name: f303d_f303-020.jpg
Notes: 16683929356 These are little galls on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I believe they are galls of the Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) flies in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. Usually the tiny tubes form under the leaves, but several of these are on top. You can look into the second gall from the left, like a sugar easter egg. Compare this photo of the same leaf taken last December - some have grown, some have failed. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2015)
CommonNames: Black Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: f303e_f303-018.jpg
Notes: 16089739203 This is a colony of tiny Popcorn Flower (Cryptantha) plants in the Boraginaceae plant family, with my pen for scale. This pen has a 0.5 mm ball, which makes the petals much more than a mm long. You need a microscope to tell these tiny flowers apart, and there are many different kinds. I really don't care too much what it is, but maybe it's the Tejon Cryptantha (Cryptantha microstachys)? It almost looks like a little cactus. These are growing on bare ground in chaparral that was cleared about 10 years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2015)

File.Name: f303f_f303-014.jpg
Notes: 16708306241 These are new sporangia of tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon - probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). This plant is growing in a mostly shady place, unlike the ones I've shown before. They are growing on a sandstone outcrop on our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2015)

File.Name: f304a_f304-005.jpg
Notes:
16534075749 This is a shot from the side of the same tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) in my [Previous] photo. I believe it's a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) with that odd bottle gourd-shaped abdomen, and I believe it's a male because of those enlarged pedipalps and because it doesn't have much of a web. This might be the male of the female spider two photos back. I've been finding a few of these lately, see this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2015)

If you look close, you can see that its making or holding a web in the finger-like spinnerets on its rear end. These spiders not only have 8 legs and 2 pedipalps, they also these 4 dextrous spinnerets! I figure this counts as a butt shot, HBBBT!

I've been twice-grounded today. My truck is in the shop because of a coolant leak (again!) and I seem to have hurt the big toe on my right foot. You don't realize how important a big toe is until you hurt one! I've been hobbling today. Hopefully both truck and toe will be good tomorrow!

File.Name: f304b_f304-002.jpg
Notes:
16532608210 Here are two photos showing the front (left) and rear (right) ends of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) - probably a male "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) with that odd bump on its abdomen. I believe it's a male because of those enlarged pedipalps visible in the front view, and because it doesn't have much of a web. This might be the male of the female spider in my [Previous] photo. I've been finding a few of these lately, see this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2015)

The photo on the right is a pretty good "butt shot" too. HBBBT!

File.Name: f304c_f304-019.jpg
Notes:
16512574177 This is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) perched on the inner circle of her web. I believe this is a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) with that bottle gourd-shaped abdomen. The spider's web is just a bit larger than a CD. This lady spider has had good luck hunting, I can see three tiny flies in her web. The thick silk bands running from top to bottom are the stabilimentum of the spider. These spiders typically decorate their stabilimentum with the body parts of their prey, but maybe it's too early in the season to see this. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2015)

It's hard not to overexpose these spider webs - even when you know better!

File.Name: f304d_f304-006.jpg
Notes: 16097287874 These are the striking florets of native Purple Needle Grass (Stipa pulchra aka Nasella pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family growing on the San Marcos Foothills Preserve. It's a restoration project that my wife is involved in at the bottom of the mountains on the Santa Barbara side. We stopped to take a short hike after dropping off my ailing truck at the garage this morning. We didn't get very far , but far enough to find these beautiful native grasses holding their own against all the non-native grasses. You can see fuzzy white pistils on the flowers to the right. (San Marcos Foothills Preserve, 4 March 2015)

File.Name: f304e_f304-011.jpg
Notes: 16718124821 I found these striking black and white mushrooms in the woods today. I believe from past forays that they are genus Nolanea in the pink-spored Entolomataceae family, but apparently that genus has now been absorbed by the large Entoloma genus. I think I can see a hint of pink in the white gills, and they have twisted-striate stalks. They are small, not much more than an inch across. Maybe they are Entoloma vernum aka Nolanea verna? (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2015)
ScientificName: Nolanea; Entolomataceae; Entoloma; Entoloma vernum; Nolanea verna
associatedTaxa: Nolanea; Entolomataceae; Entoloma; Entoloma vernum; Nolanea verna

File.Name: f304f_f304-021.jpg
Notes: 16533208299 I believe this is a Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum). You can see the ribs on the outside of the cap, which extend almost to the cap margin. It looks a bit like genus Peziza (see this photo), but the ribs (and buried stem) are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2015)

File.Name: f305a_f305-006.jpg
Notes:
16728167271 This is a not-quite-perfect shot of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly perched in the woods. It's resting on a tangle of native plants - Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae), California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2015)

Darn, the butterfly landed a few feet away from me while I was crouched down to photograph a flower - but my last photo had been a mushroom in the shade. OK, turn off the shutter delay and self-timer that I use for all my mushroom shots; bump up the ISO to 280; switch the white balance from shade to sun... and take the photo! D'oh, I was still at f/22 aperture, and the photo is not as sharp as it should be. Oh well, it's still a nice anodyne to the spiders I've been showing!

My truck is still in the shop, and I'm still grounded in the canyon with a bum foot which makes it painful to walk. I did manage an easy walk today. The problem with the truck is a bad heater hose, which they had to order. It's hopefully fixed by now so I can pick it up tomorrow. Damn cars!
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Climbing Penstemon; California Honeysuckle; California Buckwheat; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: f305b_f305-022.jpg
Notes:
16106933254 Here are top and rear photos of a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on last year's dead flower head of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea, Lamiaceae). It looks a bit like the Diaea livens spider in this photo, but the colors are less intense and the shape of its carapace is different. Close, but not the same. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2015)

The photo on the right is a pretty good "butt shot" too. HBBBT!
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Diaea livens
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae; Diaea livens

File.Name: f305c_f305-009.jpg
Notes: 16543097479 This is another perfect Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). It's just waiting for prey! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2015)

File.Name: f305d_f305-012.jpg
Notes: 16729077955 I guess this is the nest of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers, built around a fallen and somehow ensnared oak leaf. The spider has a separate orb web nearby which you can barely make out in the background. Like pirates, these spiders decorate their nests with the bodies of their prey. Pretty grisly! You can see just a bit of the spider left of center at the bottom of the nest. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2015)

File.Name: f305e_f305-029.jpg
Notes: 16541473280 This is a swollen stem of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that's been galled by a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella, family Cosmopterigidae, in the order Lepidoptera). As I understand, the moths pupate and emerge about now. There's also the web of a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) in the lower-right, and you can see the spider sticking out at the bottom of the photo. I didn't notice it when I got this shot. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2015)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth; Sheetweb Spider
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera; Linyphiidae
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera; Linyphiidae

File.Name: f305f_f303-002.jpg
Notes: 16542715429 This beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom has been brought down by insect activity. I guess the insects are not effected by the toxins. That broad leaf in the background is a native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus, Liliaceae). This might be a good year for them if our "small rains" keep up. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2015)

File.Name: f306a_f306-013.jpg
Notes:
16116367494 Lizard went a-courtin' and he did ride...

This is a brilliant male Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) with full mating colors on top of our retaining wall along the edge of the garden. I dig those dewlaps! This male was a-courtin' the lady lizard in my [Previous] photo. I interrupted their courtship by intruding with my camera, but I guess they did just fine after I left. This concrete-block wall attracts lizards, no doubt because it's warm from the morning sun. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2015)

I have wheels again! I got my truck back from the repair shop this morning, but I still got my lead photos from home this morning. It was almost too nice today, warm not too breezy.
ScientificName: Lizard went a-courtin' and he did ride...; Sceloporus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Lizard went a-courtin' and he did ride...; Sceloporus occidentalis

File.Name: f306b_f306-015.jpg
Notes:
16737267501 "I feel pretty, oh so pretty..."

This is a lady Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on top of our retaining wall along the edge of the garden. She was the object of the male's attention, see my [Next] photo. She is less blue than the male, but still has some pretty scales on her belly. I interrupted their courtship by intruding with my camera, but I guess they did just fine after I left. This concrete-block wall attracts lizards, no doubt because it's warm from the morning sun. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2015)

Alice, I didn't want to admit it at first, but I'm sure you're right that the lizards in this photo were two guys. This female lizard does have some turquoise spotting, though less extensive than the male. I hope this really is a female. I've had nagging doubts about that...
ScientificName: I feel pretty, oh so pretty..."; Sceloporus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: I feel pretty, oh so pretty..."; Sceloporus occidentalis

File.Name: f306c_f306-001.jpg
Notes: 16552288479 This is a brilliant California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling"on damp soil in our garden this morning. It must be newly-emerged to still have those blue colors on its forewings. These pretty butterflies never come to flowers, but they do "puddle" in damp soil like this garden path to take moisture. I guess they are born with full bellies and fly until they run out of fuel. I've been seeing them now and then, but this is the first one I've been able to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2015)

File.Name: f306d_f306-018.jpg
Notes:
16118366803 I can't explain what this photo shows, but I can tell how I got it...

I found this native terrestrial Shoulderband Snail (Helminthoglypta, Gastropoda) in our parking area at home today. The unfortunate large snail has been stepped on or run over, and it's oozing out. But look at that tiny snail on the top. Is it being a scavenger? Sometimes I'm glad I can say "I'm just the photographer, don't as me what it means!" (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2015)

File.Name: f306e_f306-042.jpg
Notes:
16550761590 Here is a brand new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They are pretty common on San Marcos pass - both in woodland and chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2015)

These native shrubs have an interesting distribution. The specimen map at Calflora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There is just one record from San Luis Obispo county.

File.Name: f306f_f306-030.jpg
Notes: 16736887001 These are new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family - with a pair of galled flowers towards the bottom. I've seen something similar on its relative California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica), see this photo. I have no idea what critter causes these galls. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2015)
ScientificName: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica

File.Name: f307a_f307-041.jpg
Notes:
16722779846 Here are pretty blue flowers of native Tuberous Skullcap aka Danny's Skullcap (Scutellaria tuberosa) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I wonder who Danny is? I found this little mint near a trail at Lake Cachuma today. It's said to be a fire-follower, and this place burned about 15 years ago. It's odd that I've never seen it here before, but maybe I just didn't notice it. They are small plants, just 2 or 3 inches high. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 7 March 2015)

I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I made my usual stop near Lake Cachuma and found a few interesting flowers. It was warm today - up to 78∞ on our upstairs porch and 84∞ at the river. I have a fading memory of rain...

File.Name: f307b_f307-037.jpg
Notes: 16747419621 These are pretty new flowers of Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi) in the Ranunculaceae plant family near Lake Cachuma. At home, we have the very similar Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum which is just starting to flower. Very beautiful! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 7 March 2015)
ScientificName: Delphinium parryi; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum
associatedTaxa: Delphinium parryi; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum

File.Name: f307c_f307-023.jpg
Notes: 16128592043 Here are the first new leaves and flowers I've seen this year on native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. It's a striking shrub or small tree with odd-pinnate leaves. I expect it to find it near the river, but there's also an odd shrub up here in the mountains. Last year, I found the first flowers on March 27, so this one is early but not crazy-early. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 March 2015)

File.Name: f307d_f307-004.jpg
Notes:
16541038337 Many native plants are flowering early this, so I went looking for the uncommon native Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family this morning. I found a couple of plants with flower buds, but no flowers yet. The plants have whorls of very skinny leaves, like a lily on a diet. See this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2015)

This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

File.Name: f307e_f307-042.jpg
Notes:
16746709191 Even humble Wild Blackberries (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) have mysteries!

The photo on the left is a typical flower from the east side of San Marcos Pass, it's staminate with a central core of pistils. The photo on the right is a typical flower from the west side of the pass, it's pistillate, and I don't see any stamens. Sure enough, the Jepson Flora says that most flowers of this plant are unisexual. Maybe that's why I rarely find fruit? (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2015)

All the plants I've seen on the east side of the pass (where I live) are staminate. Most of the plants I've seen on the east side of the pass are pistillate. Are they both separate clones? I really don't understand this, but if we could just get the two together...
Locality: where I live) are staminate.  Most of the plants I've seen on the east side of the pass are pistillate.  Are they both separate clones?  I really don't understand this

File.Name: f307f_f306-031.jpg
Notes: 16560425500 I believe these pretty flowers are Brewer's Red Maids (Calandrinia breweri) in the Portulacaceae plant family, growing in place in the mountains that was cleared a few years ago. It usually grows as a fire follower, but sometimes clearing the chaparral is enough to trigger these plants. What beautiful colors it has, though you have to get down on your hands and knees to appreciate them. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2015)

File.Name: f308a_f308-001.jpg
Notes:
16734239086 This is a brand new Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly taking moisture from the road in our canyon. It's the first one I've seen this year, though I have been following the growth of the caterpillars, most recently a few days ago here. In time, I will have taken enough photos of these butterflies - but not yet! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2015)

It was another lovely day today in our mountains - a few degrees cooler that yesterday, but still hot enough to bring out a sweat. It should be cooler tomorrow, with a chance of another "small rain" on Wednesday night.

File.Name: f308b_f308-007.jpg
Notes:
16139950293 This is a beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched on its host plant, the native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. I suspect "it'" is a she, and she might have just oviposited - see that little white speck just below her thorax? (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2015)

Some insects lay their eggs in a cluster, see this photo. Other insects like to spread them around, to avoid competition. That makes sense.
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever

File.Name: f308c_f308-008.jpg
Notes:
16733773516 This is a whole plant of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. Note that it has a flower stalk, but no flowers yet. It's growing in an unlikely place, under the leaves of a Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. The Dudleya is the host plant for the beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2015)

These woodland yuccas seem different, with longer and thinner leaves than the plants that grow in the direct sun. I suspect it has something to do with our fire history, though our canyon hasn't burned since the Refugio Fire in 1955.
CommonNames: Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Chaparral Yucca; Sonoran Blue
ScientificName: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae; Philotes sonorensis
associatedTaxa: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae; Philotes sonorensis

File.Name: f308d_f308-015.jpg
Notes: 16758414011 These are the first open flowers of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon. Like all lupines, they have hand-shaped "palmate" leaves - like holding your hand open with fingers extended. We have many different species. This one is perennial, with semi-whorled flowers. Last year I found the first flowers on March 11, so these are right on schedule. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2015)

File.Name: f308e_f308-032.jpg
Notes: 16758280621 Here are new flowers of native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing East Camino Cielo road in the mountains. I think of it as a fire-follower, but it's happy on disturbed roadsides too. It also grows in our canyon at home. The skinny truncate leaflets and bi-colored flowers that are not whorled are distinctive. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 March 2015)

File.Name: f308f_f308-013.jpg
Notes:
16573167219 I found this rather large and solid mushroom in the woods this morning. I guess it's a Tricholma (Tricholoma) mushroom, but I don't know what kind. (Tricholoma resplendens comes to mind.) It's growing in deep oak mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in a place where the sun never shines. The gills look amber-colored, but you can see a natural white spore print under the mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2015)

The woods are quickly drying out after our rain last weekend, and I suspect the time for mushrooms is just about over. QuÈ l·stima.

File.Name: f309a_f309-002.jpg
Notes:
16583611360 Today was a good day for butterflies, but it was breezy and I couldn't get close to any of them. Making a collage seemed the way to go. I got all these photos today, except for the one in the lower-right that I got yesterday. Going clockwise from the upper-left, here they are:

Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) perched for just a moment on a branch of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae), the first one I've managed to photograph this year (San Marcos Pass);

Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) on flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum, Liliaceae or Themidaceae), the first one I've managed to photograph this year (Paradise);

Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) on flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum, Liliaceae or Themidaceae) (Paradise);

Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) perched on native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) (San Marcos Pass);

Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) perched for a moment on a flower head of California Plantain (Plantago erecta, Plantaginaceae) (Paradise);

Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) taking off from flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum, Liliaceae or Themidaceae) (Paradise).

*Whew* That's really 11 photos today instead of my usual 6, I'm tired. It was supposed to be cooler today, but it was still warm enough to bring out a sweat in the sun, 81∞ by the river where I was today. There still a slight chance of rain on Wednesday, but it doesn't seem very likely today. (San Marcos Pass and Paradise, 9 March 2015)
CommonNames: Propertius Duskywing; California Honeysuckle; Monarch Butterfly; Blue Dick; Variable Checkerspot; Blue Dick; Sonoran Blue; Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Common Buckeye; California Plantain; Western Tiger Swallowtail; Blue Dick
ScientificName: Erynnis propertius; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Danaus plexippus; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Philotes sonorensis; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Junonia coenia; Plantago erecta; Plantaginaceae; Papilio rutulus; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
associatedTaxa: Erynnis propertius; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Danaus plexippus; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae; Philotes sonorensis; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Junonia coenia; Plantago erecta; Plantaginaceae; Papilio rutulus; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
