File.Name: f309b_f309-064.jpg
Notes: 16769785912 These are the first open flowers of a beautiful Larkspur in the Ranunculaceae plant family in our canyon at home, with the sun behind. I believe they are Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, but cp. Delphinium parryi. This genus is hard to key out and I might have it wrong. The key difference seems to be whether the stem base is narrowly attached to the roots or widely attached. Last year, I tried to feel my way down a stem, and it broke off. See this photo. That seems a pretty harsh way to identify a species! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2015)
ScientificName: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi
associatedTaxa: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi

File.Name: f309c_f309-032.jpg
Notes: 16769588891 This is a new flower spike of native Succulent Lupine (Lupinus succulentus) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing in a place that was burned almost two years ago by the White Fire. I usually see these growing along the highway, so it's nice to find them growing as fire-followers, where they should be. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 March 2015)

File.Name: f309d_f309-021.jpg
Notes:
16770574205 This is a small poppy field along the Aliso Canyon trail in Paradise. These flowers are the Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family, which lack the collar or disk below the flower that the real California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) has. See this photo for a comparison. I think of these as the "real California Poppy" in our area because so many seeds of Eschscholzia californica are scattered along the roads. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 March 2015)

The flowers appeared early this year, but they are disappearing early as well. If you want to see large patches of wildflowers like this, you better go soon.
ScientificName: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia californica
associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia californica

File.Name: f309e_f309-018.jpg
Notes: 16744585066 I believe these are new florets of native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua aka Nasella cernua) in the Poacea plant family. This handsome native grass is similar to the Purple Needle Grass (Stipa pulchra aka Nasella pulchra) in this photo, but it develops "twisted awns" as it matures - barely visible in this photo. This one is more common inland from the mountains. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 March 2015)
CommonNames: Nodding Needle Grass; Purple Needle Grass
ScientificName: Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poacea; Stipa pulchra; Nasella pulchra
associatedTaxa: Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poacea; Stipa pulchra; Nasella pulchra

File.Name: f309f_f309-036.jpg
Notes: 16150458203 This is a stem gall on a new plant of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. This plant has skinny leaves, and is approaching var. stenophyllus of that species. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 March 2015)
CommonNames: stem gall; Fleabane Daisy; Gall Midge

File.Name: f310a_f310-003.jpg
Notes:
16160649883 This is another beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon this morning, near its host plant, the native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's one of my favorite butterflies. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2015)

I had a short photo day today because a dear friend came by to visit in the afternoon. No complaints, we had a good visit and I still managed to get some nice photos in the morning. Clouds have been moving in during the day, and it feels like we might actually get a splash of rain tomorrow. It's still only a 20% chance, so we'll see what happens. It sure would be nice to get a good soak before our long hot summer sets in. This storm won't do it, but maybe the next one will.
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever

File.Name: f310b_f310-006.jpg
Notes: 16573098067 I found this beautiful green Sawfly ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family this morning. Looking at BugGuide, it might be genus Rhogogaster or Nematus, both in the family Tenthredinidae. I was hoping it would be one of our gall-producing sawflies, but I guess not. It sure is pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2015)
ScientificName: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Rhogogaster; Nematus; Tenthredinidae
associatedTaxa: Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Rhogogaster; Nematus; Tenthredinidae

File.Name: f310c_f310-011.jpg
Notes: 16160307253 I found this small Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods this morning. I think it might be species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae, see this photo. This is a good view of its mighty proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2015)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis; Harpactorinae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Pselliopus spinicollis; Harpactorinae

File.Name: f310d_f310-001.jpg
Notes:
16754285586 This is a tiny orange insect egg on new flower buds of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree that's almost on our upstairs porch. Maybe it's a butterfly egg? I know they like to scatter their eggs one by one.

Nah, I didn't notice the tiny egg at first - but I did notice a tiny Sawfly ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) trying to oviposit on the left-most bud this morning. I went to fetch my camera and tripod, but by the time I got back the wasp was gone. That's when I noticed the egg. I'm sure this egg is too large for that tiny wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2015)

File.Name: f310e_f309-024.jpg
Notes: 16780021405 Here are whole plants with flowers of native Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) in the Violaceae plant family. They look like little garden violets, but these are natives, growing on soil burned by the White Fire almost two years ago. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 March 2015)

File.Name: f310f_f309-060.jpg
Notes:
16754069396 These are new flowers of a plant of native Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis) in the Apiaceaeplant family, growing in a place that was burned almost two years ago by White Fire. I can't call it a "first flower" since other plants have sent up much taller flower heads. I had to hunt around to find this this specimen that's just beginning to flower, and shows both flowers and foliage in the same shot. I haven't seen flowers at home yet. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 March 2015)

Sanicles are another "collectors plant" in our mountains. We have 9 different species in SB county according to my flora! They all have similar flowers in little umbel-balls, but different foliage. I found three different species in flower on my walk yesterday.

File.Name: f311a_f311-005.jpg
Notes:
16167660174 This is a rather small Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It's rather small for a Crane Fly, maybe 1/2 inch long. It might be a Winter Crane Fly (Trichoceridae) though I can't make out ocelli. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2015)

Today was a crummy day day for new photos, gloomy with an annoying steady breeze. I did my best, and I always try to post today's photos even when I have "better" ones from yesterday. We did get a bit of rain today, but just a bit, probably less than 1/100 inch. *Sigh* And now the weather is forecast to turn record-breaking hot this weekend.
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Wild Sweetpea; Winter Crane Fly
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Trichoceridae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Trichoceridae

File.Name: f311b_f311-009.jpg
Notes: 16603704259 This is just a fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family this morning. It does look bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae), but maybe it's just another Muscidae. It was really a crummy day for new photos - gloomy and breezy - but this one came out surprisingly good. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2015)
ScientificName: Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Tachinidae; Muscidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Tachinidae; Muscidae

File.Name: f311c_f311-006.jpg
Notes: 16789723075 This is a small dark Sawfly ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods, on a gloomy day. This is probably the one I've been seeing in the oak trees recently, maybe ovipositing on the new buds. That's all I know about them. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2015)

File.Name: f311d_f311-008.jpg
Notes:
16167247924 I believe this is the debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) insect in the insect order Neuroptera. You can see its pincher-like jaws on the left end. I know this isn't the best photo, but what a great critter! I found this one on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2015)

See this photo and BugGuide for more photos of these interesting creatures. It was a gloomy day today - not cloudy-bright, but cloudy-dark, with an annoying steady breeze. I increased ISO and aperture, and this was still my best shot. I'll try again tomorrow.

File.Name: f311e_f311-016.jpg
Notes: 16603292809 The insect egg I found

File.Name: f311f_f311-014.jpg
Notes: 16789323665 Here are whole plants with flowers of California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family, showing their usual habitat on a shady sandstone bank. I thought a gloomy day might work to get a photo of these shade-loving flowers, but the breeze and low-light made it impossible. I have another photo of the pretty flowers here. They are not common in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2015)

File.Name: f312a_f312-028.jpg
Notes:
16772861036 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They are pretty common on San Marcos pass - both in woodland and chaparral. The shrubs are unusual, a singleton in its family here in California. The specimen map at Calflora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There is just one record from San Luis Obispo county. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2015)

What a difference a day makes! Yesterday was cool and cloudy, but today is sunny and warm, almost 80∞ on the upstairs porch, and hotter is expected this weekend.

And what a difference a day makes... now we live in a world without author Terry Pratchett. His Diskworld books are among the select group I can dip into and read over and over again, in good company with Rex Stout and P.G. Wodehouse.

Rest in peace, Sir Terry. Thanks for so many stories and endless delights.

File.Name: f312b_f312-015.jpg
Notes: 16176359724 This is a single flower head of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. Plants in the var. inezianus are more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. It's a fire-follower plant, but this one is growing in a place that was cleared and not burned. Sometimes that's enough. This is one of my favorite wildflowers. See my [Previous] photo for the whole plant. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2015)

Notes: 16798617475 Here are whole plants (left) and a single flower stalk (right) of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. It's a fire-follower plant, but this one is growing in a place that was cleared and not burned. Sometimes that's enough. This is one of my favorite wildflowers. See my [Next] photo. File.Name: f312d_f312-010.jpg
Notes: 16176151844 This is the first open flower I've seen this year of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The shrubs form their waxy buds early - sometimes in September! - and then wait and wait for the right time to open near the Vernal Equinox. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2015)

File.Name: f312e_f312-014.jpg
Notes: 16611004250 These are new florets of the perennial native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and look like they already have seeds when they are just starting to open. It's seems quite common around here - once you notice it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2015)

File.Name: f312fe_f312-023.jpg
Notes: 16178304923 Here are ripening berries of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They look like they have cloves stuck in them with those little pinhead glands. They start yellow, turn orange, then red, and finally purple if they last that long before the birds get them. I don't care for the taste much myself, too "glandular". (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2015)

File.Name: f313a_f313-036.jpg
Notes:
16620469390 Here are red, white, and blue wildflowers by the river today. No particular reason, I just thought they looked good together. From left to right, here are:

New red flowers of native Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family;

New white flowers of the Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothii ssp. decorticans) in the Onagraceae plant family; and

New blue flowers of the uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family.

See my [Previous] three photos for more info about these plants, though that larkspur is a different one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)

It was warm today, up to 87∞ by the river where I was. But then mid-level clouds moved in from the west and cooled things down. I've noticed that the temperature estimates for tomorrow are a bit lower too.
CommonNames: Scarlet Bugler; Shredding Evening Primrose; Umbrella Larkspur
ScientificName: Penstemon centranthifolius; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae; Delphinium umbraculorum; Ranunculaceae

File.Name: f313b_f313-033.jpg
Notes: 16187790613 I found this large group of new flowers of native Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family near the third river crossing at Paradise today. This is right next to a picnic area, though I don't think I've ever seen such a large mass of flowers before. Last year I found the first flowers on April 11, so these are about one month early. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)

File.Name: f313c_f313-019.jpg
Notes: 16600288477 I believe this is the Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothii ssp. decorticans) in the Onagraceae plant family. I think of it as a desert plant, but I found two separate colonies of them today along Paradise Road on the south slopes of Little Pine Mountain. This place was burned by the White Fire two years ago come May, so I guess it's growing as a fire-follower. I don't think I've ever seen them here before. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)

File.Name: f313d_f313-027.jpg
Notes: 16619926138 These are the fantastic new flowers of a Larkspur in the Ranunculaceae plant family. I believe they are Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, but cp. Delphinium parryi. This genus is hard to key out and I might have it wrong. The key difference seems to be whether the stem base is narrowly attached to the roots or widely attached. I tried to feel down the stem of a small plant nearby, and it broke away - again! That seems a pretty harsh way to identify a species! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)
ScientificName: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi
associatedTaxa: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi

File.Name: f313e_f313-010.jpg
Notes: 16187466113 I believe this is the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. They have pale, washed-out flowers, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy flower heads which look a bit like "woolly bear" caterpillars. There are lots of these starting to flower as fire-followers in places burned by the White Fire two years ago come May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)

File.Name: f313f_f313-043.jpg
Notes:
16806140342 These are wonderful new catkins of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family growing on a tree near Redrock in Paradise. The catkins look so sensual. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)

Does anyone else remember the "catkin scene" in Ken Russell's "Women in Love" (1969) movie? I can't seem to find it on YouTube, but I think it's equal to or better than the fig scene!
Locality: 1969) movie?  I can't seem to find it on YouTube

File.Name: f314a_f314-002.jpg
Notes:
16817127805 This is our resident Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) getting a drink - and a shower! - from the little water fountain in our bathtub water garden! This was another "shoot fast, think later" photo, with no time to move my camera or think about settings, and it's almost a 1:1 crop. But I think it captures the moment. The green plants are cattails. No water is wasted here, it's just a submersible pump to aerate the little pond. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

It was downright hot today - in the mid-80s at home and up to 89∞ by the river where I was yesterday. It was also quite breezy all morning while I was taking photos. Heat, wind, and Saturday traffic on the highway all make me nervous.

File.Name: f314b_f314-018.jpg
Notes: 16197145933 This isn't a wild bee, though it looks bee-like. It's a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably in the genus Abia. One indication is that it has no hairs on it's legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. I found this one today on a brand new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

File.Name: f314c_f314-021.jpg
Notes: 16609613157 This is the nymph of a different Long-horned Grasshopper (Ensifera, Orthoptera) insect, maybe a Shield-backed Katydid in the subfamily Tettigoniinae? I found it perched in a shrub of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). Its antennae go on for a long ways. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

File.Name: f314d_f314-013.jpg
Notes: 16630683689 This is a tiny Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera). It's body without the antennae is less than 1/4 inch long, but the antennae go on for twice that. I guess it's a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) as the Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymphs are more ornate. This one is hanging out on a new leaf of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I dig the details of its eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

File.Name: f314e_f314-026.jpg
Notes:
16194338434 These are sporophytes of small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants - probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). They always remind me of little surfing palm trees. Now why are their "drawers" hanging out? What are those white structures? (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

These plants are managing to fruit, but other liverworts in the canyon have given up. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: f314f_f314-034.jpg
Notes:
16815520962 Fade to black...

These are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants - probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) - going dormant on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon. They are not really turning black - there's some blue in there too, a sign of life. They will completely revive as soon as we get some rain, but maybe not until next autumn. They are real "resurrection plants". These plants are fading without fruit, but see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

File.Name: f315a_f315-016.jpg
Notes:
16641974318 These are conjoined "oak apple galls" of California Gall Wasps (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Hah - it looks suspiciously like the Rosetta spacecraft and Comet 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko! Look for links here. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

I was really looking for butterflies today. I saw a half-dozen Anise Swallowtails (Papilio zelicaon), but I never saw one come to rest for a moment. Maybe I'm just distracted, as Julie and I are heading of to Death Valley in a few days. There's lots to do before we leave...
CommonNames: California Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak; Anise Swallowtails

File.Name: f315b_f315-001.jpg
Notes:
16641673960 Here are whole plants of two different Larkspurs in the Ranunculaceae plant family:

I believe the one on the left is Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum. The stem base is narrowly attached to the roots and easily breaks away. It grows on rocky north-facing slopes in our mountains. The similar Delphinium parryi has a well-attached stem and seems to grow more in open places in the valley. This one has widely spaced flowers and smooth leaves at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

I believe the one on the right is the uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) which is growing as a fire-follower on recent burns. It has more congested flowers on its stalks and more deeply cut leaves, see this photo, on the right. It looks more like a "Pacific Giant" garden hybrid. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)

These larkspurs are among my favorite wildflowers, but they sure are tough to ID!
ScientificName: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi; Delphinium umbraculorum
associatedTaxa: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi; Delphinium umbraculorum

File.Name: f315c_f315-006.jpg
Notes: 16621596507 This is (almost) the first new flower of native Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in a rocky place on San Marcos Pass. There were some new flowers a week ago, but they were at the top of the shrub and I couldn't capture both flowers and the beautiful foliage. It's a good-sized shrub, 3-8 feet tall. So many different lupines, from finger size to shrubs! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

File.Name: f315d_f315-011.jpg
Notes:
16206379994 This is an unopened flower bud of a native California Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. There are also violet forms in our mountains that look quite different. You can see the cobwebby hairs that are its namesake. These natives never flowered last year, so I was happy to see this bud. Long time, no see! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the natives and the weedy thistles. The Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt, leave it be!
Locality: Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare

File.Name: f315e_f315-024.jpg
Notes:
16827322472 These are cucumbers you wouldn't want to slice into your salad!

Both are the fruit of Wild Cucumber in the Cucurbitaceae plant family, but they are different. I believe the fruit on the left belongs to California Manroot (Marah fabacea), and the fruit on the right belongs to Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpa). One difference is that M. fabacea on the left has 4 large seeds, while M. macrocarpa on the right has 8 to 15 smaller seeds in a gnarlier pod. The flowers are different too, those of M. fabacea being smaller and fatter and a bit more yellowish. We seem to be at the intersection between these two different species. See NatureShutterbug's comment to this this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

I got the M. fabacea photo today near Knapp's Castle, but I got the the M. macrocarpa photo a few weeks ago, on February 21 near the Painted Cave community. That's another difference, M. macrocarpa flowers and fruits earlier, mostly on the south slopes of our mountains. The other ones are more in the interior.
CommonNames: Wild Cucumber; California Manroot; Chilicothe; Manroot

File.Name: f315f_f315-015.jpg
Notes: 16828296875 Here are pretty new seed pods of native Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. We have several different kinds of Ceanothus in our mountains. This one has opposite toothed leaves with "corky stipules", and leaves that are whitish-tomentose underneath. I think you can see all the key characters in this photo. Look on the very-right base of the stem for the stipules. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

File.Name: f316a_f316-002.jpg
Notes:
16838571562 This is looking at the underwings of a beautiful California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly in our garden this morning. I reckon it was trying to "puddle" on the damp soil - if the darned photographer didn't keep bugging it! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2015)

Julie kindly agreed to take our camper-van down to Ventura this morning for a bit of service, so I had one more day to get a few photos. (Thanks Julie!) Tomorrow will be a work day, organizing and shopping for our Death Valley trip starting Wednesday. No new photos tomorrow, but I have plenty in the archive to share.
Locality: Thanks Julie!)  Tomorrow will be a work day

File.Name: f316b_f316-013.jpg
Notes:
16219497443 I guess this is a Band-winged Grasshopper (subfamily Oedipodinae, in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera), but I'm not sure what kind, and I don't have time to pursue it tonight. It's the first proper grasshopper than I've managed to photograph this year. I found it on the road in our canyon. It didn't have yellow wings when it took off. I don't know what it is, but I'll bet Alice does! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2015)

Compare this photo of another early-season grasshopper from 2012. Maybe Lactista gibbosus? But I'm sure it didn't have yellow wings when it flew away.

File.Name: f316c_f316-012.jpg
Notes: 16651746610 I found this rather large Millipede (Diplopoda, Myriapoda) on the road in our canyon this morning. I moved it off the road. It's maybe 3 inches long, and fatter than a pencil. At first I thought it might have been run over, but it looks intact and was moving. On the other hand, I checked back a half-hour later, and it was still in the same position. It might be genus Hiltonius or Tylobolus. These critters can exude fluids with a foul smell which stains your skin when you pick them up, but this one didn't. As opposed to centipedes, these creatures have two legs on each side of most of their body segments, except for the first few with just one leg per segment. Sometimes I find them parasitized. I wish I knew more about them. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2015)

File.Name: f316d_f316-005.jpg
Notes: 16651433838 I found this furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) resting on a leaf of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) in the garden this morning. Looking at BugGuide, I think it might be genus Conophorus. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2015)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Conophorus
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Conophorus

File.Name: f316e_f316-001.jpg
Notes:
16651442560 This is a small Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) perched on a stick on the edge of our garden. This was my first photo of the day, and it was woefully under-exposed - another "shoot fast, think later" photo! I did manage to brighten it up a bit, because this is a record shot - the first damselfly I've seen near home this year. It has attractive "blue dun" colors. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2015)

Better too dark than too light. You can usually brighten a photo that's too dark, but if you blow out the highlights you're out of luck.

File.Name: f316f_f315-007.jpg
Notes:
16837630681 Soft white insect cocoons on a dead stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. A few have opened. At first I thought it might be just a bit of styrofoam stuck to the stem, but it's not. I found a few more of these cocoons as I walked along, some on different plants. Alice Abelia thinks they are Braconid Wasps, and the pictures in her link look convincing. (See comment below.) (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

It's curious that the soft white plants of Fragrant Everlasting (Gnaphalium canescens) in the Asteraceae plant family were growing nearby. I wonder if there is a connection?
ScientificName: Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Gnaphalium canescens; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Gnaphalium canescens; Asteraceae

File.Name: f317a_f315-022.jpg
Notes:
16662173238 This is a shrub of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family in full flower. In the background is the ridge to the left of Little Pine Mountain, and behind that is the high ridge of Mission Pine Basin. In front of the cherry is the gray foliage of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. The cherry flowers look cream colored from a distance, but they look quite white up close - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2015)

After all my usual pre-trip anxiety, it turns out we're not going to Death Valley tomorrow. Our camper-van is still acting up, as is Julie's leg. I know about leg and back pain! There's even a chance of rain tomorrow - though rain in the desert is quite wonderful! We'll try to get out to the Carrizo Plains instead, maybe after the weekend. And there's plenty to see right at home!
ScientificName: Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Eriodictyon Traskiae; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Eriodictyon Traskiae; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: f317b_f314-007.jpg
Notes: 16662117348 This is the first new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family that I've seen this year in our canyon at home. The flowers of this shrub are a butterfly favorite later in the season, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

File.Name: f317c_f314-005.jpg
Notes:
16642232567 These are the very glandular top bracts of a flower stalk of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family, with the sun behind. I really think these glandular bracts are more beautiful than the flowers - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2015)

It's interesting that I've seen many bracts on flower stalks of these plants this year, but not so many actual flowers. Maybe it's still too early, but maybe the drought is giving them second thoughts.

File.Name: f317d_f313-011.jpg
Notes: 16227123774 These are striking new flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family growing near the river. I found the first new flower on San Marcos Pass on March 12 - see this photo - but these look like they've been going longer. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)

File.Name: f317e_f313-038.jpg
Notes: 16663171109 I found this flowering plant of native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua aka Nasella cernua) in the Poaceae plant family near the picnic area at third river crossing of Paradise. I couldn't get that large rock out of the photo with out showing the parking area! It's still an interesting place for wild plants. You can see a native Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) starting to flower just to the right of the grass. (See this photo taken on the same day.) (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 March 2015)
ScientificName: Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poaceae; Penstemon centranthifolius

File.Name: f317f_f313-003.jpg
Notes: 16661608198 *Sigh* This might be the last mushroom in the woods this year - still fresh on the left (from March 11) and looking shriveled on the right (from March 13) after the heat set in. These are photos of the same mushroom, I believe the same kind I show in this photo. It used to be genus Nolanea in the pink-spored Entolomataceae family, but apparently that genus has now been absorbed by the larger Entoloma genus. Maybe it's Entoloma vernum aka Nolanea verna? The gills have some pink (or flesh) colors in the left photo, more so on the right. It's sad to see the mushrooms go. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2015)
ScientificName: Nolanea; Entolomataceae; Entoloma; Entoloma vernum; Nolanea verna

File.Name: f318a_f318-043.jpg
Notes:
16237366384 I believe this is a mid-stage caterpillar of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) navigating between stems of a native California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) in the Onagraceae plant family. See my [Previous] two photos. I think I saw an adult in nearby Aliso Canyon last week, but I couldn't get a photo. No problem, I reckon there are many more coming. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2015)

We had more wind than rain today. There was not a drop of the wet stuff, though there were thunderheads over the back country, so maybe there was rain somewhere in the county. I walked the river trail that goes from the Sage Hill campground today, and got some nice photos.
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae

File.Name: f318b_f318-028.jpg
Notes: 16672126510 I believe these are different instars of caterpillars of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) on stems of fire-follower plants of native California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) in the Onagraceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for the interesting plant. The photo on the left shows the middle stage, and the photo on the right shows the late stage. There's also an early stage of small green caterpillars, but it didn't get the photo. I have a photo of the adult moth here. I think I saw an adult in nearby Aliso Canyon last week, but I couldn't get a photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2015)
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae

File.Name: f318c_f318-040.jpg
Notes: 16671997420 Here are two photos of California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) in the Onagraceae plant family growing near the flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. I got the photo on the left last month, and it shows the interesting basal leaves of the plants. I got the photo on the right today - the basal leaves are faded, and the plants look greener. These are growing as fire-followers on soil burned by the White Fire two years ago come May. This is another local plant with desert affinities. Caterpillars like them - see my [Next] two photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2015)

File.Name: f318d_f318-022.jpg
Notes: 16239232483 This is a new flower of native Climbing Snapdragon (Antirrhinum kelloggii) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. The long pedicels are the hallmark of this species, though this one isn't twining or u-shaped. I think of them as more of a coastal plant, but I found this one growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by White Fire the two years ago come May. It's the first one I've found in many years. These are tough plants to photograph with those long stems! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2015)

File.Name: f318e_f318-016.jpg
Notes:
16672931929 This is one of the first flowers of native California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's growing as a fire-follower in this place, but I also find it scattered in the woods, see this photo from our canyon at home. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2015)

The flowers and buds are very similar to the Desert Chicory (Rafinesquia neomexicana) - see this old photo from Fotolog. That's part of the reason I love this area so much - we have thimbleberries and huckleberries with northern affinities, but we also have plants with desert affinities like this.
Locality: Rafinesquia neomexicana) - see this old photo from Fotolog. That's part of the reason I love this area so much - we have thimbleberries and huckleberries with northern affinities

File.Name: f318f_f318-012.jpg
Notes: 16671419178 This is looking close at new florets of native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua aka Nasella cernua) in the Poaceae plant family. If you look close at those awns that run at a slight diagonal from right to left just above the center of my photo, you can see that they are indeed twisted, which is the hallmark of this species. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2015)

File.Name: f319a_f319-003.jpg
Notes:
16867830531 This is another beautiful California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly in our garden, perched for a moment on a sandstone river rock. It still has pretty blue colors so it must be newly emerged, but this one is already missing a slice of its left wing. These butterflies never visit flowers to feed. I guess they are born full, and they fly until they are empty. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2015)

Today started windy with "fog winds" as the marine layer fog got just over the top of the mountains... and then backed off. We had some breeze all day, but not too bad.

File.Name: f319b_f319-013.jpg
Notes:
16681035248 This is a small ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) with a drop of honeydew that its collected from (I believe) a "Gall-like Scale Insect" in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. The scale insect was exuding drops of honeydew, but not directly from its butt, more to the side. I know this isn't the best photo - it was breezy, the focus-plane isn't right, and the light is too contrasty. I did the best I could. It shows something really interesting! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2015)

The ants might be the same California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae that usually devil us at home in the spring. It's curious that we haven't had any problems this year, though the winged Carpenter Ants (Camponotus) coming from the walls have been an issue.
Locality: Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae that usually devil us at home in the spring. It's curious that we haven't had any problems this year
CommonNames: ant; Gall-like Scale Insect; California Velvety Tree Ants; Carpenter Ants
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus

File.Name: f319c_f319-020.jpg
Notes:
16248524453 This is a rather small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) on an old leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). It can't be much more than 1/4 inch long. It's orb web is between the spider and my camera, and the spider has a line hooked on its screen-left leg to detect vibrations. It looks like one of the large bottlecap-size spider that collect under the eaves of our house in the fall, though it's much smaller. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2015)

It looks a lot like the photos of Araneus bispinosus that Alice Abelia has been posting lately - compare this photo.

File.Name: f319d_f319-004.jpg
Notes: 16842458056 This is a small Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) with its prey at the top of its dome-like web. I believe the victim is one of the small dark Sawflies ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) that are so common on the oaks right now. This is also a pretty good "butt shot" - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2015)

File.Name: f319e_f319-035.jpg
Notes: 16248209603 These are spectacularly galled new leaves of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. The tumor-like galls are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. Specialized aphids live inside those pockets on the leaves. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2015)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid

File.Name: f319f_f318-044.jpg
Notes:
16680458288 I don't understand this photo at all!

This is a "butt shot" of a mid-stage caterpillar of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) on a stem of a native California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) in the Onagraceae plant family. See my [Previous] photos here and here. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2015)

But what's that reddish thing that sure seems to be coming from it's butt? Caterpillars don't lay eggs, right? And it doesn't look like frass. I'm happy to still find mysteries in the woods after all these years!
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae

File.Name: f320a_f320-003.jpg
Notes:
16876881802 These are brand new flowers of native Common Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family, the first I've seen this year. I found them growing along Paradise Road today. If you look close, you can see a tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) in the flower to the right. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2015)

It's an appropriate photo today, which happens to be the first official day of spring. It's also the vernal equinox. Happy equinox! It's that time of year when the path of the sun changes the most every day. We're finally getting some sunlight in our north-facing garden!

File.Name: f320b_f320-015.jpg
Notes: 16877932985 This is the whole plant of an uncommon Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothii ssp. decorticans aka Eremothera boothii ssp. decorticans) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's growing on a crumbly slope of Monterey Shale on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River today. I think of it as a desert plant, but here it is happy on a south-facing slope by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2015)
ScientificName: Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae

File.Name: f320c_f320-020.jpg
Notes: 16257767903 This is another caterpillar of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata), though this one is munching stems of Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothii ssp. decorticans aka Eremothera boothii ssp. decorticans) in the Onagraceae plant family. I guess they like these plants that used to be considered genus Camissonia . The caterpillar has pretty much stripped the stem on the right, and is now moving over to strip the other stem. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2015)
CommonNames: White-Lined Sphinx Moth; Shredding Evening Primrose
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae; Camissonia
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Camissonia boothii; decorticans; Eremothera boothii; decorticans; Onagraceae; Camissonia

File.Name: f320d_f320-029.jpg
Notes: 16876422762 Here is a nice group of Larkspurs in the Ranunculaceae plant family, probably the usually uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) which is growing as a fire-follower on the burn of the White Fire two years ago come May. It's taller, with more congested flowers on its stalks, and more deeply cut leaves than our other native species. But larkspurs are a difficult group, and I might (always!) be wrong. Whatever they are, they are sure pretty! I found this group today along the road to Upper Oso campground. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2015)

File.Name: f320e_f320-013.jpg
Notes: 16257469603 This sure looks like a plant of the Carmel Valley Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. arachnoidea) in the Asteraceae plant family, but what's it doing here in Santa Barbara County? I guess it's just a extra-tomentose form of the South Coast Range Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. commutata), but it definitely keys to the Carmel Valley form. Very interesting! See this comparison shot of the different varieties in our mountains. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2015)
CommonNames: Carmel Valley Malacothrix; South Coast Range Malacothrix
ScientificName: Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; commutata
associatedTaxa: Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; commutata

File.Name: f320f_f318-002.jpg
Notes:
16689956340 This is just a fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on an old leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I believe it's a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae. It's one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2015)

It's just a fly, but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even when I don't know what they are!
ScientificName: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae

File.Name: f321a_f321-029.jpg
Notes:
16266133734 This is a handsome Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly perched for just a moment on a new leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. I've been seeing them, but this is the first one I've managed to get a decent photo of this year. It's a large skipper with translucent orange spots on it's forewings. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2015)

It was 10 degrees cooler today than yesterday, and breezy all day, but it turned out to be a good day for insect photos in our canyon at home. I took 250 photos, kept 33, moved 13 to my "Good Stuff" folder, and posted these 6. It was one of those days.

File.Name: f321b_f321-032.jpg
Notes:
16680901207 This is a tiny nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) munching a new leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I believe it's a tiny Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). Compare this photo. It's really cute! Butterflies first, but this is my favorite photo today. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2015)

This critter is really tiny. I got the photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. The critter is about 1190 pixels long. That makes it about 4.7 mm long, about 3/16 inch. See this photo for how I figured.
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae

File.Name: f321c_f321-017.jpg
Notes: 16268069153 This is an impossibly-thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). This is the first one of these fantastic critters that I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2015)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: f321d_f321-001.jpg
Notes: 16861932266 I believe this is the larva of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). That single eye distinguishes it from a real caterpillar (Lepidopteran) which have multiple eyes in a circle or crescent. This debris-carrying larva is carrying a load of fluff. I wonder if it made the fluff or found it? I got this photo on top of our concrete retaining wall on the edge of our garden not too far from the unfortunate larva in my [Previous] photo. I don't think it's the same one. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2015)

File.Name: f321e_f321-002.jpg
Notes:
16267690703 These are small ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tearing apart an insect larva - probably the larva of a Sawfly ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). I believe the ants are California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae. Yes, they are vicious. I got this photo on top of our concrete retaining wall on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2015)

These are the ants usually devil us inside our home in the spring, see this photo. They are the ants that bite you (and sting!) when you climb in an oak tree. It's curious that we haven't had any problems this year, though the winged Carpenter Ants (Camponotus) coming from the walls have been an issue.
Locality: and sting!) when you climb in an oak tree.  It's curious that we haven't had any problems this year
CommonNames: ants; Sawfly; California Velvety Tree Ants; Carpenter Ants
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus

File.Name: f321f_f321-019.jpg
Notes:
16701350099 I believe this is a Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. The small ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on its back is protecting it from predators. It looks like it's howling! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2015)

The ants get honeydew from the scale insect in exchange for the protection, see this photo from yesterday. That one had the ant more or less sharp and the scale insect out of focus. This one has the scale in focus but not the ant. One of these days I'll get them both right!

The ant might be one of the same California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae in my [Next] photo. These are the ants usually devil us inside our home in the spring. It's curious that we haven't had any problems this year, though the winged Carpenter Ants (Camponotus) coming from the walls have been an issue.
Locality: Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae in my [Next] photo. These are the ants usually devil us inside our home in the spring. It's curious that we haven't had any problems this year
CommonNames: Gall-like Scale Insect; ant; California Velvety Tree Ants; Carpenter Ants
ScientificName: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus
associatedTaxa: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Camponotus

File.Name: f322a_f322-028.jpg
Notes:
16280798303 Here is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly perched for a moment on a grass blade. It's a Spread-wing Skipper (Pyrgiinae, Lepidoptera) without a white fringe on its hindwings. This one already looks pretty tattered. Life is tough for all these butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

"Butterflies first" has been a good rule of thumb, better than the more depressing photos of the larvae eating our oaks which follow! Today started gloomy in the marine-layer fog, then cleared up, then got gloomy and breezy again. The wind came first from the south, then from the north as a dry storm front moved over. The next few days are expected to be windy. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: f322b_f322-001.jpg
Notes:
16713023818 I believe these are caterpillar-like Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae munching a catkin of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that's closest to our upstairs deck. The larvae have simple eyes and more than five prolegs. I don't mind the catkins, but these critters have also been munching the new leaves which the trees need. The adult sawflies I've been seeing in the oaks are mostly these small ones. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

I've been thinking the oaks were flowering kind of half-heartedly this year, and maybe this is why. I don't think I've ever seen such an infestation before. There's no mention of this in "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" which is available here. I don't like it.

File.Name: f322c_f322-008.jpg
Notes:
16280567053 I believe this is a caterpillar-like Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae munching a catkin of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that's closest to our upstairs deck. This larva has simple eyes and more than five prolegs. I don't mind the catkins, but these critters have also been munching the new leaves which the trees need. The adult sawflies I've been seeing in the oaks are mostly these small ones. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

But I'm confused. This one doesn't have the little bifurcated horns on its back that the larvae in my [Next] and [Previous] photos have. QuÈ pasa?

I've been thinking the oaks were flowering kind of half-heartedly this year, and maybe this is why. I don't think I've ever seen such an infestation before. There's no mention of this in "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" which is available here. I don't like it.

File.Name: f322d_f322-003.jpg
Notes:
16874169786 I believe these are caterpillar-like Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae doing some real damage to the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that's closest to our upstairs deck. There's the large one to the right and also a smaller one to the left. The larvae seem to have simple eyes and more than five prolegs. I don't know if these are the same kind. Maybe they are genus Periclista, Tenthredinidae? The adult sawflies I've been seeing in the oaks are mostly these small ones. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

I've been thinking the oaks were flowering kind of half-heartedly this year, and maybe this is why. I don't think I've ever seen such an infestation before. There's no mention of this in "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" which is available here. I don't like it.

File.Name: f322e_f322-005.jpg
Notes: 16899941325 This is the damage the caterpillar-like Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) larvae are doing to the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that's closest to our upstairs deck. You can see one of the little devils on the top-most leaf, close to the top of my photo. I don't mind that they eat the catkins, but eating the new leaves will cause some problems. See my [Next] few photos. I don't think I've ever seen this before, at least not to this extent. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

File.Name: f322f_f322-022.jpg
Notes: 16713712319 This is a tiny caterpillar (Lepidoptera) munching a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's only about 1/4 inch long. I don't know which moth or butterfly this caterpillar will become. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

File.Name: f323a_f323-014.jpg
Notes:
16724254990 My butterfly photo today is a humble Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) perched for a moment on a grass stem near Lake Cachuma. This is the dark "spring form" - they look brighter in their "summer form", see this photo. These butterflies look moth-like, and are weak fliers. But they are real butterflies as their clubbed antennae prove. I've been seeing these for a while, but this is my first photo of the year. They are weak fliers, but hard to get close to! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2015)

Today started breezy and ended up with howling winds. This is the windiest day I can recall all this year. I took the wind as an opportunity to go to Solvang and made my usual stop by Lake Cachuma. I got some nice photos despite the wind! *Sigh* It's expected to be windy through Wednesday.

File.Name: f323b_f323-008.jpg
Notes:
16724067490 You might have to look at this photo for a moment for your eyes to get it!

Here are two large Crane Flies in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera "doing it" on a twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today. What an interesting "universal joint" they have in common! Their legs do on forever - look at that long leg of the fly on the right extending across my photo. They may seem ungainly when they are trapped in your house, but they are quite at home hanging from the leaves. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2015)

File.Name: f323c_f322-025.jpg
Notes: 16723674158 Here are two flies (order Diptera) doing what all flies do - exchanging genetic material to insure that there will be lots of little flies in the future. I believe they are Cluster Flies in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae. They are one those flies you probably don't want to see walking across your potato salad. It's a graphic shot - maybe too graphic! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

File.Name: f323d_f322-016.jpg
Notes: 16723710700 Here are two shots of what I believe is an interesting Stiletto Fly, probably genus Thereva, in the family Therevidae of the order Diptera. It's perched for just a moment on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I don't know anything about them, but I seem to find a few every year. This is the first one I've found this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)
ScientificName: Thereva; Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Thereva; Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f323e_f322-011.jpg
Notes: 16910056881 This is a tiny Weevil beetle (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on an old leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It's only about 3/16 inch long. That "thing" below the weevil is just an old bract from a flower that I couldn't remove without spooking the beetle. I don't know anything else about these critters. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2015)

File.Name: f323f_f323-015.jpg
Notes:
16909779761 Here are two different kinds of Owl's Clover (genus Castilleja) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly in the Scrophulariaceae. I believe they get their name by growing is the open areas that Burrowing Owls favor.

The one on the left is the Narrow-leaved Owl Clover aka Valley Tassels (Castilleja attenuata). It's a skinny little plant that's easier to step on than to notice! I found this one in a grassy place near Lake Cachuma today. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2015)

The one on the right is more noticeable - Purple Owl's Clover (Castilleja exserta). The fuzzy backs of the pistils look like lavender pipe cleaners! I got this photo a couple of days ago along the Upper Oso road in Paradise. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2015)
CommonNames: Owl's Clover; Burrowing Owls; Narrow-leaved Owl Clover; Valley Tassels; Purple Owl's Clover
ScientificName: Castilleja; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Castilleja attenuata; Castilleja exserta
associatedTaxa: Castilleja; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Castilleja attenuata; Castilleja exserta

File.Name: f324a_f324-025.jpg
Notes:
16299695924 This is a pollination photo. It shows (I believe) a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) snuggled into a new flower of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. Look how perfectly the beetle fits the flower! (SanMarcos Pass, 24 March 2015)

The flowers do a neat little dance as they mature. They start with stamens low and pistils high like this flower, but in time they switch to pistils low and stamens high like the old flower in the lower-right. The hell of it is that the beetle is apparently introduced, so it can't be the original pollinator of these flowers. It must have been something very similar.

My usual rule is butterflies first, but what's a rule for if not to break? I like this photo better!

*Sigh* It was very windy again today - power lines singing and the air full of oak catkins and leaves! I guess I can't complain - there's been very little wind all this calendar year, and tomorrow should be the end of it. I got my photos today by taking *many* photos of each subject and picking the keeper. You couldn't afford to do that with film!
CommonNames: Seven-spotted Lady Beetle; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccinella septempunctata; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: f324b_f324-023.jpg
Notes: 16714569907 I believe this is a small male Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly showing its upper wings on a leaf of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. I never got to see the underside of its wings to make sure, but compare this photo and the one [Next] to it. And then compare this photo of the similar early-flying Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly. I believe the Spring Azures have that wider dark border on their wings. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2015)
CommonNames: Spring Azure; Snowdrop Bush; Silvery Blue
ScientificName: Celastrina ladon; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Glaucopsyche lygdamus
associatedTaxa: Celastrina ladon; Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Glaucopsyche lygdamus

File.Name: f324c_f324-006.jpg
Notes: 16920806411 This is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly, the first one I've seen in the woods since last October. I know this isn't the best photo, but it's a record shot! These pretty butterflies have just two dark eyespots in the submargin of its hindwing, and they usually have that white dot in the orange rectangle near the margin of the forewing. This one is perched on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden, which is just starting to get some morning sun as we pass the equinox. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2015)

File.Name: f324d_f324-015.jpg
Notes: 16301663463 This is a stunning little "royal blue" Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) perched on a fallen oak leaf in the canyon. Wow, that color! It nice to see them. There's not a drop of water in the creek that I know of, so I wonder where it came from. I saw several of these today, for the first time this year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2015)

File.Name: f324e_f324-011.jpg
Notes: 16921541505 This is a large Darkling Ground Beetle (family Tenebrionidae, order Coleoptera) in the woods, trying to get away from me. It's probably a "Stink Beetle" in the genus Eleodes, but I prefer the old name "Pinacate Beetle". I don't get many photos of these large beetles because they move so darn fast, but this one got stuck for a moment by an obstacle in its path. Kids sometimes torment them. Don't let them! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2015)
CommonNames: Darkling Ground Beetle; "Stink Beetle"; Pinacate Beetle

File.Name: f324f_f324-004.jpg
Notes: 16714146627 This is a nice stand of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family, on the hillside behind our home. They seem to be having a good year which puts the lie to my thought that the early wildflowers did well but later ones won't do so well. That thought is still in the back of my mind, but I'm happy to see these sages doing so well. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2015)

File.Name: f325a_f325-015.jpg
Notes:
16744698630 This is a small diurnal (daytime) Moth (Lepidoptera) perched by new flowers of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) in the woods today. I believe the moth is genus Annaphila - maybe Annaphila diva (Hodges#9869) in the Noctuidae family, see these pictures at BugGuide. This isn't the best photo, but it's an interesting moth. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the same moth. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2015)

It's interesting that my "big book of moths" - "Moths of Western North America" (Jerry A. Powell and Paul A. Opler, UCPress, 2009) - mentions Annaphila arvalis as the moth that uses Claytonia perfoliata as a host plant. But that one has orange underwings, not white like this one. And see this photo of an Annaphila diva on the same plant.

I was a little distracted today as an old friend and former middle school student dropped by to get some info about bark beetle damage to the Coulter Pines just up the hill from us. He's working up a proposal to study the situation - which calls out for study.

I was also distracted by the wind today - strong this morning, but it finally died down in the afternoon. I hope that's the end of it. I have hopes for tomorrow as a photo day.
Locality: Jerry A. Powell and Paul A. Opler
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Annaphila; Annaphila diva; Annaphila arvalis; Claytonia perfoliata; Annaphila diva
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Annaphila; Annaphila diva; Annaphila arvalis; Claytonia perfoliata; Annaphila diva

File.Name: f325b_f325-021.jpg
Notes: 16744514000 This is a small diurnal (daytime) Moth (Lepidoptera) working new flowers of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata, Montiaceae) in the woods today. I believe the moth is genus Annaphila - maybe Annaphila diva (Hodges#9869) in the Noctuidae family, see these pictures at BugGuide. This isn't the best photo, but it's an interesting moth. See my [Next] photo for another shot. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2015)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Annaphila; Annaphila diva
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Montiaceae; Annaphila; Annaphila diva

File.Name: f325c_f325-006.jpg
Notes: 16905871846 I believe this is another small Araneus bispinosus spider in the family Araneidae, compare this photo. This one is snuggled into the fold of an old leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). The spider can't be much more than 1/4 inch long, though it looks like one of its larger cousins. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2015)

File.Name: f325d_f325-011.jpg
Notes: 16931723405 This is a tiny green aphid (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) perched on the banner of a new flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today, with the sun behind. It looks like stained glass. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2015)
ScientificName: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f325e_f325-008.jpg
Notes: 16930324222 This is a little "Click Beetle" (family Elateridae in the order Coleoptera) on a new frond of native Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. They have their thorax drawn out to points that can *click* against the wing covers - though I'm not sure if these small ones do that. The big ones do, dramatically. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2015)
ScientificName: Elateridae; Coleoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Elateridae; Coleoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: f325f_f325-013.jpg
Notes: 16905613076 This is native Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family, with flowers as open as they ever are. I usually find it growing with weedy Chickweed and Miner's Lettuce, but it is a native. The Apiaceae were once known as the Umbelliferae - because the flowers usually come in umbrella-like "umbels" with lots of flowers, but this one has an umbel of one. I think of it as a limit thing - like a circle is the limit of an ellipse, so this flower is the limit of an umbel. It has attractive foliage. I took me a few years to figure out what this is. It would be a good final exam stumper plant for a plant taxonomy class! (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2015)

File.Name: f326a_f326-032.jpg
Notes:
16755436639 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched for just a moment on a twig along West Camino Cielo road today. It's also a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2015)

There's a story here. A car drove by too fast as they usually so, and I found this butterfly in its path on the road. I picked it up by the wings and moved it away from danger. It flew away to this branch for a moment, and then shook it off and flew away. I've wondered what happens to the butterflies that get too close to my car while driving. I always wince. Maybe some of them are OK.

The big winds seem to have ended, but it was still breezy today. It was also hot - in the 80s at home, and up to 92∞ at the river. This warm weather is baking any remaining moisture from the ground.

File.Name: f326b_f326-012.jpg
Notes: 16941504185 I watched this yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) slide down a spider web zip-line to this sharp leaf of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae aka Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) today. A moment later it was gone. Look how it's holding the web so delicately, ready for escape. This is also a pretty good butt shot - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2015)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: f326c_f326-002.jpg
Notes: 16734064837 This is a small Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) resting for a moment on an old leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). A moment before, it was working a flower of the same plant. It looks like the illustration of Colletes hyalinus in my book on "The Common Bees of California" (UCPress, 2013), but the photos at BugGuide don't agree. It's a furry little creature! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2015)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Colletes hyalinus
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Colletes hyalinus

File.Name: f326d_f326-024.jpg
Notes:
16754961299 Here are two shots of the real native California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills. This is a true native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. The seed-pods will get longer and bend down to give the stems a shaggy look, see this photo. It's not a common plant in our mountains, even after wildfires. It's hard not to over-expose these white flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2015)

Hah - in the past few years, this plant has also been known as Thelypodium lasiophyllum and Guillenia lasiophylla. Those darned botanists! This is why I favor the old names.
ScientificName: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla
associatedTaxa: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla

File.Name: f326e_f326-026.jpg
Notes: 16754828519 I believe these are new flowers of the Hairy Suncup (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family - now known as Camissoniopsis hirtella . It's another fire-follower that is common after wildfires, but this one is growing in a place that was cleared and not burned. Sometimes that's enough. The stems keep producing new flowers from the front as they continue to grow. The old flowers turn reddish, then drop away to become seed pods. It's a neat system that allows the stem to grow longer and longer as long as it can. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2015)

File.Name: f326f_f326-009.jpg
Notes: 16318513444 This is a small and hairy Lupine (genus Lupinus) in the Fabaceae plant family that's growing in our canyon, with flowers. It looks a bit like Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus), but it's not quite hairy enough, and it's not growing in the desert. Maybe this one is the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus)? If you have an idea about this, please let me know. So many lupines! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2015)
ScientificName: Lupinus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus; Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus
associatedTaxa: Lupinus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus; Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus

File.Name: f327a_f327-004.jpg
Notes:
16330759703 This is a stunning California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - on a path in our garden this morning. As far as I know, these butterflies never feed, but they do come to take moisture from damp soil. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty. They are our most common butterflies at home. There were eight of them at a time here this morning! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2015)

It was darn right HOT today, up to 93∞ at the river where I was today. These first really hot days of the year are always a struggle.

File.Name: f327b_f327-011.jpg
Notes: 16924736876 This butterfly is a veteran and deserves some respect for what it's been through! I'm sure it's a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta), but it's so tattered that it's hardly recognizable. I found this specimen in the garden today, taking moisture from the damp soil that Julie watered yesterday. Compare this photo of the same kind of butterfly in better circumstances. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2015)

File.Name: f327c_f327-014.jpg
Notes: 16328227504 This is another large caterpillar of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata), though this one is on stems of non-native annual grass. I photographed another one today munching leaves of a Filaree (Erodium, Geraniaceae). I guess they are adaptable. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 March 2015)

File.Name: f327d_f327-031.jpg
Notes:
16924476006 These are new flowers of the native San Luis Blazing Star aka Small-flowered Stickleaf (Mentzelia micrantha) in the Loasaceae plant family. The leaves are instantly recognizable because they feel rough like sandpaper. They usually grow as fire-followers, but this one is growing in the path a flood that washed out a place near the river from our good rains last December. (*Sigh*) These were common on the burn of the White Fire last year - one year after the burn - but they disappeared from that place this following year. This is a different place and a different disturbance. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 March 2015)

I think that's the most interesting thing about these fire-follower plants - not that the appear after a fire, but that they disappear into the seed bank a year or two later.
CommonNames: San Luis Blazing Star; Small-flowered Stickleaf

File.Name: f327e_f327-039.jpg
Notes:
16762582118 This is a wonderful new flower of the Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Onagraceae plant family. The petals look like they've been cut with scissors, but that's how they are. It's a Farewell-to-Spring - a sign that our dry summer is approaching, too fast! This one is the first I've seen this year, and I'm sorry to see them already. Spring just started a week ago! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 March 2015)

Last year I found the first flowers of April 29. *Sigh* Everything is a month or more early this year. I wonder what will be flowering this summer?

File.Name: f327f_f323-013.jpg
Notes: 16762745130 Here are a pair of large Crane Flies in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera "doing it" on a twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I guess that's the skinnier male on the bottom and the larger (with eggs) female on top. They may seem ungainly when they are trapped in your house, but they are quite at home in the woods hanging from the leaves. HFDF! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2015)

File.Name: f328a_f328-020.jpg
Notes:
16341272293 This is a small ant-tended caterpillar on an old flower head of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The ant is tending the caterpillar, not attacking it. Maybe the ant is our common California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale, Dolichoderinae, Formicidae, Hymenoptera). I guess it's getting honeydew from the caterpillar. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the caterpillar. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2015)

But what kind of caterpillar is it? It's a pretty good match for the caterpillar of a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly, which according to my book ("Caterpillars in the Field and Garden", OUP, 2005) occurs on Ceanothus, but there's no mention of ants. It might also be the caterpillar of a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly which are said to favor Ceanothus and to be ant-tended. I guess time will tell!

No proper butterfly photo today, but these caterpillars will soon become butterflies! It was a few degrees cooler today, but there was a steady breeze that made photos difficult. I took many photos to get each of these keepers.
Locality:
Satyrium californica) butterfly which are said to favor Ceanothus and to be ant-tended. I guess time will tell!

No proper butterfly photo today

ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Callophrys augustinus; Satyrium californica; many
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Callophrys augustinus; Satyrium californica; many

File.Name: f328b_f328-018.jpg
Notes:
16773431678 This is a small slug-like caterpillar crawling down a stem of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (It's head is on the right.) The caterpillars are ant-tended, see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2015)

But what kind of caterpillar is it? It's a pretty good match for the caterpillar of a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly, which according to my book ("Caterpillars in the Field and Garden", OUP, 2005) occurs on Ceanothus, but there's no mention of ants. It might also be the caterpillar of a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly which are said to favor Ceanothus and to be ant-tended. I guess time will tell!
Locality: Caterpillars in the Field and Garden
CommonNames: caterpillar; Greenbark Ceanothus; Brown Elfin; California Hairstreak
