File.Name: f328c_f328-005.jpg
Notes: 16959576982 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera), but I can't rule out some kind of Polistes. This one is resting on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden this morning. The wasps are common, but they don't like to pose. This one is either (choose one): (a)cute or (b)scary! These wasps build their nests under the eaves of our porch and roof. Yeah, they look threatening, but I've never been stung. Their petioled abdomen is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2015)

File.Name: f328d_f327-005.jpg
Notes: 16959419572 This is a bee-like Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably in the genus Abia. One indication is that it has no hairs on it's legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. I like the way its tinted wings change the color of the sandy soil. This one is taking moisture from a wet path in the garden. Butterflies aren't the only critters that like to "puddle"! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2015)

File.Name: f328e_f328-027.jpg
Notes: 16774472839 Here are the first new flowers of Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the Fabaceae plant family that I've seen this year. The pretty flowers look fertile, but the shrubs mostly spread by runners and rarely if ever make seeds. The stems grow into gnarly spines, and it's painful to bushwack through a patch of them. Last year I found the first flowers on March 23, so I guess they are right on time. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2015)

File.Name: f328f_f328-011.jpg
Notes:
16960478245 This appears to be a rust fungus (Pucciniales or Uredinales, Basidiomycotina) on a native plant of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The fungus seems to cause a gall-like swelling of the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2015)

I hope it's not fatal. This is one of my favorite summer wildflowers, and the only plant in the mountains where I find our uncommon Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterflies - see this photo from last summer.
CommonNames: rust fungus; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Callippe Fritillary
ScientificName: Pucciniales; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Speyeria callippe
associatedTaxa: Pucciniales; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Speyeria callippe

File.Name: f329a_f329-023.jpg
Notes:
16351306554 This is a stunning Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly perched for just a moment on stems of barely alive native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's the first one I've managed to get a photo of this year. These butterflies are distinctive because the black on its forewings runs along the wing rather than across it, compare my [Previous] photo. I saw many of these butterflies "hilltopping" on the ridge above Paradise Creek today. I wonder if it's a mating thing they do, before going down the hill to lay their eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)

Today was a notch cooler than yesterday, and a notch breezier too. It was still warm enough to bring up a sweat! I bumped up the ISO to 400 for a couple of my photos today because of the breeze. I can't see much difference.
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: f329b_f329-009.jpg
Notes: 16766103397 This is a stunning not-so-pale Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly perched for just a moment on a stem of Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. These swallowtails get a little yellower as the season progresses, but you can still tell them from the much yellower Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterflies. At least I think that's right! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Mountain Mahogany; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Cercocarpus betuloides; Rosaceae; Papilio rutulus
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Cercocarpus betuloides; Rosaceae; Papilio rutulus

File.Name: f329c_f329-013.jpg
Notes:
16785582688 Here are two shots of the same mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. This one was perched on a stem of non-native Wild Oats (Avena fatua, Poaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)

Hah, the photo on the right (which I took first) was a bit too dark, so I cranked up the exposure a bit - with the result that the photo on the left is a bit too light. Like Goldilocks, sometimes it's hard to find the setting that is just right!
Locality: which I took first) was a bit too dark
CommonNames: California Bee Assassin; Assassin Bug; Wild Oats
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Avena fatua; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Avena fatua; Poaceae

File.Name: f329d_f329-006.jpg
Notes: 16765759027 This is looking close at a large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera hanging on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden this morning. This one has remarkable iridescent eyes. They always remind me of wise old philosophers contemplating the infinite, maybe as drawn by Moebius or Jean-Claude MÈziËres. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)

File.Name: f329e_f329-016.jpg
Notes:
16785422640 This is a new flower of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I usually call it the "white thistle" to distinguish it from the "violet thistle" that grows in our canyon at home. They are the same taxonomically, but they look quite different. And then there's the Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum)! All are the same species, but they all look different. See this photo for a side-by-side comparison. This white thistle is the first I've seen in flower this year. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. The non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt, leave it be!
Locality: Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt
CommonNames: California Thistle; Red Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Bull Thistle
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Cirsium vulgare
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Cirsium vulgare

File.Name: f329f_f329-034.jpg
Notes: 16971468212 This is a remarkable swollen stem gall on a plant of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. There's a normal stem on the right for comparison. I have no idea what caused this galled stem, whether insect of fungus. I believe this is the first gall I've ever found on a monocot. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)

File.Name: f330a_f330-020.jpg
Notes:
16364249003 This is a mating pair of Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterflies having their nuptials in the oak leaves about 20 feet away from the Willows (Salix, Salicaceae) along our creek. These butterflies look a lot like the more-common California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border. Compare this photo. I guess that's the larger female on the left and the smaller male on the right. Photos of insects mating are the one time you can clearly see the difference between male and female. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

Today was a good day for butterflies! It was warm but not too hot, breezy but not windy. Just right.
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii

File.Name: f330b_f330-007.jpg
Notes:
16361836154 This is a beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil on a garden path this morning. Its wings are like an open fan. I've seen a few of these on the wing, but this is the first one I've managed to get a photo of this year. This one must be newly emerged to have such bright colors. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

These butterflies seem to face the camera with wings open, unlike the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly in my [Previous] photo which tends to face away from my camera with wings folded. Different strokes for different butterflies!

File.Name: f330c_f330-015.jpg
Notes:
16982717662 This is a small Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil on a garden path this morning. You can see a bit of blue hidden between its wings. The most distinguishing characteristic of these butterflies seems to be that they have no strong markings below, just these dark chevrons. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

These butterflies tend to face away from my camera with wings folded, unlike the Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly in my [Next] photo which tends to face the camera with wings open. Different strokes for different butterflies!

File.Name: f330d_f330-003.jpg
Notes: 16363909523 This is a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil on a garden path this morning. The brown colors and white fringe on its hindwings are distinctive. I've been seeing quite a few of these lately, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph. In a drought year, wet soil attracts more butterflies than nectar! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

File.Name: f330e_f330-021.jpg
Notes: 16957907676 This is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly in the canyon at home. Its wings are quite plain on the outside, but this one shows some color coming through from the top-side. These butterflies in our canyon all seem small, and they spend their time chasing away any other butterflies that come by. I guess they are all males, like little roosters. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

File.Name: f330f_f329-032.jpg
Notes: 16797589699 This is a lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly nectaring on native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. I reckon this is a new brood. I've been seeing quite a few of them in the woods lately, mostly flying here and there in the air. I got this photo yesterday. It's a bit of a tight crop as the butterflies seem quite camera shy. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)
ScientificName: Anthocharis sara; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthocharis sara; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae

File.Name: f331a_f331-011.jpg
Notes:
16807784780 Did a bear vomit in the woods?
April Fools' Day!

Hah , I believe this is the "Dog Vomit Slime Mold" (Fuligo septica, Myxomycetes) - really! If you don't believe me, you can find it at Wikipedia. See my [Previous] photo for a closer look. This odd creature looks moist, but it feels dry, like a fibrous peanut brittle. I don't know what it's doing in the woods now when it feels so dry, but here it is. It's large, at least a foot across. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2015)

It was cooler today, and quite windy. It sounds like we're in for a real wind event over the next couple of days as a "dry storm" passes over. I'll do what I can for new photos, but the recent archives are full of photos I've been waiting to post.

File.Name: f331b_f331-010.jpg
Notes: 16807214678 mushroom Agaricus "Amber-Staining Agaricus" "Agaricus albolutescens" "Agaricus moronii" Agaricaceae Agaricales Agaricomycetes Basidiomycota Fungi oak mulch "Coast Live Oak" "Quercus agrifolia" Fagaceae "San Marcos Pass" "Santa Barbara County" California (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2015)

File.Name: f331c_f331-005.jpg
Notes:
16968872776 I found this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the mint patch this morning. I dig those eyebrows. They have fantastic eyes. It's remarkable to make eye contact with any critter so small! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2015)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, so I can use my camera as an expensive remote micrometer. The critter's head is about 400 pixels across in my original photo. That makes it about 1.6 mm across, about 1/16 inch. See this photo for how I figured.

File.Name: f331d_f330-002.jpg
Notes:
16787280117 This is a large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on the wrong end of a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the garden. I believe this one is a male, with those blue eyes and brown mane. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

The female bees nest in the fascia boards of our upstairs deck, so I spend some time studying them every day when I have a beer after my outing. Lately there's been one of these males hovering in one corner of the porch - for hours! I guess it's just waiting for a chance to grab a female.

File.Name: f331e_f329-008.jpg
Notes: 16807026240 This is a remarkable Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae, Hymenoptera) - "Ichneumonid" at least! This one is perched of a new leaf in the mint patch of our garden. It has a remarkably long ovipositor. It was here for just a moment, then it flew away. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015)

File.Name: f331f_f330-019.jpg
Notes:
16806879310 Here is another photo of the mating pair of Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterflies - just because I have it and they are so pretty! See my [Previous] photo. This one shows the pretty undersides of their wings a little better. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

They are having their nuptials in the oak leaves about 20 feet away from the Willows (Salix, Salicaceae) along our creek. These butterflies look a lot like the more-common California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border. Compare this photo.
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Salix; Salicaceae; Adelpha bredowii

File.Name: f401a_f401-032.jpg
Notes:
16382983824 This is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly perched for just a moment on the flower head of a non-native weedy composite along West Camino Cielo today. The plant is probably Smooth Cat's Ear (Hypochaeris glabra, Asteraceae), but the butterfly was really more interested in the Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) that was growing nearby. This isn't the best photo, but the butterfly sure has pretty colors. Is it male (more blue) or female (more brown)? (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

I was expecting big winds today, but it was actually fairly calm this morning - with just enough breeze to mess up this and my [Previous] photos. The winds did pick up in the afternoon, blowing like a proper sundowner. More winds are expected tomorrow. We'll see what develops.
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Smooth Cat's Ear; Deerweed
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Hypochaeris glabra; Asteraceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Hypochaeris glabra; Asteraceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: f401b_f401-029.jpg
Notes: 16382906574 I'm pretty sure this is a little Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly perched for a moment on a leaf of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii, Garryaceae). I'm confused by the blue colors on its forewing, but there it is. This isn't the best photo, as the wind was just starting to kick up. But it's a record shot for me, the first one I've been able to get any photo of this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

File.Name: f401c_f401-021.jpg
Notes: 17003841562 This is a beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

File.Name: f401d_f401-020.jpg
Notes: 17003646672 This is a little Snakefly (Agulla, Raphidiidae, Neuroptera or Raphidioptera) on a flower head of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. That little beetle below it looks like a little Carpet Beetle (Dermestidae, Coleoptera), maybe genus Anthrenus? (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)
CommonNames: Snakefly; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; Carpet Beetle
ScientificName: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Raphidioptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Anthrenus
associatedTaxa: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Raphidioptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Anthrenus

File.Name: f401e_f401-022.jpg
Notes:
17004972535 Here are two pollination photos of different kinds of Lady Beetles (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on new flowers of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. In the photo on the left, the beetle is picking up pollen from a stamen of the flower. In the photo on the right, the pistil of the flower is touching the beetle's back to receive the pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

The flowers do a little dance as they mature. They start with stamens low and pistils high like the flower on the left, but in time they switch to pistils low and stamens high like the flower in the right. The flowers remind me of an old-fashioned carousel with horses that go up and down!
CommonNames: Lady Beetles; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: f401f_f401-010.jpg
Notes:
16797348467 This is a Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) approaching the heart of a flower of Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) in the Convulvulaceae plant family. It's also a pretty good "butt shot" - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

This plant grows on the south-facing slopes of our mountains. It has bracts that conceal the flower calyx. The Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) is different, with bracts well below the flower. That one grows along the river and in the the backcountry. Always check the bracts with these plants.

This nymph looks subtly different from our usual California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) nymphs. Maybe it's the nymph of a Chaparral Katydid (Platylyra californica)?
CommonNames: Katydid; Coastal Morning-Glory; California Angle-winged Katydid; Chaparral Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Convulvulaceae; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Convulvulaceae; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Microcentrum californicum; Platylyra californica

File.Name: f402a_f402-007.jpg
Notes:
16828911759 This is a caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera) munching a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It has clown colors! In case you're wondering, its head is on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2015)

I always try to post today's photos today, at least one or two, but that becomes a struggle when it's as windy as it's been. After this photo, I went to town and did some grocery shopping. That's about all you can do in the wind. The wind has finally died down this evening. Tomorrow might be a nice photo day!
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f402b_f402-009.jpg
Notes: 16392518554 This is an adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. It might be a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). Not the best photo, as it was breezy this morning and I stubbornly stayed with ISO 280. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2015)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: f402c_f401-006.jpg
Notes: 16827368180 Here are new flowers of native Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It usually grows as a fire-follower, but this plant is growing on the side of West Camino Cielo road. I guess anything that lets the light in is good enough for these plants. The flowers are large and quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

File.Name: f402d_f401-002.jpg
Notes:
17013352732 Here are the first fully open flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family that I've found this year. You can see the distinctive umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaves that the flowers are growing from. I was shooting up to capture these flowers that are growing from vines that cover a Scrub Oak near our house. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

Last year I found the first flowers on May 3. Plants are flowering early this year, and summer is coming early. I don't expect these flowers to put on their usual show in this fourth drought year in a row. The early wildflowers did great thanks to our December rains, but the woods feel very dry now and the plants are hurting.

File.Name: f402e_f401-017.jpg
Notes: 16392206694 This is a large (not giant) Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera trying to be invisible in a shrub of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae) that is just starting to flower. They may seem ungainly when they are trapped in your house, but they are quite at home in the woods hanging from the leaves. I have a closer face shot in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: f402f_f330-010.jpg
Notes: 16988557496 This isn't the best photo, but it's an interesting fly! My first thought was some kind of Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera). My second thought is that I don't know what it is! It's small, a bit more than 1/4 inch long. I found it on the ground on the edge of the mint patch of our garden. It has very stout antennae which should be a clue about what it is. If you recognize this one, please leave a comment. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2015)

File.Name: f403a_f403-011.jpg
Notes:
16405422353 This is a closer shot of new flowers of the uncommon Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. If you look at my [Previous] photo, this is the plant on the right. Maybe I'll post a photo of the plant on the left tomorrow. These plants are growing in a place where the sun never shines. I did the best I could with low light and breeze today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2015)

This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

It wasn't full-on windy today, but it was still breezy all morning. I took 70 photos of these plants and kept just four. You couldn't afford to do that with film! It was that kind of day.

File.Name: f403b_f403-010.jpg
Notes:
16405250103 Here are two photos of the uncommon Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. The plants have whorls of very skinny leaves, like a lily on a diet. There are only a few plants with flowers in this drought year, but they are hanging on. These plants are growing in a place where the sun never shines. I did the best I could with low light and breeze today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2015)

This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

In the photo on the left, you can make out one of the large basal leaves which come before the flower stalks - look just left of the top-center. The large leaves remind me of native orchids, see this photo. Other native plants in these photos include Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens, Poaceae) and Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hiericium argutum, Asteraceae).
CommonNames: Ojai Fritillary; Thin Grass; Yellow-flowered Hawkweed
ScientificName: Fritillaria ojaiensis; Liliaceae; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae; Hiericium argutum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Fritillaria ojaiensis; Liliaceae; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae; Hiericium argutum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f403c_f403-027.jpg
Notes: 17023864142 This is a new flower of the Wide-throated Yellow Monkeyflower (Mimulus brevipes) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. It's quite uncommon in our mountains, and I only know it from this one place. They are mostly growing high up a steep shaly bank, but a few flowers were down low where I could get to them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 April 2015)

File.Name: f403d_f403-023.jpg
Notes:
16838878699 This is a pretty Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly working brand new flowers of native composite California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. These are the first flowers of this usually-summer plant that I've seen this year, so it also counts as a "first flower". I expect to find these flowers in June, but here they are in April. Last year, I found the first flowers on May 8 - and that seemed too early. This butterfly seemed happy to find them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 April 2015)

This butterfly looks plain brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year.

Usually I post butterflies first, but I make an exception for unusual plants!
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Solidago californica; Asteraceae; pointillist; Theclinea
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Solidago californica; Asteraceae; pointillist; Theclinea

File.Name: f403e_f403-008.jpg
Notes: 16402565764 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the Fabaceae plant family. This one is for Jeffrey, who collects this information. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2015)

File.Name: f403f_f403-004.jpg
Notes: 17023388742 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. This one is for Jeffrey, who collects this information. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2015)

File.Name: f404a_f404-005.jpg
Notes:
16413786104 This is another Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly perched on a stem in the mint patch of our garden this morning. This butterfly looks plain brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)

There was an annoying breeze all day today - no full-on winds, but enough breeze to keep everything in motion. We have a real chance of rain next week on Tuesday! That will be a nice boost for our summer flowers which are hurting in this dry year. We'll see what happens!

File.Name: f404b_f404-009.jpg
Notes: 16849789769 This is another shot of an adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today. It might be a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). I'll bet this is a gravid female, full of eggs. Compare this photo of probably the same bug. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: f404c_f404-003.jpg
Notes: 17035757785 At first I thought this was a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, but Alice Abelia corrected me. Now I believe it's a Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) of the order Diptera. I found it perched for a moment in the mint patch of our garden this morning. Its head is in the shade and its butt in the sun, which makes for a tricky exposure. If you tilt your head sideways to the left, you can make out a face on its butt with eyes, nose, mouth, and chin whiskers! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)

File.Name: f404d_f404-015.jpg
Notes:
16828099867 I was focused on these new bisexual galls of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera today, when this interesting little ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) wandered into my photo. I believe the ant is genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. I don't know what the ant was doing on the galls, maybe just exploring.

The galls are interesting too. These appear to be two conjoined mushroom-like galls on a leaf of of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They are the spring generation of the wasps that will make the much larger "Oak Apple Galls" on the oaks next summer, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; ant; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f404e_f404-019.jpg
Notes: 17033914492 This is an uncommonly pretty little "looper caterpillar" - probably a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae) in the Lepidoptera. This one is making its loop on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)

File.Name: f404f_f404-022.jpg
Notes: 16415128283 This is an unusual fasciated stem of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The stem is flat like a ribbon. You can see a normal stem in the background. Many plants get fasciated, some often, but I rarely find fasciated shrubs . There's some info at Wikipedia. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)

File.Name: f405a_f405-029.jpg
Notes:
17022848396 Here are two photos of uncommon flowers of the native Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns or clearings. I found this one in a place that was cleared but didn't burn, and sometimes that's enough to trigger these plants. It really looks like a little Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). I wasn't really happy with either photo in the breeze today, but putting them together works. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2015)

It was cooler today - cool enough for (thin) long pants again. There was wind from the south this morning and some clouds building over the mountains - two of my most reliable rain signs. Then the wind shifted from the north and blew the clouds away. I hope it doesn't blow our approaching storm away! The NWS rain forecast is now 90% for rain on Tuesday. We need it.

File.Name: f405b_f405-026.jpg
Notes:
17048667705 This plant is the real native California Mustard (Caulanthus lasiophyllus) in the Brassicaceae plant family. See this photo for a closer look at the flowers. It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills. This is a true native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. This one is growing in a place that was cleared but not burned. The seed-pods get long and bend down to give the stems a shaggy look. It's not a common plant in our mountains, even after wildfires. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2015)

Hah - in the past few years, this plant has also been known as Thelypodium lasiophyllum and Guillenia lasiophylla. Those darned botanists!
ScientificName: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla
associatedTaxa: Caulanthus lasiophyllus; Brassicaceae; Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla

File.Name: f405c_f405-025.jpg
Notes: 16860935700 These are new flowers of native California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road. The flowers look kind of weedy at first, like non-native Sow Thistle in the genus Sonchus. But if you look closer, you see how elegant they are. They are a fire-follower that lingers in disturbed places. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2015)

File.Name: f405d_f405-017.jpg
Notes:
16840903567 These are new flowers of California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae), with a galled flower in the top-right. I've found these galls before, see this photo. I've never been sure if they are flower or fruit galls, but I think this photo settles it that they are flower galls. That odd "crook in the neck" is typical. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2015)

Ron Russo doesn't describe this gall in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." I guess this is the one, and it's a flower NOT a berry. (Well, it may be the ovary of a flower.)

File.Name: f405e_f405-012.jpg
Notes: 17046682312 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. Last year I found the first flowers on April 28, so these are early - but at least it's the same month! They are a local hummingbird favorite. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2015)

File.Name: f405f_f405-018.jpg
Notes: 16425614094 These are the first new fruit I've noticed this year of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. Other shrubs are still flowering. The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. The ripe red fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching them. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2015)

File.Name: f406a_f406-028.jpg
Notes:
17060957681 This is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly showing its upper wings for just a moment on twigs of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I got this photo a moment after the one on the right in my [Previous] photo with wings closed. I had the exposure turned up for those dark wings, and I was startled when it suddenly opened it's wings. It was another "shoot fast, think later" photo! It's not quite sharp, but it shows the butterfly. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 6 April 2015)

I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I made my usual stop near Lake Cachuma and got a few photos. It was a crummy day for photos, but I got a few. Our new storm seems to be moving in. It was mostly clear today, but breezy and cold. It was still in the high 30s when I got up this morning. There was a ring around the sun this afternoon. We now have a 90% of rain tomorrow. We really need it.

File.Name: f406b_f406-030.jpg
Notes: 17061602275 Here are two shots of a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly showing its upper and lower wings. The butterfly almost disappears with wings closed! I was surprised to find this one today on twigs of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I occasionally find them at home in mid-winter - see this photo. I don't often find them any other time of the year. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 6 April 2015)

File.Name: f406c_f406-020.jpg
Notes:
17059960362 *Sigh* It's been that kind of year...

These are drought-shriveled new flowers of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I've never seen this before. The woods near Lake Cachuma missed a few of the little rains that have helped our plants on San Marcos Pass, and the woods look in bad shape. I haven't seen any flowers at home either. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 6 April 2015)

Damn, it will be one hell of a summer for wildfires...

File.Name: f406d_f406-017.jpg
Notes:
17060453411 Farewell to Spring...

Here are two different Farewell-to-Spring flowers in the Onagraceaeplant family that I've found in the woods lately. On the left is a Winecup Clarkia (Clarkia purpurea ssp. quadrivulnera) that I found a week ago near the Santa Ynez River in Paradise. I believe the flower on the right is the Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica), but cp. Clarkia bottae. It definitely has nodding flower buds as you can see in this photo. This flower is hosting a rave of little Beetles (Coleoptera). (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 6 April 2015)
CommonNames: Farewell-to-Spring; Winecup Clarkia; Speckled Clarkia; Beetles
ScientificName: Onagraceae; Clarkia purpurea; quadrivulnera; Clarkia cylindrica; Clarkia bottae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Onagraceae; Clarkia purpurea; quadrivulnera; Clarkia cylindrica; Clarkia bottae; Coleoptera

File.Name: f406e_f406-011.jpg
Notes: 16438596344 Here are the first flowers I've seen this year of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Compare this photo of the California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula). The flowers are less colorful and they don't come from a grown-together "connate leaf" like that species. These shrubs are free-standing, though they have twining tendencies and sometimes climb up into neighboring plants. They look quite different from the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata), see this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 6 April 2015)
CommonNames: Chaparral Honeysuckle; California Honeysuckle; Santa Barbara Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata

File.Name: f406f_f406-023.jpg
Notes: 16440798333 I believe these are new florets of native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua aka Nasella cernua) in the Poaceae plant family, though I can't make out the twisted awns - see this photo. You have to remember not to overexpose these native grasses. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 6 April 2015)

File.Name: f407a_f407-001.jpg
Notes:
17071156722 This is a happy Fremontia (Fremontodendron, Sterculiaceae) enjoying the rain today. You can see raindrops if you look close, but it's hard to photograph the rain! This is a cultivar, but it's growing like a native in a place that we never water. It might be the Mexican Flannelbush (Fremontodendron mexicanum) because it's growing erect and unbranched near the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2015)

This was my only photo today because it rained much of the day. I've been feeling under the weather all day, but at least we've had some weather to be under! We had 3/4 inch of rain today. That will be a nice boost for the summer flowers.

File.Name: f407b_f403-018.jpg
Notes: 16886359749 This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly is working still-new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. The pretty flowers are starting to fade lower in the mountains, but they are still going strong on the summits. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 April 2015)

File.Name: f407c_f405-003.jpg
Notes: 17070974632 This is looking eye-to-eyes with a little Snipe Fly (Rhagionidae) of the order Diptera on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. The fly held this pose for a while, gazing into my camera lens. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2015)

File.Name: f407d_f401-028.jpg
Notes: 16884839130 Here are tiny black aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) with good taste in flowers - in this case, a flower head of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)
CommonNames: aphids; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: f407e_f401-018.jpg
Notes: 17046399336 This is a fantastic seed head of native Silver Puffs (Uropappus lindleyi) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't usually notice the flowers and am amazed when I suddenly see the seeds - where did THEY come from? (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2015)

File.Name: f407f_f331-014.jpg
Notes:
17071543531 These are the first - and maybe the only - flowers of native California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family. This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there. I've been hoping to see more - and waiting to post - but these may well be the only flowers that I see in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2015)

This plant has clover-like "leaves of three" like Poison Oak but smoother. The flowers remind me of a locoweed (Astragalus). It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! I've never tried making tea from the leaves, but I should.
Locality: Astragalus). It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple
ScientificName: Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae; Astragalus
associatedTaxa: Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae; Astragalus

File.Name: f408a_f408-039.jpg
Notes:
16898552069 This is the first proper flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family that I've found this year, growing along the road in our canyon. There are two tiny "true bugs" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on the flower and bud, and probably more inside. Compare this photo of a not-so-happy flower at Lake Cachuma a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2015)

I was expecting wind today after our rain yesterday, but the wind never happened. It was a beautiful calm day. I went botanizing with Flickr member Mark Egger and his wife Esta today, see my [Next] few photos. I was still feeling under the weather, but it was a good day.

File.Name: f408b_f408-018.jpg
Notes:
17082808442 These are uncommon plants of the Vandenberg Monkeyflower (Mimulus fremontii var. vandenbergensis) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family growing in sandy soil on Burton Mesa near Lompoc today. The range of this native is pretty much restricted to Burton Mesa. But "rare" for plants really means "rarely common" and we found a number of them today. This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.1 - as rare as it gets. The plants are tiny - the flowers are as large as the whole plants. It's remarkable to think that the entire population of these plants lies within a few miles of this place. (Burton Mesa, Lompoc, 8 April 2015)

There are also tiny Popcorn Flower (Cryptantha, Boraginaceae) plants in this photo. I have no idea what they are. You need a microscope to tell them apart.

I met with Flickr member Mark Egger and his wife Esta to do some botanizing today. We started with uncommon Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) on San Marcos Pass, and then drove out to Burton Mesa near Lompoc on a quest for this uncommon Monkeyflower. We actually found it on our first stop.

BTW, I'm going to stealth the location of all my Burton Mesa photos. I won't show the actual location, but I will show a road intersection nearby.
CommonNames: Vandenberg Monkeyflower; Popcorn Flower; Ojai Fritillary
ScientificName: Mimulus fremontii; vandenbergensis; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Cryptantha; Boraginaceae; Fritillaria ojaiensis
associatedTaxa: Mimulus fremontii; vandenbergensis; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Cryptantha; Boraginaceae; Fritillaria ojaiensis

File.Name: f408c_f408-020.jpg
Notes: 17084094555 These are wonderful new flowers of (I believe) the native San Luis Obispo Wallflower (Erysimum capitatum var. lompocense) in the Brassicaceae plant family. These are real California wild mustards! It's hard to capture the color of these wild mustards without washing them out. (Burton Mesa, Lompoc, 8 April 2015)

File.Name: f408d_f408-024.jpg
Notes: 16896130008 I believe these are flowers and hairy stems of Carnival Poppy aka Narrow-leaved Meconella (Hesperomecon linearis) in the Papaveraceae plant family. The flowers are small, but the hairy stems are beautiful! (Burton Mesa, Lompoc, 8 April 2015)

File.Name: f408e_f408-038.jpg
Notes: 16896039750 I believe this is Purple Owl's Clover (Castilleja exserta ssp. latifolia) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly in the Scrophulariaceae. It has slightly wider bracts but skinnier lobes than the species, and yellow flowers that appear more or less banded. At least that's what my guide today - Mark Egger said, and Mark knows his Castillejas! Compare this photo of (probably) Castilleja exserta ssp. exserta. It seems a pretty subtle difference. This is the southern limit of this subspecies. (Burton Mesa, Lompoc, 8 April 2015)
ScientificName: Castilleja exserta; latifolia; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Castilleja exserta; exserta
associatedTaxa: Castilleja exserta; latifolia; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Castilleja exserta; exserta

File.Name: f408f_f408-034.jpg
Notes: 17057535946 I believe this is a native plant of Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another form of the same native thistles I show in this "triptych photo" at http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/8991707068/. We don't have this one at home. (Burton Mesa, Lompoc, 8 April 2015)

File.Name: f409a_f409-014.jpg
Notes:
16906540360 Here is another beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on the chip seal road in our canyon at home. These butterflies in the canyon all seem small, and they spend their time chasing away any other butterflies that come by. I guess they are all males, like little roosters. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2015)

I was feeling crummy today, and it was a crummy day for new photos - overcast with high clouds in the morning and breezy all day, approaching downright windy. I have a cold coming on. I went down to the creek in our canyon today and sat for a while to see what came by. This butterfly did! I'll do what I can for new photos tomorrow.

File.Name: f409b_f408-045.jpg
Notes: 16473776873 This is a tattered Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working new flowers of (I believe) the native San Luis Obispo Wallflower (Erysimum capitatum var. lompocense) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The head of this butterfly isn't quite in focus, but its pollen-dusted proboscis is. This is how butterflies work as pollinators - going from flower to flower, and carrying pollen along the way. (Burton Mesa, Lompoc, 8 April 2015)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; San Luis Obispo Wallflower
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Erysimum capitatum; lompocense; Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Erysimum capitatum; lompocense; Brassicaceae

File.Name: f409c_f409-003.jpg
Notes:
16905964358 This is a tiny aphid (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) with a hole in its butt! Actually it's just the husk of an aphid, no doubt done in by a "parasitic wasp" - maybe a Braconid Wasp in the family Braconidae of the Hymenoptera. Its final perch is a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2015)

It's been a good week for butt shots!
CommonNames: aphid; Braconid Wasp in the family Braconidae of the Hymenoptera. Its final perch is a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Braconidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f409d_f404-011.jpg
Notes:
16471216614 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of an adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera crawling away from me on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It might be a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). I'll bet this is a gravid female, full of eggs. Is she pushing one out at the moment of this shot? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)

See this photo showing more of the same critter.
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Leafhopper Assassin Bug

File.Name: f409e_f404-017.jpg
Notes:
16906036000 This is a tiny ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) crawling on a pair of new bisexual galls of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera today. I believe the ant is genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. It's a nice butt shot - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2015)

This photo has the ant's butt sharp and the galls beyond the focus. See this photo for a shot with the galls sharp and the same ant not quite in focus!
CommonNames: ant; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp

File.Name: f409f_f409-006.jpg
Notes:
17092621311 Here is a comparison of two different Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus, Phrymaceae) shrubs:

On the left is Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) from our canyon at home, taken today. The stems and calyces are quite pubescent.

On the right is the Northern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. aurantiacus) from Burton Mesa near Lompoc, taken yesterday. The stems and calyces are mostly glabrous.

I can see other differences. Santa Barbara County seems to be right at the cusp of the change. The southern form seems to have wider leaves and more sculpted petals, but that might just be environmental. These small differences keep local botany interesting year after year! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2015 and Burton Mesa, Lompoc, 8 April 2015)
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Northern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; Phrymaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Mimulus aurantiacus; aurantiacus
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; Phrymaceae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Mimulus aurantiacus; aurantiacus

File.Name: f410a_f410-013.jpg
Notes:
17104502765 This is a chilly California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly trying to warm up enough for take-off on my son's finger tips this morning. He found it on the driveway, and it crawled onto his hand. It flew away a moment later. I don't usually post photos so wide (16x9), but this one warranted it. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2015)

I'm still feeling crummy, but maybe a bit better today. I went for a drive along the river. It was hazy all day, and breezy, becoming downright windy as the day went on.

File.Name: f410b_f410-023.jpg
Notes: 16896806887 I'm sure this is a Colias sulphur butterfly, but I'm not sure what kind - Colias eurytheme or Colias philodice? Either way, it was struggling when I found it, and I first thought it had been had by a Crab Spider. Looking closer, I can see that it's newly emerged, and its right hindwing is stuck in its chrysalis. Tough luck, little butterfly. I found this one on Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata), with bracts below the flower. There were quite a few of them flying nearby. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 April 2015)
ScientificName: Colias eurytheme or Colias philodice; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata
associatedTaxa: Colias eurytheme or Colias philodice; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata

File.Name: f410c_f410-037.jpg
Notes: 16916499600 This is looking into the secret heart of a native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Liliaceae plant family. There's a rave of tiny Beetles (Coleoptera) in there! This is the first one I've found this year. I don't expect to find many in this third drought year in a row, but I'll be happy to be proven wrong. Maybe our recent rain will help. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 April 2015)

File.Name: f410d_f410-040.jpg
Notes:
16896530447 I believe this is native Chick Lupine (Lupinus microcarpus var. densiflorus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The crowded flowers are stacked in whorls on the stem, varying from yellow to white. This group in growing with weedy grasses along Paradise Road, but if you look close you can spot native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua or Nasella cernua, Poacea) in the foreground. I used to think these lupines were all introduced, but they just prefer roadsides. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 April 2015)

Lot's of our natives prefer roadsides. It's a interesting stumper!
(See my answer from many years ago. I'd make some changes now!)
ScientificName: Lupinus microcarpus; densiflorus; Fabaceae; Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poacea
associatedTaxa: Lupinus microcarpus; densiflorus; Fabaceae; Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poacea

File.Name: f410e_f410-002.jpg
Notes:
16896295097 This is just a fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae). I guess it's another Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae, though this one doesn't have as many gold hairs on its thorax that this one has. (It does have a few on the sides.) (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2015)

"Just a fly" - but I love photographing flies! They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even when I don't know what they are. HFDF!
ScientificName: Diptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae

File.Name: f410f_f409-001.jpg
Notes: 17103607185 This is just a fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. I guess it's a another Muscidae, though a different one. I love photographing flies! They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even when I don't know what they are. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2015)

File.Name: f411a_f411-016.jpg
Notes:
16908025577 This is a beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil in our canyon today. That white trim around the butterfly's wings is gorgeous, it must be newly emerged. I don't know much about these butterflies. My book says they host on composites and only have a single brood. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2015)

It was a beautiful clear spring day today, but there was breeze all day. All my photos today are taken close to the ground where the wind isn't so evident. The breeze built up into a proper wind as the day went on.

File.Name: f411b_f411-012.jpg
Notes: 16492790914 I believe this is a small male Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly showing its upper wings while trying to warm up on a leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae). I never got to see the underside of its wings to make sure, but compare this photo and this photo of the similar early-flying Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly. I believe the Spring Azures have that wider dark border on their wings. They are plain butterflies underneath, but quite pretty on top. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2015)
CommonNames: Spring Azure; Climbing Penstemon; Silvery Blue
ScientificName: Celastrina ladon; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Glaucopsyche lygdamus
associatedTaxa: Celastrina ladon; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Glaucopsyche lygdamus

File.Name: f411c_f411-025.jpg
Notes:
16907717497 This is a tattered American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly working flowers of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae). At first I thought it was a West Coast Lady (V. annabella) because of that almost orange band on the front edge of its forewing and it lacks the white spot lower down that these butterflies usually have, but this butterfly definitely had two large eyespots below the hindwings. It's tattered, and also seem to have a deformed left forewing. These butterflies are troopers! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2015)

Second thoughts (12 April 2015)
Second thoughts (12 April 2015)
I'm not sure what this butterfly is! The pattern of black on its upper wings doesn't quite match any of our Ladies, and the lack of black markings on it's hindwings is interesting. Well, you never get them all!
Locality:
12 April 2015)
I'm not sure what this butterfly is! The pattern of black on its upper wings doesn't quite match any of our Ladies
CommonNames: American Lady; Black Sage; West Coast Lady

File.Name: f411d_f410-011.jpg
Notes: 17089026836 This is another photo of a chilly California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly trying to warm up enough for take-off on my son's finger tips yesterday morning. See this photo. He found it on the driveway, and it crawled onto his hand. My son apparently uses his fingers like I do, as digging implements! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2015)

File.Name: f411e_f411-030.jpg
Notes:
17113331782 Here is the first new flower of native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our canyon. (No relation to gillyweed in Harry Potter 4!) It's mostly a summer plant that keeps producing flowers until August or beyond. Last year I found the first flowers on April 20. The year before, I found the first flowers in May and they lasted until October! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2015)

I love our summer wildflowers, which flower when no plant should be able to!

File.Name: f411f_f410-008.jpg
Notes: 16494639863 Here are the first open flowers of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family on the canyon near home. There's some confusion about what species our plants really are. The real S. mollis is said to only grow a foot or two tall, and our plants are more like S. albus at 2-4 feet tall. It definitely some kind of Snowberry. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2015)
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus

File.Name: f412a_f412-024.jpg
Notes:
17127886091 This is a lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly nectaring on (I think) native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. I reckon this is a new brood. I've been seeing quite a few of them flying in the woods, but rarely visiting a flower like this. The plants are growing in a place that was cleared but not burned last year. They seem more robust than usual, but maybe that's to be expected in a clearing? (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2015)

Today turned out to be another good day for butterflies! Yesterday started calm and ended up windy, but today started windy and ended up calm. It was a beautiful spring day.

File.Name: f412b_f412-010.jpg
Notes: 16921028527 This is another beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a mint leaf in the garden this morning. This one in showing the underside of its wing, with the alternating white and orange bands which are typical of checkerspots. Yeah, I got another photo of the upper wings spread open, but I already posted a good shot in this photo yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2015)

File.Name: f412c_f412-007.jpg
Notes: 17127609991 This is looking at the undersides of a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly. It's wings remind me of remind me of the inside-covers of fine old books, like "marbled endpapers". This one is "puddling" in the garden this morning. Yeah, I got a photo of the upper wings, but I already have a better shot in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2015)

File.Name: f412d_f412-001.jpg
Notes:
16920654187 This little Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly "puddling" in the garden this morning is a veteran, missing both of its right legs. It seems to fly OK. Yeah, it flopped around a bit, but these butterflies are tough! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2015)

These butterflies are True Brushfoots (subfamily Nymphalinea) that perch on a platform of just four legs, with their front legs reduced, T-Rex fashion.
Locality: subfamily Nymphalinea) that perch on a platform of just four legs

File.Name: f412e_f412-014.jpg
Notes: 17127183571 Here are two photos of the uncommon native Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) in the Papaveraceae plant family. The photo on the left shows a couple of whole plants, and the photo on the right shows a closer shot of a flower and seed pod. It's the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns or (less commonly) after clearings. I found this one in a place that was cleared but didn't burn, and sometimes that's enough to trigger these plants. It really looks like a little Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2015)

File.Name: f412f_f412-027.jpg
Notes:
17127017181 These are new flowers of native Whispering Bells (Emmenanthe penduliflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It's a common fire-follower plant in our mountains that we see for a few years after a wildfire, before they disappear back into the seedbank - though these are growing in a place that was cleared last year for a firebreak. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2015)

I've been seeing a lot of these in Paradise on the almost two year old burn of the White Fire, but even more on an area where a stream washed out during last December's good rain. I guess they like disturbed areas, whatever the disturbance. These are the first ones I've found on the pass this year.

File.Name: f413a_f413-003.jpg
Notes:
16953429300 Here is a mating pair of small Syrphid Flower Flies (Syrphidae, Diptera) doing what all flies do - exchanging genetic material to insure that there will be lots of little flies in the future. They are having their nuptials on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. Photos of insects mating are the one time you can clearly see the difference between male and female. I reckon that's the female fly on the right with that fat abdomen full of eggs. The male fly on the left has a skinny abdomen and "holoptic eyes" that meet in the middle of its head, but the female doesn't. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2015)

Today's photos are anything but butterflies! I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, but it was too breezy for photos at my usual stop near Lake Cachuma. It was breezy all morning today, but it finally died down in the late afternoon. Tomorrow is expected to be another windy day. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: f413b_f411-003.jpg
Notes: 16954651589 This is a tiny red-eyed Fly (order Diptera) perched on a new leaf in the mint patch of the garden. Maybe the fly is a Drosophilid (Drosophilidae)? It was here for just a moment. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, so I can use my camera as an expensive remote micrometer. The critter is about 830 pixels across. That makes it about 3.3 mm across, about 1/8 inch. See this photo for how I figured. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2015)

File.Name: f413c_f411-009.jpg
Notes: 16954464739 Here is a trio of tiny ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) "butts up" in the pistils of a stripped flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I believe the ants are genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae with articulated abdomens and thoracic spines - visible on the left ant if you look close. I don't know what critter stripped the flower, and I wonder why. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2015)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: f413d_f411-021.jpg
Notes: 17114489026 I guess this is a Western Boxelder Bug (Boisea rubrolineata) in the family Rhopalidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - though the closest Boxelder tree is about 20 miles away down the river past Buellton. This one is perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2015)
CommonNames: Western Boxelder Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Boisea rubrolineata; Rhopalidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Boisea rubrolineata; Rhopalidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f413e_f410-030.jpg
Notes: 17138675752 This is the small nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera sucking on a flower head of (I think) non-native Tocalote (Centaurea melitensis, Asteraceae). It's one of our worst invasive weeds, so I have to admire the taste of this bug! It seems to have just four antenna segments, which narrows down the choices. I won't try to guess what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2015)

File.Name: f413f_f410-010.jpg
Notes:
17114179296 "Sometimes I just like to sit and think..."

Actually this little Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) is thinking about how to get away from me! It's perched on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae) in our canyon at home. The nymph might be our usual California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2015)

My usual photo rule is "Get the eyes sharp and the rest will take care of itself". Did it work? I don't usually post photos in 16:9 aspect, but this one seemed to call for it.
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum
