File.Name: f414a_f414-022.jpg
Notes:
16532041853 This is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera), formerly known as Hemaris diffinis. It quickly working a flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Yeah, it's not the best photo, but it's a record shot as this is the first one I've seen this year. Look how its proboscis in covered with blue pollen from the flowers. This is how pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2015)

I wasn't expecting today to turn out to be an all butterfly day! We've been under a NWS High Wind Warning all day, with wind gusts to 60 MPH expected! I stayed in the canyon expecting the worst, but the winds haven't materialized yet. It was a little breezy today, but quite pleasant.
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Hemaris diffinis; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Hemaris diffinis; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae

File.Name: f414b_f414-006.jpg
Notes: 16529650124 This is a lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly nectaring a flower head of non-native Italian Thistle (Carduus pycnocephalus, Asteraceae) in our canyon. I'm glad something likes these flowers! They are one of our most invasive weeds. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2015)

File.Name: f414c_f414-019.jpg
Notes: 17152011435 This is another pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly showing her underwings on a flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The underwings of these ladies are so pretty. They remind me of the inside-covers of fine old books, like "marbled endpapers". (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2015)

File.Name: f414d_f414-030.jpg
Notes: 17151181491 This is a pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly reeling in her proboscis on flowers of (I think) non-native Smooth Vetch (Vicia villosa ssp. varia) in the Fabaceae plant family. This is a pretty common weed along roads in our mountains. You can see the five eyes under the butterfly's wings. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2015)
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Vicia villosa; varia; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Vicia villosa; varia; Fabaceae

File.Name: f414e_f414-010.jpg
Notes:
16965480879 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods today, hunched over a flower head of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Butterflies usually do this when it's windy, but the wind wasn't too bad yet. It show the pretty pattern on the lower wings. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2015)

These Variable Checkerspots are usually our most common butterflies as spring turns to summer, but it's interesting that the Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterflies are actually more common in the woods right now. See my [Next] photo. Well, it's still early in the year.
Locality: Chlosyne gabbii) butterflies are actually more common in the woods right now. See my [Next] photo. Well
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Black Sage; Variable Checkerspots; Gabb's Checkerspot
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Chlosyne gabbii
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Chlosyne gabbii

File.Name: f414f_f414-011.jpg
Notes: 16965403389 This is a somewhat tattered Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working flowers of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These small flowers were the butterfly hot spot today. I've been seeing a lot of these butterflies lately. They are actually more common than the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies in the woods right now, which is unusual. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2015)
CommonNames: Gabb's Checkerspot; Black Sage; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Chlosyne gabbii; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Chlosyne gabbii; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: f415a_f415-005.jpg
Notes:
16540507584 This is another Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly perched on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden this morning. This butterfly looks plain brown from a distance, but in this photo you can see that its color is more compilicated. It looks like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. It's one of my favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2015)

The wind finally did pick up last night, after 10 pm. It was done by 5 am. I wish all our wind events would be so photo-friendly! Today started calm, but a breeze did pick up. It sounds like the Santa Ana Winds are happening down south, but we miss them in the Santa Barbara mountains because we don't have an easy path to the desert.

File.Name: f415b_f415-039.jpg
Notes:
16542627743 This not-so-little Beetle (Coleoptera) seems to be looking at the Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) one flower back and wondering WTF! The flowers are the Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family, see my [Previous] photo.

I believe this is a Red-eared Blister Beetle (Lytta auriculata) in the family Meloidae. It's interesting that most of the photos at BugGuide are from the desert. Well, maybe we're becoming a desert! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2015)
CommonNames: Beetle; Honey Bee; Large-flowered Phacelia; Red-eared Blister Beetle
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phacelia grandiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Lytta auriculata; Meloidae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phacelia grandiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Lytta auriculata; Meloidae

File.Name: f415c_f415-038.jpg
Notes: 16542515033 Here is a large cluster of new flowers of Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It commonly grows as a fire-follower, but this plant is growing along Painted Cave Road in a place that was cleared last year. I guess anything that lets the light in is good enough for these plants. See my [Next] photo. The flowers are large and quite beautiful, though individual flowers only last a day or two. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2015)

File.Name: f415d_f415-013.jpg
Notes: 16955101187 Here are pretty new flowers of native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family. The nodding ("reflexed") buds and erect seed pods are diagnostic of the species. They are not common in our mountains, but they are also easy to overlook. They seem to be another one of those uncommon plants that is "rarely common". I've been expecting them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 April 2015)

File.Name: f415e_f415-007.jpg
Notes:
16974797330 It's not just the wildflowers that are early this year.

These are dreadlock-like staminate catkins and pistillate flowers of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The erect catkins distinguishes it from true oaks in the genus Quercus. Already! Last year I found the first catkins on May 5, and I thought that was early. I think of these as June flowers. If you look close at the catkins, you can spot a few beetles in there. I find lots if insects on these flowers, including bees and flies. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2015)

File.Name: f415f_f413-004.jpg
Notes:
17160643672 Just count the wings and divide by two!

I believe these are mating Flesh Flies (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) that I found in the mint patch of the garden. There's a discrete shadow just where it should be. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2015)

File.Name: f416a_f416-018.jpg
Notes:
16985416880 I believe this is a native plant of Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This one has violet flowers, but other plants are white. See this "triptych photo" at http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/8991707068/. I was confused because this one was growing right on the edge of Stagecoach Road as I drove by, but a little voice in my head said "Stop, that's not Bull Thistle!" Sure enough, the cobwebby hairs on the phyllaries are distinctive. This is a very beautiful native plant. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2015)

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. The non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up. If in doubt, leave it be!

It was windy today - really too windy for my kind of photos, though I got a few. I also saw the first flower of the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus, Liliaceae) on the hillside above this plant, but it was rock-'n-rollin' in the wind. I'll go back tomorrow morning when it's (hopefully) not so windy.
CommonNames: Cobwebby Thistle; Bull Thistle; Butterfly Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare; Calochortus venustus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare; Calochortus venustus; Liliaceae

File.Name: f416b_f415-025.jpg
Notes:
16550281824 Here is one of the first new flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family that I've found this year. This one seems to have a happy face! You can see its pistil on top, two of its stamens below, and the non-fertile "staminode" at the bottom. That's the "fifth stamen" - which gives this "pen-stamen" family it's name. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 April 2015)

To be honest, I found the first flowers two weeks ago on April 3. But those flowers were so tattered that I never posted the photo.

File.Name: f416c_f416-011.jpg
Notes: 16965186477 Here is (yet another) beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly perched on foliage of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the woods today. It was quite windy all day, but this was a lucky shot between the gusts. The butterfly looks freshly emerged. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2015)

File.Name: f416d_f416-009.jpg
Notes:
16984698318 Oops - this is a Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae and not a Jumping Spider like I first thought. It's the size of a jumper, but the shape of it's abdomen and head and those bristly legs should have tipped me off. I found it perched on a new leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). It's an attractive little spider, barely 1/4 inch long. Looking at the photos at BugGuide, I reckon this one is a female. Thanks for setting me straight, Alice! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2015)

D'oh, I even made this mistake once before - see this photo. At least I'm consistent with my mistakes!
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: f416e_f415-001.jpg
Notes:
16984493188 This fly (order Diptera) looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae). Here it is in the mint patch of the garden. I reckon this counts as a "beautiful bristly bug butt" so HBBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2015)

It's been a good week for beautiful bug butt photos. Also see this photo and this photo, not to mention this one which qualifies for a trifecta of HHD, HBBBT, and HFDF!

I don't think there's anything special about the mint patch, but it smells nice and it catches the early morning sunlight. I start my photo day here with a cup of coffee, and I usually find something to photograph.

File.Name: f416f_f413-005.jpg
Notes: 16985966799 This is a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) perched in the mint patch a few days ago. These flies have gray-blue bodies, red eyes, and a red tip on their butt. It's not the sharpest photo, but its butt is immaculate. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2015)

File.Name: f417a_f417-037.jpg
Notes:
16563067353 This is another fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera), formerly known as Hemaris diffinis. This one in working unusual flowers of (I think) the uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The moth is sticking its long proboscis deep into the bottom of the nectar spur to get the good stuff. Usually these plants are fire-followers, so it was interesting to find them here, in a place that didn't burn recently. It's an unusual moth on an unusual flower, sharpness be damned! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 April 2015)

Today was quite warm - up to 84∞ at the river where I was. It was also quite breezy all day. I should have bumped up my ISO for this photo.
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Hemaris diffinis; Delphinium umbraculorum; Ranunculaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Hemaris diffinis; Delphinium umbraculorum; Ranunculaceae

File.Name: f417b_f417-010.jpg
Notes: 17157217606 This is a handsome Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a violet flower of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's the same flower I showed yesterday in this photo. What a pretty combination! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2015)

File.Name: f417c_f417-002.jpg
Notes:
17182960625 I believe this is a flower of the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family, the first I've seen this year. It has a squarish nectary below a reddish spot, with (usually) a second paler spot above. I spotted this flowers yesterday, when it was too windy for a photo. I came back this morning... and it was still breezy. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2015)

Beetles and bees dig these flowers. It's like one of those old "count the animals" panels in the old comics. I count ten. (The answer is not 1,000 because most are hidden under the petals - as that great old Mad comic has it!)

File.Name: f417d_f417-013.jpg
Notes:
16975396647 This is the locally uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family, with already faded flowers. Darn, I got here too late! The shrubs have long odd-pinnate leaves with many leaflets, and look like they belongs in the desert. This is not a common shrub in our area, but they are scattered along the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 April 2015)

Many sources repeat that this is the only food plant of the California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly, but I'm sure that's wrong. This shrub is uncommon, and the butterflies are quite common later in the summer. They must have other host plants.

File.Name: f417e_f417-001.jpg
Notes:
17182136641 This is another interesting Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden. It looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae). I believe it's a Tachinid, but I've been fooled before! What an interesting face this fly has. I have another "butt-shot" of the same kind of fly in here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2015)

I love photographing flies! They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even when I don't know what they are.

File.Name: f417f_f416-001.jpg
Notes: 17181015052 This is an interesting Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden. It looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae), but it also has the bluish colors and gold fuzz on its thorax to be a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae. I believe it's Tachinid, but I've been fooled before! What an interesting face this fly has. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2015)

File.Name: f418a_f418-023.jpg
Notes:
17006178520 This is a tiny diurnal (daytime) Longhorn Moth (genus Adela, Adelidae, Lepidoptera) on one of the first new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family in the mountains. They have remarkably long antennae. This one flew into my photo framed like the [Previous] one, but it held up to tight cropping. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2015)

The shrub is named for naturalist Blanche Trask who studied the botany of the Channel Islands 100 years ago. She's one of my heros. There's a collection of her writings, well worth reading, on the web at http://naturespeace.org/blanchetraskanthology.htm.

Today was downright windy, but I got a few photos by bumping up my ISO to 400, waiting for that moment between the gusts, taking lots of photos, and dumb luck! I'll be busy tomorrow and probably won't be able to post. We're heading out to Hollister Ranch in the afternoon to visit with a few good friends.
CommonNames: Longhorn Moth; Trask's Yerba Santa; Blanche Trask
ScientificName: Adela; Adelidae; Lepidoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; lots
associatedTaxa: Adela; Adelidae; Lepidoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; lots

File.Name: f418b_f418-027.jpg
Notes:
16986051747 These are the first new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family, with a loogie of Spittlebug slime! The Spittlebugs aka Froghoppers (family Cercopidae of the order Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) make these frothy masses to protect their larvae. It's on one of my favorite native shrubs, though they smell a little skunky on a hot day. See my [Next] photo of the same flower cluster, with a different visitor. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2015)

This shrub is named for naturalist Blanche Trask who studied the botany of the Channel Islands 100 years ago. She's one of my heros. There's a collection of her writings, well worth reading, on the web at http://naturespeace.org/blanchetraskanthology.htm.
CommonNames: Trask's Yerba Santa; Spittlebug; Spittlebugs; Froghoppers; Blanche Trask
ScientificName: Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; Cercopidae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: f418c_f418-019.jpg
Notes: 16570818994 I guess this is a Flower Longhorn Beetle (Lepturinae) in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera working a new flower of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. This beetle isn't large, but it sure is pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2015)
ScientificName: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae
associatedTaxa: Lepturinae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae

File.Name: f418d_f418-014.jpg
Notes: 17192582111 These are new pom-pom-like seed heads of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae). You have to think not to over-expose these beauties! This plant was traditionally used medicinally to treat sores, and maybe as a hair tonic. "If it grows such wonderful hairs on its seeds, it must work on my head!" (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2015)

File.Name: f418e_f417-018.jpg
Notes: 16570523464 Here are two photos of an interesting Tarweed (genus Deinandra or Hemizonia) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in Paradise on the burn of the White Fire almost two years ago. I guess it's a Clustered Tarweed (Deinandra fasciculata), though this one looks subtly different. Maybe it's just that it looks so yellow (and pretty) against the dull and mostly dead background. It's unusual out here in Paradise, no doubt a fire-follower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 April 2015)

File.Name: f418f_f417-030.jpg
Notes: 17192771075 This is a leaning flower spike of (I think) the uncommon Umbrella Larkspur (Delphinium umbraculorum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The flowers go from done in the lower-left to buds in the upper-right. These plants have stronger stems, denser flower heads, and longer spurs than our other native blue-flowered delphiniums, D. patens and D. parryi. See my [Previous] photo for an interesting visitor. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 April 2015)
ScientificName: Delphinium umbraculorum; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; D. parryi
associatedTaxa: Delphinium umbraculorum; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; D. parryi

File.Name: f420a_f419-007.jpg
Notes:
17217224412 This is an Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) snuggling into a flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family, on the hillside behind our home. Hah - I was pre-focused on the flower head waiting for a White-Lined Sphinx Moth to appear - see my [Previous] photo - when this hummer swooped into my photo! I didn't even have time to look in my viewfinder before it was gone, but I got the photo. It's a great pollination shot. Look how the profile of the flower fits the hummer so perfectly, stamens leaving a bit of pollen on it's head. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2015)

I usually post today's photos today, but I missed yesterday for a good reason. I reckon this photo from yesterday is definitely my lead photo today! I apologize for missing yesterday. We met with some good friends at Little Drake's on Hollister Ranch. Our gathering started cool and windy, but it ended up very nice in the evening. Good times.

File.Name: f420b_f419-008.jpg
Notes: 17030974858 This is a large White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) working a flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family, on the hillside behind our home. I was pre-focused on a different flower head to the left, but I quickly shifted to get this one. It's not the best photo, but you can see that the flower parts are a bit too large to fit the head of this amazing moth. Someone named rskummel has a better shot showing this. Compare my [Next] photo of a hummingbird on the same flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2015)

File.Name: f420c_f420-016.jpg
Notes:
16596124354 This is another lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly getting nectar from a flower of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 April 2015)

I was having troubles with my exposure settings today, having to set my +/- settings much higher than usual. I didn't realize until I got home that I had inadvertently set my camera in Spot mode rather than my usual Center-weighted mode. I guess the lesson is to check all your settings once in a while. Hah - I should know that by now!
ScientificName: Anthocharis sara; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthocharis sara; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae

File.Name: f420d_f420-015.jpg
Notes: 16598115363 Here are photos of two small butterflies that look similar, and behave a lot alike. They both protect a patch of ground, and try to drive away all passing butterflies. It's not so hard to tell them apart when they are in my camera lens showing their underwings, but they look very similar in life. On the left is Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii), with more white on its underwings. On the right is a Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta), with more graduated colors underneath. I thought they were both Mylitta Crescents when I got the photos today. This is my first photo of a Mylitta Crescent this year. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 April 2015)

File.Name: f420e_f420-001.jpg
Notes:
17030313348 This is looking closer at new flowers of Clustered Tarweed (Deinandra fasciculata aka Hemizonia fasciculata) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in Paradise on the burn of the White Fire almost two years ago. There's a little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working the flowers. See this photo for the whole plant. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 April 2015)

This photo is for NatureShutterbug who shares the name "Deinandra". The flowers have 5 ray flowers and 6 disk flowers as expected. Yes, the petals are curled over, possibly a drought response. That's what made it look so odd.
ScientificName: Deinandra fasciculata; Hemizonia fasciculata; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Deinandra fasciculata; Hemizonia fasciculata; Asteraceae; Anthophila; Hymenoptera

File.Name: f420f_f420-014.jpg
Notes: 17217357841 This is another new flower of native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Liliaceae plant family. Compare the similar Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in this photo. The technical difference is the shape of the nectaries, but there are other more obvious differences. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 April 2015)
CommonNames: Catalina Mariposa Lily; Butterfly Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f421a_f421-011.jpg
Notes:
17228674131 This is a handsome "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, probably Closterocoris amoenus. See my [Previous] photo for the ant-like nymph. It's tucked into the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). Once I noticed this one, I found half a dozen more in other nearby flowers. The flower makes a nice light tent. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2015)

Today was a gloomy day that never did clear, but the gloom makes for nice even lighting. I don't think I could have got any of today's photos on a sunny day with contrasty light. There was some drizzle this morning - 1/100 inch in the rain gauge. There's a chance of thunderstorms for the next couple of days. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Closterocoris amoenus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Closterocoris amoenus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f421b_f421-013.jpg
Notes: 17228476281 This is the remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, probably Closterocoris amoenus. See my [Next] photo for the less ant-like adult. If you look close, you can see its proboscis stuck into the flower petal - also its faux nodal spine, and that green patch below its abdomen that helps create the illusion that it is wasp-waited like a real ant. This one is perched on the petal of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae). It's not the best photo, but good enough! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2015)
ScientificName: Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Closterocoris amoenus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f421c_f421-009.jpg
Notes:
16606394424 This is a tiny Lace Bug in the family Tingidae (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It looks slightly different from the usual lace bugs that I find on Wild Sweetpea - more spiky and less bumpy, compare this photo. I wonder if they are plant-specific? These bugs have remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but it also looks like bubble-wrap! There's a skinny bug inside the armor. You can see its antennae on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2015)

Update, 22 April - I guess they are plant-specific. Alice identifies this as Corythucha eriodictyonae and I found it on a Eriodictyon shrub. BugGuide lists a slew of different species on different host plants. I'll have to come back sometime and look for the one on our native sweetpea.
ScientificName: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; Corythucha eriodictyonae; Eriodictyon
associatedTaxa: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; Corythucha eriodictyonae; Eriodictyon

File.Name: f421d_f421-001.jpg
Notes: 17042593699 This is looking close at a lovely Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden this morning. It was a cold drizzly morning, so I had a chance to get closer than usual. You can see the facets of its iridescent compound eyes. They always remind me of wise old philosophers contemplating the infinite, maybe as drawn by Moebius or Jean-Claude MÈziËres. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2015)

File.Name: f421e_f421-005.jpg
Notes:
17021203537 This is the first flower I've seen this year of native Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's distinctive for its narrow leaves. The are also other differences, like the salmon-colored berries. This subspecies is endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. It's not rare in it's range, but it is "rarely common". The inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) -see this photo - has broader leaves and is much more common throughout the area. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2015)

The great botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen with narrow leaves from the coast in 1831. The inland variety with wide leaves is more common, but the first named collection remains official.
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata

File.Name: f421f_f421-006.jpg
Notes:
17228576345 These are aphid-galled leaves of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. The tumor-like galls are caused the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. Specialized aphids live inside those pockets on the leaves. They are strange and quite beautiful when fresh like these. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2015)

It was a gloomy day today - just right to bring out the color of these galls.
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid

File.Name: f422a_f422-001.jpg
Notes:
17052116968 I found this handsome Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) this morning on a branch of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis,Salicaceae). Maybe it's the genus Platypedia? I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, but we have different cicadas later in summer that do sing. That green scar on the stem is where I pinched off a leaf for a better view. Sorry, willow. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2015)

I have a hard time hearing cicadas. They sing at the the same frequency as my tinnitus, and I just can't pick them out. But it's not so bad to have a background chorus of cicadas!

Today was another gloomy day, with no sun at all and chilly. That's not bad for photos, but there was some breeze today, and that hurts with the dim light. Bumping up the ISO to 400 and taking lots of photos of every subject helps!
ScientificName: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Platypedia; lots
associatedTaxa: Cicadidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Platypedia; lots

File.Name: f422b_f422-006.jpg
Notes: 17237933602 This is a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) snuggled into a dead plant of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). Looking at BugGuide, maybe it's Araneus andrewsi? That plant makes one corner of its large 3-4 foot web that spans to a nearby Poison Oak plant and ends up at a chair I leave here in the canyon. D'oh, I moved my chair and ruined its web. Then I found the spider. It's gnarly looking, with a scary face on its abdomen as so many of these large spiders have. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2015)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: f422c_f422-028.jpg
Notes: 17239545575 This is a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly leaning sideways trying to catch some warming rays from the sun like a little solar collector on another gloomy day. You can see the sparkles in the eyespots of its hind wings. One good thing about these gloomy days is that it slows the butterflies down - IF you have the luck to find them! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2015)

File.Name: f422d_f422-008.jpg
Notes: 17051663188 This is a small Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly leaning sideways trying to catch some warming rays from the sun like a little solar collector. They've been quite scarce this year, but I've seen a few, e.g. here and here - both doing the same thing, leaning over to catch some rays. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2015)

File.Name: f422e_f422-019.jpg
Notes: 16616881074 I found this medium-sized Moth (Lepidoptera) in the oak leaves this morning, but I don't know anything else about it. I've been seeing diurnal (daytime) moths working the flowering Black Sage nearby, but they are quite photo-shy and I haven't managed to get a photo yet. I wonder if this is the one, grounded on a cold day? It's about the right size, and I did find it out in the open. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2015)

File.Name: f422f_f422-014.jpg
Notes: 17239258395 I found this tiny caterpillar on a leaflet of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius, Fabaceae) today. It's small, only about 1/4 inch long. Maybe it's another early-instar caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera)? It looks different to me, but early stages are different. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2015)
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: f423a_f423-006.jpg
Notes:
16628414644 This is a pretty little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) snuggled into a flower of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata, Convolvulaceae). Maybe it's genus Anthophora? It was still chilly when I got this photo, a good time to snuggle. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 April 2015)

It was another gloomy day today, unfortunately with a steady breeze. I don't mind the gloom, but the breeze makes getting my kind of photos difficult. Bump up the ISO and take lots of photos! You couldn't afford to do that with film.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convolvulaceae; Anthophora
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convolvulaceae; Anthophora

File.Name: f423b_f423-020.jpg
Notes: 16628293994 This is a pretty little female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on a flowering stem of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). At least that's what I assume - I never did get a peak at her underwings before she got away. This is her main host plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 April 2015)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: f423c_f423-021.jpg
Notes:
17250000141 This is a fantastic new flower of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's the first I've found this year, at least the first one I could get close to. We also have native White Thistles and Violet Thistles - both considered to be Cirsium occidentale var. californicum. These red ones are the most striking. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 April 2015)

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. It would be a shame to pull one of these my mistake.
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum

File.Name: f423d_f423-032.jpg
Notes: 16627813594 Maybe this is a whole plant of Hollyleaf Navarretia (Navarretia atractyloides) in the Polemoniaceae plant family - but I'm not sure! The flowers seem to have narrower bracts than I expect. The whole plant is about 2 inches tall. I found this one in a place where the good December rains brought down a flood and washed everything away, making room for new plants to appear. The small flowers are quite beautiful with maroon petals and blue stamens. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 April 2015)

File.Name: f423e_f421-014.jpg
Notes: 17248397812 This is a butt-shot of a "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, probably Closterocoris amoenus as it moved away from me. It's in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Phrymaceae or Scrophulariaceae). See this photo for the front of the insect, and the shot [Previous] to that for the fantastic ant-mimic nymph. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2015)
ScientificName: Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Closterocoris amoenus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Phrymaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: f423f_f418-025.jpg
Notes: 16629852483 This is a butt-shot of a small Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera moving away from me on a woolly flower head of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I think it might be the species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae, see this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2015)

File.Name: f424a_f424-005.jpg
Notes:
16638182104 This is looking closer at a gorgeous Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods today, working a new flower of nativeI had a choiceaceae plant family. It must be newly emerged. See my [Previous] photo for the whole butterfly. I like these butterflies because they usually let me get close without taking wing. They've been rather scarce so far this year, but it's still early for them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 April 2015)

I had a choice when I went out for photos this morning and got to East Camino Cielo road - go left into the higher mountains or right to the river at Paradise? I went left. It turns out I went into up gloomy fog, but I still got some nice photos. By the time I got home, the wind started to kick up from the north. Maybe it will blow the gloom away? Given a choice of gloom or wind, I'll take gloom every time. But gloom and breeze is a bad combo for photos, and that's what I had today.

File.Name: f424b_f424-004.jpg
Notes: 17260517575 This is a gorgeous Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods today, working a new flower of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It must be newly emerged. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot of the same butterfly. I like these butterflies because they usually let me get close without taking wing. They've been rather scarce so far this year, but it's still early for them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 April 2015)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: f424c_f424-003.jpg
Notes: 17052830957 I believe these two photos show the nymph and adult of genus Trirhabda beetles in the Chrysomelidae family of the Coleoptera, probably Trirhabda eriodictyonis. They are in the subfamily Galerucinae of this family - Skeletonizing Leaf Beetles, which describes what the larvae do to the foliage. I got these two photos on different species of Yerba Santa in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. The nymph on the left is on Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) taken today, while the adult on the right is from Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) taken yesterday near the river at Paradise. Because they're from different species of plants, I can't guarantee that they are the same species of beetle. Trirhabda diducta is quite similar. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 April 2015)
CommonNames: Skeletonizing Leaf Beetles; Yerba Santa; Trask's Yerba Santa; Bicolored Yerba Santa
ScientificName: Trirhabda; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera; Trirhabda eriodictyonis; Galerucinae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Trirhabda diducta

File.Name: f424d_f424-001.jpg
Notes: 17234027476 I believe this is the papery cone-shaped retreat of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. The cone is fairly large, about 2 inches high. The spider has a separate orb web nearby. Like a pirate, these spiders decorate their nests with the bodies of their prey! You can see a bit of the spider at the bottom of the cone. This one made it's nest in a flowering shrub of non-native (and invasive) Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum, Fabaceae). I won't deny that the shrub is rather pretty, but it doesn't belong here. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 April 2015)
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; Spanish Broom

File.Name: f424e_f424-011.jpg
Notes: 17052458637 This is a mass of Parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) pupal cases on a stem of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). I believe this is the work of Braconid Wasps in the family Braconidae. See Alice Abelia's comment to this photo. Maybe there's the husk of an unfortunate caterpillar under there? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 April 2015)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Braconidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Braconidae

File.Name: f424f_f419-004.jpg
Notes: 17233774536 This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched on prickly-pear cactus spines in the garden. These flies have amazing eyes. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2015)

File.Name: f425a_f425-003.jpg
Notes:
16650920563 Here are raindrops on new flowers of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceaeplant family. Yes, we had an unexpected (almost) 1/4 inch of rain this morning! I got this photo in light rain. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2015)

I think this rain even caught the NWS by surprise. It was followed in the afternoon by hellacious NxNW winds. I hope the winds blow themselves out before tomorrow, so I can get some new photos.

File.Name: f425b_f425-006.jpg
Notes: 17063574887 Here is a new flower of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, with raindrops. Yes, we had an unexpected 1/4 inch of rain this morning! But the time I got this photo - after a beer run to town - the wind had started to kick up and knock off the drops. Compare my [Next] photo. Gloomy weather seems to bring out the colors of these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2015)

File.Name: f425c_f424-014.jpg
Notes: 17270363171 These are interesting new flowers of native Buck Lotus (Hosackia crassifolia aka Lotus crassifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. They have characteristically flat sprays of pinnate foliage. This is an uncommon plant in our mountains that only grows on the highest peaks. If you look close, you can see that insects have drilled neat holes into the sides of a few flowers to do "nectar theft". Yes, the flowers are early this year. In past years, I've found them in late May and June. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 April 2015)

File.Name: f425d_f423-022.jpg
Notes:
16650321793 Here are three different forms of our native Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family, with red, white, and violet flowers. These are growing in different places. From left to right, here are native Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum), and white and violet forms of the California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum). The plants of the latter two seem to breed true, but they are considered to be the same variety for some reason. The red thistles grow tallest and have the most interesting foliage. The white ones seem to be the earliest to flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 April 2015; San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2015; San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2015)

These are one of my favorite native plants. If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. It would be a shame to pull a native by mistake.
CommonNames: Cobweb Thistle; Red Thistle; California Thistle
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Cirsium occidentale; californicum
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Cirsium occidentale; californicum

File.Name: f425e_f423-033.jpg
Notes:
17269921281 These are new flowers of native San Luis Blazing Star aka Small-flowered Stickleaf (Mentzelia micrantha) in the Loasaceae plant family. The leaves are instantly recognizable because they feel rough like sandpaper. This one is just starting to flower, in those neat little involucral cups. They usually grow as fire-followers, but this one is growing in the path a small flood that came from our good rains last December. (*Sigh*) These were common on the burn of the White Fire last year - one year after the burn - but they disappeared from that place this following year. This is a different place and a different disturbance. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 April 2015)

This photo was originally over-exposed. There's something about yellow that makes me do that!
CommonNames: San Luis Blazing Star; Small-flowered Stickleaf

File.Name: f425f_f423-023.jpg
Notes: 17084121659 Phacelias are a tricky plant group in our area because we have so many different kinds, but these are distinctive. I believe this is the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. They have pale, washed-out flowers, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy flower heads which look a bit like "woolly bear" caterpillars. The long "hispid" hairs are a irritating to the skin. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 April 2015)

File.Name: f426a_f426-023.jpg
Notes:
17283131071 Here is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the canyon today. It's looking at me. It's amazing to make eye contact with such a tiny critter, but here it is. I also dig its eyelashes and painted toes! (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2015)

There was an interesting story about Jumping Spiders and their amazing eyes in New Scientist a few days ago - here's the story and a video to go with it. Interesting stuff, but it seems like vivisection to me. I don't need to wire up a captive spider to know that its looking right at me!

The sun was out today for the first time in a few days, and the butterflies were digging it! There's still some wind, but not as bad as yesterday. It was a good day for leps! Julie and I are heading off to the Mojave Desert tomorrow morning. It's too late for the main flowering time, but I'm sure I'll find something to photograph. We'll be back next weekend.

File.Name: f426b_f426-018.jpg
Notes: 17282997651 This is a lovely Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly perched for a moment on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) in the canyon. I like this photo because it shows both upper and lower wings. This plain "blue" butterfly is similar to the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that one has a wider dark margin on its upper wings , see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2015)
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: f426c_f426-026.jpg
Notes: 17075876887 This is a striking Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly about to enter a flower of native Wood Mint aka Hedge Nettle (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). This plant is almost a weed in our garden. This one is growing in a large pot with Iris plants. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2015)
CommonNames: Red Admiral; Wood Mint; Hedge Nettle

File.Name: f426d_f426-014.jpg
Notes: 17075759037 This is another lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly nectaring on a flower of (*ugh*) non-native Italian Thistle (Carduus pycnocephalus, Asteraceae) in our canyon. The butterflies like the flowers, but we'll have to pull them soon before they set seed. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2015)

File.Name: f426e_f426-008.jpg
Notes: 17075600757 This is another beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly. It's perfect except for that scar below its left shoulder. You can't see it in my photo, but this one is hunched over a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. Those eyes... (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2015)

File.Name: f426f_f426-009.jpg
Notes: 17282941365 This is a humble Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly. It's on the ground now, but a moment before it was working a flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. That's interesting because I don't often find these butterflies on flowers of any kind. This is the dark "spring form" - they look brighter in their "summer form", see this photo. These butterflies look moth-like, and are weak fliers. But they are real butterflies as their clubbed antennae prove. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2015)

File.Name: f502a_f430-004.jpg
Notes:
17163900859 This is a fantastic White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) working interesting new flowers of native Yerba Mansa (Anemopsis californica) in the Saururaceaeplant family. These moths fly like hummingbirds, and they seem to fly at all hours of the day. We also saw them in Death Valley a few days before this. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 30 April 2015)

We had a fine desert trip up past Trona to Death Valley, then on to our cabin on Lone Pine Creek, and finally back home. It was warm in Death Valley (100∞+), but not so bad in Lone Pine, only in the 80s. It was almost 80∞ at home today. The desert by Lone Pine is looking disturbingly dry, though I did find a few flowers.

File.Name: f502b_f430-049.jpg
Notes: 17349988365 This is a tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly working a flower of a native Phacelia in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess that the flower is the Distant Phacelia (Phacelia distans), though I'm not sure. Compare my [Previous] photo of a different buttefly, using the flower as a ruler. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail. We have the same butterflies at home, though I haven't got a photo yet this year. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 30 April 2015)
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Phacelia; Distant Phacelia
ScientificName: Brephidium exile; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia distans
associatedTaxa: Brephidium exile; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia distans

File.Name: f502c_f429-057.jpg
Notes: 17349509871 This is a pretty little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working a flower of a native Phacelia in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess that the flower is the Distant Phacelia (Phacelia distans), though I'm not sure. We have the same butterflies at home, though I haven't got a photo yet this year. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)

File.Name: f502d_f501-026.jpg
Notes:
17161735250 This is a remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) - probably Geminaria canalis. It has a dark horizontal dividing line across its eye and a long proboscis, but what's most remarkable are those dark "eye spots" on its back that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. Is that camouflage? This one is perched on a composite flower (Asteraceae) that I can't identify yet. It's a subshrub that grows near rock outcrops that reminds me of our summer-flowering California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) at home, though that one is not reported here. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 1 May 2015)

I've found the same flies at home. See this old photo.

File.Name: f502e_f428-021.jpg
Notes:
16728896223 Ah, what we do for a photo...

I believe this is a Western Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer) in the family Tabanidae of the order Diptera. It's biting my leg below the knee as I tried to remain calm and manage my tripod to get the photo. Yep, they hurt! But those amazing eyes... This place was full of these biting flies. (Salt Creek, Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 28 April 2015)

File.Name: f502f_f429-012.jpg
Notes: 16726352354 This is a pretty green "stink bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a yellow composite flower in the desert. It may well be Say's Stink Bug (Chlorochroa sayi). The flower might be Brittlebush (Encelia farinosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Chlorochroa sayi; Encelia farinosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: f503a_f503-003.jpg
Notes:
17175763709 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

This is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly perched for moment on a stem of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", "Lamiaceae) in the garden. I've seen a few before, but this is the first one I've managed to capture a photo of this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2015)

The rest of my photos today are interesting flowers from our desert trip up past Trona to Death Valley, then on to our cabin on Lone Pine Creek, and finally back home. I took 676 photos, kept 211, and moved 107 to my "good stuff" folder. That's a pretty good "keeper" rate for me!

File.Name: f503b_f501-038.jpg
Notes: 17361321731 Julie found this brilliant flower of native Desert Mariposa Lily (Calochortus kennedyi) in the Liliaceae plant family during a pit-stop at Quail Lake on the western edge of the Mojave Desert on our way home. The flower looks kind of poppy-like, but those whorls of three give it away. This flower was growing with (mostly) weedy grasses, but it sure stands out! I'll post another photo of the whole plant tomorrow. What are those dark things inside - beetles? (Quail Lake, Los Angeles County, California, 1 May 2015)

File.Name: f503c_f501-034.jpg
Notes: 17154091417 Here is a rave of tiny Beetles (Coleoptera) on a new flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris) in the Cactaceae plant family. I only found a few of these flowers on our desert trip, but they were all covered with beetles! These cacti lack large spines, but watch out for the annoying "glochids" - the irritating small spines that are impossible to remove. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 1 May 2015)

File.Name: f503d_f429-040.jpg
Notes: 17173793880 This is an open flower of a Silver Cholla (Opuntia echinocarpa) cactus in the Cactaceae plant family. This is the only plant I found with flowers this year. Watch out for the dead "cactus balls" when you kneel in the desert - you sometimes need pliers to get them out of your knees! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)

File.Name: f503e_f428-039.jpg
Notes: 17173508080 This is a wonderful flower of native Desert Five-Spot (Eremalche rotundifolia) in the Malvaceae plant family. This was the only plant with flowers that we found in Death Valley, along the Artist's Palette drive. Very beautiful, and almost emblematic of Death Valley National Park - though it grows elsewhere as well. (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 28 April 2015)

File.Name: f503f_f429-030.jpg
Notes: 17334982676 I believe these are brilliant flowers of the Mojave Indigo Bush (Psorothamnus arborescens) in the Fabaceae plant family - but cp. Psorothamnus fremontii which looks similar. One of my books says the plants were a source of dyes for desert-dwelling Native Americans. With that intense color, I believe it. (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)

File.Name: f504a_f504-016.jpg
Notes:
17348369336 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a two-fer of pretty Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies on a flowering shrub of Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing near the Santa Ynez River. They are drinking from the same cup! Maybe they have something else in mind? I guess that is a smaller male on the left with a narrow abdomen, and a larger female on the right with a larger abdomen. It's a sweet photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 May 2015)

If you've never tried to photograph a butterfly up close, I recommend you start with these. They are pretty docile, and let you get close.

The fog spilled over the mountains this morning, bringing "fog winds" with them. I went to the river, but it was still pretty breezy. The problem with trips is that I get home with lots of new photos to post, but then I keep getting new photos that I want to post. I need another day or two for my desert photos.

File.Name: f504b_f504-019.jpg
Notes:
17186307368 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I'm pretty sure this is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly working new flowers of native Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla, Labiatae) by the river today. I photographed another one on flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata, Anacardiaceae) yesterday on San Marcos Pass. I reckon this is their time! This butterfly has rusty orange wings above, which are quite obvious when it flies. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 May 2015)

We also have the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) and the Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) which are similar brown hairstreaks.

File.Name: f504c_f430-042.jpg
Notes: 17166512687 These are new seeds of native Indian Ricegrass (Stipa hymenoides aka Achnatherum hymenoides aka Oryzopsis hymenoides, Poacea) growing in the desert of the Alabama Hills. Apparently the seeds were a staple food of Native Americans, and it's the Nevada state grass. It's quite beautiful, but hard to photograph in the harsh desert sun. My histogram was just a bump in the middle of the scale. Still, it's a nice photo - if you get close enough to make it out. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 30 April 2015)
ScientificName: Stipa hymenoides; Achnatherum hymenoides; Oryzopsis hymenoides; Poacea

File.Name: f504d_f430-033.jpg
Notes: 17373555541 Here are two photos of perennial Desert Needle Grass (Stipa speciosa aka Achnatherum speciosum, Poacea) growing the Alabama Hills. On the right is a whole tuft of grass growing by a granitic outcrop. On the left is a closer shot showing the twisted and bent awns. The native grasses have so much more character than the usual introduced weedy annuals. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 30 April 2015)

File.Name: f504e_f430-058.jpg
Notes: 16753377103 These are new flowers of native Wild Licorice (Glycyrrhiza lepidota) in the Fagaceae plant family. It smells nothing like that horrible black licorice candy they sell in stores, but the roots have an interesting taste that grows on you. (Lone Pine Narrow Gauge Road, Lone Pine, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 30 April 2015)

File.Name: f504f_f429-044.jpg
Notes: 16751088954 These are new flowers of Desert Tobacco (Nicotiana obtusifolia) in the Solanaceae plant family. The leaves have a real tobacco smell and contain nicotine. This native plant was a ritual plant for Native Americans. I always feel the vibes from ancient times when I find this plant flowering in the desert. I wonder if it still grows near ancient ritual sites? (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)

File.Name: f505a_f505-002.jpg
Notes:
17198379940 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a tiny Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. What amazing colors it has, like old brass or copper. Others of these flies are green, though those iridescent colors play tricks with your eyes.

It was a gloomy day today - almost all day, though the sun did finally poke out a bit late in the afternoon. But the gloom was perfect light for this fly, and the cold kept him still. There was a slight breeze that kept everything in motion. I took many photos to get this keeper.

File.Name: f505b_f502-004.jpg
Notes:
17199515149 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

This is a neat little Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera. That bump on its head is actually centered between its widely-spaced eyes as part of its thorax that extends over its head. This one is perched (for just a moment) on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon. I won't try to guess what kind it is. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2015)

File.Name: f505c_f428-027.jpg
Notes: 17199246699 Here is an artist taking in the view of the Artists Palette along the Artists Drive in Death Valley. This artist is my wife Julie, no doubt thinking about pigments. It's a beautiful place. (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 28 April 2015)

File.Name: f505d_f429-020.jpg
Notes: 17385211085 I believe this is the Panamint Prince's Plume (Stanleya elata) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It looks subtly different from the more widely distributed Desert Prince's Plume (Stanleya pinnata) which even grows at home, compare this photo. It has narrower petals and less showy flower heads with shorter stamen filaments. I did find this one in the Panamint Mountains. (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)
CommonNames: Panamint Prince's Plume; Desert Prince's Plume

File.Name: f505e_f429-029.jpg
Notes: 17177661797 These are handsome flowers of Desert Mallow (Sphaeralcea ambigua) in the Malvaceae) family. These flowers seem redder than most. They seem to grow mostly along the roads, like a beautiful weed - but they are native. (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)

File.Name: f505f_f501-036.jpg
Notes: 16764774123 Here are two more photos of a beautiful and surprising native Desert Mariposa Lily (Calochortus kennedyi) in the Liliaceae plant family. Julie found this one during a pit-stop at Quail Lake on the western edge of the Mojave Desert on our way home last week. The flower looks kind of poppy-like, but those whorls of three give it away. This flower was growing with (mostly) weedy grasses, but it sure stands out! It was windy when I got these photos. Maybe it's always windy here. (Quail Lake, Los Angeles County, California, 1 May 2015)

File.Name: f506a_f506-010.jpg
Notes:
17209332168 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a spiky Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae, next to it's shed skin. I found this scene on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). Both the spider and it's husk have the same spines, so I'm sure they belong together. I guess the spider needs some quiet time after molting. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2015)

It was another gloomy day, with a slight breeze that kept everything in motion. I had to bump up the ISO and take lots of photos to get these keepers. The cool weather will continue, and there's even a slight chance of rain tomorrow. I'm just about done with my desert trip photos. I've still got lots of trip photos, but my backlog of recent photos from home is growing. Of course I'm going out for new photos every day!

File.Name: f506b_f506-006.jpg
Notes:
17396891565 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here are a pair of flies (order Diptera) doing what all flies do - exchanging genetic material to insure that there will be lots of little flies in the future. I guess from the gold vestiture that these are Cluster Flies in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae, but I'm not sure. They are having their nuptials on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2015)

Photos of insects mating are the one time you can clearly see the difference between male and female. I reckon that's the female fly on the right with that fat abdomen full of eggs. The smaller male fly on the left has "holoptic eyes" that meet in the middle of its head, but the female doesn't. It also has reddish antennae while the female's are tan. I'm not sure if I could recognize these as the same flies if I saw them apart.
ScientificName: Diptera; Pollenia; Calliphoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Pollenia; Calliphoridae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f506c_f501-002.jpg
Notes: 17396311551 Here are some of the different galls I found on Great Basin Sagebrush aka Big Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) in the Asteraceae plant family. I thought it would be easy to find out whose galls these are, but sagebrush collects many different galls and my book is rather skimpy in its treatment. It's enough to say that these are the plant-built homes of different insects, probably mostly Gall Midges (Cecidomyiidae) and Tephritid Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae), both in the order Diptera. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 1 May 2015)

File.Name: f506d_f428-045.jpg
Notes: 17189043627 This is common Creosote Bush (Larrea tridentata) in the unusual Zygophyllaceae plant family, showing flowers, seeds, and even a foliose gall on the stem. The distribution of this shrub almost defines the Mojave Desert. This humble plant is the staff of life for many desert critters. The walnut-sized gall is the work of the Ceosote Stem Gall Midge (Asphondylia auripila), family Cecidomyiidae in the order Diptera. (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 28 April 2015)

File.Name: f506e_f429-033.jpg
Notes: 16776000483 This is a tough Prickly Poppy (Argemone) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's growing in a rocky place on the edge of a cliff along the Panamint-side of Highway 190 coming west from Death Valley. I didn't realize at the time that there are different kinds of these prickly poppies, so I didn't pay close attention. It has more interesting foliage than usual, so maybe this one is the less common Mojave Prickly Poppy (Argemone corymbosa)? (Death Valley, Inyo County, California, 29 April 2015)

File.Name: f506f_f430-054.jpg
Notes:
17370116636 Mushrooms in the desert? Well, why not - it does rain sometimes, and I found this one is sandy alluvium where Lone Pine Creek came up and washed out our bridge a couple of years ago. I believe this is a thoroughly dessicated pink-spored Volvariella speciosa mushroom in the Pluteacea family. See this photo for a fresher specimen. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 30 April 2015)

I want to stealth the location of our cabin, so I'll set the location to a nearby place along the creek.

File.Name: f507a_f507-017.jpg
Notes:
17413849632 Ah, the colors of summer ... I mean spring.

This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched on new flowers of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. This butterfly isn't digging the sun - it's huddled against the cold! I got this photo during one of the rare moments when the sun poked out through the clouds today. Yeah, it's overexposed and the white balance isn't quite right, but it captures the moment. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2015)

It was genuinely cloudy this morning - not the usual marine layer gloom. But it was also quite breezy. We even had a bit of real rain this afternoon, about 3/100s of an inch. Maybe we'll get more tonight.

Desert trip photos? Well, new photos are accumulating despite the crummy weather. It's time to move on.

File.Name: f507b_f507-003.jpg
Notes: 17229336529 Here are two photos of native Soap Plant aka Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. On the left are whole plants, with tall 5-8 foot flower stalks and withered leaves at the base. The photo on the right shows nearly open flowers on a different plant. The flowers open in the late afternoon and close in the morning, so I have a hard time capturing them fully open. One of these days, I'll make a point of getting to them early or late in the day. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2015)

File.Name: f507c_f507-010.jpg
Notes:
17414799865 I found this remarkable gall coming from a leaf bud of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) today. I believe it's the gall of a Fimbriate Gall Wasp (Andricus opertaus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I don't think I've ever found these before, but they are easy to overlook. I only found them on one shrub today, so maybe they are uncommon. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2015)

Here's what Ron Russo has to say in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006):

"These galls have a shredded appearance, with long, fiberlike projections emanating from the gall body. In some case, the galls are extensions of the midrib veins. In other cases, the galls consume entire buds, completely disrupting the leaf. These galls are recognized by the long, stringlike or hairlike projections that emanate from the main gall body. Upon close examination, the veins of the host leaf can be seen as prominent ridges throughout the the form of the gall.... Little else is known about this species."

Galls are so interesting!

File.Name: f507d_f507-023.jpg
Notes:
16791624554 This is a small dark Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera), only about 3/4 inch long or less. Maybe it's genus Okanagana?

Driving home from my walk this afternoon, I saw someone with an insect net beating the native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) with an insect net below. I stopped and asked if he was looking for "Timema" insects, and he was! We had a pleasant talk while he sorted out his catch. (Sorry, I forget his name.) He also had a few of these neat cicadas in his net. The timemas were too fast for me to photograph, but the cicadas were moving slow because of the cool weather today. I did get one timema photo that isn't too bad - maybe I'll post it tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2015)

File.Name: f507e_f506-004.jpg
Notes: 16791427554 This is a tiny nymph of a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Issidae. It's not the best photo - taken on a gloomy morning with a breeze, but still pretty cool. It looks like its shooting hot gas from its butt. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2015)

File.Name: f507f_f506-012.jpg
Notes: 17413780435 This is a closer shot of the spiky Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae in my [Previous] photo. It's perched on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae), and probably just shed its old skin. Despite the gloomy weather and breeze, it's a pretty good "butt shot". HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2015)

File.Name: f508a_f508-004.jpg
Notes:
17253843539 This is a neat little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera) on a flower of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. There are both nocturnal and diurnal kinds of these moths - night and day. I suspect that this one is diurnal to be in such a visible place, but I'm not sure. There are many different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2015)

This was my only keeper photo today. It was dark and gloomy with a breeze. We ended up with 2/10 inch of rain last night and today. Not much, but it can't do any harm this close to our summer dry season. This bring our total up to 17.7 inches, 54% of normal. That better than the 12.05 inches we had last year, but still much less than our normal rainfall of 34.5 inches. This ongoing drought is getting me down.

File.Name: f508b_f506-021.jpg
Notes:
17439017175 Here are two photos of the same Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly perched on California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica, Rhamnaceae). It was a cold day, and I found this butterfly leaning over towards the sun. After a while, it opened its wings. A moment later, it flew away. I'm sure it was trying to get away from me! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2015)

The Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly is very similar, but lacks the chevrons along its wing margins. Dang, this one had me confused for a while!
CommonNames: Spring Azure; California Coffeeberry; Silvery Blue

File.Name: f508c_f504-003.jpg
Notes: 17438621545 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an unusual perennial composite with an interesting common name. It has heads of pretty two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. I reckon it's a fire-follower - appearing after wildfire or brush clearing, and disappearing after a few years until the next disturbance comes along. I found this one in a place that was burned by the White Fire almost two years ago in May 2013. Last year I found the first flowers on June 22. That's how it is this year - everything is early. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 May 2015)

File.Name: f508d_f507-024.jpg
Notes:
17249691520 This is an unusual "Timema" insect - the only one I managed to photograph yesterday. I'm sure it's Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida. It's an atypical walkingstick. This one is a green striped female, an inch or so long. The males are smaller. They also come in brown forms. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2015)

While driving home from my walk yesterday, I saw someone with an insect net beating the native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) with an insect net below. I stopped and asked if he was looking for "Timema" insects, and he was! (Sorry, I forget his name.) We had a pleasant talk while he sorted out his catch. I got this photo as the critter was running away from me. They were surprising agile for such a cold day.

BugGuide has an informative info page about these interesting creatures. And Chris Sandoval - the chris in the species name cristinae - has her own interesting Timema webpage.

I don't really approve of beating the shrubs to find insects, but it does work.
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae of the Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; chris; cristinae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae of the Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; chris; cristinae

File.Name: f508e_f501-029.jpg
Notes:
16816813213 This is another photo of a remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) - probably Geminaria canalis. It has a long sharp proboscis and it's built like a jet plane. What's most remarkable are those dark "eye spots" on its back that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. Is that camouflage for when it's digging into a flower with its butt in the air? See my [Previous] photo and this one for more photos of this remarkable fly.

This one is perched on a composite flower (Asteraceae) that I can't identify yet. It's a subshrub that grows near rock outcrops in the desert that reminds me of our summer-flowering California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) at home, though that one is not reported here. HFDF! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 1 May 2015)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Jumping Spider; California Aster
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae; Asteraceae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geminaria canalis; Salticidae; Asteraceae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia

This one is perched on a composite flower (Asteraceae) that I can't identify yet. It's a subshrub that grows near rock outcrops in the desert that reminds me of our summer-flowering California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) at home, though that one is not reported here.
