File.Name: f509a_f509-022.jpg
Notes:
16851300073 I found this large Pacific Gopher Snake (Pituophis catenifer) lying across East Camino Cielo road today. I stopped and herded it off the road. It's quite scary looking with that triangular head! I had a good look at the whole snake stretched across the road, and I'm sure it's a Gopher Snake, but it's a feisty beast! It shook its tail like a real rattlesnake, and even struck at me (or my camera) a good two feet. After I got it off the road, it posed for me before going away up the hill. Some gopher snakes make good pets in captivity, but not this one! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2015)

I drove up into the higher mountains today - probably the wrong way to go, as it was gloomy and windy. The sun was out when I got back home.

*Sigh* Flickr is making changes again...

* That ugly Yahoo! menu bar is gone - yahoo!

* I hope they provide some way to opt out of auto-tagging. I take some pride in my tags, and these auto-generated tags are worthless.

* I prefer black backgrounds on my photostream page, but I only see it when I upload. I have a bookmark for "http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/?details=1" that I use instead.

* The Flickr "home page" is pretty useless, with photos too large that don't always connect. Fortunately the [People] link still works well.

* I'm worried about the new "Uploadr". Why would I ever want to upload thousands of photos at one time? What will that do for bandwidth?

File.Name: f509b_f509-021.jpg
Notes: 17471037625 I found this large Pacific Gopher Snake (Pituophis catenifer) lying across East Camino Cielo road today. I stopped and herded it off the road. I had a good look at the whole snake stretched across the road, and I'm sure it's a Gopher Snake, but it's a feisty beast! It shook its tail like a real rattlesnake, and even struck at me (or my camera) a good two feet! It sure seems to have a triangular head, which is the best sign our venomous snakes. After I got it off the road, it posed for me before going away up the hill. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2015)

File.Name: f509c_f509-002.jpg
Notes: 17468725702 These are new flowers of native Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. It's another fire-follower plant in our mountains, but it also grows along mountain roads in disturbed places. They are real snapdragons - you can "snap" the flowers. Compare this old photo to see what they can do in a better year! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2015)

File.Name: f509d_f509-011.jpg
Notes: 17282675110 Here are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's growing with an interesting background, including a small Yucca and Late-flowered Mariposa Lily in the upper-left. It's beautiful, though the plant itself - below the yucca - doesn't look so hot. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2015)
CommonNames: Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Yucca; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f509e_f509-012.jpg
Notes:
17282379120 Beauty and the weeds...

This is a new flowering plant of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family growing with the weeds along East Camino Cielo road today. I did some weeding before I took this photo. For some reason, these beautiful plants seem to prefer roadsides. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2015)

File.Name: f509f_f509-006.jpg
Notes:
17467319092 Darn! The deer have munched all the flower stalks of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family in this place. There will be some side-stalks I hope, but every main stem is eaten. The same thing has been happening in our garden. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 May 2015)

I believe those seedlings on the ground are all native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. You can see a stem of the parent shrub in the background.
ScientificName: Delphinium cardinale; Ranunculaceae; Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Delphinium cardinale; Ranunculaceae; Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: f510a_f510-001.jpg
Notes:
17321503380 Here is a not-quite-mating pair of Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) in tandem, working a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden. Photos of insects in mating position are the one time you can clearly see the difference between male and female. I reckon that's the white-maned male on the left and the all-black female on the right. They are not actually "doing it" - but that male is certainly thinking about it! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2015)

Today was just about a perfect day - sunny and warm, but not too hot (about 82∞ on the porch), and hardly a breeze. For some reason I didn't find many butterflies or other insects on this perfect day. I guess the cold and gloomy week before set them back. Maybe tomorrow.
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: f510b_f510-020.jpg
Notes: 17508652385 Here are lots of new flowers of Mexican Pink aka Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. laciniata) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family, growing on a sandstone outcrop. This subspecies has narrow leaves. I don't usually see so many flowers at one time. The species name "laciniata" sounds like "lacerate" - irregularly slashed and jagged as if torn. Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse in his later years. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2015)

File.Name: f510c_f510-014.jpg
Notes: 16887977303 These are the real first flowers of native composite California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. (I want to try to forget about those flowers I found on April 3, see this photo!) I think of these as summer flowers, and the month of May is a much more reasonable time to find them. Jeffrey Caldwell has records of 25 species of butterflies visiting the flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2015)

File.Name: f510d_f510-029.jpg
Notes: 17321672169 This is a whole plant of native Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family in full flower. It's a perennial that grows from a woody caudex. It's another fire-follower plant in our mountains, but it also grows along mountain roads in disturbed places. See this closer shot of the flowers from yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2015)

File.Name: f510e_f510-016.jpg
Notes: 17504975352 This is uncommon native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family - with a new flower stalk. The basal leaves will completely shrivel up by the time they flower in a month or two. See this photo for the early basal leaves and this photo for the flowers. I saw early flower stalks last year, but few flowers. I hope they make it this year. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2015)

File.Name: f510f_f510-031.jpg
Notes: 17506570321 These are tiny new acorns on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) - scarcely more than pistillate flowers. There's already a black acorn at the bottom that I suspect is galled. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2015)

File.Name: f511a_f511-014.jpg
Notes:
17353467799 This beautiful "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It's perched in a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae), but its just starting to leave because I pestered it so. You can see its long proboscis folded under its head. See my [Previous] photo, outside the light tent. The nymphs are remarkable ant-mimics, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2015)

Today was nice enough, but cooler and breezier than yesterday. There were high clouds that made white balance difficult. The insects seem to be coming out, but the weather will turn cool again soon, with a chance of rain at the end of the week.
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f511b_f511-015.jpg
Notes: 16916234834 I believe this beautiful "true bug" is Closterocoris amoenus in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It's perched on a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae). You can see its long proboscis folded under its head. I pestered it enough to drive it away from the throat of the flower where it belongs, see my [Next] photo. The nymphs are remarkable ant-mimics, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2015)

File.Name: f511c_f511-028.jpg
Notes: 17511828446 This is a humble non-native Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly working a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. I believe this is a female, with two dark spots on its forewing. It's not an exciting butterfly, but it has its charms and this is the first one I've managed to capture this year. It was really interested in the Black Sage nearby, but I drove it away by getting too close. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2015)
CommonNames: Cabbage White; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Black Sage

File.Name: f511d_f511-012.jpg
Notes: 17535206312 This is a clutch of tiny nymphs of Lace Bugs (Tingidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The whole leaf is less than an inch long. These tiny bugs do some damage to the leaves. See this photo for the remarkable adult. They seem quite host-specific. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2015)
ScientificName: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Tingidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f511e_f511-030.jpg
Notes:
16916406833 I found this interesting caterpillar by the creek today. I believe it's preparing to form a chrysalis on this shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) by the creek today. I believe it's the caterpillar of a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly. I guess those small Buckeye's in our canyon aren't all males! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2015)

I'm almost more interested in that tiny wasp on the leaf below. I wonder what it's waiting for...

File.Name: f511f_f511-034.jpg
Notes:
17509698116 I found this interesting white caterpillar snuggled into the pocket of a dead leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) today. The photo reminds me of our cats, who also like to snuggle. I assume it's Lepidoptera, but I have no idea what it will become. It's fairly large, not quite pencil-thick. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2015)

I think it's remarkable how many butterflies and moths we have in our mountains - and how little I know about most of their caterpillars!

File.Name: f513a_f513-011.jpg
Notes:
17620871125 I believe this is an Orange Sulfur (Colias eurytheme) butterfly, with a dark margin on the upper side of its wings - but cp. the Clouded Sulfur (Colias philodice). This one is working new flowers of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. I got this photo a full stop underexposed, but you can brighten a photo much better than you can darken it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2015)

This was the only butterfly I saw on this cool and breezy day with clouds moving in for our good chance of rain tomorrow and the next day. The NWS is starting to back off from its original forecast, but I hope we get something. We need it!

I couldn't upload to Flickr last night, and I couldn't even connect this morning. But everything seems back to normal tonight. I hope it stays that way. I was sorry to miss yesterday - I had my photos all lined up and ready to go.
ScientificName: Colias eurytheme; Colias philodice; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Colias eurytheme; Colias philodice; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: f513b_f513-008.jpg
Notes: 17433803609 This is the first new flower head of a native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata aka Monardella breweri ssp. lanceolata) plant in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in a sandy place along the Santa Ynez River. The flowers are beautiful with those speckled petals that have little balls on the tips. This plant has an intense peppermint smell that is shocking when you walk through a patch of it without knowing the source. It seems to be uncommon here. This is one of my favorite genera of plants, that really comes into its own on the dunes near Lompoc and Guadalupe. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2015)
ScientificName: Monardella lanceolata; Monardella breweri; lanceolata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Monardella lanceolata; Monardella breweri; lanceolata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: f513c_f513-019.jpg
Notes: 16999320813 This is looking close at a single flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's growing on the burn of the White Fire almost two years ago. The flowers are making white pollen. It looks a bit like a sea anemone. These plants grow large after a burn, 6-8 feet, sometimes more - but this plant was small enough to look down on. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2015)
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle

File.Name: f513d_f513-013.jpg
Notes: 17619124205 This is the whole inflorescence of a native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's growing on the burn of the White Fire almost two years ago. These plants grow large after a burn, 6-8 feet, sometimes more. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2015)
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle

File.Name: f513e_f513-002.jpg
Notes: 17616558412 These are new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an unusual perennial composite with an interesting common name. It has heads of pretty two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. I reckon it's a fire-follower - appearing after wildfire or brush clearing, and disappearing after a few years until the next disturbance comes along. I found this one in a place that was burned by the White Fire almost two years ago. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2015)

File.Name: f513f_f512-014.jpg
Notes:
17430802930 These are pretty new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. I think of this as a weed in ranch country. I had errand in the Santa Ynez Valley yesterday, and got this photo along the way. I didn't see any Milkweed Bugs or Tarantula Hawks yet, but this is just the beginning of their season. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 May 2015)

It's been tough to have Flickr lately. I couldn't upload last night, and I couldn't even connect this morning. I managed to upload this photo after several tries this afternoon, but I'm not sure if I can get another one through. I'm not happy.

File.Name: f514a_f514-014.jpg
Notes:
17658228665 "What, sowbugs again?"

This House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird is bringing Sowbugs to feed her babies. Actually this baby look almost ready to leave the nest! Now how does the parent manage to get two sowbugs at a time? It was drizzly this morning, so I set up my tripod under our front porch and spent some time watching the wrens. We have four different active nests, mostly at the corners of our house. This nest is the most advanced. The wrens are better at finding bugs than I am! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2015)

There was light rain and drizzle this morning, but it didn't amount to much. But then the rain picked up in the afternoon, and we ended up with a bit over 4/10 inch - so far. It's been unusually cold for May, we never got above the mid-40s today. We have a good chance of more rain tonight and tomorrow. Bring it on!

I have to admit that Flickr is working well tonight. I like my new photostream page with the white background - but also with smaller photos that have borders. I don't like how I can't click on a photo I've just uploaded on the page until it's fully loaded. I usually click back to my "?details=1" page. But that's a minor annoyance.

File.Name: f514b_f514-006.jpg
Notes:
17631251026 These nesting House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) are better at finding bugs than I am right now - but they have hungry babies to feed! Actually these babies look almost ready to leave the nest. From left to right, the parent has a crane fly, a spider, and a cricket. The parents arrive every couple of minutes with a new snack. It's great fun to watch! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2015)

It was drizzly this morning, so I set up my tripod under our front porch and spent some time watching the wrens. They collected more critters including caterpillars and grubs, but mostly sowbugs. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: f514c_f514-003.jpg
Notes: 17469149090 This is looking close at some kind of Pillbug (Armadillidiidae, Oniscidea, Isopoda, Crustacea) - maybe Armadillidium vulgare. This is the kind of woodlouse that can roll up into a ball or pill. ("Take two and call me back!") I found this one out in the drizzle this morning. Here it is on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) with raindrops. It's surprisingly handsome for such a common critter. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2015)
ScientificName: Armadillidiidae; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Armadillidium vulgare; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Armadillidiidae; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Armadillidium vulgare; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f514d_f514-009.jpg
Notes: 17629840986 This is a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family - with raindrops today! If you look into the throat of the flower, you can see the triangular flap of the stigma hanging down like an epiglottis. This stigma is touch sensitive. It's great fun when hiking with kids to tickle the open stigma with a grass blade, and watch it close. It happens in seconds! (See this PDF.) (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2015)
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f514e_a719-100.jpg
Notes: 17653009292 This is a remarkable Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) - probably Geminaria canalis. This is an old photo from the archives (July 2010), but I've been thinking about it since we found the same fly in the desert near Lone Pine a couple of weeks ago. It has real eyes on the left, but what's most remarkable are those dark "eye spots" on its back that reflect light just like Jumping Spider (Salticidae) eyes. I reckon that's camouflage. This fly digs into flowers with its long proboscis, and sticks its butt in the air - and it sure looks like a long-nosed creature from above. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 July 2010)

File.Name: f514f_f504-023.jpg
Notes: 17467225990 This is an especially handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly perched for a moment of a leaf of native Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla, Labiatae) by the river a week ago. I couldn't see under its wings, so I won't try to guess what kind it is. It's also a pretty good "butt shot" - HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 May 2015)

File.Name: f515a_f515-006.jpg
Notes:
17519774688 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family - with raindrops. This one is growing in our canyon at home. They are beautiful flowers that belong here, but they've been having a hard time with our extended drought. The plants are hanging on. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2015)

Today started clear in the early morning, but rain-looking clouds soon moved in. We did have a few rain drops, though not enough to measure. It continues to be cool, too cold for insects. The winds kicked up this evening. I hope the winds are done tomorrow.

File.Name: f515b_f515-005.jpg
Notes: 17680655576 This is the first flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family that I've seen in the canyon this year. I think they are especially beautiful when they are just starting to open. See this photo for flowers and this photo for whole plants. I never saw a flower last year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2015)

File.Name: f515c_f515-004.jpg
Notes: 17703355012 This is a galled leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe it's the work of a Midrib Gall Moth (Sorhagenia nimbosa) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the Lepidoptera. This is the first (and so far, the only) one I've found this year. It's a real gall, not just a leaf-roller. You can see how the cell structure of the leaf is changed. This shrub has buds, but no flowers yet. Other shrubs in the canyon are in full flower. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2015)
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: f515d_f515-007.jpg
Notes: 17704858105 I believe this is the tumor-like gall of a Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia californica) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. I don't think I've ever seen this one before in the mountains, but see this old photo at Fotolog, taken at Morro Bay in 2008. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2015)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: f515e_f515-009.jpg
Notes: 17516264398 This is the beautiful chrysalis of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. It's hanging from a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) - in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. That's one of the plants the caterpillars favor. These caterpillars like to change plants when they pupate, but this one didn't travel very far. You can see a leaf of the monkeyflower below it. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2015)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Wild Sweetpea; Southern Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: f515f_f514-019.jpg
Notes:
17082655873 "We fill up our guts,
and we turn it into shit,
then we get rid of it..."
- "Surf Song", "Have Moicy" (Michael Hurley etc.), 1976

Here is one last photo of the House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) in my [Previous] photos. After bringing in all those tasty bugs for the babies, they have to take out the poop! I'm in awe of how hard these dedicated parent birds work to raise their young. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2015)

File.Name: f516a_f516-039.jpg
Notes:
17755257811 Here is a mating pair of Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterflies having their nuptials on a new flower bud of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. I'm sorry they weren't showing the upper sides of their wings, which are gorgeous. They look like Blues at first, but they are larger and the colors patterns are slightly different. I spotted these butterflies about five feet down a steep embankment along the road today. I couldn't have got this photo but for the recent rain that loosened the soil just enough for a foothold. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)

Today started sunny and calm, but it turned cloudy and breezy in the middle of my walk today. The clouds seemed to come from nowhere. It was still a good photo day - butterflies, a wasp, a mushroom, a native plant in flower, and a new gall!

File.Name: f516b_f516-013.jpg
Notes: 17754359025 This is a two-fer of interesting butterflies working new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The upper one is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) with that white dot on the forewing, and the lower one is a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini). What a combination! I know, the photo isn't quite in focus. I had to extend my tripod legs, get in position - one quick shot and they were gone. But sharpness isn't everything! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Vanessa virginensis; Basilarchia lorquini
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Vanessa virginensis; Basilarchia lorquini

File.Name: f516c_f516-030.jpg
Notes: 17567248569 I first thought this was a small Carrot Wasp (family Gasteruptiidae) of the order Hymenoptera, but bleu.geo corrected me - see his comment below. It's not Gasteruptiidae, but subfamily Campopleginae of the family Ichneumonidae. As usual, the details seem pretty obvious once pointed out. This one has slender hind tibea rather than enlarged, and a low placement of the abdomen on the thorax. There's some interesting info at the BugGuide info page which I don't have time to digest right now. I found this one spread out on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) today when I was looking for galls. The weather had turned cloudy and cold, but the sun was just coming out. In a moment the wasp was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)

File.Name: f516d_f516-044.jpg
Notes:
17565785889 Well blow me down... (as Popeye used to say),
that 4/10 inch of rain two days ago was enough to bring up a mushroom!

And not just any mushroom... I believe this is the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom. It has yellow-green colors on its smooth and polished cap, a ring or partial-veil skirt on the stem, and a saclike volva at the base. This one was quite large - not as large as my foot, but at least 8 inches long. I expect to find these in the fall, this is the first one I've seen in the late spring. This mushroom is responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. Admire it because it's beautiful, but don't eat it! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous! There is some evidence that this mushroom has been introduced from Europe. It doesn't seem likely as I found this deep in the woods, but I suppose tiny mushroom spores can travel around the planet.

File.Name: f516e_f516-019.jpg
Notes:
17128803164 These are new flowers of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. These shrubs are free-standing, though they have twining tendencies and sometimes climb up into neighboring plants. They look quite different from the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata), see this photo. And they are very different from the California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula), see this photo. Today was mostly cloudy - just right for these delicate flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)

For some reason, these wide-leaved plants are more common east of San Marcos Pass, while the narrow-leaved Santa Barbara Honeysuckle is more common west of the pass. I have no idea why.
CommonNames: Chaparral Honeysuckle; Santa Barbara Honeysuckle; California Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Lonicera hispidula
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Lonicera hispidula

File.Name: f516f_f516-026.jpg
Notes: 17563109440 These are interesting new galls on a bud of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). They sure seem to be growing from the cup of that tiny acorn between them, which is scarcely more than a pistillate flower. The galls are a bit larger than garden peas. Ron Russo doesn't mention this one in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), nor does Joyce Gross. I haven't seen these before, and I only found them on one particular shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)

File.Name: f517a_f517-018.jpg
Notes:
17612702398 This is a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on a flower cluster of native Turkish Rigging (Chorizanthe staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Up close, both fly and flower are handsome. D'oh - I got this photo right after my [Previous] photo when I had reduced my ISO and aperture, and I forgot to set it back. It was silly to try to get this photo at f/16 on a cloudy day, but I took lots of photos and got this one keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2015)

It was cold and gloomy and breezy all day today, a crummy day for photos. As warm as it's been this year, this has been a cold month of May. I took quite a few, but deleted most of them. But I got a few nice ones!

File.Name: f517b_f517-017.jpg
Notes: 17773385276 This is a whole plant of native Turkish Rigging (Chorizanthe staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I think of this as a summer flower, but I've found them before in early May. Note that the basal leaves are totally shriveled. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2015)

File.Name: f517c_f517-016.jpg
Notes:
17175710394 This is an interesting gall on an interesting oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) in our mountains. I don't think I've ever seen a gall like it on an oak. It forms a pouch on the leaves, with red colors that remind me of manzanita galls. You can see a couple of exit holes from the gall, so it must be "polythalamous" - containing more than one larvae. The exit hole reminds me of a Cynipid Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2015)

This oak grows as a "scrub oak" or (in this case) a copse of small trees. It's been called many names - Quercus pacifica or Quercus wislizenii or Quercus parvula or Quercus parvula var. shrevei or Quercus shrevei. See the discussion with this photo. I think I prefer Quercus pacifica as a name, though that one usually grows on the Channel Islands as the Island Scrub Oak. It looks like a Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but the leaves are flat and shiny underneath; without "hairy armpits"; the catkins are longer, more airy, and pinker; and the plants appear to be semi-deciduous - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once. This small tree gets "sucker growth" on its trunk, which may be a clue.

"By their galls you will know them." But I've never seen this gall before, so it's no help.
CommonNames: oak; Cynipid Wasp; Island Scrub Oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus pacifica; Quercus wislizenii; Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus shrevei; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus pacifica; Quercus wislizenii; Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus shrevei; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: f517d_f516-021.jpg
Notes:
17794699412 These are tiny gall capsules on leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. A couple of them on the right have emerged. They sure look like the spring bisexual generation of the California Jumping Gall Wasp (Neuoterus saltatorius) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I don't think I've ever noticed the adult galls in our mountains. They are said to fall to the ground in early autumn, and jump around like tiny "jumping beans" from the action of the larvae inside. Sounds pretty cool, I'll be looking for them. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)

On the other hand, they might be the spring generation of some other kind of gall. I don't know.
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; California Jumping Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Neuoterus saltatorius; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Neuoterus saltatorius; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: f517e_f516-031.jpg
Notes: 17797454881 I believe this is a new gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Galls are induced by a some critter, but the plant makes them, and they typically have structures like hairs that are characteristic of the plants they are on. But these galls look totally different from the oak leaf! They are beautiful and strange. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f517f_f516-024.jpg
Notes: 17796541585 On the right is another odd gall of the Fimbriate Gall Wasp (Andricus opertaus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). If you look close on the screen-right side of the top acorn cup on the left, you can see the tiny spider that made those webs! I didn't notice it when I got the photo. See this photo for more info about the gall. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2015)

File.Name: f518a_f518-021.jpg
Notes:
17655224678 "Casting a line..."

I believe this is a handsome little Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly positioning its proboscis into a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. These beautiful flowers really are butterfly magnets. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2015)

The sun came out this morning and it was a bit warmer. A few butterflies were out! There was a persistent little breeze that kept everything moving, but it could have been worse.

File.Name: f518b_f518-035.jpg
Notes: 17220212724 This is a small Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) gathering pollen from a stamen of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. It was breezy and the plant was swinging to and fro. I took lots of photos and got this one right at the end of one of its swings. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2015)

File.Name: f518c_f518-017.jpg
Notes: 17841617615 Here are two photos of a remarkable moth (Lepidoptera) that I found on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's small, only about 1/4 inch long, but quite beautiful with that yellow coat and iridescent dress. It was spinning 'round and 'round, like a puppy chasing its tail. I believe it's an uncommon Rectiostoma fernaldella moth in the family Stenomatidae of the superfamily Gelechiodea. I found it in the book "Moths of Western North America" (Powell and Opler, UCPress, 2009), but then on BugGuide as Hodges#1027. It's my impression that this moth is rarely seen. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2015)

File.Name: f518d_f518-041.jpg
Notes: 17220764833 This is a small California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working a new flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family by the mailbox. It's a pretty little critter. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2015)

File.Name: f518e_f518-012.jpg
Notes: 17654404559 I found this tiny but perfect Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) perched on the tip of a leaf in the mint patch of our garden this morning. I guess it's a tiny Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, so I can use my camera as an expensive remote micrometer. The critter is about 1370 pixels across. That makes it about 5.4 mm across, less than 1/4 inch. See this photo for how I figured. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2015)

File.Name: f518f_f517-001.jpg
Notes: 17220063513 This is a neat little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera) hanging under a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. It was a cold morning, and this little moth must have been trying to warm up. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2015)

File.Name: f519a_f519-028.jpg
Notes:
17879784075 This is a neat photo showing female and male Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterflies side-by-side on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. That's the female on the left and the male on the right. They look very different. Maybe they get their common name from the strikingly different female, but she doesn't look at all snake-like. It's nice to see them both together. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)

It was mostly sunny today, but breezy and cool. I went up into the higher mountains along East Camino Cielo road.

Damn, there's a new oil spill at Refugio Beach...

File.Name: f519b_f519-035.jpg
Notes: 17691924830 I guess this is a pretty Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. But that's only a guess. We also have Lupine Blues (P. lupini) and Square-spotted Blues (Euphilotes battoides bernardino) that are very similar. I never did get a look under its wings to check for speckles. I don't care. It's a beautiful butterfly! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Golden Yarrow; Lupine Blues; Square-spotted Blues

File.Name: f519c_f519-019.jpg
Notes: 17691059718 This is looking closer at leaves and a flower head of locally uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. See my [Previous] photo. This shrub has long odd-pinnate leaves with many leaflets. The indigo flowers are remarkable, and hardly look like legumes at all. The shrub really looks like it belongs in the desert, along with mesquite and acacia. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)

File.Name: f519d_f519-018.jpg
Notes:
17255607284 This is a locally uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family, in full flower. D'oh - I've probably driven right past this shrub hundreds of times, and I didn't notice it until today. It's also scattered along the Santa Ynez River, but it's never common. The shrub has a light and airy look, like mesquite or acacia in the desert. Here it is in the mountain chaparral. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot of the interesting flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)

Many sources repeat that this is the only food plant of the California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly, but I'm sure that's wrong. This shrub is uncommon, and the butterflies are quite common later in the summer. They must have other host plants.

File.Name: f519e_f519-009.jpg
Notes:
17254976004 These are the first new flowers of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. You can see the white flower mostly hidden by the green bracts. Like the Senecio in my [Previous] photo, this is another plant I think of as having summer flowers - but all of our flowers have been early this year, so I'm not surprised to see them. I didn't post my first photo of them last year until mid-July! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)

I guess this early flowering is the work of our unusually warm winter this year. It's like the timetable has been shifted forward a month or two. I am wondering what will be flowering in late summer and autumn!

File.Name: f519f_f519-042.jpg
Notes:
17256735633 These are the first new flowers of Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family, growing in the higher mountains. I think of these as summer and even autumn flowers - but all of our flowers have been early this year, so I'm not surprised to see them. I didn't post my first photo of them last year until July! These flowers are great butterfly magnets. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)

I am wondering what will be flowering in late summer and autumn!
ScientificName: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: f520a_f520-018.jpg
Notes:
17296760383 This is a beautiful little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched with wings open, trying to warm up on a cool morning. We've had chilly weather all week. I've been getting lots of butterfly photos, but not so many other insects. I wonder if it's because the butterflies have large wings to catch more warmth from the sun? Of course it may just be that they are easier to see! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2015)

It was another cool morning, with the sun going in and out of the clouds. Every time it changed, the wind seemed to pick up. Even with the sun out, it's pretty cool for May, with highs only in the 60s.

File.Name: f520b_f520-012.jpg
Notes: 17728867228 This is another pretty Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly trying to warm up on a leaf in the woods. At least it's some kind of Blue (Polyommatinae, Lepidoptera) - it's a confusing group and I couldn't see under the wings of this one either. It has its wings open, trying to warm up on a cool morning. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2015)

File.Name: f520c_f520-027.jpg
Notes: 17730223149 This is a perfect Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", "Lamiaceae) in the garden, with the mint patch behind. The butterfly looks newly emerged, which makes sense because I haven't seen them for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2015)

File.Name: f520d_f520-019.jpg
Notes: 17295559693 This is another beautiful Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2015)

File.Name: f520e_f520-005.jpg
Notes: 17916045951 This is the chrysalis of the caterpillar in this photo, on a shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) by the creek today. You can see its old skin rolled up on top, and its new wings, antennae, and eyes at the bottom. In that previous photo I guessed that it might be a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly because I thought I saw some blue at the base of the spines, and maybe it is. The other alternative is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona), but there weren't any scrophs growing for quite a ways. I guess time will tell. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2015)
CommonNames: Bitter Gooseberry; Common Buckeye; Variable Checkerspot

associatedTaxa: Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Junonia coenia; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: f520f_f519-008.jpg
Notes:
17728520929 Here is a big truck on a narrow mountain road... driving through a forest of non-native (and invasive)Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum, Fabaceae). I won't deny that the shrub is rather pretty, but it doesn't belong here. But how do you get rid of an infestation like this? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)

The big trucks are coming from the Bee Rock Quarry in the mountains above Lake Cachuma. They are delivering quarry rock all the way across the mountains to a construction project on Gibraltor Road. Watch out for trucks if you're on East Camino Cielo! That's lake Cachuma in the background.

File.Name: f521a_f521-002.jpg
Notes:
17927470696 This is the remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a Closterocoris amoenus "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. You can see its long proboscis folded under its head. This one is perched in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae). The orange flower acts as a natural light tent, looking bright on a gloomy day. This nymph helps the illusion of having an ant-waist by having a light green belly. It's a remarkable mimic. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2015)

I mostly find these insects inside Monkey Flowers, but not always. See my response to Jeffrey's comment on this photo - which also shows the adult.

If gloom were rain, we wouldn't be in a drought this year! It was gloomy and chilly all day today, with just enough breeze to make photos difficult. There is a 20% chance of rain tonight, but I'm not holding my breath. (I guess I will close the car windows!)
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f521b_f521-021.jpg
Notes: 17330879974 This is a new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing deep in our canyon. They are just starting to flower. These plants have maple-like palmate leaves which are quite different from our common Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). Thimbleberries are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are quite uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I guess it's a relict plant in our mountains from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change, but our ongoing drought will be a real test. I've never found the berries here. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2015)

File.Name: f521c_f521-012.jpg
Notes:
17952346945 Here is a collage of native plants of Bedstraw (genus Galium) in the Rubiacae plant family. All have leaves in whorls of four, and small 4-petaled flowers. Clockwise from the top-left, here are:

* California Bedstraw (Galium californicum has dull leaves, and seems to be the last of these to flower. There is also a tiny-leaved form that seems to be distinct.
* Phlox-leaved Bedstraw (Galium andrewii) is an unusual "suffrutescent perennial" that forms low mounds in the woods.
* Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) has shiny leaves that are quite attractive. It's less of a climber that the previous two species.
* Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) is more of a free-standing plant that is woody at the base. This plant is dioecious with separate male and female plants.

Missing from this collage is the San Diego Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) which has already finished flowering. See this photo. And of course there is the non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) that has become part of our flora.

Plants in the genus Galium are a kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some are bisexual and others are unisexual, and some have berries and others have dry seeds. They are one of my favorite genera - even if I get confused by them! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2015)
CommonNames: Bedstraw; California Bedstraw; Phlox-leaved Bedstraw; Climbing Bedstraw; Narrowleaf Bedstraw; San Diego Bedstraw; Common Bedstraw
ScientificName: Galium; Rubiacae; Galium californicum; Galium andrewii; Galium porrigens; Galium angustifolium; Galium nuttallii; Galium aparine; Galium
associatedTaxa: Galium; Rubiacae; Galium californicum; Galium andrewii; Galium porrigens; Galium angustifolium; Galium nuttallii; Galium aparine; Galium

File.Name: f521d_f521-009.jpg
Notes: 17952623951 This is a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. We rescued the plant from a slide along Paradise Road many years ago, and it's done very well here. We never water it. It's growing out from the wall and weighed down by a growth of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). I've been worried that the flower stems would break under the weight, so they are hanging on so far. The leaves have a strong camphor smell. They make good smudge sticks and have a reputation for quieting the dead. Maybe so! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2015)
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f521e_f518-003.jpg
Notes: 17329050224 Here is a common Honeybee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) "butts up" in a native flower of White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden. See my [Next] photo. The flowers start out folded up tight, but heavy Carpenter Bees (Anthophoridae) or Bumble Bees (Bombus) can lever them open. Once opened, they stay open, and the honeybees can get in. I'll try to get a photo tomorrow showing both open and closed flowers. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2015)
CommonNames: Honeybee; White Sage; Carpenter Bees; Bumble Bees

associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Anthophoridae; Bombus

File.Name: f521f_f519-040.jpg
Notes: 17951932391 This is a "butt shot" of the same Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly in this photo a couple of days ago. It's working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2015)

File.Name: f522a_f522-004.jpg
Notes:
17367195403 This is a closer shot of the little "looper caterpillar" - a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) - visible in my [Previous] photo on the back stamen of the left flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's finished munching the stamen, and is moving on to the petal. Is that a sign of its preferences - first the pistil, then the stamens, finally the petals? (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2015)

About the Refugio Oil Spill...

The authorities have been keeping a close rein on the media, and keeping all interested people away from the site. (I guess they've learned from the Gulf War.) But see this photo set by Santa Barbara photographer and Flickr person John Wiley.

Julie went to the Coal Oil Point Reserve today to act as a volunteer docent. She reports that there is no oil on the beach other than the usual seepage, but they did find an oiled sea lion on the beach. A NOAA team finally arrived and took it away. I wonder how far that sea lion swam, covered with oil as it was?
Locality:
I guess they've learned from the Gulf War.) But see this photo set by Santa Barbara photographer and Flickr person John Wiley.

Julie went to the Coal Oil Point Reserve today to act as a volunteer docent. She reports that there is no oil on the beach other than the usual seepage

File.Name: f522b_f522-003.jpg
Notes:
17987677071 Here are "closed" and "open" flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flowers start out in a closed position, like the flower on the left. It takes a heavy bee like a Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa) or Bumble Bee (Bombus) to lever them open. Once opened, they stay open. The Honey Bees can't get in until the larger bees get there first. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2015)

The blue-colored pistil of the flower also does a little dance as the flower matures. It starts off to the side when the flower is closed, then moves into the center when the flower is open. You can see the pistil in the center of the flower on the right, but the one of the left going to the side has been eaten.

Hah - did you notice that little caterpillar on the rear stamen of the flower on the left? I didn't either until after I got my photo, but then I investigated. See my [Next] photo!
CommonNames: White Sage; Carpenter Bee; Bumble Bee

File.Name: f522c_f522-012.jpg
Notes: 17987044911 Here is the first flower I've seen this year of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. An orange strand of California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae plant family is just starting to get a strangle hold on it. Many small insects - beetles and little diurnal moths - like these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2015)
ScientificName: Erigeron foliosus; Asteraceae; Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae
associatedTaxa: Erigeron foliosus; Asteraceae; Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae

File.Name: f522d_f520-009.jpg
Notes: 17799577959 Here are two photos of the same small Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) working the same new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum, Asteraceae) as my [Previous] photo. In the photo on the right, you can see it gathering pollen grains. It's a handsome little fly on a beautiful flower. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2015)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f522e_f518-022.jpg
Notes:
17799142979 This is a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family - in the sun with a dark shady canyon beyond. You can see why it's one of my favorite wildflowers. Note the tiny ants. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2015)

This is not a weed! If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. It would be a shame to pull one of these natives by mistake. If in doubt, leave it be!

File.Name: f522f_f521-010.jpg
Notes: 17797422710 This is a seedhead of native California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon at home. It's interesting. I got this photo yesterday on a gloomy day. I got another photo today in sunlight, but I prefer the earlier shot. I'm tired of the gloom, but it's not bad for photos! The cloudy gloom is good for reducing contrast, just right for these seeds and many other photos that suffer with contrasty light. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2015)

File.Name: f523a_f523-020.jpg
Notes:
17835728568 This is a little Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the woods. Its long antennae go from here to there! I guess this is a Bush Katydid (Scudderia), as the nymphs are more ornate than our other green Katydids. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2015)

It was chilly and gloomy all day yesterday, but it finally cleared after noon today. It remained breezy, but it did warm up to the low 70s - warmer by 10 degrees than its been all week. I have my hopes up for a fine photo day tomorrow.
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Scudderia
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Scudderia

File.Name: f523b_f523-011.jpg
Notes: 18024250841 This is another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly (I guess) working flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. Yeah, it's not quite in focus - but sharpness isn't everything! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2015)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: f523c_f523-004.jpg
Notes:
18019277712 Here are tiny insect grubs - maybe fly maggots (Diptera)? - munching the spores of a rust fungus (Pucciniales or Uredinales, Basidiomycotina). You can see them if you look close in the middle of the "hot dog gall" in my [Previous] photo. The tiny grubs/maggots look as happy as hogs in the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2015)

The little critters really are tiny - just 380 pixels long in this 1:1 macro photo. That comes to about 1.5 mm long. See this photo for how I figured.

File.Name: f523d_f523-005.jpg
Notes: 17399541754 This is a rust fungus (Pucciniales or Uredinales, Basidiomycotina) making a hot-dog-on-a-stick-like gall on an old petiole of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's might be the rust fungus Pileolaria effusa, but I don't know anything else about it. Well, I do know one thing - insect grubs (maybe maggots) like it. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2015)
ScientificName: Pucciniales; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Pileolaria effusa
associatedTaxa: Pucciniales; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Pileolaria effusa

File.Name: f523e_f523-009.jpg
Notes: 18022082061 Maybe this small and hairy lupine is the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus) in the Fabaceae plant family? That's just a guess, I'm not at all sure of the ID. The leaflets are short and fat and quite hairy. This grows along the road in our canyon. If you have an idea about this, please let me know. So many lupines! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2015)

File.Name: f523f_f523-016.jpg
Notes: 17834749619 This is a shrub of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in full flower. The flowers are usually full of insects, but it's been chilly this morning so they're quiet. The flowers are plain but quite handsome in mass - and important to the pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2015)

File.Name: f524a_f524-023.jpg
Notes:
17876100698 This is a male Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly perched on a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae). This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

Today was a beautiful day! After the morning fog burned off, it was sunny and not too breezy. It's still cool for May, only in the 60s, but hopefully it will warm up over the next week.
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; California Bay

File.Name: f524b_f524-005.jpg
Notes: 18064665221 This is a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly rolling up its proboscis on a stem of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly looks plain brown from a distance, but if you look closer you can see that its color is more complicated. It's like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. It's one of my favorites. This must be a new brood. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; pointillist; Theclinea
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; pointillist; Theclinea

File.Name: f524c_f524-050.jpg
Notes: 17875856020 This is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly sitting on new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see just a bit of its rusty orange upper wings that are more obvious when it takes wing. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

File.Name: f524d_f524-045.jpg
Notes:
18063648501 This is a "belly shot" of a little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - maybe Zelus tetracanthus in the subfamily Harpactorinae. You can see its might proboscis folded under its head. I found this one perched on a flower of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I tried to nudge it into a better position, and it fell away into the grass stems below. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

I don't know much about Assassin Bug anatomy - but what is that round structure in the middle of its abdomen, like a belly button?
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Harpactorinae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Harpactorinae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f524e_f524-013.jpg
Notes:
18058923332 Here are photos of two different Bordered Plant Bugs (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. Probably they are both Largus californicus, but they sure look different! The one on the left is working flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family, and the one on the right is favoring native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

Identifying insects is hard enough without such variation!
CommonNames: Bordered Plant Bugs; Coffeeberry; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
