File.Name: f524f_f524-019.jpg
Notes: 18035173916 These are interesting "midrib galls" on leaves of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I reckon the gall on the right has completely taken over the leaf. They might be the galls of a Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae, Diptera). Ron Russo describes two other midge Ceanothus galls in his book "Plant Galls of California and Other Western States" by Ron Russo (UC Press, 2006) that favor midribs, but both are different from this. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

File.Name: f525a_f525-023.jpg
Notes:
18106853991 This is a large bee-size Fly (Diptera) working new flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. The fly looks like the Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae, but there there are no cacti nearby for miles and thousands of vertical feet. If not the same fly, I guess it's in the same Syrphidae family. It's stuffing its large proboscis into this small flower. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 May 2015)

Today started cold and gloomy. The morning fog finally broke after noon, but it never did warm up past the mid-60s. Normally I would go to the river on a day like this, but not on Memorial Day weekend. I went up into the higher mountains and got some nice photos.
CommonNames: Fly; Trask's Yerba Santa; Mexican Cactus Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Syrphidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae; Syrphidae

File.Name: f525b_f525-003.jpg
Notes: 17482268914 This is a honeybee-size Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) on flaky sandstone near the FAA tower on East Camino Cielo road, about 3600 feet. Maybe it's genus Paravilla? Compare this photo by Alice Abelia at BugGuide. The coastal marine layer fog was still in the air, so the white balance is a little funky. It's an impressive fly, with pollen-covered legs and a frosted body. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 May 2015)

File.Name: f525c_f525-017.jpg
Notes:
18104050985 I believe this is a Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) working a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. It's the plainest of our "plain brown hairstreaks" - and you mostly recognize it process of elimination. It doesn't have obvious white postmedian lines or rusty orange upper wings. This is the first one I've seen this year - at least the first one I recognized. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 May 2015)

I love that name - "gold-hunter's hairstreak". I wonder where it came from?

File.Name: f525d_f525-009.jpg
Notes: 18104625051 This is another male Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly on the ground, showing its copper-colored upper wings. Their host plant is the native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family that I show on my [Previous] photo. I always find them together. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 May 2015)
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry

File.Name: f525e_f525-014.jpg
Notes:
17482730853 These are spiky ripe berries of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are doing surprisingly well in this drought year. The berries look like the ocean mines in an old movie. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside. Tasty! (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 May 2015)

I'm pretty sure these shrubs are host plant of the Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly in my [Next] photo, though I've never found the caterpillars. We don't have these butterflies at home where we have different gooseberries.
CommonNames: Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Tailed Copper; Arota Copper

File.Name: f525f_f524-036.jpg
Notes: 18103430271 This photo doesn't quite show a gall on a gall, but it comes close. Here are two different galls growing on new acorns of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. On the right (and behind) are the odd squash-like galls I show in this photo. These galls look acorn-like, but they don't have the "pistil tips" that the real acorns have. I'm more interested in the capsule-like galls on the left. The round galls seem to grow from a square base-pad. I can't identify either of the galls, but ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

File.Name: f526a_f526-014.jpg
Notes:
18139861962 Here is a handsome little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) snuggled into a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flower is like a big feather bed! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2015)

Today started sunny, but the marine layer fog soon spilled over the mountains. The fog burned off before noon, but then another middle layer of clouds moved in to make it just as gloomy as the morning was. That's when I got this photo, as the bee was grounded by the cooler weather. The clouds also brought some wind.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f526b_f526-001.jpg
Notes: 18144088821 This is a little Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera), probably the nymph of a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). It's sitting in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae). I wonder if it's been munching the stigma of the flower? Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2015)
CommonNames: Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f526c_f526-004.jpg
Notes:
17956138329 This is another photo of the remarkable ant-mimic nymph of a Closterocoris amoenus "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. You can see its long proboscis folded under its head. This one is perched in the throat of a flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae). This nymph has a faux nodal spine on its back. It's a remarkable mimic. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2015)

I mostly find these insects inside Monkey Flowers, but not always. See my response to Jeffrey's comment on this photo - which also shows the adult.

This bug is hiding behind the triangular white flap of the stigma hanging down like an epiglottis in the throat of the flower. This stigma is touch sensitive. It's great fun when hiking with kids to tickle the open stigma with a grass blade, and watch it close. It happens in seconds! (See this PDF.)
ScientificName: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f526d_f526-013.jpg
Notes: 18138339692 This is an odd one for several reasons. First, I'm pretty sure this is the Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I've only found it in our canyon once before, years ago. Somehow, a seed got here. Second, it's unusually small. The whole plant is only about 2 inches tall. these plants can almost grow as tall as I am. Finally, it's growing on a rock that is covered with dry black "leaves" of a small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta), see this photo. Pretty cool! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2015)

File.Name: f526e_f526-002.jpg
Notes:
18142226221 These are the first new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. They are just starting to flower higher in the mountains, though I reckon it will be a while before they flower in our canyon. Many monardella species have a sweet peppermint smell, but these smell dark and musty. They are one of my favorite summer flowers. Last year I found them on June 7, pretty close. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 May 2015)

I got this photo yesterday in the clouds. There's a pretty little Metallic Wood Boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera) snuggling into the flowers trying to keep warm.
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Metallic Wood Boring Beetle

File.Name: f526f_f524-008.jpg
Notes: 18140891355 These are gorgeous new flowers of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. This plant was almost buried by non-native weedy grasses. I did a little weed clearing before I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

File.Name: f527a_f527-013.jpg
Notes:
18176563272 This is a furry little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) preparing to insert its long proboscis into a surprisingly deep flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. There are many different kinds bee flies, so I won't try to guess which one this is. It seems rather small. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the same fantastic fly. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2015)

Today was a nice day, after the morning marine layer fog fog burned away. It was still pretty cool for May (almost June!) with high temps just a hair short of 70 degrees. It's expected to warm up for the next few days.

File.Name: f527b_f527-012.jpg
Notes: 17991837250 This is a furry little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) digging into a new flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. There are many different kinds bee flies, so I won't try to guess which one this is. It seems rather small. This one is actually at rest on the flower, but they often just hover before them. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2015)

File.Name: f527c_f527-021.jpg
Notes:
18179305801 I guess this is a female California Orange-winged Grasshopper (Arphia ramona). It's a Band-winged Grasshopper in the subfamily Oedipodinae of the Acrididae, Orthoptera. This one has blue tibia and orange wings when it took flight a moment later. It looks heavy with eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2015)

Albums are not working well tonight at Flickr. I'm sure I've photographed this grasshopper before, but I can't get my Grasshopper album to completely load. I guessing at the ID. Alice?

File.Name: f527d_f526-011.jpg
Notes: 17555238024 Here are a little Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) and an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly sharing a flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They have a division of labor, as the fly is collecting pollen and the butterfly is sucking nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2015)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Umber Skipper; Cobwebby Thistle
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Poanes melane; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Poanes melane; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f527e_f527-017.jpg
Notes:
17991006939 These are the surprising white berries of San Diego Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family. The plants look pretty ratty as summer comes, turning red and looking dead - but they should come back again next year. I've never tried to nibble. There's something unappetizing about white berries. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2015)

See this photo for a collage of other different kinds of Galium in our mountains. Some have berries and some have dry nutlets.

File.Name: f527f_f527-023.jpg
Notes: 17989066148 This is a beautiful new flower of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden, with buds and an old fruit by the side. Note the little Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) in the flower, weighed down by full pollen baskets. This cactus has short spines. We may have collected a piece of this in Baja many years ago. It seems quite happy here, and its grown. A neighbor is a retired authority on local cacti, and he thinks this may be a hybrid. See this photo of the little "cactus corner" in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2015)

File.Name: f528a_f528-014.jpg
Notes:
17594409384 This is a tiny diurnal (daytime) micro-moth (Lepidoptera) working a new flower of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's quite pretty with iridescent gold/bronze scales when the light is just right. There were quite a few of these on the flowers. I don't know what kind of moth they are. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2015)

Today was a lovely day - not too breezy and a bit warmer than it's been, almost 80∞ on the upstairs porch. Insects are still pretty scarce, but I hope this warmer weather will bring them out.

File.Name: f528b_f528-026.jpg
Notes:
18030200919 These are new galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are certainly strange and beautiful, but they are actually part of the oak tree! (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2015)

(Does the lighting look strange? I rotated this photo 90∞ to the right because I like horizontal shots better than vertical ones on my computer screen. This makes it more of a morning photo than a high noon shot.)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f528c_f528-020.jpg
Notes: 18028373330 Here is a mass of aphid galls on leaves of a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. The tumor-like galls are caused the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. Specialized aphids live inside those pockets on the leaves. These galls must do some damage to the shrubs when they form a mass like this. For some reason I mostly see masses like this on West Camino Cielo road, but not on East Camino Cielo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2015)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid

File.Name: f528d_f528-003.jpg
Notes:
18027713058 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) "butts up" in a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The honey bees have a hard time getting into these flowers until larger Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa) or Bumble Bees (Bombus) get there first to "unlock" the flowers. See this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2015)

It's been a good week for butt shots!
CommonNames: Honey Bee; White Sage; Carpenter Bees; Bumble Bees
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Bombus
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Bombus

File.Name: f528e_f526-021.jpg
Notes: 17594513753 This is a "butt shot" of the same handsome little Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that I show in this photo. It's snuggling into a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I can't explain what I see here. Is that a stinger? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2015)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f528f_f524-010.jpg
Notes: 18028131929 Here are a pair of beautiful bug butts on native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - with more bugs in the top- and bottom-right! These flowers don't look very special, but they sure attract the insects. I won't worry now about what kind of bugs they are. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2015)

File.Name: f529a_f529-036.jpg
Notes:
18251365615 This Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly is keeping its compound eyes on that Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) on the same flower! The flower is the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Its been the star insect attractor in the woods lately. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2015)

Today was warm and lovely, with moderate breeze. It was quite hot today, up to 90∞ at the river, where I was. It felt even hotter out on the exposed dry flats along the river bed. I probably won't have time to post tomorrow. Julie and I are going out to dinner with Flickr-member R.J. Adams and friends, before his talk on California spiders for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. I'm looking forward to this.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Honey Bee; Cobwebby Thistle
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f529b_f529-038.jpg
Notes: 18247391602 Here is a two-fer of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies on a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. The butterflies really are variable - compare the amount of red/orange on their upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2015)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f529c_f529-006.jpg
Notes: 18064464879 This is a gorgeous Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly perched on the non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", "Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. Yipes, it looks like a target! They mostly sit with wings closed, but every once in a while they pump their wings open and then close them again. I got this photo during on of those moments. Not quite sharp? Who cares! (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2015)

File.Name: f529d_f529-031.jpg
Notes: 18250265715 This is the whole inflorescence of a native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's growing on the burn of the White Fire two years ago, almost to the day. (See this photo from May 27, 2013 .) The plant is a good eight feet tall, full of flowers and starting to release seeds. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2015)
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle

File.Name: f529e_f529-022.jpg
Notes: 17629212743 I believe this is a Western Side-blotched Lizard (Uta stansburiana elegans) on a hot rock by the Santa Ynez River today. I can see a dark patch behind its right foreleg. They have smoother scales and different colors than our usual Fence Lizards. These lizards are incredibly fast and usually hard to capture with a camera, but this one posed for me. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 May 2015)

File.Name: f529f_f527-010.jpg
Notes: 18061912520 This is looking almost straight down at a furry little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) inserting its long proboscis into a surprisingly deep flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. There are many different kinds bee flies, so I won't try to guess which one this is. It seems rather small. See this photo for a view from the side. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2015)

File.Name: f531a_f531-027.jpg
Notes:
17708505493 This is a remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are fast and alert, hard to capture with a camera. I got this photo when I first came to the flower - and never saw the wasp again. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2015)

The rest of my photos today are butterflies from yesterday, which was a remarkably good day for butterflies. I couldn't post yesterday because I was busy meeting Flickr-member R.J. Adams at his talk on California spiders for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society last night. It was a good presentation.

We had a little "meet up" of Flicker people there. Along with R.J. and myself, Alice Abelia and Michelle C. Torres-Grant (flygrl67) were there. What a pleasure to meet all these people face to face!
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f531b_f530-030.jpg
Notes: 18324349552 Look close in the shadows... This Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly is ovipositing - laying eggs - under a leaf of well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. Well, this shrub is a "scroph" too, along with the more typical Bush Monkey Flower and Climbing Penstemon. I look forward to finding caterpillars in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2015)

File.Name: f531c_f530-008.jpg
Notes:
18141496799 Here is a handsome Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) butterfly perched on non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", "Lamiaceae) in the garden. This skipper is small with warm colors. If you look close, you can see hyaline (translucent) spots near the forewing tips. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2015)

Compare this recent photo where the identity of the butterfly isn't so clear.

File.Name: f531d_f530-004.jpg
Notes: 18327284685 This is a gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the flowers of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", "Lamiaceae) in the garden. It must be newly emerged to have such brilliant colors. This one is entering the flower in the "right" way - probably because it's too big to do "nectar theft" like the butterfly in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2015)

File.Name: f531e_f530-015.jpg
Notes: 18138752870 This is a Colias sulphur butterfly, but I'm not sure what kind - an Orange Sulfur Colias eurytheme or maybe a Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii aka C. alexandra harfordii)? It does seem to have yellow areas on the forewing enclosed by the rather wide dark border, which suggests the Orange Sulfur, but I found this one in the mountains at home away from agricultural areas which suggests a Harford's Sulfur. Whatever it is, it's doing "nectar theft" on the wrong end of a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2015)
CommonNames: Colias; Orange Sulfur; Harford's Sulphur; Waverly Sage
ScientificName: Colias eurytheme; Colias harfordii; C. alexandra harfordii; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Colias eurytheme; Colias harfordii; C. alexandra harfordii; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae

File.Name: f531f_f530-040.jpg
Notes: 18327386661 This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly working a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", "Lamiaceae) in the garden. This lady is distinctive with just two large eyespots below, but it also almost always a white dot in that first orange rectangle on the forewing. These are the butterflies that host their larvae in the native Fragrant Everlasting (Gnaphalium canescens, Asteraceae) plants, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2015)
CommonNames: American Lady; Waverly Sage; Fragrant Everlasting
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae; Gnaphalium canescens; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae; Gnaphalium canescens; Asteraceae

File.Name: f601a_f601-021.jpg
Notes:
18179823078 This is a little California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working a new flower spike of the locally uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. If you follow the line of sight of the butterfly, you can find a tiny crab spider hiding between the flowers. The indigo flowers are remarkable, and hardly look like legumes at all. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2015)

It was breezy today, becoming downright windy as the day went on. I'm a little distracted tonight, as Julie's mom and two sisters are visiting for a few days. I'll do what I can for new photos, starting with shorter descriptions.

File.Name: f601b_f601-023.jpg
Notes: 18181239309 This is a locally uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family, in full flower. The shrub has a light and airy look, like mesquite or acacia in the desert. Here it is in the mountain chaparral. This shrub has long odd-pinnate leaves with many leaflets. The indigo flowers are remarkable, and hardly look like legumes at all. If you look close, you can see a little California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly on one of the flower stalks. See my [Next] photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2015)

File.Name: f601c_f601-029.jpg
Notes: 18180735359 This is looking closer at the beautiful new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's a summer wildflower that is not common in our mountains, but they are well worth looking for. Plants in the open seem to be heavily browsed by deer, but this plant is growing from the base of a Chaparral Currant shrub which gives it some protection. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2015)

File.Name: f601d_f601-039.jpg
Notes: 18178702628 Here are two photos of the beautiful flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's a summer wildflower that is not common in our mountains. These are the first new flowers I've found this year, but it looks like they've been flowering for some time. Plants in the open seem to be heavily browsed by deer, but the plants on the left are hiding in a hedge of Poison Oak, and the plants on the right are growing from the base of a Chaparral Currant shrub. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2015)

File.Name: f601e_f601-013.jpg
Notes: 17745598073 This is a new flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Many monardella species have a sweet peppermint smell, but these smell dark and musty. They are one of my favorite summer flowers. They are the only plant on which I've found uncommon Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterflies, see this photo from last year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2015)
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Callippe Fritillary

File.Name: f601f_f601-041.jpg
Notes: 17745383103 This is a fasciated stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. The stem is flat like a ribbon, but still green and even flowering. You can see normal round stems in the background, as well as another fasciated stem on the right. Many plants get fasciated, some often and others (like this one) rarely. There's some info at Wikipedia. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2015)

File.Name: f602a_f602-019.jpg
Notes:
17781190634 Here are two side-by-side photos of what I guess are female (left) and male (right) Western Lynx Spiders (Oxyopes scalaris) in the family Oxyopidae. They were sitting about four inches apart on the same plant of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). They look very different at first, with their different color and size. But if you look closer, they have similar spiky legs and eyes, and similar patterns on their abdomens. You can see the male's swollen pedipalps tucked under its head. I didn't see any hanky-panky, but I'll bet they're thinking about it! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2015)

Today was nice enough, but breezy - especially in the morning when I was out getting my photos. Julie and her family went off to Solvang, so I had some time to myself.
ScientificName: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Oxyopes scalaris; Oxyopidae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f602b_f602-023.jpg
Notes: 18215746710 This is a Mugwort Weevil - my name, but it's what they should be called. It's a weevil beetle in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that I find on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. I believe it's genus Lixus, possibly Lixus semivittatus - see this photo at BugGuide. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2015)
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Lixus; Lixus semivittatus
associatedTaxa: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Lixus; Lixus semivittatus

File.Name: f602c_f602-020.jpg
Notes:
18398765522 This is another photo of the chrysalis I found on a shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) by the creek. See this photo and this one. I first thought it might be a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia), but it's probably another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. It's strange and beautiful. You can see the butterfly's future antennae and proboscis etched onto the front. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2015)

I wonder what the bump is on the screen-left bottom?
CommonNames: Bitter Gooseberry; Common Buckeye; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Junonia coenia; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Junonia coenia; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: f602d_f602-029.jpg
Notes: 18214975230 Here are two side-by-side photos of a furry little Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) in and out of a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. (Yep, they're still going.) You have to admire how deep the flower goes to handle the fly's long proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2015)

File.Name: f602e_f602-001.jpg
Notes:
18403495491 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) collecting pollen grains from the anther of a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on the native side of our garden. Compare the size of the anthers in relation to a Honey Bee in my [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2015)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, so I can use my camera as an expensive remote micrometer. The critter is about 1050 pixels across. That makes it about 4.1 mm across, a bit over 1/8 inch. See this photo for how I figured.

File.Name: f602e_f602-009.jpg
Notes:
18213802020 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) trying to break into a "locked" flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on the native side of our garden. The flowers start in this closed position until larger Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa) or Bumble Bees (Bombus) get there first to "unlock" the flowers. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2015)

Looking closer, I can see that other bees have already done "nectar theft" on this flower, probably in the same manner as this one. Look at that small orange-rimmed tear at the base of the flower!
CommonNames: Honey Bee; White Sage; Carpenter Bees; Bumble Bees
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Bombus
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa; Bombus

File.Name: f603a_f603-009.jpg
Notes:
18253060220 I guess this is a very pale uncommon Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterfly working a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I now believe it is Comstock's Fritillary (Speyeria callippe comstocki) - thanks RJ! I have a photo of a fresher specimen from last year here and a photo showing the underside here. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

This is the earliest I've ever found one of these butterflies, and the first time I've found one on a plant other than Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae.

It was really a crummy day for new photos, but I got some nice ones today. The marine layer fog spilled over the mountains bringing "fog winds" with them. I tried to go to the river, but they're working on the road and the traffic control lady said it would be a a 25 minute wait. I went back to the mountains, and got these shots.
Locality:
Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae.

It was really a crummy day for new photos
CommonNames: Callippe Fritillary; Golden Yarrow; Comstock's Fritillary; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Speyeria callippe; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Speyeria callippe comstocki; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: f603b_f603-042.jpg
Notes:
17817379114 I'm pretty sure this is a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly perched on a plant of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae) in the woods today. It's another small brown hairstreak butterfly, but this one has "white frosting" on its wings and pointed forewings. I actually got this photo looking down. The butterfly was tilted over trying to catch some rays on a chilly day. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

This completes my collection of these small brown hairstreaks - along with the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) in this photo and the Gold-hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) in this photo.
CommonNames: Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak; Golden Yarrow; Hedgerow Hairstreak; Gold-hunter's Hairstreak
ScientificName: Satyrium tetra; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum
associatedTaxa: Satyrium tetra; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum

File.Name: f603c_f603-055.jpg
Notes: 18253328089 This is a mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. This one is trying to get away from me on stems of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). You can see its strong proboscis folded under its narrow head. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)
CommonNames: California Bee Assassin; Assassin Bug; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f603d_f603-019.jpg
Notes: 18434987582 Here is a mating pair of Bordered Plant Bugs (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - almost certainly Largus californicus. That's quite a "universal joint" they have in common! These true bugs are having their nuptials on a plant of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)
ScientificName: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f603e_f603-001.jpg
Notes:
18434240462 Here are two photos of a different ant-mimic nymph of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This one appears different from the Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) nymphs that I've been showing in flower of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) - compare this photo. It's smaller and darker, and seems to have two faux "nodal spines" instead of just one. I don't know what this is. It moves in an ant-like manner, but those long antennae caught my eye. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

I know, these aren't the best photos. It was gloomy and breezy this morning, a tough combination. I did the best I could.
CommonNames: White Sage; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f603f_f603-028.jpg
Notes: 18439812761 This is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on a flower bud of Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. I have no idea whose eggs they are. They look like little fossilized digit bones, or maybe those horrible "eggs" in the first Alien movie! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

File.Name: f604a_f604-001.jpg
Notes:
18470385242 This is an impossibly-thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). Yep, it's climbing away from me. These critters have an amazing sense of when they're being watched - and they don't like it! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2015)

This was my only keeper photo today. It was chilly, cloudy, and breezy - a bad combination for new photos. There is a slight chance of a bit of rain tonight and tomorrow. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f604b_f603-046.jpg
Notes: 18288259559 This is another handsome male Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia, Rosaceae). Those copper/bronze upper wings are very beautiful. This one looks poised for takeoff, but it's really just trying to get warm on a chilly day. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

File.Name: f604c_f603-020.jpg
Notes: 18474251795 This is a pretty little Wasp (Hymenoptera) on a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It doesn't have any sign of having a "wasp waist". I reckon it's a Stem Sawfly (Cephidae, "Symphyta") - maybe Calameuta clavata. See my [Previous] photo for a "butt shot" of this insect. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

Notes: 17851092494 This is a beautiful "butt shot" of a pretty little Wasp (Hymenoptera) on a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It doesn't have any sign of having a "wasp waist". I reckon it's a Stem Sawfly (Cephidae, "Symphyta") - maybe Calameuta clavata. See my [Next] photo for the whole insect, here. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

File.Name: f604e_f603-050.jpg
Notes: 18287060959 This is a beautiful "butt shot" of a mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It seems to have a face on it's butt! This one is moving away from me on a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). See this photo for the rest of the insect. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

File.Name: f604f_f531-002.jpg
Notes:
18472336985 This is a dusty Pinacate Beetle (genus Coelocnemis in the family Tenebrionidae, Coleoptera). The genus Eleodes is similar, but this one has two rows of fine, golden brown hairs on the lower half of the inside margin of the hind tibiae, which are barely visible in my photo. It's also known as a Stink Beetle or Stinkbug or Acrobat Beetle or Clown Beetle, but I prefer the old name. Wikipedia says that "the name pinacate is Mexican Spanish, derived from the Nahuatl (Aztec) name for the insect, pinacatl..." Kids like to torment these large beetles - don't let them! The beetles put their heads on the earth, think of them as wise. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2015)

This beetle is dusty because it was turned over on its back and had a hard time getting up. Sure, I helped it. These beetles are said to release a brew of noxious chemicals from their rear ends. I can't vouch for that.
CommonNames: Pinacate Beetle; Stink Beetle; Stinkbug; Acrobat Beetle; Clown Beetle

File.Name: f605a_f605-012.jpg
Notes:
17887922023 This is a male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on new flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I know the photo is a notch overexposed, but the upper wings are mostly white anyway, with black chevrons along the margin. And it's a record photo, the first one of these butterflies I've seen this year. Its host plant is native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) which grows nearby. This shrub isn't flowering yet, but see this photo from a few months ago. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 June 2015)

The sun was in and out of the clouds with "fog winds" at home this morning, so I went down to the Santa Ynez River to find some sun. It was still pretty breezy. I did my best for new photos.
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Coastal Goldenbush; Chaparral Mallow
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma menziesii; vernonioides; Asteraceae; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae

File.Name: f605b_f605-024.jpg
Notes:
18320313288 This is a pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on new flowers Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I know the photo is a notch overexposed and not quite sharp, but it shows the sparkles in the butterfly's eyespots pretty well. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 June 2015)

I've been confused by this plant before, but I checked the CalFlora "What Grow Here" page, and this is what came up. This is a useful way to use CalFlora!

File.Name: f605c_f605-006.jpg
Notes: 18509323801 This is the tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It might be a nymph of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). They are surprisingly pretty when they are this size! This one is the first I've managed to photograph this year, on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I'd like this photo better if that tiny stick/stem wasn't there. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2015)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry

File.Name: f605d_f605-037.jpg
Notes: 17886291353 These are new flowers of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. I reckon this is var. stenophyllus with skinny filiform leaves. Other plants in the mountains have wider leaves and are var. foliosus. I like the just-opening flowers best. Note the tiny Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) on the flower at the bottom. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 June 2015)

File.Name: f605e_f605-004.jpg
Notes:
17883594214 This little bird crashed into the glass sliding door on our upstairs porch this morning and was stunned. Julie fixed a little bed for it in a basket and we left in what sun there was this morning. When I checked back 10 minutes later, it was gone. A successful wildlife recovery effort!

I'm pretty sure this is an adult female Black-Throated Gray Warbler (Dendroica nigrescens). It's a migrant that we see in the fall and spring as it flies from here to there. It's a striking bird with those contrasting colors and yellow eyebrows, but normally quite camera shy. Live long and prosper, little bird - and watch out for glass doors! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2015)

I like how monochrome this photo is - except for that little yellow patch above its beak.

File.Name: f605f_f531-011.jpg
Notes: 17885318183 I'm pretty sure this is a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the canyon. That's an interesting genus name for these flies! I'm making it nervous - it has its front legs up in the air, getting ready to take off. Flies have been scarce this week because of the chilly weather - at least interesting flies have been scarce. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2015)

File.Name: f606a_f606-004.jpg
Notes:
18357574259 Here are two photos of the adults that come from the ant-mimic nymphs in my [Previous] photo, both shots on the same native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I now guess they are Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), though for a while I thought they were different. Compare this photo inside a flower of Bush Monkey Flower. The adults are less ant-like, but they are beautiful. Julie said they remind her of Hopi pottery. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2015)

It was nice enough today, but still a bit chilly for June. That's expected to change tomorrow. I've been distracted tonight because TCM is showing on of my favorite movies - John Huston's "The Man Who Would Be King" (1975). The TV is right around the corner from where I work, so I mostly listen - but sometimes I have to get up. We pay up the nose for satellite TV, mostly for this one channel. I reckon it's worth it.
Locality: 1975).  The TV is right around the corner from where I work
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: f606b_f606-008.jpg
Notes:
18538893292 Here are two photos of remarkable ant-mimic nymphs of a "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. They are remarkable mimics - they look like ants, move like ants, and even have a faux "nodal spine" like some ants have for real. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2015)

Looking at photos on BugGuide, I guess they really are the same Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae) critters that I've been finding inside of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) - compare this photo. I thought that these bugs in the open looked smaller and darker, but maybe that's just an illusion of the orange "light tent" of the flowers.
CommonNames: White Sage; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: f606c_f606-025.jpg
Notes: 17921924713 This is a tattered female Blue (Polyommatinae, Lepidoptera) butterfly on Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's no doubt an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly, though there's no hint of sparkles on her worn wings. In another photo that's not very good I think I can see her ovipositing. This is their main host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2015)
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Lepidoptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Plebejus acmon
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Lepidoptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Plebejus acmon

File.Name: f606d_f606-011.jpg
Notes:
18354504560 "Seeds of life..."

These are the eggs of the same Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I found ovipositing a few days ago - see this photo. She laid quite a clutch of eggs! I lose count everytime I try to count them, but there are more than 50. These are under a leaf of well-named but non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. This shrub is a "scroph" too, along with the more typical Bush Monkey Flower and Climbing Penstemon that they usually favor. I look forward to finding tiny caterpillars in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2015)

File.Name: f606e_f606-026.jpg
Notes: 18537265142 This is a handsome little Metallic Wood Boring Beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera) - probably genus Acmaeodera. There are many different kinds, mostly on flowers. I found this one on a leaf of a flowering shrub of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2015)
ScientificName: Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Acmaeodera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: f606f_f606-027.jpg
Notes: 18353317590 These are the fantastic new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on the flower to the right. I've wondered what insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for - certainly not this tiny fly! Maybe I discovered it in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2015)
CommonNames: Woolly Blue Curls; Syrphid Flower Fly

File.Name: f607a_f607-039.jpg
Notes: 18585997951 This is a humble Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched (unusually) in a shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. usually these butterflies stay closer to the ground. It has a handsome beard! This one is approaching the "summer form" of these butterflies. Compare this photo of the "spring form". (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2015)

File.Name: f607b_f607-043.jpg
Notes:
17962450293 This isn't a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides), but it sure has a cat face! It's kind of spooky how orb weaver spiders so often have faces on their butts.

I believe this photo shows a male Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. I can see enlarged pedipalps in another photo, so I'm sure it's a male. What I find most interesting, is that it has its own house decorated with bodies of its prey near the female's house. See my [Previous] photo. Sure, the female builds a larger house, but that the male has any structure shows that it intends to be around for a while. This goes against the usual female-eats-male thinking about spiders. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2015)
CommonNames: Cat-Faced Spider; Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers

File.Name: f607c_f607-023.jpg
Notes: 18555968676 This is a complicated photo that rewards study. I believe it shows Labyrinth Spiders (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. These spiders make a tangled cobweb near their real orb web, and build a little house on the edge of it. On the right in the fallen leaf is (I guess) the female spider, with a house half-built - or at least half-decorated with the bodies of her prey. The spider in the top-left is interesting. I believe it is the male spider that goes with the female on the right. He seems to have a house of his own, see my [Next] photo. Look how their webs are connected. This goes against the usual female-eats-male thinking about spiders. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2015)

File.Name: f607d_f607-026.jpg
Notes: 18394358588 This is a small dark "true bug" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera inserting its proboscis into a fruit of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I don't know what kind it is. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2015)

File.Name: f607e_f607-031.jpg
Notes: 18395550149 These are strange and beautiful galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I gently turned the leaf around to get a better look at the galls. These mushroom-like galls are actually part of the oak tree! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2015)

File.Name: f607f_f607-037.jpg
Notes: 18393870360 This is another odd gall of the Fimbriate Gall Wasp (Andricus opertaus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). If you look close, you can see that the gall is an extension of the midrib of the upper leaf. I never noticed these before this year, but now I seem to find them on many shrubs. File.Name: f608a_f608-029.jpg
Notes:
18000260654 This is a stunning Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon. The butterflies seem made for these flowers, but I bet they're really waiting for the native Humboldt Lilies (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae to appear. I've seen buds, but no flowers yet. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2015)

Today was warm, like yesterday. We got into the 90s on our upstairs porch. Unfortunately, it was also pretty breezy. We actually have a 30% chance of a bit of rain tomorrow. There is a little low pressure system coming down from the north that might entrain moisture from the remnants of tropical storm Bianca coming from the south. There's no sign of an approaching storm now, but we'll see what happens.

Notes: 18596065376 This is another remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are fast and alert, hard to capture with a camera. Those large "bug eyes" seem to see everything. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2015)

File.Name: f608f_f608-040.jpg
Notes: 18615387432 This is another nest of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. The nest looks like paper, decorated with the with the bodies of her prey. See my photo yesterday. I found this nest today in a flowering plant of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. It's interesting because it sits almost horizontal, instead of hanging down like they usually do. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2015)
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; California Buckwheat

File.Name: f609a_f609-002.jpg
Notes:
18653952792 A large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree came down in the woods yesterday afternoon, completely blocking our neighbor's road. I heard it fall - a crack followed by a crunch. Once you've heard that sound, you know it! I went down to check it out, saw that no one was under it, and went back home to email the owner who happens to be away right now. Another kindly neighbor did some limb trimming in the evening, and decided that it need a team of professionals. Hah - I could have told him that! Hopefully it will all be cleared away tomorrow and our neighbor can get back home. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2015)

Why would such a large tree suddenly fall on a day with little wind? Everything is wet in this photo during our rain today, but this tree fell on a hot day with little wind. All the trees are drought-stressed. I hope this is just a freak occurrence of the "breaking point" and not a sign of things to come.

We had 0.95 inches of real rain today, and it rained all day! It might be all over now, but who knows? Our average rainfall for June is just 0.14 inch, so this is pretty interesting. It will help our summer wildflowers, and hopefully not bring up more weeds.

File.Name: f609b_f608-042.jpg
Notes:
18471984319 "It was beauty killed the bee..."

I found this motionless Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) inside the yellow flower of (I think) a Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. The flower just opened in the morning for the first time. I'm not sure if the bee is dead or just dreaming. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2015)

File.Name: f609c_f608-007.jpg
Notes: 18469668880 This is a gorgeous yellow flower on a native "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cactus in the Cactaceae plant family, with more buds on either side. I believe this is the Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis), probably from the Santa Monica Mountains. It has long spines. I think we collected a piece of it many years ago, and it's doing well in the "cactus corner" of our garden - see this photo. This is the first flower of the year, caught in the first light of the morning. (Well, the first light to get here, in our shady north-facing slope home.) (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2015)

File.Name: f609d_f607-047.jpg
Notes:
18652347082 This is looking into a large dandelion-like seed head of native Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers aren't much to look at, but the ribbed seeds are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2015)

(I decided I didn't care for the original photo, and replaced it with another of the kind of same seed head from the same day.)
Locality: I decided I didn't care for the original photo

File.Name: f610a_f610-032.jpg
Notes:
18685988792 This is a lovely female Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly perched on a stem of her host plant, native Longstem Buckwheat aka Wand Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. See this photo for the copper-colored male. Maybe they get their common name from the strikingly different female and male, though she doesn't look at all snake-like. See this photo for both male and female side-by-side. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 June 2015)

It was gloomy at home this morning, so I drove up into the mountains to find some sun. The marine layer fog was 3,500 feet deep, but I did find some sun (and breeze) above it. Our good rain yesterday has turned into fog today.

My dear friend Cynthia Carbone Ward has written a rather nice piece about my photo efforts. You can read it here.
CommonNames: Gorgon Copper; Longstem Buckwheat; Wand Buckwheat

File.Name: f610b_f610-020.jpg
Notes:
18690453315 This is a fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera), formerly known as Hemaris diffinis. It's working a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 June 2015)

Yeah, I know - the photo isn't quite sharp. I was trying to get the little ambush bug in my [Previous] photo, when this moth suddenly appeared nearby! I cranked my focus and captured it full-frame... but not quite in focus. It's still a beautiful moth on a beautiful flower. This may be the insect these flowers are made for - see this photo.

File.Name: f610c_f610-024.jpg
Notes: 18689613835 This is the small but mighty nymph of an Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a flower head of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's probably a nymph of the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). This one has green colors that the adults lack. Note that it has a little tiny Beetle (Coleoptera). This is first one I've seen this year, and I can't recall ever finding a nymph before. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 June 2015)

This is one of my favorite almost-summer wildflowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 June 2015)

File.Name: f610e_f608-012.jpg
Notes: 18501066978 Here are a mating pair of "true bugs" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on buds of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. That's quite a "universal joint" they have in common, compare this photo. I reckon that's the larger female on top, full of eggs. I don't know what kind of bugs these are, but it's interesting how different that male and female look. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2015)

File.Name: f611a_f611-026.jpg
Notes:
18102251204 This is a mighty female Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) on a cluster of new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. They are our largest wasps - almost 2 inches long. This one has orange antennae curled to the outside. The color of these large wasps is amazing - "so blue it's black" with those contrasting orange wings. They are said to have a remarkably painful sting, though they've never bothered me. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 June 2015)

D'oh - I increased my aperture to f/6.3 for an earlier photo, and forgot to set it back to my usual f/11. No problem! In fact there's always a trade-off between DOF and sharpness due to diffraction effects. There's an easy to understand explanation here. (If you want the math, look here.) I prefer f/11, though I go to f/22 for mushrooms that don't have such sharp features as insects or flowers.

I had my usual errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, and made the best of it with a few stops along the way. Just as well. The marine layer fog was in and out all day at home, several times. Every time it came in, the temperature dropped 10 degrees and the "fog winds" kicked in.

(in comment) OK, I got it. The problem seemed to be this one line, and when I removed it it worked fine. Curious. Is this a well known malware site? (The name does sound a bit fishy, now that I look at it.) Here's the offending line, with DOT and [ instead of < in the link:

"There's an easy to understand explanation [a href="http://wwwDOTbiginmicrostockDOTcom/pages/photography/diffraction-leads-to-loss-of-image-sharpness.html" rel="nofollow"]here[/a].
Locality: The name does sound a bit fishy

File.Name: f611b_f611-016.jpg
Notes: 18719445762 This is a "butt shot" of a mighty female Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) on new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. They are our largest wasps - almost 2 inches long. This one has orange antennae curled to the outside. The color of these large wasps is amazing - "so blue it's black" with those contrasting orange wings. They are said to have a remarkably painful sting, though they've never bothered me. HBBBT! (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 June 2015)

File.Name: f611c_f611-012.jpg
Notes: 18725918031 These are new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an unusual perennial composite with an interesting common name. It has heads of pretty two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. I reckon it's a fire-follower that lingers. As I (don't quite) recall, this place near Lake Cachuma last burned 10 or more years ago. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 11 June 2015)

File.Name: f611d_f611-005.jpg
Notes:
18725060391 This is a tiny Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a mint leaf in the garden. What amazing iridescent colors these flies have, mostly green or blue or copper. The iridescent colors play tricks with your eyes, and seem to constantly change. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2015)

These tiny flies are predators. Note the scooped-out face between the eyes - it's a trait shared by other insect predators like robber flies and dragonflies. Maybe it helps them with depth perception to catch their prey on the wing?

File.Name: f611e_f605-009.jpg
Notes: 18101378443 This is a "butt shot" of a "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera, maybe genus Chinavia. That little red and black beetle nymph on the right seems interested. These are also the first (very early) flowers I've seen this year of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). This is a really important summer flowering shrub that grows along the Santa Ynez River that is very important for pollinator insects. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 June 2015)
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f611f_f602-027.jpg
Notes: 18716953452 This is a fantastic little furry Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) digging deep into a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. (Yep, they're still going.) I should have posted this last Thursday, but I plumb forgot. It's a nice butt shot, HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2015)

File.Name: f612a_f612-043.jpg
Notes:
18567647959 This is a slender little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - maybe Zelus tetracanthus in the subfamily Harpactorinae. This one is perched on new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae) plant family. Look how it's holding its front legs up in attack position! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2015)

It was an interesting weather day - beautiful and calm in the morning, but thunder storms came up in the afternoon in the backcountry and the winds picked up. I heard some thunder off in the distance. The clouds moved towards us... and then backed off. So much for our "Flash Flood Watch" this morning. It was still a dynamic day!

File.Name: f612b_f612-031.jpg
Notes:
18755567101 This is a Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) in front of her nest. I found this wasp on the edge of the chip seal road in our canyon at home - the sand is native, but some of the gravel isn't. I only found it because it's right below the chair I keep in the canyon, near the native thistles where I also find these wasps - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2015) This is a Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) in front of her nest. I found this wasp on the edge of the chip seal road in our canyon at home - the sand is native, but some of the gravel isn't. I only found it because it's right below the chair I keep in the canyon, near the native thistles where I also find these wasps - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2015)

It's interesting that she was also digging several faux holes nearby, possibly to confuse predators? I'm pretty sure this is her real hole, I saw her come in and out several times, disappearing for almost a minute before I saw her again. I was thinking of Niko Tinbergen - moving tiny rocks about to confuse the wasps!

I believe this is Closterocoris amoenus (Miridae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). You can clearly see its proboscis folded under its head. It's a beautiful creature! This one is perched on a bud of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2015)

Notes: 18131582693 This is another mating pair of Metallic Wood Boring Beetles (Acmaeodera, Buprestidae, Coleoptera) with a looming Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) in the background! It gives a good sense of scale. There are many different kinds of these beetles, mostly on flowers. I found these on native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. File.Name: f612f_f611-006.jpg
Notes: 18565473689 This is another photo of a tiny Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a mint leaf in the garden. What amazing iridescent colors these flies have, mostly green or blue or copper. The iridescent colors play tricks with your eyes, and seem to constantly change. See this photo from yesterday. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2015)

File.Name: f613a_f613-025.jpg
Notes:
18166202144 I believe this is a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum, Asteraceae). It's darker - almost black - than the similar Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly that is more common in our woods, see this photo. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 June 2015)

It was a very nice day - IF you got above the fog monster like I did! Marine layer fog brings fog winds. It was still a little breezy in the higher mountains above the fog, but not to bad.

Notes: 18167862923 This is a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly on new summer flowers of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. It's another small brown hairstreak butterfly, but this one has "white frosting" on its wings and pointed forewings. But if you look closer, you can see that its anything but plain brown! This butterfly has complex colors like the Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus). Its like a pointillist painting - many color swatches combine to make what looks like brown. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 June 2015)

Notes: 18599766980 This is an impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae) in the order Diptera perched for just a moment on a leaf of a flowering shrub of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. I believe the fly is Stenopogon californiae, though apparently you have to study its genitalia to be sure. I dig its whiskers! It's always chance to find one of these flies that don't visit flowers except to find prey. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 June 2015)

Notes: 18166429513 These are stunning new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's a summer wildflower that is not common in our mountains, but they are well worth looking for. Plants in the open seem to be heavily browsed by deer. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 June 2015)

File.Name: f613e_f613-021.jpg
Notes: 18164090284 The fog monster is totally in control today - but I got the better of it by going up above the 3000 foot thick marine layer. This is looking east from the high point near the FAA tower between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro. That's La Cumbre Peak in the back-left. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 June 2015)

File.Name: f613f_f613-039.jpg
Notes:
18164888863 The fog monster is totally in control today!

This is looking west across San Marcos Pass today, along the east-west axis of the Santa Ynez Mountains. That's a small ridge near Painted Cave in front with native Gray Pines aka Digger Pines (Pinus sabiniana) and a few Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) - mostly dead from Bark Beetles and drought. That's Brush Peak on the left and Broadcast Peak in the distance. We live down under that mass of marine layer fog coming from the ocean on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2015)

"Fog" isn't the right word when it's 3000 feet thick! This is our typical "June gloom" weather pattern. It finally cleared around 3:00 pm. I don't mind the fog, but the "fog winds" are unacceptable. I went up into the higher mountains today to get away from it.

(I believe the fog monster - aka Phog monster - was invented by Phil Mann, local TV weathercaster for years in the 1980s and 90s.)
Locality: I believe the fog monster - aka Phog monster - was invented by Phil Mann
CommonNames: Gray Pines; Digger Pines; Coulter Pines

File.Name: f614a_f614-028.jpg
Notes:
18198579833 This is a remarkable Fly (order Diptera) working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon. I believe it's an atypical Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae), almost certainly Thevenetimyia californica. What a mighty proboscis it has! This fly is quite large, at least as long as a honey bee, though much more slender. See my [Previous] photo. Note that little tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) in the air below it. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2015)

Today was a lovely day! The marine layer fog didn't quite spill over the mountains, though it was close, and some of the "fog wind" did come over. The Live Oak Music Festival is coming up next weekend, and I'm starting to already feel busy. I'll do what I can for new photos. We'll start organizing our camping stuff tomorrow. Our kids (and grandkids!) arrive on Wednesday. The camping line-up starts on Thursday afternoon. We been going to this great festival for 20+ years!

File.Name: f614c_f614-004.jpg
Notes: 18818421715 I first thought this is a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae, Hymenoptera), but blue.geo says (in his comment below) that it's a Sphecid Wasp (family Sphecidae, genus Chalybion or Chlorion). I've got to hit the books on this one. It has "so blue it looks black" colors, thick antennae, and twitchy wings. Julie spotted this one on a garden path this morning. It certainly has a resemblance to the mighty Tarantula Hawk, but is much smaller and maybe quite different. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2015)

File.Name: f614d_f614-012.jpg
Notes:
18197126083 This is the large caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in the canyon. It's in a "praying position" - probably praying that I will leave it alone! I tried to tickle its forehead with a grass stem to get it to bring out its brilliant orange "osmetarium", but this one didn't want to play. (But see < href="http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/14261403394/">this photo.) I have a photo of the adult here. This is the first caterpillar I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2015)

This native butterfly favors this non-native brood plant. We try to remember to trim the flowers of this invasive weed, but we always save a few plants for the butterflies. It must have found other Apiacea plants before Fennel was introduced, but which ones are out in mid-summer? My best guess is Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta, Apiaceae), though those plants looks pretty sad by summer.

File.Name: f614e_f614-023.jpg
Notes: 18194549704 Here is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is as open as the flowers ever get. The seed heads look almost the same, without the yellow centers, and have a sweet maple sugar smell. They are irresistible to collect, but the old flower heads always seem to get dusty and full of spider webs. They are better in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2015)

File.Name: f614f_f614-010.jpg
Notes: 18630689369 These are neat little mud nests of a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera). A couple of years ago, one of them made a nest on the antennae of my truck! (See this photo.) The adult wasps are pretty distinctive with a "double abdomen" - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2015)

File.Name: f615a_f615-044.jpg
Notes:
18226029304 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly rising up over new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. I'll bet these butterflies are waiting for the native Humboldt Lilies (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) to flower, see this photo from last year! But the lilies aren't doing so well this year. Between the deer and whatever critter eats the buds, there will be few flowers this year for the butterflies.

Today was another lovely day - quite warm, almost 90∞ on the upstairs porch. We've started organizing for the Live Oak Music Festival next weekend. Tomorrow will be a busy day. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: f615b_f615-042.jpg
Notes: 18225634324 This is a butterfly twofer on a new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. On the left is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly, missing a good-sized chunk of its left wing. On the right is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly, the one with just two eyespots on its hindwings. I also saw Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterflies working the flowers, but I couldn't get a decent photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2015)
