File.Name: f615c_f615-001.jpg
Notes: 18661804779 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly visiting a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). It's an unusual flower for these butterflies, but any port in a drought? The butterfly is missing a bird-sized chunk of its wings. It also seems to be missing its abdomen, but I assume it's just folded under. It *surely* couldn't be ovipositing! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2015)

File.Name: f615d_f615-033.jpg
Notes: 18821566006 This is a tiny diurnal (daytime) micro-moth (Lepidoptera) working a new flower of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's quite pretty with iridescent gold/bronze scales when the light is just right. There were quite a few of these on the flowers. I don't know what kind of moth they are. I've seen them before on the same flowers, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2015)

File.Name: f615e_f615-007.jpg
Notes:
18660493909 Here are a pair of the small Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that are working the flower stamens of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. There are more different kinds.

I believe the one on the left is genus Megachile (possibly subgenus Chelostomoides) in the family Megachilidae of Leaf-cutter Bees. These bees collect pollen on the underside of the abdomen, and you can see this one rubbing the stamen with its butt. This particular bee has quite large mandibles. It's hard to make out in this photo, but I believe it's actually biting the filament of the stamen, no doubt to get some stability.

And I believe the one on the right is a Yellow-faced Bee aka Masked Bee in the genus Hylaeus of the family Colletidae. These bees lack specialized pollen-carrying hairs, and carry pollen in their gut to regurgitate upon returning to their nests. This bee is smaller than the bee on the left, compare stamen size.

The large bees get your attention, but these small bees are really interesting. the longer you stand before a plant, the more different kinds you see! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2015)
CommonNames: Wild Bees; White Sage; Leaf-cutter Bees; Yellow-faced Bee; Masked Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachile; Chelostomoides; Megachilidae; Hylaeus; Colletidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachile; Chelostomoides; Megachilidae; Hylaeus; Colletidae

File.Name: f615f_f615-051.jpg
Notes: 18841390492 These are brilliant red berries of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland. The berries are juicy when ripe, but they taste quite bitter to me. Maybe I haven't collected them when they are dead ripe? See this article. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2015)

File.Name: f616a_f616-003.jpg
Notes:
18691966679 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) inside a gorgeous yellow flower of a native "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cactus in the Cactaceae plant family - probably the Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis). I think we collected a piece of the cactus many years ago in the Santa Monica Mountains, and it's done well in our garden. The flower has an interesting effect on the bees. They act stoned after a minute in the flower, and have to leave and fly around for a while until they come back again - as they always do. I've tried various web searches for this behavior, with no luck so far. (San Marcos Pass, 16 June 2015)

I didn't really have time for photos this morning, but I got a couple while I was waiting for Julie to come down to start collecting our camping stuff for the the Live Oak Music Festival next weekend. Then I went off to town.

Our kids (and grandkids!) don't arrive until 5:00 pm tomorrow, so I probably will have time to post a few photos. Then on Thursday it's off to the line-up at Live Oak! We been going to this great festival for 20+ years.

File.Name: f616c_f615-018.jpg
Notes: 18880313801 This is another unlikely butterfly tipping its proboscis into a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). You have to look close to see the path of its proboscis against the dark background, but it's all there. I'm not sure, but maybe this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) with those spots on its wing that let in the light. They are pretty common in the "mint patch" nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2015)

File.Name: f616d_f613-001.jpg
Notes: 18689267748 This is a beautiful emerald-green Metallic Sweat Bee (Agapostemon, Halictidae, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) working a new summer flower of (I think) native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has full pollen baskets. It's a small plant, only a foot tall, but the flowers are rather pretty if you get down on your hands and knees to appreciate them. The flowers are almost identical to the Stephanomeria virgata which hasn't started flowering yet, but it has fewer petals and is much smaller. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 June 2015)
ScientificName: Agapostemon; Halictidae; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria virgata

File.Name: f616e_f603-031.jpg
Notes:
18849659726 This is a tiny "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) sticking its ovipositer into a new seed of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Maybe it's a Chalcidid Wasp in the family Chalcididae? I think it's ovipositing on some critter inside the seed. It sure looks like the ovipositor is coming from the middle of its abdomen rather than the rear. But compare this photo of the same critter. I think the ovipositor is folded under its abdomen from the rear, and the wasp is guiding it down with the junction of its hind legs. I assume there's some grub inside the seed that this wasp is aiming for. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2015)

This nice photo slipped through the cracks and sat forgotten in my "good stuff" folder for a couple of weeks. This is why I need a catch up day every once in a while!

File.Name: f616f_f613-017.jpg
Notes: 18687839130 Here is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's a nice shot of the flowers too. Sure, I really enjoy photographing swallowtails - especially from the side like this. They are large and beautiful and satisfying. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 June 2015)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: f617a_f617-007.jpg
Notes:
This is another gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon. There are still three more buds on the plants, so these fine flowers will be around for a while more. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2015)

I wasn't really looking for new photos today, but I had to pick up the chair I leave in the canyon for the Live Oak Music Festival starting tomorrow, and of course I brought my camera along for a few pics! The line-up of cars and trucks and campers at Live Oak is really the first night of the festival. There will be lots of people jammed into a large dirt field trying to get dibs on there favorite camping place for the festival. There will be some good impromptu music jams too!

(Opps - the kids and grandkids just got here! I'll be a while before I post my last photo...)

File.Name: f617b_f617-002.jpg
Notes: Here are two shots of the same kind of Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The one on the left is digging into the flower for nectar, while the one on the right is collecting pollen on her abdomen. They seem to do both, sometimes one following the other. I believe these bees are genus Megachile (possibly subgenus Chelostomoides) in the family Megachilidae of Leaf-cutter Bees. This particular bee has quite large mandibles. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2015)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; White Sage; Leaf-cutter Bees
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachile; Chelostomoides; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachile; Chelostomoides; Megachilidae

File.Name: f617c_f617-009.jpg
Notes: This is a lovely new flower head of a succulent Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family growing in our canyon at home. They've just just started to fully open their flowers. They are one of my favorite wildflowers. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2015)

File.Name: f617d_f617-012.jpg
Notes: These are new florets of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family growing on a rocky bank in our canyon. This grass is a perennial with short awns, which distinguishes it from weedy annual grasses like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. You have to know something about grasses to recognize it, but it's surprising common when you look for it. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2015)
CommonNames: California Brome; Ripgut Grass; Red Brome
ScientificName: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens
associatedTaxa: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens

File.Name: f617e_f614-002.jpg
Notes: This is yet another mating pair of Bordered Plant Bugs (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - almost certainly Largus californicus. They seem to spend a lot of their time doing this! These true bugs are having their nuptials on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden this morning. I guess that's the larger female on the right, full of eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2015)

File.Name: f623a_f621-037.jpg
Notes:
18478956213 This is our campground music jam at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. The musicians over the weekend included Ewan, Wil, John, Josh, Jake, Zach, Spencer, Charlie, Bill, Aubry, Ted, and a few more who just wandered into the jam. These are people I love! We mostly meet just once a year for the festival, and make music like we've been playing every night! (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 June 2015)

Our camp flag reads: "Night Owl Camp - kickin' out the jams since 1995". I believe our friend Jacob who made the flag got the date wrong - we've actually been going since 1994, so this was our 22nd festival. It's a convenient festival for us because it's just a few miles down the road from home. We've made wonderful friends here. Bless you all!

"Peace, Love, Dirt" - too much sun, too much dust, too much drink, too much smoke, too little sleep... Live Oak is always a memorable weekend!

(I'll get back to my usual butterflies and wildflowers tomorrow.)

File.Name: f623e_f621-001.jpg
Notes:
18477928003 This is our camp the Live Oak music Festival last weekend. It's not quite so neat inside! You can see our camp flag: "Night Owl Camp - kickin' out the jams since 1995". I believe our friend Jacob who made the flag got the date wrong - we've actually been going since 1994, so this was our 22nd festival. We have a way of staying up all night jamming, so if you want to sleep, don't camp by us! But who goes to a music festival to sleep? (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 June 2015)

We've had some irate neighbors before, but not this year. We had to move our camp about 100 feet this year because a large group took "our spot". But we actually ended up in a better place, with some afternoon shade from the trees. It was quite hot this year, up to 100 degrees.

File.Name: f624a_f623-012.jpg
Notes:
19127115962 This is a locally uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly taking moisture in the garden. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name. There's nothing toxic about it, and it has pretty good camouflage among the fallen leaves. You can see the "metal marks" on its wings, like silver threads woven into the scales. I find them most years, usually later in the summer. This year and last are the first times I've found them in June. On the other hand, they are so well camouflaged that it's pure luck to spot one on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2015)

It's good to be taking my usual photos again after the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend!

File.Name: f624b_f623-006.jpg
Notes:
18509527554 This is a twofer of Duskywing (genus Erynnis) butterflies. The one on the right is definitely a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly perched on a flower head of Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in our garden. But I'm not sure of the incoming butterfly on the left. It might be the same kind, or it might be a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly. It does seem to have a white fringe on its hindwings, so these are the only two choices. Sometimes it's hard to tell them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2015)

It's not all peace and love among the butterflies! The incoming butterfly tried to knock the other off the flower. It didn't work.
CommonNames: Duskywing; Funereal Duskywing; Lavender; Mournful Duskywing
ScientificName: Erynnis; Erynnis funeralis; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Erynnis tristis
associatedTaxa: Erynnis; Erynnis funeralis; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Erynnis tristis

File.Name: f624c_f624-007.jpg
Notes: 18511089993 This is a handsome Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly perched on a narrow leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa, Asclepiadaceae) milkweed in the garden. It was working the flowers, but I like this photo best. It's darker - almost black - than the similar Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly that is usually more common in our woods, but lately these dark duskywings have been more common. Sometimes it's hard to tell them apart. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2015)
CommonNames: Funereal Duskywing; Butterfly Weed; Mournful Duskywing
ScientificName: Erynnis funeralis; Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae; Erynnis tristis

File.Name: f624d_f624-018.jpg
Notes: 19105066626 This is a small Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly unrolling its proboscis on a flower head of a Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in our garden this morning. It's probably a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides). (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2015)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: f624e_f623-004.jpg
Notes: 18943265020 This is a "Woolly Bear" caterpillar - the larva of a Tiger Moth (family Arctiidae) in the order Lepidoptera, the first one I've seen this year. My son found this one about 10 feet off the ground on the trunk of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I had to climb up a shaky ladder to get this photo, and try to arrange my tripod on the rungs to be stable. Not bad, considering! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2015)
CommonNames: Woolly Bear; Tiger Moth; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: f624f_f617-013.jpg
Notes:
19134142951 I guess this is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working new flowers of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. It does have speckles in the eyespots of its hind wings. This butterfly was also working new flowers on a nearby California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2015)

This should have been the first photo last Wednesday (June 17), but our kids and grandkids arrived and I never did get around to posting it in the resulting chaos. Here it is.

File.Name: f625a_f625-015.jpg
Notes:
19162577855 This is the fantastic new flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. This is the first flower I've seen this year, though there are quite a few more buds getting ready to open. You can see wild bees and other critters in the flower. It was breezy today so I couldn't get closer. I'll be back! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2015)

I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila aka Apoidae, Hymenoptera. See this photo and Bernhard's comments.

It was a lovely day today with high temps about 80∞ instead of 100∞ like last weekend at the music festival. There was a pretty steady breeze to deal with.

Notes: 19136236796 This is another fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working a new summer flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Hah - I was focused close on the flower when the moth suddenly appeared in my viewfinder! As usual when I try to photograph two things, neither is quite in focus. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2015)

File.Name: f625c_f625-027.jpg
Notes: 18974419428 These are small Ambush Bugs (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a summer flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure they are the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). Dig those crab claw-like front legs! I think I interrupted a private moment... sorry about that. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2015)

File.Name: f625d_f625-048.jpg
Notes: 19135711216 These are stunning new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's a summer wildflower that is not common in our mountains, but they are well worth looking for. This plant was heavily browsed by deer earlier in the season, but it's making up for it. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2015)

File.Name: f625e_f625-040.jpg
Notes: 19164955191 Here is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly digging into a pretty new summer flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. What else can I say? (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2015)

File.Name: f625f_f625-053.jpg
Notes: 18540681223 Here are new summer flowers of the Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. It has distinctive "ternate" leaves in whorls of three, vaguely visible in the background. These are growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. The shrub really does have bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. These are classic "hummingbird flowers" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting on this day. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2015)

File.Name: f626a_f626-004.jpg
Notes:
19003945980 I found this first flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon today. Unfortunately, there won't be many more. Drought and deer and the mystery grub that likes the flower buds have taken down the rest of them. Of course there are other canyons, and I'll have to do some hunting! They are beautiful flowers, almost too pretty to be native. But they are. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2015)

Believe me - if you drive over San Marcos Pass on Highway 154, the last thing you would think of is lilies and orchids in the summer-dry woods... but here they are! (See my [Previous] photo for the orchid.)

I just watched all of President Obama's speech at the funeral of the Rev. Clementa Pinckney. It was a powerful speech, well worth finding if you missed it. Be sure you get the whole thing, about 1/2 hour long.
Locality:
See my [Previous] photo for the orchid.)

File.Name: f626b_f626-013.jpg
Notes:
18569966493 This is the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family - in full flower. The basal leaves completely shrivel up by the time they flower, leaving a bare asparagus-like stalk about two feet tall. See this photo for the early basal leaves. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2015)

Believe me - if you drive over San Marcos Pass on Highway 154, the last thing you would think of is orchids and lilies in the summer-dry woods... but here they are! (See my [Next] photo for the lily.)

File.Name: f626e_f625-017.jpg
Notes: 19163137066 Here are a mating pair of Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) inside a fantastic flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. See this photo two clicks back. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae. See this photo and Bernhard's comments. File.Name: f626f_f625-051.jpg
Notes: 19001469260 Here is a mass of spiky ripe berries of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are fruiting like there's no tomorrow - and I hope that's not an omen! The berries look like the ocean mines in old movies. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside. Tasty! (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2015)

File.Name: f627a_f627-004.jpg
Notes:
19193952756 Here is another photo of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a flower of (I believe) Showy Milkweed (Asclepias speciosa) in the Asclepiadaceae or Apocynaceae) plant family in our garden. We got the plant from my Mom's garden maybe 10+ years ago, and this is the first time its ever flowered because the deer usually eat the buds. It's a California native from further north with large nickle-sized flowers that smell like jasmine. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2015)

It was an interesting weather day! It started warm and humid, with some breeze. Then clouds started moving in producing a "mackerel sky" with more breeze. Finally we got a bit of real rain! It felt like a real desert rain with those large drops. We're getting some tropical moisture from Baja and Arizona, and we actually have a 20% chance of rain tonight, tomorrow, and tomorrow night. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Asclepias speciosa; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Asclepias speciosa; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae

File.Name: f627b_f627-006.jpg
Notes: 19219261875 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working an interesting Milkweed (Asclepias, Asclepiadaceae or Apocynaceae) in the garden. We got the plant from my Mom's garden maybe 10+ years ago, and this is the first time its ever flowered because the deer usually eat the buds. I believe it's the Showy Milkweed (Asclepias speciosa) which is a California native from further north. It has large nickle-sized flowers and it smells like jasmine. It's been slowly spreading, I guess by runners. Butterfly and milkweed flowers is a beautiful combination. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2015)
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Asclepias; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae; Asclepias speciosa
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Asclepias; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae; Asclepias speciosa

File.Name: f627c_f627-014.jpg
Notes:
19032500289 The Wood Rats have been nibbling this cactus fruit on a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden. If you look close, you can see tiny black ants and cactus seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2015)

I believe the wood rats live under our front porch, but I figure it's cool as long as I never see them. We've seen other signs of their presence, such as nibbled succulents. At some point we'll have to do something about it. We live in the woods, and the part of woods lives under our house!

File.Name: f627d_f627-020.jpg
Notes: 19030541150 These are small flowers of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe the plant is var. stenophyllus with skinny filiform leaves. There's a small Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on the upper flower, maybe genus Andrena? If you look close, you can see that it does have loaded pollen baskets on its hind legs. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2015)
ScientificName: Erigeron foliosus; Asteraceae; stenophyllus; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Andrena
associatedTaxa: Erigeron foliosus; Asteraceae; stenophyllus; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Andrena

File.Name: f627e_f627-030.jpg
Notes: 19030123268 This is a fungus on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) growing in our canyon. I have no idea what it is, but I've seen something similar on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves, see this photo. The fungus makes an odd "blister" on the leaf, exposing the dark spore-bearing structures on the reticulated surface within. I guess this is how oak leaves get "skeletonized". (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2015)

File.Name: f627f_f626-006.jpg
Notes:
18596897633 I can't resist posting another photo of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) growing in Kinevan Canyon, taken from slightly above. Unfortunately, there won't be many more. Drought and deer and the mystery grub that eats the flower buds have taken down the rest of them. Of course there are other canyons, and I'll have to do some hunting! They are beautiful flowers, almost too pretty to be native. But they are. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2015)

I didn't use flash or do any photo tricks to get that black background, but I did wait a while for a beam of sunlight to get to the flower with a shaded background. It was worth the wait!

File.Name: f628a_f628-041.jpg
Notes:
19251959385 This is a pretty Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working new flowers of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. This is the first one I've managed to get a photo of this year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2015)

It was a beautiful day today with calm winds, but it was hot and muggy. It wasn't quite "90 and 90", but it was in the 90s today with 30% humidity. I was trying to keep the sweat off my camera lens!
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Strymon melinus; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: f628b_f628-019.jpg
Notes: 18630949143 This is a Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly perched for just a moment on a twig of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not proud of this photo, but it's a record shot. They are well camouflaged and quite camera shy which makes them hard to get close to. I was happy to get this photo even though it is a very tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2015)
CommonNames: Great Basin Wood-Nymph; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak

File.Name: f628c_f628-009.jpg
Notes: 19065002699 This is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on a new plant of an "everlasting" or "cudweed" on a garden path, probably Gnaphalium stramineum or Gnaphalium luteo-album in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe she is ovipositing - look close and you can see her abdomen curled under onto the plant. These ladies are distinctive for having just two large eyespots under their hind wings. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2015)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Gnaphalium stramineum; Gnaphalium luteo-album; Asteraceae

File.Name: f628d_f628-004.jpg
Notes: 19063211420 This is the nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It might be a nymph of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), compare this photo from a few weeks ago. They are surprisingly pretty when they are this size! This one is on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It's looking at me! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2015)

File.Name: f628e_f628-023.jpg
Notes: 19244389862 I guess this is some kind of Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) insect in the insect order Neuroptera, though it's not green at all. (Does that suggest genus Eremochrysa?) The wings have a distinctly blue cast. This one is perched on a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae). Sorry, this is the best photo I got before it took wing. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2015)

File.Name: f628f_f628-032.jpg
Notes: 19250101435 This is another strange and beautiful gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I gently turned the leaf around to get a better look at the gall. You have to remember that these fleshy mushroom-like galls are actually part of the oak tree, though they are induced by a wasp. You can see a couple of new acorns on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2015)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f629a_f629-020.jpg
Notes:
19286422341 This is a male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family on a dry bluff above what's left of the the Santa Ynez River. Its host plant is native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) which grows nearby, see my photo a few photos back. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2015)

Damn, there's a new wildfire on Burton Mesa near Lompoc. This is one of our richest botanical places, and Alice Abela's home ground. We smelled the smoke at home, 30 miles away. It's going to be one hell of a wildfire season.
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Scale Broom; Chaparral Mallow
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae

File.Name: f629b_f629-017.jpg
Notes: 19255960816 This is another uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name. There's nothing toxic about it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2015)
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Calephelis nemesis californica; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f629c_f629-031.jpg
Notes: 19255539056 Here are a pair of small Blue (Polyommatinae, Lepidoptera) butterflies on different composite flowers (Asteraceae) near the river. They both have speckles on their hindwings. The one on the left is definitely an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly, though I'm not so sure about the butterfly on the right. It's smaller and just looks different. The butterfly on the left is working new flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides). The one on the right is visiting new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2015)
CommonNames: Blue; Acmon Blue; Coastal Goldenbush; Scale Broom

File.Name: f629d_f629-034.jpg
Notes: 19275097242 Here are two photos of bees snuggled into the same flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) in the Malvaceae plant family. The bee on the right is a common (all too common) Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera). The bee on the left is a more interesting native - probably Diadasia ochracea, in the tribe Emphorini. These bees seem to specialize in mallow flowers, see the link in Bernard Jacobi's comment below. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2015)

Notes: 19284636931 This is another impossibly-thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). These insects look just like a bit of debris on the leaf until you look closer and see what it is! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2015)

File.Name: f629fe_f629-016.jpg
Notes: 19274511682 This is a new flower on what sure looks like the Carmel Valley Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. arachnoidea) in the Asteraceae plant family - but what's it doing here in Santa Barbara County? I guess it's just a extra-tomentose form of the South Coast Range Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. commutata), but it definitely keys to the Carmel Valley form. Very interesting! See this comparison shot of the different varieties in our mountains. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2015)

File.Name: f630a_f630-035.jpg
Notes:
19285308396 Here is a triptych of photos of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) growing in Kinevan Canyon. I've photographed the flower on the right before. It's right along West Camino Cielo road which is vulnerable to "drive-by pickers" who got the other two buds along the road over the weekend. The two plants on the left are more like it, hidden away in Kinevan Canyon. I count 20 flowers on the large plant, which must be 10-12 feet tall. I didn't get to them - down a steep embankment, past a "bob wire" fence and a fallen tree. Just as well. They are well-protected. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2015)

It was another hot and muggy day, so I walked through the cool Kinevan Canyon looking for something to photograph. I found it! The Mesa Fire on Burton Mesa near Lompoc is mostly contained at 320 acres. There's no smoke in the air at home, 30 miles away - though the winds up the Santa Ynez Valley brought some smoke yesterday.

File.Name: f630b_f630-039.jpg
Notes: 19305034922 These are fantastic new flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Every flower looks just a bit different. These are all growing along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2015)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f630c_f630-043.jpg
Notes: 19314786151 These are fantastic new flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Every flower looks just a bit different. These are all growing along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2015)

File.Name: f630d_f630-013.jpg
Notes: 18690102083 These are new flowers of native Coastal Brookfoam (Boykinia occidentalis) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. It usually grows along flowing summer creeks in our mountains, and it's as common as it's habitat - which is scarce, especially in the drought year! This one happens to be growing on a dry rock outcrop, though there is a developed spring just above which must leak a bit. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2015)

File.Name: f630e_f630-051.jpg
Notes: 19122954880 These are new summer flowers and buds of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on a sandstone rock ledge. It favors cool rocky places. It's a tough one to photograph because the flowers are spread out on long stalks that sway in the slightest breeze. The foliage is quite pretty early in the year, but it almost disappears as summer moves on. They've been flowering for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2015)

File.Name: f630f_f629-038.jpg
Notes: 18689517273 I believe these are native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. There are still new flowers at the ends of the scorpioid flower/seed heads, but these plants will be done pretty soon. They look like... *something* I can't quite put my finger on. Any ideas? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2015)

File.Name: f701a_f701-042.jpg
Notes:
19154303459 These are more fantastic new flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Every flower looks just a bit different. These are all growing along East Camino Cielo road in the mountains. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2015)

When I was here a few days ago, only one plant had flowers - see this photo. Now there are many, and many more getting ready to open. I took the opportunity to document the diversity of these amazing flowers. When I get up here again, I won't know which flowers are new, so this was my chance.

I have a special fondness for our summer flowers that probably goes back to my teaching years when I used to work for a living. School always got busy in April and May before the summer break, so it was a special pleasure to get out in the woods in June to find new photos. I'm retired now, but I still love the summer flowers like this one!

Today was an interesting weather day. It was still warm (about 90∞) but quite cloudy all day. We had a 20% chance of a shower, but nothing has happened yet. It was also breezy, so getting these photos was a challenge. I'll get back to butterflies and other insects tomorrow.
Locality: about 90∞) but quite cloudy all day.  We had a 20% chance of a shower
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f701e_f701-051.jpg
Notes: 19313392176 Here is a nice grouping of summer flowers in our mountains. On the top is California Goldenrod (Solidago californica, Asteraceae), left of that is Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae), and on the bottom is California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica, Onagraceae). The deerweed is just about done, but the other flowers are just beginning. Our hot dry summer isn't the normal time for wildflowers, but they're out there if you look for them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2015)
ScientificName: Solidago californica; Asteraceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: f701f_f701-035.jpg
Notes: 19153144049 These are the first new flowers I've found this year of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers look a lot like their cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), but the foliage is quite different. Like its cousin, these shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This plant is male. It's not very exciting, but this shrub is pretty much endemic here, with a few outliers. It seems quite common in our mountains and deserves a photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 July 2015)
CommonNames: Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; B. pilularis; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; B. pilularis; dioecious

File.Name: f702a_f702-027.jpg
Notes:
19361125322 When I first saw this butterfly, I thought is was a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly. Looking closer on my computer screen, I'm pretty sure it's another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly. They do get yellow colors as the season advances, sometimes enough to confuse you. This one is working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2015)

It's interesting that this one only has one tail, and the other one doesn't look torn by a bird attack. I also saw two more Pale Swallowtails today in the garden at home that also only had one tail. I don't know what this means.

BTW, that nice neutral-colored background in this and the past few photos is our chip seal road.

It was a beautiful day today - moderate breeze, 10 degrees cooler than yesterday, and not so muggy.
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Pale Swallowtail; Cobwebby Thistle
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Papilio eurymedon; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Papilio eurymedon; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f702b_f702-032.jpg
Notes: 19340958976 This is an incoming Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) flying into a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. You can see it's very long proboscis, which is just right for exploring these flowers. Sure this is a lucky shot, but it's the kind of luck that comes from sitting by the flower for an hour this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2015)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f702c_f702-010.jpg
Notes: 18746065293 This is a Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) sharing a flower with an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly and lots of "tiny black ants" which aren't happy about the intruders. The flower is the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family which still has a couple of buds to go. These wasps are not easy to photograph! They are alert with excellent eyesight. They have a way of disappearing whenever I get close. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2015)
CommonNames: Sand Wasp; Umber Skipper; Cobwebby Thistle
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Poanes melane; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: f702d_f702-013.jpg
Notes: 19178870050 This is a handsome Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly working a new flower of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. Those "tiny black ants" in the flower are not happy! The flowers of this plant are just about done, but there are still two more small buds yet to open. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2015)

File.Name: f702e_f630-003.jpg
Notes:
19366211385 This is common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) is butts up in one of the last flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2015)

I'm sorry to see these flowers fade. I've gotten many photos on this shrub.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: f702f_f628-043.jpg
Notes: 19179835589 This is a butt-shot of a handsome Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working new flowers of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It features both its real butt (aka abdomen) and that faux "face" that it wiggles around to distract predators. I have another shot of this same butterfly in this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2015)
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: f703a_f703-014.jpg
Notes:
19369473126 This is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a flower of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden. This beautiful butterfly just appeared in the garden today, and these are my first photos. It looks like an ordinary sulfur with its wing folded, but it looks very different with wings open. See my [Previous] photo for a hint. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2015)

We're heading into the 4th of July weekend which always makes me nervous. Fireworks are illegal in our area, but that doesn't stop some people. (There are rumors that the Mesa Fire near Lompoc was started by kids and fireworks.) There's an extreme danger of wildfire in the mountains right now. At least the heat is turned down a notch into the 80s, but it was breezy.
Locality: There are rumors that the Mesa Fire near Lompoc was started by kids and fireworks.)   There's an extreme danger of wildfire in the mountains right now.  At least the heat is turned down a notch into the 80s

File.Name: f703b_f703-015.jpg
Notes:
19209109999 This is my best shot today of a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly flying between flowers of non-native hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden. I know it's not the best photo, but it does show the iridescence of its upper wings. This beautiful butterfly just appeared in the garden today, and these are my first photos. I'll try to do better! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2015)

Getting a sharp photo of the open wings of the male butterfly must surely be holy grail of local butterfly photographers. It's our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why.

File.Name: f703c_f702-001.jpg
Notes:
19368458386 This is a beautiful little Fly (order Diptera) on a leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae). My first thought was Drosophila. Then my second thought was Muscidae. My third thought is that I don't really care because it is a striking fly! I found it perched here, but it was really interested in a dead wasp an inch away. D'oh - I tripped over my tripod and spoiled the next shot. It's still pretty cool. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2015)

A photo of Helina impuncta at Diptera.info looks pretty close, but maybe not orange enough.
ScientificName: Diptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Drosophila; Helina impuncta
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Drosophila; Helina impuncta

File.Name: f703d_f701-002.jpg
Notes:
19388019892 I guess this is a small bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) ovipositing on a new berry of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The photo on the left isn't so sharp, but you can see its ovipositor slipping under the sepal of what used to be the flower. It's all done in the photo on the right. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2015)

I admit - I'm dumbfounded by these tiny miracles that happen every day that we rarely have the privilege to witness!
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: f703e_f630-004.jpg
Notes: 18771427664 This is a rather large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. They have wonderful patterned eyes and antennae like a TV. This one is taking moisture from a leaf in the mint patch of our garden. (We just watered.) The maggots and pupae live in our compost bins, see this photo. I believe they are good maggots to have in your compost, possibly predators. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2012)

File.Name: f703f_f628-005.jpg
Notes:
19207148129 This is the worst biting fly in our mountains. I swatted it on my arm, then posed it on my knee while sitting down - a reach with my 105mm macro lens. I believe it's a Biting Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera), maybe a Rocky Mountain Bite Fly in the genus Symphoromyia, but I can't find it at BugGuide. It does kind of look like a Snipe Fly with that segmented abdomen. These flies have a distinct brown-orange color. They are the worst biters in our mountains because they are so fast and alert - they fly away before you can swat them. Fortunately they are only on the wing for a few weeks. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2012)

Compare my photos here and here. It's interesting that I can't see any tool for puncturing skin. I guess it's a "slash & lapper" - like a deer fly.
CommonNames: Biting Snipe Fly; Rocky Mountain Bite Fly; Snipe Fly

File.Name: f704a_f704-058.jpg
Notes:
18800538934 Here are more red, white and blue flowers for Independence Day! I found all these native flowers in our mountains today. Here are:

Red - Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family, still hanging on;

White - Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family - this is an unusual one, but they are quite variable; and

Blue - Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family, still hanging on.

Happy birthday USA! It's a good day to reflect on our revolutionary heritage. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2015)

I always dread the Fourth of July because of people and fireworks and wildfires, but the weather is cooperating today. It's been mostly cool, in the 70s, and not too breezy. I did go out today, up into the mountains to look for red, white, and blue flowers. Today has been quiet so far, and I hope it stays that way. Now for some BBQ!
CommonNames: Scarlet Larkspur; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily; Chaparral Penstemon
ScientificName: Delphinium cardinale; Ranunculaceae; Calochortus weedii; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Penstemon heterophyllus; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Delphinium cardinale; Ranunculaceae; Calochortus weedii; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Penstemon heterophyllus; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: f704c_f704-028.jpg
Notes: 19415672762 This mighty California Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus, Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) has captured an interesting Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Maybe the unfortunate bee is genus Melissoides with those shaggy chaps-like hairs on its legs? It's a tough world out there - hard for the bee but good for the assassin bug. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2015)

File.Name: f704d_f704-045.jpg
Notes:
19421654095 First you notice the pretty little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly, then the flowers of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). In between is a small Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that has captured the butterfly - look just left of the butterfly's head. Be careful little butterflies, some of these flowers have teeth! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2015)

The angle of the unfortunate butterfly's wing is just right with the sun to bring out amazing iridescent blue colors. So beautiful in death...

File.Name: f704e_f704-056.jpg
Notes: 19421427705 This is another fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working still-new flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. These moths are constantly in the air, hovering before flowers. They look so soft and furry! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2015)

Yeah, the flower is a bit overexposed, but the butterflies look good. I've been seeing lots of them lately. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2015)

File.Name: f705a_f705-023.jpg
Notes:
19452424775 This is another large caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly munching a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae) in the canyon. The plant is an invasive weed in our mountains, and the caterpillars do some damage to it. I tried to tickle its forehead with a grass stem to get it to bring out its brilliant orange "< href="http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/14261403394/">osmetarium", but this one didn't want to play. You can make out the tiny white egg of a parasite above the second proleg. I have a photo of the adult butterfly here. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2015)

This native butterfly favors this non-native host plant. We always save a few plants for the butterflies and try to remember to trim the flowers before they set seed. Two stumpers remain. What was its host plant before Fennel was introduced? And why don't we have many more butterflies since their host plant is so common in the mountains??

It was another cool day only in the 70s, but the wind kicked up. I think it was "fog wind". There was some fog this morning from the valley side, and it soon cleared without a breeze. Then around 11:30, the fog monster appeared over the mountains from the ocean side. The marine layer fog never did quite come over the pass, but the winds did. I still managed to get a few decent photos.

File.Name: f705b_f705-013.jpg
Notes:
19445458072 On the left is one of the last flowers of Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. And on the right is an important pollinator of these flowers - a Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera, probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. It was perched for just a moment on a leaf of the plant before it flew away. My photo is over-exposed and not quite sharp, but you can see a bit of its long proboscis folded below its abdomen. These flies seem made for the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2015)

These flies are camera shy and fast! I'm still trying to get a decent photo:

http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/9253036022/
http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/9187096001/
http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/9171867450/
http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7498271622/

It must be tough being an insect that is dependent on one flower when that plant stops flowering for the year. The sand wasps are facing the same problem as the thistle runs its course.
CommonNames: Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Metallic Small-Headed Fly

File.Name: f705c_f705-018.jpg
Notes:
19263282910 Here are the largest and one of the smallest galls on different Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. Both are the work of tiny Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but the galls vary greatly in size.

On the left is the large gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis). It's a couple of inches across, about the size of a small tangerine. These galls generally have a more interesting texture than the similar "oak apple" galls on Scrub Oak or Valley Oak.

On the right is the tiny gall of a "Pumpkin Gall Wasp" (Dryocosmus minusculus). It's 490 pixels across in my 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.9 mm. (See this photo for how I figured.)

I found both of these galls today, the first of either kind that I've found this year. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2015)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Cynipid Wasps; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Pumpkin Gall Wasp

I'm pretty sure the fly is genus Eristalis. What a face - and proboscis! It's a remarkable honeybee-mimic. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2015)

There's the tiny stalked egg of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) in the upper-right.

File.Name: f705f_f704-005.jpg
Notes: 19263282910 This is the first flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family that I've seen this summer. The flowers look like they have stockings on their pistils, and they close up during the heat of the day. You have to love these summer flowers that flower against all expectations in the driest part of our year! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2015)

File.Name: f706a_f706-022.jpg
Notes:
19297523379 This handsome little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) is flying into a new flower head of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. I watched this fly for a while. It never touched down, preferring to get pollen in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2015)

D'oh! I knew we had old friends coming over for dinner, but I thought it was Tuesday night. Nope, they came tonight, and I didn't notice until they drove up. Oh well, tomorrow is free, and I already have a bunch of good photos ready to go.
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: f707a_f707-007.jpg
Notes:
19484120806 Extraordinary camouflage!

I believe this is the tiny "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). It's less than 1/4 inch long. They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they become the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with stamens and pistils of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2015)

I wasn't feeling very inspired for new photos today, since I already had all the photos I didn't get around to posting yesterday when friends arrived. I pulled up a chair at the buckwheat growing by the mailbox, and actually looked for this critter. I noticed it wiggling when the wind wasn't, and knew I had one. It was cool and breezy all day, working up to a pretty good blow this evening.
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: f707b_f707-004.jpg
Notes: 19509677305 This odd beast is a "compost maggot". I happened to notice the sun was shining into our compost bin this morning, so I dug my hand into the sticky black mess and pulled out a handful of these maggots. They don't like light and quickly crawled away, but I separated this one onto an oak leaf on the side for a photo shoot. I believe it's the maggot of a Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera. It's rather large, almost an inch long, and quite active. I have a photo of the attractive adult fly here. I believe the maggots are beneficial in your compost, see the article at Wikipedia. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2015)

File.Name: f707c_f706-017.jpg
Notes: 19321274360 This is a striking Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on the hairy edge of a fantastic flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family. I believe the bee is subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae. See this photo and Bernhard's comments. "Calochortus Bee" would be an appropriate name for these bees since I don't find them anywhere else. They are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2015)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily; Calochortus Bee

File.Name: f707d_f706-029.jpg
Notes: 19321100328 This is a particularly gorgeous flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family. Every flower looks just a bit different, raging from almost white (center flower here) to yellow to dark maroon like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2015)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f707e_f706-007.jpg
Notes:
19320466690 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of (I think) a native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing in our garden. I like that blue pollen! The tiny bee has pollen baskets of its hind legs and (if you look close) a beard. Maybe it's a Mining Bee (genus Andrena, family Andrenidae) - though I find these tiny black bees really confusing. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2015)

The plant is a native perennial that grows as a guest in our garden. It's quite glandular. It doesn't seen to mind summer water at all, and will flower all summer if the deer don't eat it first.

File.Name: f707f_f706-030.jpg
Notes: 19501363012 Here is a trio of large galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on a branch of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I reckon the galls are new, though they look sun-checked and weathered. Some little rodent has mistaken the one on the left for a real apple. I guess the bitter taste - rich with tannins - is an effective deterrent. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2015)

File.Name: f708a_f708-037.jpg
Notes:
19353105089 This is a dramatic shot of a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) - probably Pepsis mildei because of those orange antennae, and maybe a male because the are straight. It's working a new flower head of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river, scattering the tiny beetles in the process. They are our largest wasps - almost 2 inches long. The color of these large wasps is amazing - "so blue it's black" with those contrasting orange wings. They are said to have a remarkably painful sting, though they've never bothered me. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2015)

It's interesting to think that these wasps that are hell with tarantula spiders visit flowers!

The marine layer fog moved in this morning, and didn't leave home until almost 4:00. It never got out of the low 60s, more like June than July. I went to the river to find some sun. I did, but I also found some breeze. There were large thunderheads over the back mountains.

They're not mating but they sure are thinking about it! (Butterfly foreplay?) A moment before, the female was all aquiver - but I prefer this sharper photo. See my [Previous] photo for separate shots of the genders. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2015)

The female has darker colors above, whereas the male is mostly white with dark chevrons along the wing margins. See my [Next] photo of the two together. Their host plant is Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) which grows nearby. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2015)

File.Name: f708e_f708-006.jpg
Notes: 19542226581 Here is a mating pair of Bagrada Bugs (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. These bugs are native to northern Africa, and are now a major agricultural pest in Lompoc. They've been here for a while. If these bugs are really destructive pests as reported, I don't think there's any chance of containing them. They seem well established in the backcountry on these native shrubs. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2015)

This photo is discreetly dark, in the shade of the central pedestal of the flower. I believe the bees are subgenus Pygoperdita of the genus Perdita of the Andrenidae, Anthophila, Hymenoptera. File.Name: f709a_f709-002.jpg
Notes:
19558853772 I believe this is a mature Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. This may well be the adult of the nymph I show in this photo as well as this photo. All of these photos were taken on the same leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) along our driveway. I guess that - like me - these critters don't travel much. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2015)

It was a crummy day for new photos today, mostly cloudy and breezy all day. It never got out of the 60s today. I wore sweat pants and a long sleeve shirt all day and never regretted it. I'm not complaining, but this is unusual for July.
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: f709b_f709-008.jpg
Notes:
19539182916 These are ripening seedpods of native Soap Plant aka Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. The light-colored jackets they are wearing are the remains of the flowers. See this photo from last May. I never did get back in the evening to get a photo of the open flowers. So it goes. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2015)

*Sigh* - sometime in the last week the county or the local Volunteer Fire Department weed-whacked this spot along the road. Most of the plants and their seeds are gone into the shredder, but they missed this one. I wish these road crews had someone aboard who could tell them what not to cut. Then again, these interesting natives may be here just because the roadside was cut!

File.Name: f709c_f708-004.jpg
Notes: 19377046128 This is another uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name. There's nothing toxic about it. You can see the "metal marks" on its wings, like silver threads woven into the scales. I've seen more of these than usual his year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2015)

Note the little Beetle (Coleoptera) in the foreground. This is the same wasp I have a "butt shot" of in my [Previous] photo. See my [Next] photo for more of this insect. In case you can't find it, it has arrows on its abdomen that point to its butt! HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2015)

D'oh - I see that I forgot to post this and my [Previous] photo to the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group. It's not the first time! Well, HBBBT anyway!

File.Name: f709f_f703-001.jpg
Notes:
19537850176 This is a walking-away "butt shot" of a nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I found this one on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It has a grumpy face on it's butt! HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2015)

This one might be a nymph of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). I got this photo last Friday. In the meantime, I think it's made its final molt to become an adult. See my first photo today, on the same leaf.

File.Name: f710a_f710-030.jpg
Notes:
19404020840 This is another Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly perched for a moment on the dirt along my trail today. It's not a great photo - the butterfly's head is obscured by a branch that got in the way. But this is an uncommon local butterfly, which is hard to photograph. They are well camouflaged and quite camera shy which makes them hard to get close to. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2015)

It was sunnier and just a bit warmer today than yesterday, but still pretty cool for July - not much more than 70∞.

File.Name: f710b_f710-019.jpg
Notes:
19565419196 Here are three interesting - and beautiful! - "summer galls" on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. All are the work of different Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). I found them all in the woods today. From left to right, here are:

* A colony of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus), on the underside of a leaf;
* An unpinched gall of (I guess) the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae), on the underside of a leaf ; and
* A gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella), on a twig.

The summer galls are just beginning, and I hope there will be many more. The nicely fill the gap as insects become scarce in late summer. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2015)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Wasps; Crystalline Gall Wasp; Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp

File.Name: f710c_f710-008.jpg
Notes:
19404344719 These are a pair of odd galls on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I wonder if these are the actual galls of the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? Compare this image by Joyce Gross. These might be the same galls that produce black acorns and "oak honey" on both Coast Live and Scrub Oaks, see this photo from last year. I've seen a few black acorns this year, but no honey flows. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2015)

Does it sound like I'm confused by this? I am.

You can see the shriveled pistils of the female flowers on the fruit, compare this photo. I'm not sure if this is normal seed opening or the work of critters. Is that a maroon-colored seed on the right? (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2015)

File.Name: f710e_f710-004.jpg
Notes: 19402323208 I found this interesting Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning. I'm not sure what kind it. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2015)

I got this photo last Saturday, and I've saving it for the Fly Day Friday group. I have another photo of this remarkable fly here. There's the tiny stalked egg of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) in the upper-right.

ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Eristalis; Chrysopidae; Neuroptera

File.Name: f711a_f711-007.jpg
Notes:
19619221375 These are the underwings of a flawless American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on the Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in our garden this morning. See my [Previous] photo for the upper wings of the same butterfly. They remind me of the inside-covers of fine old books, like "marbled endpapers" - with eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)

Today was still cool and breezy - but a little warmer and less breezy than it's been for the last week. I'm not complaining - the cool weather has been a treat!

File.Name: f711b_f711-003.jpg
Notes:
19622065631 This is a flawless American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on the Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in our garden this morning. It must be newly emerged to have such brilliant colors. This one is interesting because the first bar on the edge of its forewing is orange rather than white, like a West Coast Lady (V. annabella). It does have that white spot lower down that these butterflies usually have, and it definitely has just two large eyespots below on the hindwings, see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)

I've been thinking about beauty... Butterflies emerge from their chrysalis beautiful, and it's downhill from there as life and birds take their toll. We're different in that we grow into our beauty. On the other hand, tattered butterflies have a certain beauty too. See this old photo from Fotolog on 28 July 2005. It's another American Lady butterfly, making do. Is it beautiful?
CommonNames: American Lady; Lavender; West Coast Lady
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; V. annabella
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginensis; Lavandula; Lamiaceae; V. annabella

File.Name: f711c_f711-001.jpg
Notes: 19429388128 This is another photo of the small Robber Fly (Dioctria, Asilidae, Diptera) perched today on a leaf close to where it was yesterday in the mint patch of the garden. You can see its face a little better in this photo. Thanks again Alice for helping me figure out what it is. I'm not used to robber flies being so small! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)

File.Name: f711d_f711-013.jpg
Notes: 19610180582 This is another photo of a newly-mature Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. This photo is on a different leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) along our driveway, just a few inched away from its original leaf. It's stepping onto a leaf of native Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae) - trying to get away from me. See this photo for more of the story. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry; Western Bracken Fern
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: f711e_f711-022.jpg
Notes:
19430234789 This is a family shot of one plant of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family. Starting from the upper-right, here are a bud, and opening bud, a flower in all its glory, and finally a spent flower in the upper-left. What's missing is a seed pod, but you can't have everything. They are fabulous summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)

(Mark, I think we'll do just fine when you come on Wednesday. The plants have lots of buds.)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f711f_f711-026.jpg
Notes: 19428615278 This is the first ripe nut I've seen this year on native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers that smell like perfume, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting edible nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)

File.Name: f712a_f712-018.jpg
Notes:
19460615720 This is a mating pair of atypical Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae, Diptera), maybe genus Thevenetimyia or Geron? They are having their nuptials on a leaf of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. This is a photo I'm proud of! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)

It was warmer today, into the 80s, with manageable breeze. Not too hot, not too cool - in fact it was just right!
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Thevenetimyia; Geron; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Thevenetimyia; Geron; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: f712b_f712-010.jpg
Notes:
19026932593 This is another large caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - showing its osmeterium! That's the brilliant orange "thing" on it's forehead that looks like gummy bear antlers. There's some info at Wikipedia. It's an old naturalist trick to tickle its forehead with a grass stem to bring it out. This time it worked, but I needed a helper. By the time I got back to my camera, it was already half withdrawn. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)

This caterpillar is munching a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae), as they all do. See the comments with this photo for some discussion. We always save a few plants of this weed for the butterflies and try to remember to trim the flowers before they set seed.

File.Name: f712c_f712-022.jpg
Notes: 19640466822 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic Clearwing Moth (family Sesiidae, Lepidoptera) on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). As I was trying to adjust my tripod for a killer shot, the moth fell away. It might be spider-prey, but I can't see it. Maybe the moth is a Sycamore Borer (Synanthedon resplendens)? There were Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) trees nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)
CommonNames: Clearwing Moth; Coast Live Oak; Sycamore Borer; Western Sycamore
ScientificName: Sesiidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Synanthedon resplendens; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
