File.Name: f712d_f712-030.jpg
Notes: 19459062810 This is a different mature Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. It has interesting turquoise colors on its body. This one has its front legs to threaten the invader - me! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: f712e_f712-007.jpg
Notes: 19646624795 This is another large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera in the mint patch of our garden. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. They have wonderful patterned eyes and antennae like a TV. These are our "compost flies" - see this photo. They might actually do some good in the compost bin, see the article at Wikipedia. I'm really surprised I don't see more of these flies. There are dozens (at least!) of the large maggots in the compost bin. Maybe we have an invasion coming up? (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)

File.Name: f712f_f712-040.jpg
Notes:
19638965932 On my walk on Friday, I had to wade through a patch of non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Stipa miliacea aka Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family. *Sigh* This is what I found in the bottom of my shoes after I got home, with a metric ruler for scale.

I reckon this has become the worst weed in our mountains, that spreads along trails into the backcountry. The seeds don't stick in your socks, but they are tiny and easy to transport. See this photo for the patch I had to walk through. Normally I wear gaiters when I have to walk through bristly grasses like foxtail or ripgut, but I didn't think of it for this grass. I should have. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)
CommonNames: Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice

File.Name: f713a_f713-003.jpg
Notes:
19668150142 "Strange fruit hanging from the buckwheat flowers..."

This strange fruit is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) that has become a predator's prey. I believe the predator is a Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), but I can only see one antenna and a leg in that greenish shadow above the bee's head. I found this drama on a flowering shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by our mailbox today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2015)

I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, but I stopped by the mailbox on my way out and found this photo. It was another pleasant day - in the 80s, but not too hot. The breeze picked up by the time I got home, so I called it a day.
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: f713b_f713-007.jpg
Notes:
19648442526 Here are colonies of new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The galls are made of individual capsules that (usually) grow together to make a brilliant mass like this. It beats me why a gall insect which wants to be left alone would make such an obvious mass as this! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 July 2015)

Not all the scrub oaks at Lake Cachuma are looking dead - compare my [Previous] photo. This one looks fine, and has galls as well.
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: f713c_f713-012.jpg
Notes:
19486252738 I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I made my usual stop by Lake Cachuma on the way. I was shocked by the Scrub Oaks (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) which look half dead. No doubt this is drought stress. We've had 18.85 inches of rain on San Marcos Pass this year, but Lake Cachuma has only had 9.17 inches - less than half. It's showing. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 July 2015)

I was actually looking for galls today, and I found some - see my [Next] photo. But any galls that were initiated on those brown leaves have failed.

File.Name: f713d_f711-016.jpg
Notes: 19678113021 This is a twofer of the nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, Orthoptera) and a little "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera). I'm not sure what either are. I found the grasshopper a foot away from the one in my [Previous] photo, but I'm sure it's a different kind - maybe a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes)? I'm not at all sure what the bug is, though family Lygaeidae comes first to mind. Whatever they are, it's kind of neat to see them together in the garden like this. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Lygaeidae

File.Name: f713e_f711-017.jpg
Notes: 19677749641 This is the handsome nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera - probably Oedaleonotus phryneicus in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). This one is perched in the mint patch of the garden. See this photo for a tiny version of (maybe) the same insect - in the same place at least. Now it has little nubs of "training wings". (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)

File.Name: f713f_f712-032.jpg
Notes: 19484947940 This is an older tumor-like gall of a Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia californica) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. See this photo for a fresher specimen. This one is interesting because you can see exit holes - including two that look like they're just being made. These galls are said to be particularly hard hit by parasites. Those exit holes may not all be the midge flies that induce the gall! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush Bud Gall Midge; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Rhopalomyia californica; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: f714a_f714-006.jpg
Notes:
19678259926 This is a stunning Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly perched for a moment on a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. It's confusing, because our Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies get creamy colors in the summer, compare this photo. But I don't think they ever get this yellow! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2015)

Today was nice enough, but hazy and a bit cooler - barely up to 80∞. We had some marine layer fog this morning - from the valley side, which means no "fog winds". (I'm not sure why, but it's true.)
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; White Sage; Pale Swallowtail

File.Name: f714b_f714-002.jpg
Notes:
19516244658 "Sometimes I get to sit and think..."

This is the handsome nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera - probably Oedaleonotus phryneicus in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). This one is perched in the mint patch of the garden. It has the little nubs of "training wings" - but this hopper is short-winged anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2015)

File.Name: f714c_f714-033.jpg
Notes: 19703893315 This is a mating pair of small Ambush Bugs (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a summer flower head of native staminate Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. This shrub is pretty much endemic in our mountains, with a few outliers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 July 2015)

File.Name: f714d_f714-039.jpg
Notes: 19081109254 This is another family shot of one plant of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family with lots of flowers. There are flowers, buds, and a seed pod in this photo. They are fabulous summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2015)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily

File.Name: f714e_f714-011.jpg
Notes: 19516620659 The whorled leaves on the stem should give a clue what this native plant is. These are new female pistillate flowers (left) and male staminate flowers (right) of Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) in the Rubiaceae plant family. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants that look quite different. Both plants have small yellow flowers, but the lady plants look white from a distance because of the developing fruit, while the male plants look yellow. Galium plants are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. This one is a proper sub-shrub, not a climber. It's the last of our natives to flower. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 July 2015)

File.Name: f714f_f714-019.jpg
Notes: 19515017900 These are the tangled stems and flowers of non-native Skeleton Weed (Chondrilla juncea) in the Asteraceae plant family. I hope this isn't the next "worst weed" to invade our mountains. So far, I've only noticed it in a few places - Live Oak Camp, Paradise, and this infestation along East Camino Cielo road in the mountains. They are slowly spreading. They do have rather pretty flowers that remind me of genus Stephanomeria with those long pistils and sleeve-like stamens. They make me nervous. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 July 2015)

File.Name: f715a_f715-027.jpg
Notes:
19548441839 This is another mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family by the river today. I guess it's a female because of the large size and coiled antennae. It looks a lot like the (probably) Pepsis mildei in my [Previous] photo except for the size and the color of the antennae. I found them about 20 feet apart on the same part of the dry river bed. I can't say if they are the same or different. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2015)

My flicker-friend couldn't make it today to look for Calochortus as planned, and the marine layer clouds and wind spilled over the pass this morning. So I went to the dry Santa Ynez River looking for some sun. It was sunny, but also quite breezy - which made getting photos difficult. It was surprisingly cool today, only in the 70s.
ScientificName: Pepsis; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Pepsis mildei

File.Name: f715b_f715-015.jpg
Notes: 19548129379 This is another mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) - probably Pepsis mildei because of those orange antennae, and probably a male because they are straight. That tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) on the flower head in the lower-left has a ringside seat! I found these critters on native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2015)

File.Name: f715c_f715-029.jpg
Notes: 19547491139 This isn't a great photo, but it's a remarkable fly! Look close - it has two wings, halteres, and fly-like eyes and antennae. This is a wasp-mimic Thick-headed Fly (Conopidae, Diptera) on a flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). The fly is surely genus Physocephala, but I'm not sure if it's Physocephala burgessi or Physocephala texana. It's one of those. The fly took wing a moment after I got this photo, and flew with the wind. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2015)

File.Name: f715d_f715-035.jpg
Notes: 19112999613 This is another mature Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I found this one on spent flower heads of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. I know, I've been showing a lot these lately, but I can only show what I find! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2015)

File.Name: f715e_f715-001.jpg
Notes:
19544899650 Here is a collage of composite wildflowers in the Asteraceae plant family that are just starting to flower along the Santa Ynez River. Clockwise from the upper-left, here are:

* Cliff Aster (Malacothrix saxatilis) - it appears to be var. tenuifolia which is quite glabrous, though it might be a less hairy form of var. commutata which seems more common here;
* California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia), not flowering yet in the mountains;
* California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica), on the river bed;
* Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides, on the river bed.

I'm not sure why so many of our summer flowers are composites, but here they are. The most important one for pollinators is Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum), see my [Previous] photo. It was quite windy along the river today, so making a collage of small photos seemed right. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2015)
CommonNames: Cliff Aster; California Aster; California Brickellbush; Hairy Golden Aster; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia; commutata; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Brickellia californica; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia; commutata; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Brickellia californica; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: f715f_f715-018.jpg
Notes:
19725228122 This is a whole flowering shrub of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family, in its natural habitat. It's growing in a dry overflow channel of the Santa Ynez River. This whole section of the river bed was burned by the White Fire a couple of years ago. This flowering shrub is a stump sprout with some old burned stems visible. n fact, all the plants you can see in this photo are stump sprouts. This is our most desert-like habitat, but it was flooded last year by our good December rains! Hah, that's two paradoxical situations - a flood in a drought year, and a desert-like habitat in a river bed! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2015)

Scale Broom is one of my favorite summer-flowering shrubs. Many insects depend on its flowers. It keeps the pollinators alive during our dry summer.

File.Name: f716a_f716-011.jpg
Notes:
19141055893 This is a small Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a still-new flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The butterfly is probably a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides). Those long stamens and pistil are overkill with a critter like this, but look at this photo of a larger cousin. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2015)

Today was much like yesterday - marine layer fog and "fog wind" in the morning, then relatively cool and breezy in the afternoon.
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Woolly Blue Curls; Woodland Skipper
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: f716b_f716-019.jpg
Notes:
19573180618 This is a bumper crop of nearly ripe fruit of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. The ripe red fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit and pits are actually poisonous. The flesh is thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching and boiling them to remove the bitter and poisonous "cyanogenic glycosides" that they contain. (Store-bought cherry pits and apple seeds also contain them!) When the fruit are dead ripe, the birds and critters usually get them first. I've never tried preparing the cherry pits, but I've always wanted to. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2015)

Some native shrubs have no fruit/seeds at all in this drought year. Others like this one are fruiting like there's no tomorrow. I hope that's not an omen!

File.Name: f716c_f716-020.jpg
Notes:
19138294034 These are new flowers of native Laurel Sumac (Malosma laurina) in the in the Anacardiaceae plant family. My Mom used to react to this like its cousin Poison Oak, so be careful if you're sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2015)

This shrub is common in our mountains up to about 1500 feet elevation, but beyond that it gets scarce. There is one plant at the top of San Marcos Pass right where East Camino Cielo road turns off from Highway 154. I got this photo near the junction of the highway and Painted Cave Road. That's right on the edge of what I consider to be San Marcos Pass - from the Trout Club to the base of Cold Springs Bridge, and from Lizard Mouth to Knapp's Castle.

File.Name: f716d_f716-012.jpg
Notes: 19760305885 These are small white berries of native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Galium is an interesting genus partly because some native species have berries, while others have dry nutlets. I have no idea if the berries are edible. They're too small to matter much, and there's something unappetizing about white berries. They disappear from the plants pretty quickly, so something out there likes them! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2015)

File.Name: f716e_f716-010.jpg
Notes:
19573332179 These are pretty little flowers of native Common Rush-rose aka Peak Rush-rose (Helianthemum scoparium) in the Cistaceae plant family. It's an interesting family that also includes several non-native species in the genus Cistus that become weedy in the mountains. This one is a bushy perennial with the stature of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) that grows in dry places, often on burns as a fire-follower. I usually find it without leaves, as a "broom" plant. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2015)

I figure than every native plant that is still flowering in this drought summer deserves some respect - as well as a photo!
ScientificName: Helianthemum scoparium; Cistaceae; Cistus; Lotus scoparius
associatedTaxa: Helianthemum scoparium; Cistaceae; Cistus; Lotus scoparius

File.Name: f716f_f711-012.jpg
Notes:
19571222950 This is the skinny butt of a Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It's crawling away from me on a leaf in the mint patch. I have lots of photos of these impossibly-thin insects, this one is the most recent. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2015)

D'oh! I posted "beautiful bug butt" photos last Thursday and forgot to add them to the group. I'll add them this week.

File.Name: f717a_f717-005.jpg
Notes:
19761499596 This is looking closer at the "tent" of web-spinning caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. You can see them in there, both inside and out, along with lots of frass. They made this web on a flowering stem of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae) in the canyon, see my [Previous] photo. This is the first such colony I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2015)

This is the work of next year's butterflies' caterpillars. The caterpillars may kill a branch or two, but they do not do too much damage to the plants. They spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. This is the first "tented stem" I've seen this year. Tents now = butterflies next year!

It was a tough day for new photos because I had to attend a meeting in the middle of the day. Fortunately the meeting was at our house, so at least I didn't have to travel far. It was clear today without morning fog, but quite breezy. It felt like "fog winds" without the fog, so maybe the marine layer didn't quite spill over the mountains, but the winds at the top of the temperature inversion did?

associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; frass; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

Notes:
19599357298 There are still pretty new flowers of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae) in the canyon - and now there's something new! There's a colony of web-spinning caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on the right. This is the first such colony I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2015)

File.Name: f717c_f717-011.jpg
Notes: 19599046460 These are small flowers of native Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe the plant is var. stenophyllus with those skinny filiform leaves. I love our summer flowers that appear during our hot dry summer when nothing should be able to produce flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2015)

File.Name: f717d_f717-015.jpg
Notes: 19598872150 This is a small flower and fruit of native Spanish Clover (Acmispon americanus aka Lotus purshianus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The flower is quite pretty if you get down on your hands and knees to see it! It seems kind of weedy, growing along the edge of the road in our canyon, but it is flowering in mid-summer during the drought which makes it special. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2015)

File.Name: f717e_f717-010.jpg
Notes:
19163504954 This is just a fly (order Diptera) in the mint patch in the garden. I guess it's some kind of Muscidae - superfamily Muscoidea at least. It has pretty orange colors, maybe the same kind of fly as this photo, though it doesn't seem as bristly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2015)

My favorite fly photo this week is this one, but I already posted it a few days ago - which breaks the rules for the Fly Day Friday group. But if you came here from that group, check it out!

File.Name: f717f_f712-001.jpg
Notes: 19785742025 This is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) putting its hands together on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. There are too many different kinds to sort through, so I'll leave it at that. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2015)

File.Name: f718a_f718-008.jpg
Notes:
19813761655 This is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly perched for a moment on a leaf in the mint patch of our garden. These butterflies have a brown trailing edge on their hindwings, unlike our other duskywings with a white trim. This one has been working the flowers of a nearby hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2015)

We had hurricane weather today! Not a real hurricane in California, but moisture from Hurricane Dolores (now downgraded to a tropical storm) is streaming over our mountains. No rain to speak of yet, though we had a few drops and there has been thunder. See this photo from today.

Julie and I are heading off to our cabin near Lone Pine in the Eastern Sierra to meet our kids and grandkids. Our original plan was to leave on Monday, but we may put it off a day because of the weather. Both our home and Lone Pine are under flash flood watches through Monday!
Locality: now downgraded to a tropical storm) is streaming over our mountains.  No rain to speak of yet

File.Name: f718b_f718-003.jpg
Notes: 19805907822 This is a neat little Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a stem of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. (That's a green leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae) in the background. The two grow together here.) The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. It's small, a bit over 1/4 inch long. I looked around at BugGuide, and I'm still not sure what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2015)
ScientificName: Membracidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Membracidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: f718c_f718-013.jpg
Notes: 19786844356 This is a fine new flower head of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. The long pistils of the flower look like they've been dipped in sugar white wearing white stockings. These flowers normally shut down pretty early in the morning, but they stay fresh longer on cloudy days like today. I was lucky to get this shot during a moment when the sun came out. I love our summer flowers that appear during our hot dry summer when nothing should be able to produce flowers! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2015)

File.Name: f718d_f718-024.jpg
Notes: 19624768798 This is a fine group of summer-flowering plants of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. I love our summer flowers that appear during our hot dry summer when nothing should be able to produce flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2015)

File.Name: f718e_f718-025.jpg
Notes: 19786190006 This is an abundance of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus or Calochortus weedii) in the Liliaceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road in our mountains. It's odd, but there seem to be even more flowers in this drought year! Other plants in the photo are the white-stems of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia) and flower buds of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa), both in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2015)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily; California Aster; Sawtooth Goldenbush

File.Name: f718g_f718-019.jpg
Notes: 19189423074 We had hurricane weather today! Not a real hurricane, but moisture from Hurricane Dolores (now downgraded to a tropical storm) is streaming over our mountains. This is looking towards Mission Pine Mountain at the layers of clouds that remind me of the atmosphere of Jupiter. We have had some sprinkles - like virga that made it to the ground. We're under a flash flood watch until Monday. I hope it rains like hell! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2015)

File.Name: f719a_f719-016.jpg
Notes:
19844388805 This is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a still-open flower of succulent Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family growing in our canyon at home. You can see a hint of the dogface pattern on its upper wings - but they always perch with their wings folded up. The flower in tangled up in a shrub of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)

Rain to the north of us, and rain to the south, but just a few drops here. I feel cheated! But there is still hope. Its clouded up nicely with "showers likely" tonight according to the NWS, and we're still under a flash flood watch until Monday evening. We've decided to delay heading off to our cabin near Lone Pine in the Eastern Sierra to meet our kids and grandkids. We'll leave early Tuesday morning. I may even have time to post a few photos tomorrow. We'll be back in a week.
CommonNames: California Dogface; Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Bitter Gooseberry
ScientificName: Colias eurydice; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: f719b_f719-012.jpg
Notes: 19656161970 This is a small advance army of web-spinning caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly colonizing a new stem of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae) in the canyon. If you look at this photo, they are moving from the stem of the right to the stem on the left. The stems are touching, so the transition was easy. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: f719c_f719-005.jpg
Notes: 19843368855 Here are two photo of adult (left) and nymph (right) "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on seed heads of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. I found them on the same shrub, just a few inches from each other. They don't look anything alike, and probably aren't. The adult on the left may be the Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens (family Pentatomidae). I'm not sure about the nymph of the right. At first I thought they might be the same, but the nymph has 4-segmented antennae which rules out the family Pentatomidae. At least I did find them together! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)
CommonNames: White Sage; Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug

Notes: 19656402149 This is the same Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) nymph as my [Previous] photo. It's chowing down on that long stamen just left of center in that photo. I guess the grasshopper is a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes). The plant is native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. How cute! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)

File.Name: f719e_f719-024.jpg
Notes: 19654516880 This is the nymph of a cute little Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on the first flower I've seen this year in the canyon of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm not sure, but maybe the grasshopper is a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes)? Notice that tall flower stamen by itself just left of center that seems to be catching its eye, and see my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)

File.Name: f719f_f719-020.jpg
Notes: 19847141191 I'm pretty sure this is a new gall of the Coyote Brush Stem Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia baccharis) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon. I've seen these before, but I don't believe I've ever gotten a photo before. These stem galls are polythalamous which means they contain multiple larve, maybe one at each bend of the stem. I don't see any exit holes. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)

File.Name: f720a_f720-014.jpg
Notes:
19878140851 This is another fantastic diurnal (daytime) California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) working new flowers of non-native Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) on the edge of the garden. It was too fast for me on this cloudy day, but I still like the motion blur of this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2015)

We did get about 0.03 inches of rain last night. It was enough to get the porch wet and show up in the rain gauge, but that's all. It did the soul some good, but it doesn't help the wildlands.

I got all my photos today in the 20 foot span between the Cat Mint and the pendant branches of the White Sage which are nearly touching the ground from above the retaining wall. We're getting ready to head off for our cabin near Lone Pine in the Eastern Sierra to meet our kids and grandkids. We leave tomorrow morning. We'll be back in a week.
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Nepeta cataria

File.Name: f720b_f720-003.jpg
Notes: 19684740140 I'm pretty sure this is a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) perched on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden today. Their distinguishing feature is the hyaline (translucent) spots on its forewings which aren't really visible in this photo - there was no sun anyway on this cloudy day! But you can see three spots on the foreground wing that fit the bill. These butterflies have been hanging out here, while working the flowers of nearby hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2015)

File.Name: f720c_f720-009.jpg
Notes: 19864964382 Here is the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) trying to hide "ostrich style" on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. This is the first one I've seen near home. I've been concerned. Late summer wouldn't be the same without these songsters! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2015)

File.Name: f720d_f720-006.jpg
Notes: 19684079728 This is a handsome "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera, maybe the Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens). This one is on a stem of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. It definitely has 5 segments on each antennae, compare this photo. These pretty bugs have just appeared. They appear to be sucking the new seeds of the shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2015)

File.Name: f720e_f719-011.jpg
Notes: 19249036034 This is a large black Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) perched for a moment on a seed head of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. Maybe it wondering where have all the flowers gone? See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)

File.Name: f720f_f719-001.jpg
Notes: 19871390295 These are just about the last flowers of our native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) on the dry edge of our garden. Now the flower heads are becoming seed heads. It's too late for the Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) to collect nectar (see my [Next] photo), but other insects are moving in to harvest the seeds - see this photo. We'll put a bucket under the plant to collect seed. The secret to growing this plant seems to be to use fresh seed. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2015)

File.Name: f727a_f727-003.jpg
Notes:
19882727818 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a small Jumping Spider (Salticidae) holding a tiny Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insect as prey. I found this scene on a leaf in the mint patch of the garden this morning. I wasn't really looking for new photos today with so many trip photos to process, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2015)

We got home from our week in the Lone Pine desert last night. We'll soon be heading off for our cabin on the North Umpqua River in Oregon, probably leaving next Sunday. I love our mountains in the harsh dry summer, but it's also a good time to be somewhere else where's there still water in the river!

File.Name: f727b_f725-012.jpg
Notes: 20075713521 This is a slender little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - maybe Zelus tetracanthus in the subfamily Harpactorinae, a species that we also have at home. It has a small micro-moth (Lepidoptera) skewered with its ice pick-like proboscis. I found this scene on the shady side of a Common Rabbit Brush (Ericameria nauseosa or Chrysothamnus nauseosus, Asteraceae) that's just starting to flower. It was on the shady side of the flower head, so I bumped up the exposure to bring out some detail. I like the high-key lighting. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

File.Name: f727c_f723-004.jpg
Notes: 20069925975 This is a small Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly ovipositing on a still flowering stem of Desert Trumpet (Eriogonum inflatum) in the in the Polygonaceae) plant family. If you look very close, you can see a tiny egg below the tip of her abdomen. I was surprised to find both butterfly and flowers in the drought-dry desert! I haven't found them at home yet. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 23 July 2015)

File.Name: f727d_f724-054.jpg
Notes: 20074719531 I'm pretty sure this is a Reakirt's Blue (Hemiargus isola aka Echinargus isola) butterfly. Here are two views, showing both lower and upper wings. I found it working new flowers of native Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family along the Mount Whitney Trail not far from the start at Whitney Portal. This is a butterfly we don't have at home. (Mount Whitney Trail, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)
CommonNames: Reakirt's Blue; Hemp Dogbane; Indian Hemp

File.Name: f727e_f724-101.jpg
Notes: 19880185088 This is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly working new flowers of native White Yarrow (Achillea millefolium, Asteraceae). I saw the last one of these at home a couple of months ago, but I guess it's still spring up high in the mountains. I got this photo a ways up the Mt. Whitney trail, at the first stream crossing. (Mount Whitney Trail, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)

File.Name: f727f_f722-021.jpg
Notes:
19445201684 This is looking up at a thunderstorm on Mount Whitney, with a bit of the Alabama Hills visible in the foreground on the right. This was the last of the monsoon/hurricane weather that dropped some rain in Southern California last week. We did get a few drops at our cabin in the desert along Lone Pine Creek - but only a few drops. It looks wetter up high! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 22 July 2015)

It's shocking how little snow there is. Mount Whitney always has snow fields - but they are just about gone in this drought year. I can only see one little patch below the summit block.

File.Name: f728a_f728-010.jpg
Notes:
20094080412 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a neat Treehopper (family Membracidae) insect in the order Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera perched on a stem in the garden this morning. It looks large in my photo, but it's really quite small, a bit over 1/4 inch long. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2015)

I'm a little distracted because we're heading off to Oregon on Sunday and I still have a load of desert photos from our trip to our cabin near Lone Pine. I reckon we brought some desert heat home with us. It's been quite warm, and it's expected to get downright hot over the next few days.

File.Name: f728b_f724-045.jpg
Notes: 20101423265 This is a Sawfly Wasp (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) - probably genus Tenthredo. It's a wasp, but note that it has no sign of a "wasp waist" like all sawflies. I found this one on a flower head of Ranger's Buttons (Sphenosciadium capitellatum) in the Apiaceae plant family. What a cute name for a plant! (Whitney Portal, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)

File.Name: f728c_f725-027.jpg
Notes: 20100945165 This is a cute Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) on a desert shrub of (maybe) Bush Seepweed (Suaeda nigra) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. It was growing in a place that has some moisture. I'm never sure, but maybe this grasshopper is subfamily Melanoplinae. Alice suggests species Aeoloplides tenuipennis, and who am I to argue? It has three tiny red hitch-hiking mites (Acari). (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

Notes: 20073631866 This is a Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera. My first thought was a Band-winged Grasshopper in the subfamily Oedipodinae, but Alice thinks it's a Western Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus complanatipes) in the Melanoplinae. It's perched on some kind of desert shrub that I don't recognize from the photo. So many shrubs! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

File.Name: f728e_f725-024.jpg
Notes: 19911417930 A lone Damselfly (Zygoptera, Odonata) in the desert! Well, it's only about 1/4 mile away from Lone Pine Creek, so I guess it makes sense. This one is perched on a dead stem of Mojave Cleomella (Cleomella obtusifolia) in the Cleomaceae or Capparaceae plant family. It was working this summer-flowering plant quite diligently. I'll post another photo of the flowers in a day or two. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

File.Name: f728f_f723-028.jpg
Notes:
20104328561 Here are three different butterflies that played hide and seek with me during our desert trip last week. I found them all along different parts of Lone Pine Creek, from Whitney Portal to the Alabama Hills. I couldn't get close to any of them! From left to right, here are:

* Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly on Water Birch (Betula occidentalis, Betulaceae) by our cabin - at least that's what I'd call it at home;
* Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly on Water Birch (Betula occidentalis, Betulaceae) near Whitney Portal ; and
* Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly on a plant I don't recognize near Whitney Portal.

Missing is the lone Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) butterfly that I saw cruising through the trees many times near our cabin. It never landed for a photo. (Lone Pine Creek, Inyo County, California, 23-24 July 2015)
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Water Birch; Mourning Cloak; Water Birch; Lorquin's Admiral; Monarch Butterfly
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Betula occidentalis;  Betulaceae; Nymphalis antiopa; Betula occidentalis;  Betulaceae; Basilarchia lorquini; Danaus plexippus
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Betula occidentalis;  Betulaceae; Nymphalis antiopa; Betula occidentalis;  Betulaceae; Basilarchia lorquini; Danaus plexippus

File.Name: f729a_f728-016.jpg
Notes:
20102650776 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!
(I got this one yesterday.)

This is a stunning Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly showing all of its eyespots. I found this one on a flowering shrub of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's proboscis is going under its body, so I'm sure it's doing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. Most insects on this plant seem to know this trick. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2015)

It's interesting that we seem to have spring and summer populations of these butterflies. The butterflies seem smaller in spring and rarely visit flowers. Summer butterflies are larger and do visit flowers.

*Damn* There's a wildfire near our cabin on the North Umpqua River in Oregon. The "Cable Crossing Fire" is about 10 miles from our cabin. It closed the highway last night, and it's still active. This is where we plan to go in a few days. There's some info here - but not much.

File.Name: f729b_f725-015.jpg
Notes:
20120539602 Here are ripe fruit on a shrub of Desert Olive (Forestiera pubescens) in the Oleaceae plant family. The fruit look tasty, but they taste quite bitter. I wonder how they are pickled?? These are an attractive deciduous desert shrub. This is another photo I got in a dry wash - but I reckon there is still some moisture hidden in the ground. I can see "rolled-leaf" galls on some of the leaves to the left. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

It is pretty cool to find new flowers and fruit in the desert, in a drought year!

File.Name: f729c_f725-023.jpg
Notes: 20102037726 This is a new summer flower of Mojave Cleomella (Cleomella obtusifolia) in the Cleomaceae or Capparaceae plant family. The plant looks a lot like a Lotus aka Acmispon in the Fabaceae plant family, but it has free stamens that are not fused except at the base. It looks quite strange when you don't know it. This is another photo I got in a dry wash - but I reckon there is still some moisture hidden in the ground. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)
ScientificName: Cleomella obtusifolia; Cleomaceae; Capparaceae; Lotus; Acmispon; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Cleomella obtusifolia; Cleomaceae; Capparaceae; Lotus; Acmispon; Fabaceae

File.Name: f729d_f725-022.jpg
Notes: 19507044443 Here is a new flower head of Common Rabbit Brush (Ericameria nauseosa or Chrysothamnus nauseosus, Asteraceae). I got this photo in a dry wash - but I reckon there is still some moisture hidden in the ground. I often see this plant flowering along the roads later in the summer. I guess the roads draw off just enough extra water to give this plant a chance. It's pretty cool to find any summer flowers in the desert in a drought year! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

File.Name: f729e_f725-031.jpg
Notes: 19939782720 This is a beautiful plant with summer flowers of native Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family. You can see a pair of the long seedpods hanging below the flowers. I got this photo in a dry wash - but I reckon there is still some moisture hidden in the ground. This was an important plant for Native Americans who used it for fiber and cordage. We also have this plant at home, though I've never seen them so large - more than head high. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

File.Name: f729f_f725-029.jpg
Notes: 19940998549 This is the foliage of real native Wild Licorice (Glycyrrhiza lepidota) in the Fagaceae plant family. It smells nothing like that horrible black licorice candy they sell in stores, but the roots have an interesting taste that grows on you. See this photo of the flowers. I got this photo in a dry wash - but I reckon there is still some moisture hidden in the ground. I got all of my desert photos today in this same place. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

File.Name: f730a_f730-003.jpg
Notes:
20149587562 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I found this uncommon Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly perched on a mint leaf in the garden at home this morning. These butterflies are beautifully colored, though this specimen is worn and tattered. Compare this photo and this one. I don't usually find them in the mountains. Their host plant is Mistletoe (Phoradendron) which is uncommon in the mountains, but more common along the river. Yet here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2015)

It was almost "90 and 90" today with temps in the low 90s and relative humidity up to 82% (at the river). We had monsoon moisture today, with lots of thunder. There was no rain in our mountains, but I could see it coming down over Mono Creek in the backcountry.

There's a frustrating lack of news about the Cable Crossing Fire near our cabin in Oregon, but it sounds like it's burning away from us on the other side of the river. Now there's the Stouts Fire burning near Canyonville and maybe a separate Milo Fire. Yipes - it was 108∞ in Roseburg today! Despite the heat, we're committed to going up there on Sunday.
Locality: at the river).  We had monsoon moisture today

File.Name: f730b_f725-040.jpg
Notes: 19534750164 Here is one of my twin grandkids on the funky bridge over Lone Pine Creek at our cabin. The desert beckons! The trees are Willow and Water Birch. It's a wild world out there. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)

File.Name: f730c_f722-007.jpg
Notes: 19969176798 Here are my grandkids in the rocks of the Alabama Hills. What fun! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 22 July 2015)

File.Name: f730d_f724-057.jpg
Notes: 19535821083 Here are male staminate catkins and female pistillate spiny burs on a shrub of native Bush Chinquapin (Chrysolepis sempivirens) in the Fagaceae plant family. They're not typical flowers, but they work for this shrub! (Mount Whitney Trail, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)

File.Name: f730e_f724-050.jpg
Notes: 19968214308 This is a "beautiful bug butt" shot of a Sawfly Wasp (family Tenthredinidae, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) - probably genus Tenthredo. It's a wasp, but note that it has no sign of a "wasp waist" like all sawflies. I found this one on a flower head of Ranger's Buttons (Sphenosciadium capitellatum) in the Apiaceae plant family, see my [Previous] photo. HBBBT! (Whitney Portal, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)
ScientificName: Tenthredinidae; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Tenthredo; Sphenosciadium capitellatum; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Tenthredinidae; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Tenthredo; Sphenosciadium capitellatum; Apiaceae

File.Name: f730f_f724-038.jpg
Notes: 20161623841 This is a whole inflorescence of native Ranger's Buttons (Sphenosciadium capitellatum) in the Apiaceae plant family. The plant looks vaguely like a composite, but those divided leaves looks parsley-like. What a cute name for a plant! The flowers are inhabited by many tiny Rove Beetles (Staphylinidae, Coleoptera). (Whitney Portal, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)

File.Name: f731a_f730-030.jpg
Notes:
20160729496 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!
(I got this one yesterday.)

This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - on summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). This is the first one I've seen this year, at least in our mountains. I hope there will be many more as the season progresses. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 July 2015)

Hot and muggy weather continues - up to 99∞ and 80% humidity at the river today where I was. Despite the weather, we're committed to head off to Oregon on Sunday. Yipes - it's been 113∞ in Redding, 108∞ in Medford and Roseburg, and even 103∞ in Portland over the last couple of days! But the weather is expected to cool down over the next few days, so we should be OK.

The rest of today's photos are some of the interesting plant galls I found on our desert trip last week. I reckon that's the end of my trip photos for a few weeks. Tomorrow I'll post some of the photos I taken this week, and then we're on the road.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: f731b_f725-036.jpg
Notes: 20192264751 I believe these are old galls of the Spiny Bud Gall Wasp (Diplolepus bicolor), Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) growing on a stem of Interior Rose (Rosa woodsii var. ultramontana) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can see a chewed exit hole in the gall on the left. I got this photo in a dry wash - but I reckon there is still some moisture hidden in the ground. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 25 July 2015)
CommonNames: Spiny Bud Gall Wasp; Interior Rose
ScientificName: Diplolepus bicolor; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rosa woodsii; ultramontana; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Diplolepus bicolor; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rosa woodsii; ultramontana; Rosaceae

File.Name: f731c_f723-021.jpg
Notes: 19563687934 Here are two different kinds of gall I found on Great Basin Sagebrush aka Big Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) in the Asteraceae plant family. This sagebrush collects many different galls and my book is rather skimpy in its treatment. It's enough to say that these are the plant-built homes of different insects, probably mostly Gall Midges (Cecidomyiidae) and Tephritid Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae), both in the order Diptera. They both appear to be stem galls. Neither one does the plant much good. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 23 July 2015)
CommonNames: Great Basin Sagebrush; Big Sagebrush; Gall Midges; Tephritid Fruit Flies
ScientificName: Artemisia tridentata; Asteraceae; Cecidomyiidae; Tephritidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Artemisia tridentata; Asteraceae; Cecidomyiidae; Tephritidae; Diptera

File.Name: f731d_f724-105.jpg
Notes: 20191484291 This is a gall among the flower buds of some kind of Rabbitbrush, probably the Common Rabbit Brush (Ericameria nauseosa or Chrysothamnus nauseosus, Asteraceae), but maybe not. It looks to me like the gall of a Tephritid Fly (family Tephritidae, Diptera), though I'm not sure. It turns out that Rabbitbrush has many different kinds of galls, like the Great Basin Sagebrush in my [Next] photo. (Mount Whitney Trail, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)
CommonNames: Rabbitbrush; Common Rabbit Brush; Tephritid Fly
ScientificName: Ericameria nauseosa; Chrysothamnus nauseosus; Asteraceae; Tephritidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Ericameria nauseosa; Chrysothamnus nauseosus; Asteraceae; Tephritidae; Diptera

File.Name: f731e_f724-015.jpg
Notes: 19998643259 I guess these are galls of a Willow Leaf Gall Sawfly (genus Pontania, "Symphyta", Hymenoptera) - maybe Pontania viminalis? The galls have little presence on the upper side of the leaf. I found these galls on some kind of Willow (genus Salix, Salicaceae) along Lone Pine Creek, maybe a Red Willow (Salix laevigata). See my [Previous] photo. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)
CommonNames: Willow Leaf Gall Sawfly; Willow; Red Willow
ScientificName: Pontania; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Pontania viminalis; Salix; Salicaceae; Salix laevigata
associatedTaxa: Pontania; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Pontania viminalis; Salix; Salicaceae; Salix laevigata

File.Name: f731f_f724-018.jpg
Notes: 19996880688 Here are leafy branches of some kind of Willow (genus Salix, Salicaceae) along Lone Pine Creek. Maybe it's Red Willow (Salix laevigata)? It already has buds for next year, though I've never seen it in flower. There's a neat gall of the Willow Rosette Gall Midge (Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) low down on the screen-right branch, and a different gall that looks like red candy stuck to a leaf in the upper-right. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 24 July 2015)
CommonNames: Willow; Red Willow; Willow Rosette Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salix; Salicaceae; Salix laevigata; Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: f801a_f801-014.jpg
Notes:
19591709534 This is a handsome little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched with wings open (for just a moment!) on a spent flower head of Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in our garden. It was really interested in the Thyme (genus Thymus) flowers nearby. They are pretty little checkerspot-like butterflies. They seem pretty uncommon in this drought year, but maybe more will appear later in the year? (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2015)

It was still pretty warm today, in the 90s at home, but I do believe it's starting to cool down. I hope so, because it was still 103∞ in Medford today! Despite the heat, we're heading off to our cabin on the North Umpqua River in Oregon tomorrow. I'll be back in a few weeks, hopefully with a few new photos.

File.Name: f801b_f801-008.jpg
Notes: 19591232484 This is a handsome little Fly (order Diptera) on a mint leaf in the garden. I believe it's an atypical Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) without bristles, maybe genus Gymnoclytia in the subfamily Phasiinae. I noticed this fly - and then waited quite a while for it to appear again in a different place. This is a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2015)

File.Name: f801c_f727-006.jpg
Notes: 20213136195 This is a fantastic nymph of a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a mint leaf in the garden. I first found these on seed heads of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae) along with adults of (I think) the Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug (Thyanta pallidovirens (family Pentatomidae). See this photo. I thought at first they might be the same, but the adults have five antennae segments and the nymph only has four segments. They can't change that, can they? Whatever it is, it's really handsome. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2015)
CommonNames: White Sage; Western Red-shouldered Plant Bug
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Thyanta pallidovirens; Pentatomidae

File.Name: f801d_f730-017.jpg
Notes: 20204593952 This is looking closer at a new flower head of native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family - with a little Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) waiting for a careless insect to come by on the left. See my [Previous] photo for the whole shrub. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 July 2015)
ScientificName: Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: f801e_f730-020.jpg
Notes: 20025955059 This is a whole shrub of native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a beautiful shrub with especially handsome foliage, 7 or 8 feet tall. This one looks part dead, no doubt drought stress in this drier that usual summer. Compare this photo from last September. This shrub only grows in the higher mountains. You have to admire these late-season plants that flower in mid-summer, even in a drought year. They keep the insects alive! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 July 2015)

File.Name: f801f_f730-024.jpg
Notes: 20217945281 Here are two photos of native Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. It really has bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. It also has distinctive "ternate" leaves in whorls of three, visible on the left. It also has classic "hummingbird flowers" - though I only saw small wild bees visiting on the day I was there. These are growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 July 2015)

File.Name: f825a_f815-038.jpg
Notes:
20893088611 This is a remarkable female Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly on a new flower head of non-native Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got this photo at about 1500 feet elevation in the Oregon woods. The females look very different from the males, which are more like the butterfly in my [Previous] photo. I assumed at first that that one was a male, but I can see now that it's a different species. Fritillaries are a difficult group! I'll post a photo of what I believe is the female in a day or two. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)

(D'oh - I had my males & females reversed. It's the FEMALE that is colored striking black and white, and the male that is more orange. I no doubt have this wrong in other photos in the archive.)

We got home from Oregon on Sunday. It was hot when we left, but it cooled down for most of our visit, at least until the last week. It's drought-dry in Oregon too, but having water in the river helps mitigate it! It felt later in the year than it was. Flowers and insects were mostly scarce, but I did get some nice photos to share. It will take me a while to catch up.
Locality: D'oh - I had my males & females reversed.  It's the FEMALE that is colored striking black and white

File.Name: f825b_f812-057.jpg
Notes:
20262928824 This is some sort of Greater Fritillary (genus Speyeria) butterfly working a native Thistle (Cirsium?) in the Asteraceae plant family. At first I assumed without thinking that it's a female Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly to go with the male in my [Next] photo, but now that I look close I can see that it's something different. Fritillaries are a difficult group, so I won't try to guess. I got this photo in the Oregon woods at about 5,000 feet elevation. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)

I don't know the butterfly or the flower - but I don't live in Oregon, so I don't have to! If you can help with IDs, I would appreciate it.
CommonNames: Greater Fritillary; Thistle; Great Spangled Fritillary

File.Name: f825c_f814-010.jpg
Notes: 20698120319 This is a subtle but pretty Golden Hairstreak (Habrodais grunus) butterfly perched on an alder leaf along Boundary Road in the Oregon woods. It was a gloomy day. This butterfly would look brighter in the sun. Apparently these butterflies use Golden Chinquapin (Castanopsis chrysophylla, Fagaceae) as their host plant in Oregon, and it was growing nearby. My book says that they also occur on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in California, though I've never seen them at home. I'll look for them now that I know what to look for. (Boundary Road, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 14 August 2015)
CommonNames: Golden Hairstreak; Golden Chinquapin; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak
ScientificName: Habrodais grunus; Castanopsis chrysophylla; Fagaceae; Quercus chrysolepis
associatedTaxa: Habrodais grunus; Castanopsis chrysophylla; Fagaceae; Quercus chrysolepis

File.Name: f825d_f815-021.jpg
Notes: 20696430380 This is a pretty little Checkered-Skipper (genus Pyrgus) butterfly perched on an old flower head of non-native Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa) in the Asteraceae plant family in the Oregon woods at about 1500 feet elevation. I can't say that I know Oregon butterflies, but this might be the Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly with those blue hairs on its body. On the other hand, the dark underwings look more like the Two-banded Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus ruralis). I reckon it's one of those. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes
CommonNames: Checkered-Skipper; Spotted Knapweed; Common Checkered-Skipper; Two-banded Checkered-Skipper
ScientificName: Pyrgus; Centaurea maculosa; Asteraceae; Pyrgus communis; Pyrgus ruralis
associatedTaxa: Pyrgus; Centaurea maculosa; Asteraceae; Pyrgus communis; Pyrgus ruralis

File.Name: f825e_f815-031.jpg
Notes: 20697176679 I believe this is a Common Wood Nymph (Cercyonis pegala) butterfly perched on a leaf in the Oregon woods at about 1500 feet elevation. They have two eyespots under their forewings that are about the same size. (The lower eye is hidden, but you can see that it's there.) This distinguishes them from the Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) that we have at home, with the lower eye smaller. These butterflies (both of them) are hard to photograph. They don't visit flowers and they don't pose for photos. I was lucky with this one. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes
CommonNames: Common Wood Nymph; Great Basin Wood-Nymph

File.Name: f825f_f815-016.jpg
Notes: 20890975211 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a new flower of non-native Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa) in the Asteraceae plant family, though it's not at all spotted. This is a nasty weed that takes over clearings in the woodland. At least it's not it's not spiky like our invasive Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) at home, and the flowers are quite pretty. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2015)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Spotted Knapweed; Yellow Star Thistle
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Centaurea maculosa; Asteraceae; Centaurea solstitialis
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Centaurea maculosa; Asteraceae; Centaurea solstitialis

File.Name: f826a_f826-036.jpg
Notes:
20727374949 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I found this different large Praying Mantis (order Mantodea) in the mountains back home. By coincidence, this one also has the remains of a Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly in its grasp - just like my [Previous] photo! This one is sprawled across a late-summer flowering plant of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. I don't know (or much care!) what kind of mantid it is, and I won't figure it out tonight. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 August 2015)

We got home from Oregon late on Sunday - just in time for a new heat wave. It's been warm, up to 100∞ by the river today, but a bit cooler in the mountains where an odd wind has been blowing from the ocean despite high pressure to the east. I'm thankful for small favors, but it was still in the low 90s at home. The woods feel very dry, and I'm trying not to think of that F-word that rhymes with "ire".
ScientificName: Mantodea; Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Mantodea; Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: f826b_f818-023.jpg
Notes: 20921028971 Just a few minutes after finishing off the Gray Hairstreak butterfly in my previous photos, I noticed that the same large Praying Mantis (order Mantodea) had captured another butterfly - this time a Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae)! This is really nature red in tooth and claw as the poet Alfred Tennyson said. This is a pretty gruesome photo. Look how the butterfly's head is dangling of to the side. They are effective predators! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2015)

File.Name: f826c_f818-018.jpg
Notes: 20726704499 This is the same large Praying Mantis (order Mantodea) in my [Previous] photos holding its prey up in the air. It has an unfortunate Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly, the same one I show three photos back. The mantid doesn't have black spots under its front legs, but it does have a plate on its chest that looks like a bulletproof vest. That's no doubt a clue as to which mantid it is. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2015)

File.Name: f826d_f818-017.jpg
Notes: 20725142168 This is the large Praying Mantis (order Mantodea) in my [Previous] photo making a move on the Gray Hairstreak butterfly in the upper-right. It's lightning fast! See my [Next] photo. This is the same butterfly I show two photos back, on a flower of non-native Fuller's Teasel (Dipsacus fullonum) in the Dipsacacea plant family. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2015)
CommonNames: Praying Mantis; Gray Hairstreak; Fuller's Teasel

File.Name: f826e_f818-016.jpg
Notes:
20920073691 This is a large Praying Mantis (order Mantodea) on a plant of non-native Fuller's Teasel (Dipsacus fullonum) in the Dipsacacea plant family in the Oregon woods. This one does not have black spots under its front legs. I don't know (or much care!) what kind it is - but it's a mighty predator. See my [Next] few photos, and remember the Gray Hairstreak butterfly in my [Previous] photo on the next plant over to the right. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2015)

Hah - is it Praying Mantis or Preying Mantis? I always forget and both names are appropriate!

File.Name: f826f_f818-014.jpg
Notes: 20919660501 This is a common Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working flowers of non-native Fuller's Teasel (Dipsacus fullonum) in the Dipsacacea plant family growing in the Oregon woods. I found this one on the cleared (and weedy) power line easement about 1/4 mile from our house. We have the same butterflies at home. This pretty creature will meet a bad end - see my [Next] few photos. Yep, this is the same one. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2015)

File.Name: f827a_f827-009.jpg
Notes:
20751676060 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a pretty little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly on a new flower head of a Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm not sure what kind of skipper it is, so I'll leave it at that. I'm actually more interested in the flower. I love these late-summer flowers that choose this time to flower, even in a drought year. They keep the insects alive! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2015)

It was downright HOT today - in the mid 90s at home and 105∞ down by the river a few miles away! But mad dogs and photographers do go out in the midday sun to see what they can find. The hot weather is expected to last another day or two before a Pacific trough knocks it down. I'll be glad when it's done. In the meantime, I'm trying not to think of that F-word that rhymes with "ire".

File.Name: f827b_f814-020.jpg
Notes: 20318279683 I'm pretty sure this dragonfly is an Eight-spotted Skimmer (Libellula forensis) in the Libellulidae family of the order Odonata. I got this photo along Boundary Road on an overcast day that felt like summer rain, and I guess it was grounded by the funky weather. It's perched on a sedge leaf coming up through Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor aka Rubus armeniacus, Rosaceae), dusty from traffic on this gravel road. You can see a bit of a berry on the far right. (Boundary Road, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 14 August 2015)
ScientificName: Libellula forensis; Libellulidae; Odonata; Rubus discolor; Rubus armeniacus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Libellula forensis; Libellulidae; Odonata; Rubus discolor; Rubus armeniacus; Rosaceae

File.Name: f827c_f812-001.jpg
Notes: 20752344569 I believe this is another Cross Spider (Araneus diadematus or kin) in the Araneidae family of orb-weaving spiders. This one is no doubt a female. It's sitting on an orb web, and you can also clearly see its dingus-like "epigynum" at the base of its abdomen, just above the rear legs. This organ is the target for the male's sperm. See my [Previous] photo for *maybe* a male. This spider is quite large, and I kept running into them as I walked through the woods - often hanging on to my tripod legs. Sure it's spooky when you walk through a web... *Where's the spider!* (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 12 August 2015)

File.Name: f827d_f804-001.jpg
Notes: 20750714268 I guess this is a Cross Spider (Araneus diadematus or kin) in the Araneidae family of orb-weaving spiders. This one might be a male, perched on a leaf rather than a web. (See my [Next] photo.) It's quite large, and I kept running into them as I walked through the woods - often hanging on to my tripod legs. Sure it's spooky when you walk through a web... *Where's the spider!* This was the first photo I took on our recent Oregon trip, and it's a keeper. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2015)

File.Name: f827e_f811-012.jpg
Notes: 20751697289 This is a butt shot of a well-camouflaged little yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae). It's getting ready to balloon away (to the left) from this flower of non-native Tansy Ragwort (Senecio jacobaea, Asteraceae) in the Oregon woods. HBBBT! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 11 August 2015)

File.Name: f827f_f812-026.jpg
Notes:
20945400641 I believe this is a male Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly on a native Thistle (Cirsium?) in the Asteraceae plant family. I can't identify the thistle, so help will be appreciated! I got this photo at about 5000 feet elevation in the Oregon woods. The males look very different from the females, see this photo. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 12 August 2015)

D'oh - I had my males & females reversed when I posted my previous photo. It's the FEMALE that is colored striking black and white, and the male that is more orange like this one. I no doubt have this wrong in other photos in the archive. In my defense, fritillaries are a difficult group that we don't often see at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. Live and learn!

File.Name: f828a_f828-015.jpg
Notes:
20776581030 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's perched on summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 August 2015)

It was very hot today - up to 106∞ at the river where I was, and even up to 100∞ on our upstairs porch at home. Yipes - it was still 80∞ at midnight last night! Mad dogs and nature photographers go out in the midday sun... and I did my duty today. I was feeling a bit sun-sick after a while, and I reckon I earned all these photos. I'll get back to my Oregon shots tomorrow.

File.Name: f828b_f828-021.jpg
Notes: 20776273280 This is an uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly sitting on a new flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name, but there's nothing toxic about it. This one didn't want to open its wings, but you can still see the "metal marks" on its wings, like silver threads woven into the scales. (OK, the silver threads look gray in this photo taken in the shade, but they're there!) These butterflies have been having a good year, both in June and July, and now again. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 August 2015)
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Calephelis nemesis californica; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

This is a tachinid fly without bristles. This one is working flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. File.Name: f828e_f828-035.jpg
Notes:
20937117716 This is a striking Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera on new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. The beetle is quite large, about an inch long without the antennae that go on for another inch. There are many different kinds of these beetles, so I'll leave it at that. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 August 2015)

This Isocoma goldenbush and the Scale Broom in my next photos are maybe the most important late-summer flowering shrubs in our area. *Lots* of insects wait for them.
